fields.Project Description:
“Only Players Left Alive” is a participatory post-apocalyptic playground.

The project will unfold as a series of immersive zones inside a modest physical structure — one or several rooms transformed into remnants of a future civilization. The aesthetic draws from Bombay Beach itself: found materials, traces of former life, handmade signage, ritual objects.

The environment is divided into symbolic zones: a DMV that issues new identities, a Church for confessions and invented rituals, a Market where participants exchange stories, gestures, or invented currency, and open areas for spontaneous encounters. Professional performers and facilitators inhabit the world continuously, guiding interactions while preserving the internal logic of the society.

The soundscape may include fragmented radio transmissions, ambient music, and recorded voices reflecting on memory and survival. Lighting remains soft and transitional, encouraging a sense of suspension between reality and fiction.

Only Players Left Alive functions as a temporary autonomous social organism, where participation becomes both an artistic gesture and a tool for self-discovery.

This is not entertainment. It is a living question:

“If the world ended tomorrow, what kind of society would we choose to rebuild?”
fields.Support Needed: The see may sink tomorrow. The ocean will remain.
fields.Notes: dulcinee poc
fields.Abridged Project Text: This is a post-apocalyptic social simulation. Playground where visitors receive new identities and enter temporary social roles. Through immersive interactions, rituals, and exchanges, participants experiment with identity, explore connection, choice, and survival.
fields.Technical Support: Sound speakers, indoor space, printing
fields.Audience Engagement:
Upon entering, visitors receive a new identity generated through a short AI-based prompt or questionnaire. They are assigned a social role within the surviving society — such as a memory keeper, emotion smuggler, ritual witness, body archivist, translator of desire, or time traveler. Each role carries a simple task or intention that encourages interaction with others.

The experience is non-linear. Visitors may stay briefly or linger, drifting between observation and participation. There is no single narrative arc; instead, the project evolves through accumulated interactions.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Set in Bombay Beach — a place shaped by environmental change, artistic reinvention, and temporary community — the project resonates with the surrounding landscape. It asks participants to imagine not only what survives collapse, but what becomes possible afterward. The work invites reflection on identity, adaptability, intimacy, and collective imagination, empathy, turning play into a method of inquiry. 
In my perspective it resonates with a theme “The Last Judgement”.
You can play and experiment only in judgement-free environments.
fields.Risk & Safety: No
fields.Previous Showing (Grant):
This project I crafted specifically for BBB.
The narrative and performances will reflect the festival's values and themes. And the number of participants is limited only by the space's capacity.

Previously I made just immersive role-plays for 20-30 ppl with the themes of “Sin City” and “The Glitch in the Matrix”.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Bombay Beach has been an important point of reference for me since 2024. I am drawn not only to the work shown there, but to the people and the way the community is formed — through collaboration, experimentation, and mutual respect. I know several people involved with the Bombay Beach Institute personally and have long felt aligned with its values and approach.

I regularly attend events connected to the Bombay Beach Biennale, and over time this place has become a meaningful part of my creative environment. I value being part of something larger than my individual practice and contributing to a shared process rather than working in isolation.

This project feels timely, as I will be relocating to New York shortly after Bombay Beach. I see this work as a way to contribute to the community and to leave behind something considered and relevant, reflecting the core of my practice: work with identity, presence, and social interaction.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
I believe that carefully designed experiences and games can be a form of art — not only as entertainment, but as a way to invite reflection and create social impact. This perspective is central to my practice.

For me, Bombay Beach represents freedom and an expanded understanding of what art can be. The Biennale supports work that questions, engages people directly, and exists in dialogue with its environment and community.

In the context of impermanence and post-apocalyptic landscapes, Bombay Beach feels like an ideal experimental site for participatory and socially engaged art. I believe this project belongs in a setting like this — among people open to experimentation, reflection, and shared experience — where meaningful social impact can emerge.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Part way through. I already crafted the narrative. I already know what immersive actors, performers and facilitators I would like to invite. Then it depends on a space.

fields.Theme Engagement:
The coffee ceremony at the center of Cafe Bosna is judgment slowed down. Coffee grounds are read not to predict fate, but to reflect on patterns, choices, and unseen influences. This quiet divination resists spectacle and urgency, creating space to pause, observe, and witness without verdict. Judgment becomes awareness rather than punishment.

As a Temporary Autonomous Zone, Cafe Bosna suspends dominant systems of value and evaluation. No one is ranked or assessed. Roles blur. Performers are not elevated above participants. The space dissolves binaries - sacred and profane, observer and observed, innocent and guilty. In a culture saturated with constant judgment—social, algorithmic, political—this suspension becomes a subtle but radical gesture.

At Bombay Beach, a landscape shaped by collapse and reinvention, Cafe Bosna reframes the “last judgment” not as an ending, but as what comes after. What remains is relation: shared presence, ritual, memory, and the capacity to gather, reflect, and dance together in the aftermath.

fields.Project Description:
My persona “Rev. Earl E Gaytes”, an East Texas Televangelist trying to crowdfund a Private Jet, will be holding a satirical Quesadilla Communion at 11am. There are a couple satirical hymns, a sermon, an offering, and an emotionally manipulative altar call asking for Cheesus to fill their hole…in their soul. There may be other cheese based religions, but his interpretation on religion is the one true whey–-the rest are curds set apart…to be fried.
The plan is that the Temple of the Scientific Method would host Saturday morning at 11am, the day after the drag show–using the same chairs and space from the community center. Will be sourcing a grill and volunteers for quesadilla making and a dash of planted participation for scripture reading.
fields.Abridged Project Text: “Come to Cheesus” in an Old Time Religion styled tent revival as Rev. Earl E. Gaytes preaches about the one true whey of salvation. Expect a cheesy sermon, boxed wine communion, some food & fellowship to follow.
fields.Technical Support:
As a part of the sound committee I’ll already have the sound system from the drag show.
With it being in the daylight, I would need printed out posters/vinyl signs for some powerpoint slides/banners…potentially in an satirical Westboro Baptist protest sign aesthetic (also to potentially be adopted with/copromoted with or at the drag show).
Looking to source a blackstone from my camp at Mars. Tortillas. Shredded Cheese. Boxed Wine/Cab Sav Franzia.
fields.Audience Engagement: It’ll be performance art out in the open. People may see vinyl posters printed out with Cheese related PowerPoint verses if passing by the Temple to the Scientific Method, participants singing along, reading verse, and finally eating quesadillas & some boxed wine.
fields.Theme Engagement: Why wait until the Last Judgement when you can judge someone today? As a 2010 graduate of Christ for the Nation Institute of Dallas, Texas–-I’ve had a lot of fun and healing from writing and performing as an extension of my @TinyClosetOnWheels art car–that has showed up at Bombay Beach for the last two Biennales and convivium. There’s a theme of binary worldviews, Rev Earl’s vs the world, and the end-times. The absurdity of a cheese-based fundamentalist religious cult has existed in some other contexts & festivals, but so do most cults, building off and appropriating another tradition, just to claim exclusivity. Mimicking the tropes of certain brands of fundamentalism demonstrates reproducibility.
fields.Risk & Safety: No. Other than regular safeguards tied to the operation of the Propane Griddle.
fields.Location Submittal: Temple of the Scientific Method
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): I performed some of it at Saguaro Man April 2025, it is performance art itself so its context is primarily meant for those in-person. I plan to have more posters, change up songs and tailor it for the context. https://www.instagram.com/reel/DJUsK3KRYT1/?utm_source=ig_web_copy_link&igsh=MzRlODBiNWFlZA==
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): As a gay guy who grew up in the Assemblies of God in Iowa, or as I like to say ‘former’ Assemblies of God (FAG), my religious upbringing has been central to my art, including my“Tiny Closet On Wheels” satirical mancave. I have performed this at a festival in Arizona, a clip of which I’ve recorded and have posted on my Instagram.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): Religious extremism can occur anywhere, and is central to political power on the national stage. LGBT+ people are under attack, even if people don’t recognize or consider Southern California to be the case, internet algorithms can decentralize extremism and radicalize where not always expected.

fields.Project Name: Community Patchwork/Quilting Bee of the Damned
fields.Project Description:
I am bringing supplies, instructions, and creating a space where a community tapestry is made throughout the weekend.
The first part is a discussion of the ethos of the project: community building, environmental stewardship, a tangible and meditative craft. I explain that the end product is a tapestry that everyone contributes to using second-hand textiles (fabric, ribbons, clothing, yarn, embroidery floss, buttons, etc) that are cast-aways from their original projects. With these materials, they create a patch. There are suggestions for size and shape of the patch, but nothing strict is set in place.
Those that are ready, I give safety reminders and let them create. 
Those that need some guidance get instruction on basic hand stitches and practice. These instructions are reinforced with zines specific to the project. In addition to sewing instructions, the zine will also include a timeline, safety tips, and the overall goal.
For those less inclined towards sewing, there are also fabric pens and a hot glue gun (if electricity is available).
Ideal circumstances/planning: there are multiple facilitated sessions to stop by and hand-sew/embroider patches a few times throughout the weekend (maybe 3?). Participants can even take some supplies and equipment with them, and return their finished product later. On Sunday, there is a final putting together of the patches and a reveal. I will be creating a patch that has an embroidered explanation of the project.
As participants finish their patches, they are invited to sew their patch to the tapestry, or request that I do that for them. Participants can also serve in the role of hand-sewing patches to the tapestry for those that don’t want to..
I’ll offer examples and inspiration/ideas for patches, but I prefer to see what emerges when audience members are left to their own devices. The final tapestry will probably be crooked and disjointed, and will drape weirdly, but that’s part of the fun!
I have considered quilting and also bringing a sewing machine, but decided against those options, given that those would be more complicated projects. I accept that I might never again see the equipment I offer for use by audience members (scissors, pins, markers, etc), or it might get damaged or broken.
Time is flexible.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Together we steward cast-aside fibers and create a Biennale tapestry. Sew in community, and merge tattered scraps into something new. Unlike the gods who would condemn and abandon the world they created, we explore the latent potential in each textile.
fields.Technical Support:
I’ll need a setting that can be protected from the wind, since there will be small pieces of fabric that can easily be blown away.
Ideal: electricity/outlet for a hot glue gun, lighting if scheduled later in the day, tables to work on, chairs for folks to sit in, trash bins. 
The final thing: a long-term resting/display place. I would be tickled to take home this tapestry. However, if there would be a place that this could hang or work as a throw blanket, I’d love that. Unfortunately, I don’t think it would survive the outdoor elements at Bombay Beach, but might be a good addition to an inside area.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Audience engagement is crucial! If no one participates, then I guess I’m making my own tapestry.
If audience members just want to watch, that’s okay. They will have the most fun if they go with the activity and make a patch that gets added to the tapestry. I hope that they’ll take the piece with them and work on it throughout the weekend. 
There will be community conversation, or silence.
They will return the piece, in whatever state it’s in, on Sunday. There will be a final hour where we work together to fashion the installation together for its final reveal.
fields.Theme Engagement:
These scraps have been cast aside into a heap of the damned. They are the imperfect bits that no one wants when the prime pieces have been cut away. 

I reject the utility of judgement. Analysis and heuristics and frameworks have their place, but they serve more to describe than to cast aside. Let’s celebrate what we see in the scraps at our hands. If we continue the cycle of not working with what we have, we are no better than those pompous gods who exclude and excise. We will destroy the world.
fields.Risk & Safety:
Potentially hazardous materials: scissors, sewing needles, sewing pins, seam ripper, hot glue gun
There will be warning signage, info in the zine, as well verbal warnings/instructions about pokes from needles and pins, cuts from the scissors, and burns from the hot glue gun. If people appear intoxicated, I’ll ask them not to participate at this time.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, this is a novel art installation.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): Spring 2024 changed my life: I went to BBB 2024 (my first BBB), and I met a textile artist who taught about how textile art can be environmental stewardship, creativity, and community. I want to bring these together at BBB 2026 and give back and create the space to celebrate all of these things.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): Together, the ragtag audience and artist gather together in the patchwork environment of Bombay Beach, and make whatever is going to emerge from these imperfect melange of fabrics. It’s things thrown together that one might not expect beauty from, but we’re going to do what we can to find the beauty.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I could drive to Bombay Beach right now and do this for a handful of people with the supplies I own. However, to scale it for the Biennale, I’d want to purchase additional supplies and prepare in some additional ways. I am a skilled hobby sewist, and conf

fields.Project Description:
okay you is a morning movement practice taking place at 10am Saturday and Sunday at Henderson Hall for Mz Aqua Community Enrichment Program.
Each session is structured around interoception: the body's capacity to sense its own interior states. Most movement practices train people to produce external shapes. This one trains attention inward: to breath, to sensation, to the felt experience of being alive in a body right now. The instruction is cue-light and sensation-focused. Participants are guided through breathwork, gentle vinyasa sequencing, and extended rest.

The sonic environment is integral to the practice. Sessions are scored with ambient music from Falling Over Water: slow, textural, and deliberately non-narrative, creating conditions for sustained interior attention rather than distraction or entertainment. On at least one morning a collaborating guitarist may join, extending the sonic dimension into something closer to live composition.

The timing is deliberate. Ten in the morning catches people before the day's programming fully ignites. The idea is to offer a way to arrive in the body before everything else begins.

okay you is part of a broader research practice exploring psycho-spiritual ergonomics: the design of experiences and environments that develop human capacity rather than extract from it. This is that work made simple and available to anyone who shows up.
fields.Support Needed: Mz Aqua Community Enrichment Program
fields.Abridged Project Text:
okay you, you're ok

Come breathe, stretch, and start your day with one of the oldest calm technologies: gentle yoga and ambient music designed to develop interoceptive awareness and genuine presence. No experience necessary. Bring a mat if you have one.
fields.Technical Support:
Indoor space at Henderson Hall. The room should accommodate a number of participants lying down with space to extend fully in all directions.

Electricity would be a plus, as I will bring some speakers and equipment for the music.

Participants will be advised to bring a mat or something to lie on. No other equipment required.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Drop-in, fully participatory, no registration. People arrive, find space on the floor, and are guided through the session from there.
The offering is designed to be genuinely accessible: gentle enough for someone who has never done yoga, substantive enough to be interesting for someone who has practiced for years. The instruction is sensation-focused: participants are guided toward felt interior experience rather than correct form.

Some people will arrive intentionally. Some will wander in, unsure what they walked into, and stay. Both are ideal. The session asks nothing of participants except presence and a degree of curiosity about their own interior states. 
Morning timing is deliberate: catching people before the day has fully claimed them, offering a low-stakes on-ramp into embodied awareness before the rest of the programming begins.
fields.Theme Engagement:
In the moment of the Last Judgement, all is reduced to presence. 

okay you is a morning practice in exactly that reduction. Not as eschatology but as somatic discipline. Interoception is the capacity to perceive your own interior states honestly: what the breath is actually doing, what the nervous system is actually holding. It is presence as a trainable skill.

The body offers a version of The Last Judgement every morning. okay you is just a structured invitation to show up for it.
fields.Risk & Safety: No
fields.Location Submittal: Henderson Hall, 2&B, 2184 2nd
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I am visiting from elsewhere

fields.Project Name: ‘All In’ at the End of the World
fields.Project Description: At the edge of the Salton Sea, Frick Frack Blackjack brings a cashless casino to Bombay Beach. Players wager objects—stories, relics, dares—instead of money. Beneath flickering lights and desert wind, each hand becomes theater. The space unfolds from open daytime tables to high-stakes sunset rounds, transforming risk into ritual and strangers into co-conspirators in a temporary autonomous zone/ outlaw  speakeasy.
fields.Support Needed: We’re flexible and excited to collaborate. We’re happy to bring the fully realized Frick Frack Blackjack experience, or create something entirely new and site-specific that has never been presented before. We can scale the project to fit the resources available and are committed to working thoughtfully within whatever budget is provided.
fields.Notes:
3/16: confirmed they will be in attendance. They didn’t ask about schedule or placement..
3/14: confirming they are still coming
fields.Abridged Project Text: At the edge of the Salton Sea, Frick Frack Blackjack rises from the ruins of Bombay Beach. No cash. No limits. Wager a secret, a relic, a dare. In a town shaped by decay, we gamble with meaning and where risk becomes ritual and luck is whatever you’re brave enough to bet.
fields.Technical Support: Electricity for lights and sound. If an abandoned building is available, we’d love to transform it into a casino. If not, we can create a self-contained pop-up outdoors, adapting the build to whatever location best fits the aesthetic and flow.
fields.Audience Engagement: People don’t just watch. they play. Guests sit at the table, wager personal objects, tell stories, and negotiate value in real time. Some stumble in at 3am, drawn by light and laughter. Spectators quickly become players, pulled into shared risk and ritual.
fields.Theme Engagement: Frick Frack enacts a living Last Judgment. Players place their values on the table and face chance, community, and consequence. Worth is weighed without money but story, risk, and vulnerability. Each hand becomes a playful reckoning of what we choose to keep, lose, or become.
fields.Risk & Safety: N/A
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Frick Frack Blackjack has been performed in evolving forms at festivals and pop-ups, but each iteration is site-specific and rebuilt for its environment. If awarded the grant, we would premiere a custom Bombay Beach edition featuring a new game, Trixster’s Hand—a tarot-infused blackjack variant where players draw character archetypes that alter the rules mid-play. Fate cards shift odds, reverse outcomes, or demand dares, turning each round into a theatrical reckoning aligned with The Last Judgment theme.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): I want to create this work now because we’re living in a moment of collective reckoning—social, economic, environmental. Bombay Beach embodies collapse and reinvention. Through a cashless casino, I ask: what are we worth without money? Who judges value—fate, community, or ourselves? I’ll collaborate with local artists and builders, shaping the space through experimentation and shared story. Frick Frack becomes a ritual of risk, reflection, and renewal at the edge of a receding sea.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): Bombay Beach is already a wager against disappearance, art rising from ruin beside the Salton Sea. Frick Frack belongs here because it mirrors that spirit: risk, reinvention, and communal myth-making. At the Biennale, the casino becomes a playful reckoning with value in a landscape shaped by loss and resilience.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The core Frick Frack structure is fully developed and performance-tested. We are in final touches on a new, site-specific expansion: Trixster’s Hand, a custom tarot-based game with original archetype cards and rule shifts. The concept is built; refinement

I use neon paint designed to glow under black light. At night, the artwork will illuminate under two 300-watt black lights. The piece follows a theme of
love, life and spirit.

fields.Project Description: I want to create walking art exhibits. Being a makeup artist, my canvases are the people that surround me. And with the theme being The Last Judgement my beautiful sinners and saints will be 3 to 7 pre-selected models consenting to be a part of my vision. You as the viewer will not completely be able tell which person is a sinner or saint…the idea being the people/canvases can walk amongst the crowds as their own art pieces throughout the afternoon/evening. Ideally 3 highly done up people minimum (who I have already attained), but I could potentially stretch up to 7 if I have more interest. This will be a temporary exhibit of people mostly aimed around Saturdays festivities. However depending on timing I might try to add a few Friday and Sunday. I am helping with the Drag Show on Friday so I do want to give that its proper attention. I'm asking for $1000 to cover the majority of the costs of this project so I can do things like add prosthetics and high quality element / sweat proof makeup for the desert (and partying) environment. The full cost of the project will be about $1700 but as this is my first year asking for a grant I wanted to make sure money is able to be spread around as much as possible to everyone, so I can contribute the extra $700.
fields.Support Needed: Thanks for putting this weekend together every year!
fields.Abridged Project Text: You can never tell who the saints and sinners walking amongst us are. I do not aim to make it obvious with my wandering art. Maybe more to show it will actually never be clear. Inner demons and deities are a part of us all.
fields.Technical Support: I will need electricity for my airbrush and airbrush needs ventilation so open windows or big space for instance. Pharoah and I have been trouble shooting that in some conversations figuring out if there will be electricity on John's lot. And I was going to reach out to Dada to see if I might be able to use his space. But I'm also open to suggestions.

fields.Audience Engagement: I imagine the artworks moving through the people. The sinners and saints will go about their afternoons and evenings as they had previously planned or unplanned. A demonic looking entity watching a beautiful opera take place amongst a crowd of onlookers? Or maybe an angelic being dancing in Subclub? Perhaps a wayward deity perusing one of the art galleries Bombay has to offer? The model will have full freedom to choose their own adventure after I've made them up and people can interact with them as the model feels fit. Photographs and videos of course will be a part of that and highly encouraged. I am planning to send them off with a card that would have a scannable QR code with my Instagram and BBB's Instagram so anyone who wants to make stories or posts can tag the artist and the event.
fields.Theme Engagement: I was directly inspired by this years theme of The Last Judgement which is, I think, pretty apparent from my project description. Even though the iconography I'm using for the makeup of the saint or sinner might seem obvious at first, such as horns on a perceived sinner, I may then add holy images to the paint job to make people think twice about what they're looking at. For example, I've always loved in the bible how angles are described as terrifying creatures even though they're always depicted as these beautiful humanoid beings in most art. The Last Judgement's description always lends itself to a lot of open ended creativity. At its bare bones it's about the salvation of some and damnation of others. So in that way my interchangeable sinners and saints will directly reflect our society. We are unable to tell who someone is on the outside, and looking inward at how we conduct ourselves is where someone's truth lies.
fields.Risk & Safety: It'll involve alcohol paints and 99% alcohol and some cleaners to keep my makeup and equipment clean. All will be kept in my kit and closed tightly when not in use and kept out of reach of any young kids.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, this is a concept I came up with just for the Biennale 2026.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): I'm creating this project because I love collaboration and immersive art. I've always enjoyed exhibits where people can participate in the creativity of the art at their own leisure. I find immense delight designing creatures that make people pause and double take when they're out in the world like "Wait what was that? That person definitely was not a person.". My makeup art and art direction allows me to make people into something completely unrecognizable. I know I can create a really striking walking exhibit around Bombay with models representing this year's theme of The Last Judgement, for onlookers to have engaging interactions with the saints and sinners. The innovation and expressiveness of Bombay along with its environment will really allow this art exhibition to flourish at the 2026 Biennale.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): I love the artistic freedom, collaboration and community that Bombay has and brings out in people. Helping with the Biennale in past years has always given me huge creative bursts and the theme this year works so well with the harsh desert and beautiful seascape. I also think it'll be a really enjoyable thing for community members to be able to choose to be art pieces, and wander around their town and enjoy the festivities. I want to give my talents to the community in the form of something intriguing and exciting for people to participate in.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I would say I'm partway through conception for this project. I want to finish final looks for some people and buy specific things, but the concept itself is locked in.

fields.Project Description:
This participatory mapping project visualizes interconnected systems shaping Bombay Beach. Three sessions unite residents, experts, and stakeholders to collectively map knowledge about water, environment, economy, community, and futures. The resulting maps are visually striking, displaying dense networks of handwritten text, color-coded connections, drawings, data, and stories on 6- to 15-foot paper. At the Biennale, the maps debut as both artwork and testimony, inviting visitors to add their knowledge to our collective reckoning.

The project uses Giga Mapping, a systems design method for complex problems. We work together on large paper, markers in hand. We draw connections between hydrology and health policy, lithium extraction and water rights, the past and 2050. The maps grow dense, layered, sometimes contradictory. That's what reality looks like. This is the opposite of reports that simplify until nothing useful remains.

Session 1 (early March) maps the Colorado River watershed and Salton Sea systems. How do water law, ecology, agriculture, tribal rights, and policy intertwine? Participants include longtime residents who recall a full Sea, scientists studying the dustbowl, water experts, and community members with asthma from the exposed lakebed. We map the forces that created this crisis and continue to shape it.

At the Biennale, these maps are presented with materials for visitor contributions. Attendees join the process, expanding our knowledge and revealing gaps.

Session 2 (early April) focuses on Bombay Beach: social networks, businesses, history, resources, and challenges. We identify past, present, and future residents, tracing links between poverty, service access, community identity, and assets. Residents share knowledge rarely seen in official documents.

Session 3 (May 2-3, during the Bombay Beach Environmental Conference) synthesizes all prior information for Future Mapping. Participants visualize possible futures, share preferred scenarios, and discuss potential interventions. The session culminates in a collective future scenarios map, providing a shared foundation for advocacy and knowledge ownership.

Each session lasts a full day, drop-in. We work in English and Spanish. Everyone contributes, whether you draw, write, tell stories, or ask questions.

After the Biennale, all maps remain in Bombay Beach as living documents and a growing archive. They serve as a reference for planning, journalism, and advocacy. Most importantly, the process builds community. Until now, conversations about our future have happened in fragments. This process brings us together, looking at the same picture, building shared language for what we are up against and what we are fighting for.
fields.Support Needed: The budget is structured into two phases to provide funding flexibility. Full funding supports the complete three-session project and lasting community infrastructure. Partial funding for Phase 1 still delivers significant value: an impactful participatory artwork for the Biennale, foundational systems mapping with community and experts, and the basis for future sessions through other funding sources. Either way, this project puts community knowledge at the center and creates something Bombay Beach controls and uses long after the Biennale ends.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Residents map the forces shaping Bombay Beach: water politics, toxic dust, lithium extraction, community survival. Large-scale collaborative drawings debut at the Biennale, inviting visitors to add knowledge. The maps become living documents and permanent infrastructure for advocacy and planning.
fields.Technical Support:
For All Three Mapping Sessions (at BBAC): Reserved use of Bombay Beach Arts and Cultural Center's workshop space for full day. Tables to work on 6 by 20 foot paper rolls. Wall space to display completed maps from previous sessions for reference and continuity. Basic electricity for coffee makers and lighting. Laptop and projector to display context materials and guiding questions throughout the session. Screen or white wall surface for projection. Access to kitchen for food service. 
For Biennale Exhibition: Wall space (indoor strongly preferred to protect maps from wind and dust). Approximately 30-60 linear feet to display multiple large-format maps. Table space (6 by 20 feet) for printed copies where visitors can add comments, draw connections, and contribute knowledge. Container or box for markers, sticky notes, and contribution materials. Simple signage explaining the project and inviting participation. If outdoor mounting is necessary, we need weather-resistant mounting system and protective covering.
Exhibition Placement: I am in conversation with Brenda Ann Keneally about placing exhibition at Henderson Hall, creating meaningful context alongside her Bombay Beach installation. I invite Biennale organizers to share their ideas or suggest alternative locations to help us place the exhibition where it will reach and engage the most visitors.
Support from Biennale: Promotion through Biennale channels to maximize diverse participation during the exhibition phase.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Bombay Beach residents, Salton Sea stakeholders, and Biennale visitors participate in an empowering process of collective sense-making.

Prior to the Biennale, residents and stakeholders gather for full-day sessions and collaboratively create the initial systems maps. A longtime resident might recall stories from the heyday; a scientist could provide data on toxic dust and its health effects. While a farm worker explains irrigation systems, a traveller draws parallels to the situations of the Aral and the Dead Sea. All contributions merge into a single map, resulting in a richer, more comprehensive picture than any single view can offer.

During the Biennale, engagement shifts, but the method remains consistent. Visitors encounter large, complex, and colorful Session 1 maps. Some stumble upon the maps late at night and leave thoughtful or funny notes. Others study the maps in detail, contributing specific knowledge. Everyone participates in collective sense-making, whether adding notes, drawing connections, or writing questions on printed copies.

After the Biennale, the project evolves into ongoing community engagement. Sessions 2 and 3 build on insights from Session 1 and the Biennale. By Session 3 at the Environmental Conference, the focus shifts beyond mapping to identifying action areas, discussing priorities, and planning next steps.

After Session 3, the maps remain open for input, allowing the archive of community knowledge to grow and supporting future action.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Last Judgement asks: what comes after the end of the world, and who gets to decide?
Bombay Beach has been written off for decades. Journalists call us a ghost town in a toxic wasteland. Investors treat us as a sacrifice zone. Policy makers render verdicts about our future without asking us. We are tired of being judged by systems that don't understand us.
This project flips the script. The community becomes the jury examining the forces that shape our lives: water law, environmental policy, economic extraction, and community change. Each mapping session is a testimony in which residents speak their own truth rather than being spoken about. We are rendering our own verdict on what happened, what's happening, and what should happen next.
The theme says salvation is dirty, dusty, and DIY. These maps will be exactly that: messy, contradictory, beautiful for not simplifying what's complex. We're not waiting for outside experts to save us. We're building our own knowledge base.
"What was worth it after all?" is the central question of Future Mapping. After decades of boom and bust, after being studied to death, what do we actually value? What do we fight to preserve? What do we let go of?
The Biennale is the ceremonial opening of this reckoning. Session 1 gathers testimony. The exhibition invites the world to witness. Sessions 2 and 3 deliver the verdict and chart what comes next. Not repentance or bacchanal, but collective self-determination in a place told it has no future.
fields.Risk & Safety: no
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): no
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
After ten years of building relationships, I finally have the trust to ask people to spend a day mapping what they know and care about. The Biennale makes this work visible and is a powerful way for residents to speak to visitors descending on their town, engaging them through the artistically created map. The Environmental Conference gives us a place to turn maps into action. We deserve tools for self-determination, not more research that takes from us. Maps we make become tools we control. 

I have lived part-time in Bombay Beach for 10 years. I have contributed art to the Biennale, reported on Salton Sea water issues for international magazines, and run the Bombay Beach Environmental Conference for three years. I know most people here. More importantly, they know me: not as just another journalist looking for a dramatic story or an artist using this place as a blank canvas, but as someone who shows up, listens, and stays.

My neighbors are tired of being studied and frustrated by costly, fruitless reports. They mock the millions poured into yet another Salton Sea restoration study. They've watched the sea shrink, dust storms worsen, and asthma rates climb. Meanwhile, developments like lithium extraction and data centers arrive with grand promises but little transparency. Long-time residents fear displacement, and tensions ebb and flow in our chat groups among residents, artists, and newcomers. All while the health and environmental crisis worsens around us.

We live in a sacrifice zone where climate, environment, poverty, health, class, race, and displacement collide. People want real solutions that require understanding how everything connects: how Colorado River water law affects health, how art and lithium development relate to gentrification, and how crisis-causing systems now propose to fix it.

This project creates space for that understanding to emerge. I teach planet-centric design and have led Giga Mapping sessions, but this is my first time doing so with the people I live with. I will still be here after the grant ends and the season is over. That makes a difference.

We are exploring questions I do not have answers to: How do these systems work? What do we value enough to fight for? What futures are possible when we see the whole picture? Giga Mapping does not offer solutions, but helps us think together. My job is to guide the process, create space for hard conversations, and ensure everyone’s voice is heard. 

To this end, I am collaborating with two local institutions: The mapping sessions will be hosted at the Bombay Beach Arts and Cultural Center (BBAC), which has a year-round presence in the community. Imperial Valley Equity and Justice (IVEJ) will co-facilitate the sessions, bringing their deep roots in farmworker communities and expertise in environmental justice organizing. This ensures Spanish-speaking community members and marginalized voices of the Imperial Valley are not just present but centered in the process.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
This project belongs in Bombay Beach because the complexity we're mapping is unique to this place. Nowhere else do you find a community on the shore of an accidentally created, rapidly shrinking inland sea that is both an ecological crisis, toxic dust bomb, lithium bonanza, last refuge, and improbable art destination. Our geography means we face climate change, water politics, environmental racism, and economic extraction at once.

The Biennale has already changed how people see Bombay Beach. It is no longer just a dying town, but a place where things happen, and culture is created. It brings diverse perspectives that can enrich our mapping work.

The Biennale's stated commitment to place and community roots creates space for this project. While the weekend brings exceptional music, art, opera, and philosophy to Bombay Beach, few offerings emerge directly from the environmental and social realities residents navigate daily. This project addresses that gap. It creates infrastructure for residents to articulate our own knowledge of the systems shaping our lives. It amplifies voices rooted here, not those visiting for a weekend.

This supports the Biennale's promise of lasting impact beyond the event. Most projects end when the festival is over. This one creates permanent community infrastructure: knowledge we use for advocacy, planning, and self-determination long after the last DJ packs up.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The concept is fully developed, and the partnership with Imperial Valley Equity and Justice (IVEJ) and the Bombay Beach Arts and Cultural Center are confirmed. The timeline is set: Session 1 in early March, Biennale exhibition late March, Session 2 early

fields.Project Description:
ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ is a multisensory socially-immersive screening encounter. The program centers around a 1 hour video collage combining complete short films with original, found, and remixed footage from a dozen artists around the world, all centering on the long isthmus between nature and technology. A peat bog eats an archaeologist, human-fly hybrids alternate in montage with desert nuclear testing, the ultrawealthy prepare for Martian exodus. Can we imagine that which we could never be ready for? ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ adds a few more imaginative ingredients into the perennially popular desert pastime of preparing for what’s to come. 

Scents, tastes, mists, and vibrations will ritualistically commune through the participants’ collective sensorium during the screening. The floor will then open  to the relief of post-ECOTECHNO discursive flow. Or, in terms more suited to the flotsam kitsch: It’s not Smell-O-Vision, it’s not AromaRama, it’s not a Four Dimensional eXperience, it’s ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ!

ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ will take place in Dadaonysus’s Red Light Theatre (and hopefully the Drive-in). Doors will open 30 minutes before showtime, and remain open as long as the post-screening conversation flows.
fields.Support Needed: I budgeted this event to be able to operate at various levels of funding. The budget we presented here represents the most maximalist vision for ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ. With a smaller budget, we could potentially forgo the bass shakers and scents and still be able to present it as an enhanced screening through our other techniques (hand-misting, fans, etc). Scalability, an oft-forgotten part of sustainability, is something I always keep in mind while planning.
fields.Abridged Project Text: ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ is an immersive screening encounter that engages all seven senses to take stock of our long-negotiated relationship with and between nature and technology.
fields.Technical Support:
We probably won’t need much in terms of technical support besides scheduling.

I met Dadaonysus (Will) through my friends Mara and Reed this year. We hit it off and they agreed to let me use the Red Light Cinema on their lot, which already has electricity/sound/projector/screen all accounted for on it.
That said, I saw the drive-in when I was there and thought it would be neat to have screenings both at Red Light and there. Dada offered to assist with carting the projector and sound over. I understand that BBB probably has a bunch of stuff planned for that space—if it’s free for a a 1h (+ setup and disassembly) slot somewhere during the weekend, I’d take it.
fields.Audience Engagement: ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ is something people watch, move through, and participate in, probably in that order. It might be stumble-able given the structure of the biennale, and the short-film nature does mean that one won’t be narratively hobbled from enjoying the screening if one does encounter it in the middle, but the program is designed as a complete event in the form of a ritual, a détourned version bearing only structural resemblance to the pop-cultural ritual of going to the cinema. We’ll still have refreshments.
fields.Theme Engagement:
I conceived of the project before learning of the theme, but “The Last Judgement” rhymes with ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ in two main ways:

1. Many of the films I select for ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ engage thematically with a kind of anthropogenic apocalypse, situating themselves around “The Last Judgement” interpreted as an end, a new beginning, or some kind of processural adjustment to bare life on the planet. Are we ready for the last judgement, or is ‘we’ an object unknown to judgement in the last instance?

2. The participants have the last judgement on the works :)
fields.Risk & Safety: It doesn’t really involve anything particularly dangerous to the general public but we’ll do a default warning at the start for flashing lights and scents for those with epilepsy and/or fragrance sensitivity.
fields.Location Submittal: Dadaonysus’s Red Light Theatre.  2132 3rd St.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): no
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I was taken this winter to visit Bombay Beach and Mars College by my good friend Mara, during one of my stays in LA (I’m from LA but live/work in Chicago.) Mara’s wife Reed is an anthropologist who completed her PhD fieldwork through several years living around Bombay Beach and the Salton Sea area; over time, the two of them had painted a compelling picture of the place. Mara and I had originally meant to meet Dadaonysus in Bombay, but due to some timing differences we ended up hanging out in Long Beach first, and drove down without them. Pushing through 111 through the midday haze, the town racked into focus in one frame, a bricolage of windblasted objects from various eras vibrating with the sun, a 20 degree above historical average day in February. 

My practice combines filmmaking, curation, and event organizing. I started programming short films because I was frustrated with the way my short film was programmed. It was pigeonholed into being a “trans short” at film festivals, and thus shown alongside a group of other short films that felt completely unrelated, creating a context that harmed my work. This drove me to curate interventions within complete contexts to enhance the films presented. “Geographies of Public Sex” brought materiality into the leather scene’s naive utopianism surrounding public sex. “Free Yr Dick” brought the lost underground back into queer film festivals. “Disorderly Conduct” handled the social construction of crime. When I was curating my last shorts program, “Cyberslime,” a multisensory presentation (all five!) of post-internet genre films with an experimental edge, I was presented with more compelling work than I could contain in one theme. I found myself splitting off films into a separate category I named “ecotechno”, and shelving them for a future context.

In Bombay Beach, I knew I’d found the right context for an exploration of “ecotechno.” I quickly sketched out what would become ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ, an aesthetically adventurous exploration of the wide estuary we’ve long navigated between nature and technology. To put it in simple, wide questions: How does technology mimic nature? How does nature adapt and respond to technology? What can technologists learn from nature? Why do we, as humans, draw the distinction between technology and nature in the first place? Bombay Beach, as a complete work of nature/technology shot through with art, was a natural fit for the proposed project. From the Army Corps’ levee bursting to the Australian lithium miners, it’s always been ground zero for this region’s technical engagement with nature.

In terms of community engagement, collaboration, etc, I don’t want to pretend ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ is a long culmination of a community-engaged collaborative process, which it isn’t. What it will be is, hopefully, the opening words in a long and productive conversation between an underground world of moving images and the town, the sea, the people, and the non-people as a complete natureculture.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): I’d like ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ to be part of the biennale because I saw Dulcinee’s talk at Mars and was enticed by the social organism of the event as she sketched it. The films in ECOTECHNO 4DXYZ deal with the denouement (literal, spiritual, imaged/imagined) of humanity’s technologically mediated waltz with the environment it finds itself in, and the communities of the Salton Sea area occupy a similarly contingent space with regards to the capricious nature of megaprojects in the desert, from irrigation to lithium mining. And, like the Salton Sea powerboaters of the latter half of the 20th Century, we’ll of course be approaching this with a sixpack and deck chairs.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I currently have a list of films that I’m interested in for this program, all of which are made by filmmakers with whom I’ve been in contact. Once I finalize the budget and scope of the screening, I can confirm filmmaker and sound artist interest and edit

fields.Project Description: The Colorado is a feature documentary with a strong music component. People often liken The Colorado to Koyaanisqatsi of Reggio & Glass in terms of its storytelling ethos, although with nine topical chapters and VoiceOver, The Colorado is much more contextually oriented.
fields.Support Needed: I was inspired to make The Colorado after a visit to the Salton Sea area. The film is broadly about the Colorado River Basin but the Salton Sea area--as well as the delta--play a prominent role. It would very special to bring The Colorado to BBB on the film's and BBB's 10th anniversary.
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Colorado, narrated by the stage legend Marc Rylance, takes us on an epic journey through the prehistoric settlements of the region, the period of European exploration, the dam-building era, modern industrial agriculture and immigration, and the impact of climate change.
fields.Previous Screenings:

fields.Audience Engagement: People experience The Colorado within the traditional cinema framework, whether it is in a movie theater or on the banks of the Salton Sea.
fields.Theme Engagement: The Colorado resonates with End Times narratives as it sees through a glass darkly, literally and figuratively. The Salton Sea--which figures prominently in the film--is the embodiment of this ethos, as is the delta of the river.

fields.Project Description:
"Harpazo" is a 24-minute-long surreal, seriocomic, narrative short (or featurette if you prefer) centered on the friendship between Zeke and Harpo, two defiant figures within the doomsday-focused religious community they grew up in. Described by Hollyshorts as "a delightful and wildly offbeat film,""very queer, very funny," and "a bleak but heartfelt classic," it is our unique coming-of-age story for the “doomer” generation. It has made a lot of people laugh and some people cry.

It can be projected on any surface that won't be knocked over by wind, and at any time as long as it's dark enough to run a projector.
fields.Support Needed: We're not total newcomers; we volunteered at Convivium last year and met a bunch of y'all but that wasn't in the drop-down menu :) So much admiration for the work you all do! Thank you for your consideration!
fields.Abridged Project Text: A surreal, seriocomic narrative short film about fear, judgement, the lust for death, and queer friendship at the end of the world. A tenacious and ultimately hopeful film debut shot in Bombay Beach under the Buck Moon of 2023, only growing more relevant with each tick of the doomsday clock.
fields.Technical Support: Yes to electricity, sound, projection and screen. If it doesn't fit into film programming at the Drive In during one of your program blocks, we can bring our own crappy projector and speaker and set it up somewhere else. But electricity or a generator would still be helpful in that case! If not being shown at the Drive In or a place with seating, people maybe should be advised to bring their own beach/camping if they want to sit down. Crossing our fingers for the Drive In because ideally this film should be watched from the roof of a rusting car.
fields.Audience Engagement: "Harpazo" can be projected anywhere (and we're happy to do it ourselves) - we're not precious about it. It's a long narrative short, so I imagine people sitting/lounging and watching it, maybe alongside other films at the Drive In (such a cool venue- would be my first choice!) but it could also be a midnight screening on the beach (Kevin has has previously volunteered his blow up projector for this purpose), or run on a loop somewhere with people passing by just to take in its aesthetic moments. The important thing to me is not how it's presented, but just that it is present in Bombay Beach, its spiritual home, at the Biennale.
fields.Theme Engagement: Oh, yes. Even though we made it in 2023 (and wrote it in 2021), yes, very much so. "Harpazo" is the original biblical term for The Rapture; The entire film is about the Last Judgement, and what that means for the characters. I'm sure you've already gleaned that from the description so without repeating myself I'll just say that it is tied to the idea of the Last Judgement in a pretty literal way, inspired by the upswell in the U.S. of both Gen Z "doomerism" and evangelical fervor for the end of the world - the fictional setting (for which we used Bombay Beach as a backdrop), and the accelerated timeline of the story put take those two things which feel as though they're always simmering just beneath the surface of modern American life, and put them in a pressure cooker. I don't think it's even too much of a wild coincidence that the theme of our film matches so perfectly the theme of the Biennale - it's clearly just indicative of where a lot of our heads are at as artists!
fields.Risk & Safety: No, it's pretty risk-free.

fields.Project Description:
Tiny House of Soft Displacement
An installation and short film by Mona Shahab and Desdemona Dallas

Tiny House of Soft Displacement is an immersive installation built around the short film Ballad of Boobi Jean. The piece centers on a small, inhabitable structure—approximately the footprint of a modest living room—that visitors can physically enter. Blurring the line between memory and mirage, the "tiny house" explores the layered identities of the first-gen experience through the lens of domestic surrealism. 

Inside, the space resembles a stylized domestic interior: upholstered furniture, layered textiles, inherited objects, framed images, and decorative details that merge California desert nostalgia with subtle references to Iranian home life. The interactive installation blends California kitsch with nostalgic Persia and the babble of diasporic longing. The house feels lived-in but slightly off—part stage set, part personal archive.

The installation remains open to visitors throughout the Biennale (for one or multiple days, depending on placement). Guests may enter freely during open hours, sit in the space, and explore its details.

At scheduled screening times, Ballad of Boobi Jean will be projected inside the tiny house. The Ballad of Boobi jean is a film about the fabulously idle daughter of clever immigrant hoteliers, who spends her days lounging in a forgotten California resort town, once a destination for Hollywood’s elite. But boredom, houseflies, and a whisper of ambition soon pry her from the desert’s dusty comforts—in this absurdist short film. The film has a four minute run time.

During these screenings, the installation functions as an intimate micro-cinema. Seating is integrated into the environment, and the film’s sound fills the structure. Between screenings, the film environment remains visible and experiential, but without full projection, allowing the installation to operate as a sculptural and atmospheric space.

The work reflects on inherited ambition, displacement, and the absurd desire to “arrive” somewhere that may never fully exist.
fields.Support Needed: We would love to have an opening reception where we host the first screening of the Ballad of Boobi Jean, I do not yet know the dates of the festival so just added a random date above. Happy to discuss the best time frame for the first screening. Additionally if there is any other short film programming involved in the biennale we would love for Ballad of Boobi Jean to be included and welcome other spaces to share the film with audiences at Bombay Beach.
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Tiny House of Soft Displacement imagines a home away from home—a third-culture dream space where delusion & nostalgia coexist. This interactive installation, a playful companion to the Ballad of Boobi Jean short film, invites viewers into an intimate, surreal interior shaped by memory & longing.
fields.Technical Support:
The installation requires a small, defined footprint that can function as a semi-contained interior environment. Ideally, the space is partially or fully enclosed to protect the objects from the elements. 

We will provide all primary furnishings and set dressings for the tiny house, including furniture, textiles, and decorative objects. If available, we would welcome additional seating or floor cushions from the Biennale to increase comfort and capacity.

The project requires access to electricity to support video projection and audio playback. A projection surface (or wall within the structure), projector, and basic sound system will be needed. Audio should be audible within and just outside the structure without requiring full sound isolation.

Ballad of Boobi Jean will be screened at scheduled times throughout the event. During these times, the installation functions as an intimate cinema space. Outside of screening times, the projection can be turned off or minimized.

fields.Audience Engagement:
Visitors can engage with Tiny House of Soft Displacement in two primary ways: as an open installation and as a scheduled film screening.

Throughout the Biennale, the tiny house will remain open for visitors to freely enter and move through. People may stumble upon it, step inside, sit, or spend a few moments exploring the textures, objects, and atmosphere of the space. No instructions are required—the layout naturally supports casual entry and short or extended visits.

At designated screening times, the installation shifts into a viewing space for Ballad of Boobi Jean. Visitors can choose to arrive at the start of a screening or wander in partway through. Seating is integrated into the environment, allowing people to sit comfortably or come and go as they please.

Between screenings, the projection is off (or minimal), and the space functions as a sculptural, inhabitable environment rather than a formal cinema. This allows for a continuous flow of engagement throughout the day and night.
The piece works both as something people intentionally attend and something they encounter unexpectedly—whether during peak festival hours or late at night. Its small scale creates an intimate experience, whether shared with a few others or encountered alone.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Tiny House of Soft Displacement engages The Last Judgement not as a singular, divine reckoning, but as a quiet, ongoing process of self-evaluation shaped by migration and memory.

For those living between cultures, judgement is rarely final—it is ambient. It appears in questions of legitimacy, belonging, and visibility: Am I enough of one thing? Too much of another? What parts of myself are preserved, and what must be left behind? Within the installation, the domestic space becomes a site where these judgements accumulate. Objects, textures, and images act as emotional evidence—fragments of a life continually sorted, kept, or discarded.

Boobi’s world reflects a kind of soft reckoning. Rather than a dramatic verdict, the work lingers in ambiguity, where identity is unstable and meaning is constructed through repetition and imagination. The “tiny house” becomes a symbolic container for this process: a space where past and present coexist, and where building a home is also an act of editing oneself.

Set within Bombay Beach—a landscape shaped by collapse and reinvention—the project resonates with aftermath. If The Last Judgement implies an ending, this work asks what comes after: how we live with what remains, and how we reassemble meaning from fragments.
fields.Risk & Safety: There is no physical risk involved in this instillation

fields.Project Name: Pickle That Up presents THE SACRAMENT OF THE HOLY BRINE
fields.Project Description:
Pickle Clergy roll the cart to our location for the night, and prepare the altar. The anchor of this installation is the pickle cart. It's a 1970s SnapOn tool cart, painted gold and green. Gold sequin fabric is draped over the cart and a collection of jars are intentionally placed upon the altar: a variety pickle jars, decorative jars that glow gold, and a jar filled with water representing the Salton Sea glows green. 

As the clergy set up the installation, curiosity builds. If anyone asks what we're doing, we cryptically tell them "the service will begin when the divine pickle glows." Once the neon pickle is LIT, the Sacrament of the Holy Brine is ready to be be shared and received. "Draw near and Partake of the Preserved" we whisper.

You approach the alter, here, you take communion. An item from the menu of your choosing, yes, and treated with the gravity of ritual. The pour is deliberate. A phrase is spoken. The moment is held.

This is the Sacrament of the Holy Brine:
salted sea, preserved unforbidden fruit, time made drinkable.

You consume what has endured. You ingest survival. You remember that preservation is not stagnation; it is a strategy.

A question is asked—softly, strangely:
* What are you carrying?
* What are you avoiding?
* What are you ready to let ferment?

Your confession determines which pickle dimension you inhabit:
* sharp or sweet
* spicy or sour
* crisp or collapsing

After communion, you are anointed.

A fingertip of brine touches your wrist, your temple, your throat. Salty sacrament. Bombay Beach baptism.

No promise is made that you are fixed.

Only this:
You are saved by the Holy Brine.
Saved not from the world, but for it.
Still here. Still fermenting. Still alive.
fields.Support Needed: Participating in BBB 2024 was a great learning experience for me.   I fell deeper in love with the local community and became fascinated by the many perspectives that coexist. I started filling out the BBB X form two other times, and I'm not sure how to continue those applications (apologies for the duplicates!).   I haven't communicated our tithe/offering plan to the Fire Dept yet, and would like our contribution to be a surprise. I do not have a location. Possible locations are the Sub Club, the Estates, etc.   If there are any curated events who would like to collaborate and have a hosted pickle bar at their happening, I am open to bringing The Sacrament of the Holy Brine to pretty much anywhere. Being near pavement will make transportation easiest.
fields.Abridged Project Text:
Pickle That Up brings THE SACRAMENT OF THE HOLY BRINE to all creatures great and small in Bombay Beach. 

Witness the miracle of fermentation! 
Visit our Altar of Acidity, receive the sacrament, and become one the faithful dill-sciples.
If the neon pickle is glowing—come and be preserved.
fields.Technical Support: The only thing I need is a space to exist in. Pickle That Up is a self-contained gift that I bring to community events. If there's a good fit, I'd love to collaborate with another artist's activation and add to the interactivity.
fields.Audience Engagement:
People will stumble upon this experience. The pickle bar is mobile and can pop up in areas all over Bombay Beach at dusk or after dark. 

A member of the Pickle Clergy will present participants with "The Cuke-mandments" (menu), and folks will select one form of communion to receive. The menu will have a variety of pickles and pickle themed items. 

If folks wish to, they can be anointed and receive a certificate of preservation:
"Be it known to all Dill-sciples:
That _____________ (Name of the Anointed)
Has this day undergone the Rite of Trans-fermentation.
Once a Cucumber, now a Pickle. Preserved forevermore."
The certificate is sealed with a stamp and given to the new Dill-sciple

We will have a tithe/offering collection where participants can contribute if they feel led to do so. Net proceeds will be donated to the BB Fire Dept.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Literal:
Yes, the theme is incorporated through the things we say, the actions we do, and the gift we share. 

Personal: 
I grew up at the intersection of two faiths, the daughter of a devout Christian mother and a culturally Jewish father. In my youth we were members of a Messianic Jewish congregation. After leaning into my Christian roots and attending a Baptist university, I faced a profound turning point when my Jewish grandmother passed away. I couldn't reconcile a theology that suggested my kind, generous Grandma Helen was eternally damned. That contradiction sparked an identity crisis that led me to walk away from my previous beliefs. I refused to accept that the kindest soul I knew was ""unworthy"" of peace.

That loss launched a decade of deconstruction and a total reimagining of my identity. I used to hide "Raychill Pickles" away, afraid of being judged by a conventional world. Today, I step into the light as a clown and performer with a singular mission: bringing joy and love to this timeline. I'm no longer worried about a "Final Judgment"; I'm too busy leaving a trail of whimsy and pickles behind me.

Lawrence Kohlberg’s stages of moral development also help explain how people understand the idea of the Last Judgment as they mature. The dice aspect is a fun way to incorporate a game of chance into gifting away cocktails and other things, and the bar theme can be educational, and can shed light on why humans have vastly diverse grasps of what's "right" or "wrong". This could be considered particularly relevant when

fields.Project Name: Sun Shine Dreams: Legends of Bomabay Beach, Unconditional Love and Stuff
fields.Project Description:
Sun Shine Dreams is an immersive multimedia installation centered around layered personal histories of Bombay’s long-time residents, aka Legends. A newly gutted warehouse will become a portal into the emotional landscape of the spirits who have forged a path for Bombay to become fertile destination for a new generation of artists and independent thinkers. Grounded in an ambient soundscape of intimate chronologies and reflections, a hyperlocal collection of crafts, pastimes, obsessions, family photographs and all manner of ephemera, establish an undeniable atmosphere that begs attention to the minutia of daily life inspired by The Bombay we love. Every piece in the vast collection is a like a universal sacred prayer to the Goddess of Time and Eternity.  “How does the residue of each of our lives compare to Our Sun Shine Dreams?”
This Question will guide the arch of the narrative that inspired the physical warehouse space and publication that will accompany the installation. The hybrid “Zine” of the same name will be appx 100 pages. The project’s narrative will weave the hard scrabble practicalities of living in a remote desert spot with the essential magic we make to sustain life here in a place where creativity and community trump traditional currency. The installation and printed matter will situate the personal histories of OG “Bombians” in an historical timeline tracing the trajectory of The Salton Sea from formation to present. Sun Shine Dreams breaks the barriers of traditional audience vs author documentary form to allow for participatory, meta reflection best suited to educate the emotions of both the story tellers and listeners. The books will be available for a suggested donation and money with benefit the weekly legends coffee gathering and future Legends creative activities.
On the Saturday afternoon there will be a 45 minute performance and of the intergenerational BBC, Bombay Beach Chorus to honor The Legends Lost in the past year. We will dedicate the New Henderson Hall during the performance
fields.Support Needed: I found my tribe among the Legends of Bombay beach, kindred spirits who give endless inspiration and encouragement.

fields.Audience Engagement:
Though the installation will be set up for self guided engagement, Many of The Legends will be in the space over the weekend to answer questions, act as “docents” and supplement information. And of course, spin Bombay yarns.
On the Saturday, there will be a choral performance and memorial for Legends who passed away this year and a dedication to Miss Aqua who provided the space before her recent passing. We plan a Legend’s book signing on the Sunday afternoon. Suggested donations in exchange for Sunshine Dreams publication will go to support Legends Coffee Tuesdays and Legends other creative activities and events.
and also sign books.
fields.Theme Engagement: The last judgement will be the one we place upon ourselves. This gathering memory matter is but a prompt for the immediate personal introspection and evaluation of tug at heart personal history. How have we invested in our own lives and the life of our community. This will be a space in which we gauge the love that we have given which will be all that remains. The history of the living will be no judge. we have only ourselves to face.

fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
To BB Biennale Folks,
I met my friend Herb at the time when his mother Betty was dying. I never did get to see Betty, though I got to know her in death after I purchased eight large bins of Betty’s prolific crochet work that Herb had on offer at his yard sale a few months after Betty’s passing. In addition to a seemingly endless supply of untouched yarn and many balls made from the tail ends of once full skeins, there were finished placemats, bookmarks, tissue holders, key chains and even a crocheted cathedral among them. The cache held thousands of stitches, each representing half a minute or so of Betty’s life. 
The obsessive nature of Betty’s life’s work, parallels my photographic practice as one who also renders existence in fractions of seconds. I immediately connected to Betty though her work.  I thought of each cross stitch as a photograph. The collection conjured a picture of Betty’s life with more punctum than any photographic representation*. I saw the parallel between how Betty spent her minutes on earth and how I spend mine. Questions of authorship and judgment followed. I was purchasing Bettys work to photograph and display. Was this a higher purpose than Betty had intended? Had I appointed myself the one to appraise the value of how each of us, Betty and I engaged the same unit of time. I was amazed by the repetition and volume of what Betty had made. I calculated her days by the intricacy of each piece. What was she thinking as she made them. Was she escaping a racing mind or was this a Zen practice? Does each piece mean a reprieve from boredom or a deeper investment into a contented life? What about me? Are the minutes I spend unraveling Betty’s minutes and then re representing them any different in value. Time is Time in the end it is all we have and we don’t ever really have it. We have the illusion of it. We fill it according to our own needs or lack of. 
Bombay itself is a place where we linger in close proximity to the void that is the San Andreas Fault. It is a constant reminder of emptiness we all recon with. 
Sunshine Dreams: Legends of Bombay Beach, Unconditional Love and Stuff is a meditation on the lives we live. The spiritual battle for freedom from attachment and the hours we spend in community. The role of art in integration of body and spirit. The need to forge monuments and be remembered and remember.

Punctum is a word that is an art school staple. It comes from The Book Camera Lucida by Roland Barthes. While punctum is literally a small hole in the physical body like the tear duct, the theory of punctum in photography is a detail in a picture that has deeper emotional meaning. Death is talked about as being punctum inherent in all photographs as they are markers of mortal and finite existence.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): At its core Sun Shines Dreams is A Peoples History of Bombay Beach and this descriptions should answer all the above questions.
