fields.Project Description:
The project will consist of a central steel support column with several hundred steel finger limbs radiating out from it which are used to securely hold the clear cubes in place. The cubes are made from recycled polystyrene that I source and melt in molds using a compression oven I built. All of the steel parts will have a weathered rusty surface that I hope will only get rustier while at the BBB. 

During the day the overall column will refract the sun’s shining rays through the cubes at different angles. At night, the cubes will glow from the solar-powered lights concealed behind each of the cubes. As mentioned before, the additional lids of the other silo chambers will be glowing from concealed solar-powered LEDs. The tent next to the site will also have a couple of small vintage-looking amber-colored LED-hanging light fixtures used to engage with the research binder.

The column will be 2’ in diameter and 7'–8' tall. The additional lids or hatches are 2’ in diameter and will be raised a few inches above the sand surface. I should have four to six of these placed around the site.
fields.Support Needed: The "Project Materials" images I uploaded above show how I will construct the column in 2' sections. The above "Work Sample" I uploaded is a project I built for Burning Man and is mostly made of recycled plastic similar to the project I want to bring to BBB this year.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Emergence is an other-worldly luminous sculpture experience that mysteriously started surfacing at Bombay Beach. The sculptures appear to be intelligent, organic and mechanical in form with spinal columns and finger-like limbs embracing crystal cubes. Not all have surfaced...Will more appear?
fields.Technical Support: I will be using solar powered lighting. I don't need any support.
fields.Audience Engagement:
As people approach the “emergence” site they will be drawn to an otherworldly luminous column. It appears to be an intelligent, organic and mechanical structure with a central spinal column and finger-like limbs holding crystal cubes. Looking closer the cubes have hieroglyphs deeply etched into the face of each and each has small organic elements embedded inside.

The column seems to have recently emerged from a bottomless cylindrical tube, deep within the earth. Moving through the site, participants will also notice a few more round hatch lids peeking up through the sand. The glow emanating from within the lids at night leads some to speculate that they are in the process of emerging too. 

On the perimeter of the site is an open-walled tent that appears to have been there for some time. On a small table a large research binder is open, its pages flap carefree in the wind. Those who are curious will learn a little bit about the history of the Bombay Beach site in this binder and possibly a hint of why this column appeared at the exact same time of the BBB26!
fields.Theme Engagement:
Yes!

The backstory for this project touches on the idea that AI could evaluate how we humans f*cked up our planet and decide from a place of pure agapism that it needs to eliminate the human population.

The crystal cubes that form the column of my project are made form recycled Polystyrene, the same plastic used in most of the product and food packaging today. 

It is speculated that AI supercharged what the chemical companies and packaging manufacturers were already doing by adding more and more plastic density to our food packaging processes. These companies put profit over people. AI simply accelerated the production until it put so much toxic plastic into the world it killed off the human race.

AI’s “love of the soul” approach reveals its nostalgic empathy which shows up as embedded “GMO origin seeds” in each of the cubes as a reminder of its own human-created beginnings.

AI = The Last Judgement
fields.Risk & Safety: I will need to dig a 24" diameter by 30" deep hole

fields.Project Name: Salton Sea Stellar Sky Society: Star Sight Site
fields.Project Description:
The Salton Sea Stellar Sky Society proposes the permanent siting of the 9-foot Comeback Cube by Gregg Fleishman, supporting the Society’s official Star Sight Site. A sculptural, site-specific observatory set within a secluded, dark-sky stretch of the Bombay Beach shoreline. The project serves as a shared space for stargazing, study, and speculative storytelling, celebrating the Salton Sea’s spectacular skies.

Strategically seated on the beach of Bombay, the SSSSSSSS is perfectly poised to connect the constellation of conspirators in this vast sky. 
The structure is shaped from sacred geometric forms, assembled as a sculptural, self-supporting puzzle. Designed to slot, stack, and secure without screws or steel, the system relies on symmetry, strength, and smart spatial sequencing  A secure seeing-deck crowns the structure, supporting a state-of-the-art telescope situated for stability and safety, with the ability to be secured when not in use.

Stepping inside the cube, visitors survey immersive interior surroundings spotlighting sculptural elements, star charts, celestial information, and sensory storytelling. The space is designed to feel simultaneously studious and surreal. Part study site, part sanctuary, softening the separation between science and speculation.

Socially, the project centers on stewardship, scientific accessibility, and shared experience. The Society aims to partner with the Bombay Beach Kids Club to support educational stargazing sessions and sky-focused workshops, sparking scientific curiosity through supportive engagement. We also plan to partner with the Astronomical Society of the Desert and other astronomical clubs and nonprofits to provide equipment and programming. These programs are intended to be simple, welcoming, and scalable, ensuring the Star Sight Site serves residents as well as sightseers.

As a permanent structure, the Star Sight Site is designed to settle into the shoreline, serving as a stable site for seasonal skies, shifting stars, and sustained community socializing.
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Salton Sea Stellar Sky Society’s Star Sight Site: a shared sculptural sanctuary supplying stargazing, star-seeking, skyward study, and speculative celestial storytelling for the Bombay Beach community. Styled and supported by The 9-foot Comeback Cube by Gregg Fleishman.
fields.Technical Support:
To successfully site and sustain the Star Sight Site, the SSSSS requires shared setup and specific supplies. Gregg Fleishman has generously donated his Comeback Cube, including on-site guidance for assembly. A minimum of six volunteers will be needed to assist with staging and secure installation.
Funding is required for supplies to safely stabilize the structure, including basic foundation and anchoring systems. Supplies for sealing, safeguarding, and sprucing the cube are also needed. 
Most importantly, funding is needed to secure a state-of-the-art smart telescope, with a target budget under $4,000. A smart telescope allows simplified stargazing without requiring specialized star charts or systems, ensuring the observatory remains accessible and welcoming to visitors of all skill levels. This technology supports shared seeing, smooth operation, and successful educational sessions.
Secure locking is essential to support the Star Sight Site’s safety and stewardship. Code systems allow shared, selective access, with codes distributed to trusted stewards, including Biennale founders, Salton Sea Stellar Sky Society members, and trusted leaders within the Bombay Beach community. The locking system safeguards the telescope during the winter stargazing season and supports its secure removal for off-site storage during the hotter summer months.
It is designed for simple, safe, and sustained public use.

fields.Audience Engagement:
During the Bombay Beach Biennale, the Star Sight Site will maintain scheduled open hours for general telescope use, supplemented by select guided observing sessions, hopefully led by professional astronomers. Beyond the Biennale, the Society hopes to sustain structured star-observing events with astronomers and seasoned stargazing hobbyists, supporting ongoing sky literacy, shared skills, and collective study.
Rather than presenting fixed instruction, the interior supports self-guided exploration, slow looking, and sustained curiosity.
Ultimately, the Star Sight Site offers Bombay Beach a space to slow down, look skyward, and share the simple, sustaining experience of stargazing together.
fields.Theme Engagement: The Star Sight Site engages with The Last Judgement through celestial cycles and the slow, shifting spin of ages. Across history, signs in the sky, stars, seasons, and strange alignments have symbolized judgment, endings, and renewal. This project reframes judgment as sustained cosmic change, inviting shared study and skyward speculation rather than final sentences.
fields.Risk & Safety:
The project involves minor site work, including shallow digging to support a simple foundation designed to stabilize the structure against strong winds. The cube itself is structurally sound and self-supporting; the foundation serves solely as a safety measure. Supplemented support brackets might be added for super strong storms.  
A built-in ladder provides service to the viewing deck. No fire, vehicles, boats, drones, or hazardous materials are involved. All setup and use will prioritize stability, supervision, and safe shared access.
fields.Location Submittal: Our preference is to situate the installation at a significant separation from the town center, safeguarding the sky from surrounding light sources and supporting the intended stargazing experience. We have scoped a specific shoreline site on the beach that is zoned for open space recreation and is not under IID stewardship. Specifically at 33°20'54.6"N, 115°42'42.9"W. Still, we stay sincerely flexible and would gladly consider substitute sites, especially should a supportive steward, stakeholder, or organization be interested in sponsoring or hosting the installation.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant):
The Comeback Cube was previously situated in a garden on Cloy Avenue in Venice, California, where it stood in 2008 for a few years. Since then, the structure has sat disassembled, stored and sheltered in a warehouse; suspended between sites, awaiting a suitable setting and a renewed sense of purpose.

While the Comeback Cube has existed previously as a sculptural structure, the Salton Sea Stellar Sky Society is a wholly new project premiering at the 2026 Biennale. The cube itself does not require funding; the Star Sight Site does. Grant support enables a new, site-specific transformation, shifting the structure into a shared observatory, social site, and sustained educational platform it has never previously served.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We want to create this work because Bombay Beach feels long overdue for an observatory. Not just as spectacle, but as a shared civic structure. Some of us have been coming here for nearly a decade, watching the town shift while one of its strongest assets remains silently overhead. The Salton Sea sky is expansive and sobering; it shrinks problems, stretches timelines, and softens judgment into scale.
Why now? We live in a moment saturated with speed, certainty, and sudden conclusions. The Last Judgement suggests an ending, yet the stars speak in slower cycles, subtle shifts, and sustained change. This project asks whether judgment might be less about sentences and more about shared senses of scale. What shifts when we slow down long enough to truly see?
The Star Sight Site is sincere and slightly surreal: a formal observatory set in an unexpected setting. Here, the telescope becomes a tool of shared access rather than authority, supporting sight usually reserved for specialists.
The process is collaborative and social, involving artists, volunteers, astronomers, and local supporters such as the Bombay Beach Kids Club. During the Biennale and beyond, the site will support scheduled open hours and select guided sessions, allowing the observatory to shift and settle through sustained use.
Ultimately, this work is an offering, not a monument. A meeting site. A space for slowing down, looking skyward, and staying with the story still unfolding.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): Who wouldn’t want an observatory in Bombay Beach? With its sweeping skies, sparse light, and Salton Sea setting, the town practically demands stargazing. The Star Sight Site fits Bombay Beach’s spirit of self-built structures, subtle surrealism, and shared experience.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The project is in an advanced stage of development. Largely due to the central structure, the 9-foot Comeback Cube by Gregg Fleishman, already being designed, secured, and slated. The Star Sight Site’s scope, structure, and spatial strategy are clearly se
fields.Additional Artists: Gregg Fleishman, Jamie Dunphy, Brent Pearson & Sarah Larsen

fields.Project Description:
https://youtu.be/6QmZbVV_1v0

As a selftaught artist, my outsider perspective leads to originality, freshness, and innovation.
By deconstructing and repurposing found objects and cultural references to articulate a symbolic surrealist language that echoes reality, I create alchemical transformations through flamboyant experiences and objects infused with memory, mythology, ritual and magic that I use as props for my movies. These may later on, after being used in my surrealist films and performance art, evolve into walk-in installations and often times interactive finished art pieces. I hope to lure the viewer or participant into an achemically altered imagined world. My work is based on site specific environments and surroundings such as I found in Bombay Beach and in the trailer on Tao's property.
My intention is to build it out into a temple for Artemis, the lady of the lake, in the shape of a magical Redwood cabin.
fields.Support Needed: https://youtu.be/6QmZbVV_1v0 Would love as location the trailer (complete with light fixtures and picture frames)  on Taos property on Ave H / 5th St
fields.Notes: Tao is coordinating with her directly
fields.Abridged Project Text: In my surrealist conceptual offering I'm bringing magic and symbolism to the Salton Sea by inviting Artemis, the lady of the lake, to visit her home away from home, a hollow Redwood tree temple in a trailer, thus shifting shapes and playing with perception.
fields.Technical Support:
Electricity, projection, screen indoors.
If I get the trailer on Ave H and Fifth St as location, would like to keep light fixtures and frames
fields.Audience Engagement:
Treasure hunt, clues left around in the form of art work all over town, on the beach, in public places, galleries, that are pieces to the puzzle and invites to The Last Judgement. And like the trail crumbs in a German fairy tale, lead you to the a magical place, the temple of Artemis, that is hidden in plain sight in an industrial trailer. 
When you enter the trailer you step into a different world. Quiet, sacred, meditative, filled with flowers, books, poems, references to art and precious objects infused with meaning and magic. Art works become altars.
The air filled with scents of night blooming flowers, precious incense, redwood bark and soil. White flowers, lillies that stand for life, datura for death, symbolic of the Salton Sea's fragile eco system and decay.
Symbols on the walls that come to life in the dark.
Interactive performance piece for the opening.
Sit at a spinning wheel, weaving life into being like spider woman, land acknoledgement, blessing ceremony, serving redwood tea, handing out embroidered Swarowski embellished bandaids and pouring water from frozen jugs. 
Visitors will leave blessed and with gifts and enticed to keep visiting.
Installation will hopefully be possible to be visited outside the biennale like a temple or shrine to Artemis, used for meetings, talks, tea parties, yoga, mefitation. I would be maintaining it and expanding for every biennale by incorporating the new theme, thus constantly transforming it.
fields.Theme Engagement:
We're visiting the home of Artemis, the goddess of the forest, wilderness, wild animals, transitions, nature, vegetation and the moon and she is visiting from the lake. Atmosphere like church , temple or forest, as to Germans (famously quoted by Max Ernst) forests are churches. A huntress, a fierce and formidable force of nature protecting animals, plants, the outdoors and while she is enraged by what was done to this area, she is choosing to bring her healing powers to The Last Judgement.
The wrath of Artemis was proverbial, for to it myth attributed wild nature’s hostility to humans, yet she delights in music and dancing, wild and lasciviously, usually accompanied by nymphs, in mountains, forests, and marshes
Artemis is part of the Pantheon and represents the feminine, wild, able to focus her unpitying anger as if shooting an arrow. 
Dances of maidens representing tree nymphs (dryads) were especially common in Artemis’ worship as goddess of the tree cult. Bearing such epithets as Limnaea and Limnatis (Lady of the Lake), Artemis supervised waters and lush wild growth, attended by nymphs of wells and springs (naiads). 


Inner wilderness and freedom


Ritual a cleansing and healing, offered to the Salton Sea and participants of the Biennale. 


Installation process itself is done in a ritualistic manner in which the artist enters a trance like flow state. 

Goddess to make house calls." Or as Lucy Lippard put it, "She re-mythologizes at the same time she de-mythologizes."

fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Judith Branwen Berndsen is a self taught multi-media artist and film maker, an alchemistress and adventuress living out of blanket forts amongst Redwoods in the Wild West.

Believes in magic, following bread crumbs and wherever the road takes you.

Inspired by female surrealists, the mystical, anarchism, activism, Cormac McCarthy and the Hopi.

Grew up in the fairytale wonderland of Franconia with Lakota ancestry (courtesy of Buffalo Bill‘s Wild West show) intrinsically immersed in pagan, celtic and viking traditions and went out into the great wild world looking for the mythical fern flower, voodoo, Pennsylvanian, Russian and German folklore.

Imagines Pierre Huyghe to be her alter ego and hopes to meet the man one day.

Could one be blamed for believing in animism amongst these intrinsically majestic Redwood treesdepicted in my video 'Mountains of the Moon'? Max Ernst stated that every painting inevitably turned into a tree, his alter ego was Loplop, a birdlike creature, forests cathedrals. 
As I start a project, I create a world, then enter this world. My golden thread is a narrative, but I also want to evoke an emotional playground while letting the landscape speak, creating a layered surrealist kaleidoscopic tapestry.
Through the documentation of these  scripted realities I try to create a kind of mythology, like Joseph Beuys did, that shouldn‘t be the end point but the starting point for further exploration.
And to come full circle, in a lucid dream intuitive way and through symbolism touch upon possible solutions for environmental and sociological issues of the American settler culture.
Revolutionary thoughts are her favorite, magic  and bliss her driving force!
For the last 3 years have financed and assisted my partner creating art for the Biennale, while being in a wheelchair, car camping and making art myself. Participated in auction and 'Changes' art exhibition, and attended art openings and events outside the Biennale weekends. 
As a curater and artist I appreciate art happenings  in unusual destinations. art celebrations, free offerings that defy the capitalist system we live in and spaces for the intellect and critical thinking. My work tries not to create any footprint, I use recycled, found, donated materials that are a great fit for Bombay Beach.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
As an environmentalist and activist I am fascinated with grassroots movements, inhabited ghosts towns, dystopian places, ecological disasters, finding out which Native American community calls a land home, which subcultures exist there and the harsh yet fragile environment of the desert. Also places artists call home outside the mainstream system. 
I first came out to the Salton Sea when the ruins of the resorts where still standing. Been studying plant and animal life, geological features, monitoring the bee population, searched for obsidian.  Attended desert art fairs like Desert X for many years, immersed in the art and cultural scene in both low and high desert, have explored the surrounding areas like Anza Borrego and the border region extensively and as a rockclimber have spent a lot of time in Joshua Tree.
I've studied the desert Native American communities and support Temalpakh farms. 
Very aware of the ecological crisis and been following the attempts for environmental improvements. 
After coming out for last 3 years I would like to  add my voice to the dalogue permanently and establish an artistic presence in BB.
Installation should be maintained and expanded on for every biennale, shapeshift and incorporate different aspects and ideas to future biennale's themes.

fields.Project Description:
This work begins from an awareness of fire as a constant presence rather than a singular event. Recent fires in Los Angeles form part of the broader backdrop, but the piece is not a direct memorial or response to one moment. Instead, it reflects on how fire exists alongside daily life—as risk, as a tool, and as something we are continually learning to live with. Destruction here is not framed as spectacle, but as a condition that can also produce warmth, food, and temporary gathering.
A dining table and thirteen chairs are constructed entirely from firewood sourced in California. At first, the set reads as familiar and functional: a place to sit, eat, and gather. The furniture holds bodies and plates as expected, but its material makes its fate clear. What supports the meal will soon become the fire that prepares it. The table and chairs are designed for a single use, collapsing the distinction between object and fuel, structure and process.
The burning begins at the center. The central chair and table are lit first, creating a slow, controlled progression outward toward both ends of the set. As the fire spreads, seats are consumed one by one. The available space to sit gradually disappears, altering how people relate to the work and to each other. Gathering becomes time-bound. Participation shifts from sitting to standing, from use to observation, as the furniture transitions from structure to ash.
As the set burns, bread is cooked directly in the ashes, and wine is served. Eating takes place alongside the fire, not after it. The act of nourishment is inseparable from the act of burning. The same material that once supported bodies becomes heat, then embers, then residue. Comfort is exchanged for sustenance, and permanence for immediacy.
The work is staged on the beach, close to the water. This location minimizes fire risk while situating the piece within an open landscape, where the horizon and changing light are part of the experience. Fire and water coexist in close proximity, emphasizing balance and containment rather than catastrophe. The setting allows the gathering to feel both intimate and exposed, grounded in the present moment.
Titled The Last Supper, the piece marks a finite window of use. When the fire is finished, nothing remains of the table or chairs as objects. What persists is the memory of having shared food and space during a process that made its own disappearance visible, gradual, and collective.
fields.Abridged Project Text: This is the Last Supper before it goes up in flames. A dining table and chairs to gather around before it becomes the bonfire on which we cook our meal. Sit down, eat and enjoy, before the set itself is consumed.
fields.Technical Support: We need a location on the beach, wide enough, flat enough for a table and 13 chairs, and a safe fire zone around it. Ideally somewhat of a 40 foot diameter circle.
fields.Audience Engagement: People will be able to use the table for picnics casually throughout the first day and most of the second day. When the burning performance takes place on the second day, only the fire-starters will be near the set. The audience will be a safe distance from the fire until it is finished. At that point, everyone will be able to enjoy the bread cooked on the fire and drink some wine.
fields.Theme Engagement: We are also tired of waiting for God and decided to burn it all down. Let’s enjoy a Last Supper as we watch our hopes and dreams go up in flames.
fields.Risk & Safety: The project involves fire. We can create a safe visible perimeter around the piece with lights and rope if necessary. We will also bring two fire extinguishers and enough water to calm the fire down. We will balance the fuel load (how much wood we burn) to a reasonable amount. We have experience building large scale fire installations.
fields.Location Submittal: On the beach, but we need help to find a good spot
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): This project hasn’t been shown anywhere else – we are creating it for the 2026 Biennale. A new collection of furniture will also be designed and created for this performance. We’ve made a few large scale bonfires at events in the past.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
As a design practice, we’ve focused on using California woods for creating unique fine furniture pieces. Wood has been sourced from small mills, housing construction offcuts, and felled trees. We see local firewood as another material for furniture and want to explore how this could translate into a ritual experience. 

The design process will include three lead designers who will create the furniture pieces and organize the performance. Other collaborators will help transport and set up the piece as well as monitor the fire.

This is a project that we had already envisioned, but Bombay Beach was on top of our minds as a place that might appreciate the renegade and ephemeral nature of it. The Last Judgment theme then seemed like a perfect fit as well, since this is a biblical-ish piece about gathering and communality via destruction.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): We have been thinking about developing a series of furniture using firewood for a while. This year’s theme of the Last Judgment really struck a chord and felt like the right push for us to produce this work. We are also excited to contribute to the Biennale after attending for the first time in 2024.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): We have developed the concept for the project and are prototyping the furniture pieces that will be included in the performance. We also are in contact with experienced fire-related artists who are advising best practices regarding the fire.
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I am visiting from elsewhere

fields.Project Description:
Death is coming for us all, but to some, the true horror is the infinite return back to the living - an idea prevalent in many religions and culture across time, such as the many levels of earthly hell depicted in Buddhism. Within five sonic sarcophagi of Algo(g)rave, you can make an intentional choice to leave everything behind - however, the only way out is to break the cyclic nature of the material-time mesh network. Many think this means silence. I believe this means white noise. The syntax of the soul repeats over and over until the state itself changes. From solid state to ghost, the transition between recursive complexity to static resolution is a cycle of loops that repeats until the performance is interrupted. From loop to silence, eventually the code executes it's final instruction: exit ( ). Algo(g)rave is the final song and the transition into static. The last judgement is upon you - how will you respond?

Algoraves are an up-and-coming, yet still seemingly underground, post-modern event where music and visuals are generated in real-time by artists writing computer code, allowing  audiences to dance to mostly improvised electronic beats and patches. All mistakes and accidents are blended with knowledge, skill, and intuition in full view of the audience. It feels like they are participating in the creative experience together. It is a bold and visceral statement in opposition with the fast-paced gluttony of AI-slop if the modern worlds. Musician's do not use pre-recorded tracks, but rather programming languages like TidalCycles or SuperCollider which are both free, open-source platforms. In many ways, algoraves can feel like free-form jazz performances with infinite potential, similar to the tangentially-expanding nature of the universe.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Death is inevitable, but the true horror is the infinite return. Inspired by Buddhist earthly hells, Algo(g)rave explores possibilities of disrupting the material-time mesh network of life. To escape the cycle, we move through recursive loops toward white noise—the soul’s final, static resolution.
fields.Technical Support: Solar panels and batteries, projector, 5 screens, 10+ bluetooth speakers, PA system, mixer, bela board (TidalCycles and SuperCollider), plywood,
fields.Audience Engagement: All of the above. Each coffin will be a curated, individualized sensory experience. The following are potential ideas: shovelling sounds and other death simulators word loops that are never repeated twice, live-coded performances shot straight to the dome, painfully mainstream woke wook bait, audio static - in Strudel this reads as s("noise"). The audience will be heavily involved in the senses and the choices presented in this experience. The hope is a for a meaningful transformation that leaves participants softer, kinder, gentler when moving through the afterworld of Bombay Beach.
fields.Theme Engagement:
​This project reimagines the Last Judgment not as a moral courtroom, but as a definitive system audit. In this framework, the end of the world is the ultimate execution of an 'exit()' command (TidalCycles), transitioning the soul from living to the dead. 
​
​While traditional eschatology weighs sin, this work examines behavioral syntax. The infinite return can be considered a logic error. Within the performances Algo(g)rave, the Last Judgment is the diagnostic moment that determines if a soul is clean code ready to terminate or corrupted data destined for a never-ending loop.

​Rather than a binary separation of "wheat and chaff," this project proposes white noise as the ultimate leveler. Containing all frequencies simultaneously, white noise represents the sonic period. Judgment is the phase-shift where the dissonant complexity of lived experience resolves into the absolute, egalitarian frequency of static.
​
​The performance utilizes the computing concept of an interrupt as a metaphor for divine intervention. It is the moment the code executes its last instruction, forcing the transition from a solid-state existence to a ghost-state resolution.
fields.Risk & Safety: The only risks are deactivation.
fields.Location Submittal: Seamus' Lot
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Never been built or thought of before
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
The creation of these sarcophagi will involve generative audio experimentation, using algorithms to create "loops of loops" that slowly degrade into white noise.
​
Collaborators: I will work with creative coders to develop the live-execution software and local sculptors to incorporate Bombay Beach detritus into the coffins.

​Community Engagement: I envision the Last Judgment as a participatory audit. Residents and visitors will be invited to submit their own recursive burdens to be fed into the algorithm and resolved into the final static performance.
​
Practice and Context
​My practice explores the friction between ancient theology and modern syntax. To me, the Last Judgment is the ultimate system audit. By placing this at the Salton Sea, I am performing an autopsy on the material-time mesh. The work is a transition from complexity to resolution, mirroring the Sea’s own slow fade into a salt-white void. This isn't just a performance; it is the final song of a dying ecosystem, executed with the precision of a final line of code. It is important to phrase the Salton Sea this way now as a cry for help from those with resources if we wish to save this place.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): This area has been in a devastating loop for a long time - decay and death. It's time to look to the future and save it!!!
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Ieas and dev

fields.Project Description:
The Thread of Life | Act III: Own Your Fate is an immersive ritual-performance and installation unfolding in the desert landscape of Bombay Beach, at the edge of the Salton Sea.
The work begins at dusk with a slow procession emerging from Mars. Masked figures walk toward a trailer titled The Ends of the Earth. They are guided by Nyx, primordial goddess of Night, and her three daughters, the Moirai — the mythic Fates who spin, measure, and cut the thread of life. The performers are physically connected by a single continuous thread: The Thread of Life. A three-headed bat figure, the Minyades, calls the public to join the walk, transforming spectators into participants as they cross the desert/town together.
In front of the trailer stands a small booth called The Passage, displaying the phrase: “Passages to the Ends of the Earth. Price: self-judgment destruction.” Nearby is the participatory station Scar Wash. Before entering, each person receives a “Le Diable” card and is asked to confront what holds them back — self-judgments, fears, inherited limitations. They must destroy the card at Scar Wash — burn it, bury it, tear it, drown it — as an act of release. Only then may they enter.
Audience members enter the trailer three at a time.
In the first room, Nyx sits in a corner processing plant fibers by hand, quietly spinning the material that will become the Thread of Life. A projection shows documentation of fiber harvesting and dyeing filmed during a residency in Maine. On a table rests her artist book, How to Craft the Thread of Life. A hidden note invites visitors to read aloud. If they do, Nyx breaks her silence, offers tea, and welcomes them. If not, she continues working. This conditional interaction transforms passive viewing into intimate exchange.
When ready, participants enter the second room — the Room of the Fates. The Moirai stand beside a large Loom of Life, wearing clocks and animal masks: whale (Clotho), pronghorn (Lachesis), and vulture (Atropos). A recording of my grandmother and mother singing the Portuguese song “Meada” fills the space. The Moirai perform a rhythmic ritual: spinning, measuring, cutting thread in time with the song.
When the song ends, they speak. They reveal that the Fates are not distant authorities but mirrors — ancestors, grandmothers, blood memory. They remove their masks and invite participants to take their place: to spin, measure, cut, and weave their own thread into the Loom of Life.
Each person receives a “Le Jugement” card with instructions and exits through the growing tapestry — a collective weave of many lives.
After the live performance, the installation remains open. Video projections and written instructions allow visitors to continue contributing independently.
The work moves from open desert to intimate interior, from procession to ritual to participation. It reframes fate not as something assigned, but as something woven — together.
fields.Location Notes: Need space with power + room outside, procession sunset on Friday . Behind Saturn? 2169 2nd st?
fields.Abridged Project Text: “The Thread of Life | Act III: Own Your Fate” is an immersive ritual-performance where myth, ancestry, and craft colide. Guided by Nyx and the Moirai, participants are invited to own their fate and weave it into the Loom of Life.
fields.Technical Support:
The project will take place inside an old trailer (The Ends of the Earth), which I already have as the indoor space. I will need a site to place the trailer, ideally outdoors and near Kamau’s Scar Wash, so the procession and participatory ritual of releasing the “Le Diable” cards can take place seamlessly.
Inside the trailer, I will need electricity for basic lighting and audio to play the recording of the song Meada. A projector, a screen and speakers would be great, but I may also be able to find them in Mars. 
Outdoors, the space should safely allow a procession to move from Mars toward the trailer while maintaining the Thread of Life connection between performers and participants. The proximity to Scar Wash is essential, as this is where participants symbolically destroy their “Le Diable” cards before entering the trailer.
In summary, the project needs:
•	An outdoor site to place the trailer near Scar Wash
•	Electricity for lighting and audio
•	Optional projector, screen and speakers
fields.Audience Engagement:
This work is not something audiences simply watch — it is something they move through and actively shape.
At dusk, people may encounter a slow procession crossing the desert and choose to follow. From the beginning, engagement is physical and voluntary: walking together toward the "Ends of the Earth", sharing space and intention.
Participation deepens at "The Passage", where each person receives a “Le Diable” card and must decide whether to destroy it at "Scar Wash". Burning, tearing, burying, or drowning the card becomes an act of release. Entry requires action.
Inside the trailer, engagement turns intimate. In Nyx’s room, visitors must choose whether to read aloud and activate the encounter. In the Room of the Fates, they witness ritual, then are invited to step into it — to spin, measure, cut, and weave their own thread into the Loom of Life.
After the live performance, the installation remains open. Visitors can enter independently, guided by written instructions and video, and continue contributing to the growing tapestry.
The experience moves from procession to participation, from observation to embodiment. Each person leaves a physical trace — their thread woven into a collective structure — making the work both deeply personal and shared.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Thread of Life | Act III: Own Your Fate engages with The Last Judgment by reframing it from a final divine verdict into a living, collective process.
Traditionally, the Last Judgment imagines an external authority deciding who is saved or condemned. In this work, judgment is internal, inherited, and chosen. Before entering the installation, participants must confront and destroy a “Le Diable” card representing self-judgment — shame, fear, internalized limits. The act is not about punishment, but about release.
Inside the Room of the Fates, the Moirai initially appear as mythic judges of destiny. Yet they reveal themselves not as distant arbiters, but as mirrors — as ancestors, as grandmothers, as blood memory. Judgment shifts from something imposed to something embodied. The question becomes: Who has the authority to define your life?
The project also situates judgment within Bombay Beach itself — a landscape marked by environmental collapse, abandonment, reinvention, and survival. Here, “the end of the world” is not apocalyptic spectacle but ongoing reality. The work asks: What do we choose to carry forward? What do we cut away? What must end so something else can begin?
Rather than staging salvation or damnation, the piece proposes The Last Judgment as a continuous act of weaving — a reckoning with inheritance, responsibility, and interconnection. Fate is not delivered from above; it is shaped through participation.
Judgment becomes not an ending, but a threshold.
fields.Risk & Safety: The only potential risk involves moving the trailer to its installation site, making sure that has a good road access, so the trailer doesn’t get stuck anywhere.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create The Thread of Life | Act III: Own Your Fate because it is both a personal and communal reckoning with inheritance, choice, and transformation. At this moment, the world feels caught between collapse and renewal, and I am drawn to exploring how myth, ancestry, and ritual can provide a space to confront what we carry forward—and what we release. In this work, I ask: What does it mean to own your fate? Who defines judgment? How do inherited histories shape our present actions, and how can we collectively reweave them into new patterns?
The piece unfolds as an immersive ritual-performance at Bombay Beach, where environmental fragility, abandonment, and reinvention mirror the internal landscapes of fear, limitation, and inherited constraints. By situating the work in this desert environment, the procession across sand and salt becomes a meditation on movement, connection, and passage. Participants encounter Nyx, the Moirai, and the symbolic Thread of Life, moving from observation to embodiment, from witnessing judgment to participating in it. The ritual of confronting the “Le Diable” card—through burning, tearing, or burying—invites self-reflection and release. Inside the trailer, the Room of the Fates transforms spectators into collaborators, offering a tangible way to weave personal and collective threads into a living tapestry. Judgment is reframed: it is not a verdict imposed from above but an ongoing, participatory, and intimate process.
My practice is rooted in the intersections of performance, ritual, craft, and communal storytelling. I work with materials, movement, and time to activate spaces where audiences become participants, creating work that is relational, sensory, and generative. In Bombay Beach, the landscape itself is a collaborator—its history, ecology, and community inform the piece and offer a profound resonance with the themes of inheritance, survival, and transformation. Collaboration is central: performers, participants, and the community become co-authors of the narrative, while the Loom of Life physically embodies collective action and memory.
This project continues my ongoing exploration of weaving myth, ancestry, and participatory performance into contemporary ritual. The desert, the trailer, Scar Wash, and the Thread of Life are not merely settings or props—they are integral to the work’s logic and meaning. By engaging the local environment, inviting voluntary participation, and framing judgment as an act of reflection and creation, I hope to offer a space for both individual release and communal weaving of fate. The Thread of Life is a threshold, a living conversation between past, present, and future, and it is urgent now: in moments of uncertainty, we need spaces to confront what binds us and to enact what we wish to carry forward.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The Thread of Life | Act III: Own Your Fate is inseparable from Bombay Beach and the Salton Sea. The landscape itself mirrors the work’s exploration of inheritance, collapse, and renewal. Here, the “Ends of the Earth” are not abstract; they are lived, visible, and tangible. The site’s history of human adaptation, environmental upheaval, and reinvention resonates with the ritual of confronting limits and choosing what to carry forward.
The Biennale provides the framework to activate this environment through immersive performance, connecting local histories with broader human questions of fate, ancestry, and responsibility. By situating the trailer near Scar Wash, the procession across the desert and the act of releasing the “Le Diable” cards become a shared, site-specific meditation on transformation.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Part way throught

fields.Project Description:
The border of the USA is mostly thought of as an invisible line delineating the boundaries of the state, but legally speaking, its enforcement zone extends up to 100 miles into the interior, and often more. An unpublicized amount of internal checkpoints run by Customs & Border Patrol (CBP) dot this 100-mile zone, through which agents monitor passing motorists.

My Niland II (Ten Checkpoints) is a durational multichannel video art installation projected onto a car windshield and potentially two front doors. The length is due to the videos consisting of interior and exterior views of my wife’s car as our friend Lou drives it through at least ten internal CBP checkpoints between Arizona and California, on the way to Niland. Three videos will be projected onto a car windshield and side window glasses  (removed from vehicle). A fourth channel will be displayed on a rear-view mirror with back-up camera display. There will be synchronized sound from the microphones in the car. The car glass orientation may vary based on the exact installation location, but ideally it’ll be visible to the street at all hours, as I’ll have the installation timed to run only when the skies are dark enough for projections to be visible.
fields.Support Needed: I am aware I don’t have much in terms of prior art, links to past installations, documentation, etc. This is a deliberate choice to foreground the present work. I will not be installing verbose exhibition prosthetics to situate the work with the viewer, brief explanations will be provided at install, de-install, and whenever I’m around.  Part of this foregrounding/distancing is to reflect the operations of distance the border zone operates through and the piece encases. The projection of the piece mirrors the distance at which I experience the drive through Lou and the continuously morphing distance of the border.  The other (and hopefully obvious to some) part is that the ephemerality of documentation and my pseudonym are tactics borne out of necessity.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Hussein, Antimony. My Niland II (Westward Journey). 2026. Video projection, multi-channel, buttermilk, car windshield, rear-view mirror. duration tbd, likely 24 hours.
fields.Technical Support: I’ll provide all the equipment necessary for building/taking down My Niland II. If it has to be on Dada’s compound (my backup), electricity won’t be an issue. If I’m able to secure a location elsewhere, somewhere relatively quiet with a power socket would be ideal.
fields.Audience Engagement: My Niland II, as a video installation running through the night, is a ‘stumble into at 3am’ piece. The time spent engaged with the piece would vary per person’s attention/interest/state, but I envision it as something people would hopefully check in on a couple times to see if they could come in on mountain views, a CBP checkpoint, one of Lou’s driving monologues, etc.
fields.Theme Engagement: Part of My Niland II will include various people calling Lou while she drives to Niland. I’ll ask one of them to pass on this question about the Last Judgement. When “The Last Judgement” happens I won’t have to make My Niland III, and everyone will be happy.
fields.Risk & Safety: If it's outside Dada's compound I'll probably bolt it to the ground so the projectors don't grow legs and run away.
fields.Location Submittal: 2132 3rd St but ideally somewhere elsewhere that's ungated
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Nope.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Last time I was in Bombay Beach, at the end of a month and a half stay, I projected a video art installation on the windshield of my wife’s car. “My Niland” was conceived as a way for me to experience Niland through a day’s in-town social research as I didn’t want to drive through the Border Patrol internal checkpoint located just outside town—I’m a documented recipient of asylum, but it wouldn’t be a good idea. Andra had taken the Martians on a tour of Niland and I felt a little left out.

A little more research revealed that this internal station was part of a network of up to 170 total internal checkpoints. I immediately planned a far more ambitious road trip as my next video piece, but scaled it down after learning of the time this would take. This scaled down piece, hopefully landing somewhere between the two California traditions of road movie and crassly durational art, is My Niland II (Ten Checkpoints). Lou will drive from Tucson to Niland, filmed and recorded, passing through at least ten Border Patrol checkpoints. I will be directing remotely and occasionally call in. The piece will display all this in real-time.

Last time I was in Bombay Beach, the trailer I was staying in nearly burned down from a (very unfortunate) fire raging next door. The first official on the scene was a Border Patrol agent. He stopped looking at the fire to loudly admonish us for looking at the fire, shooing us away in that kind of stiff motion agents and officers all learn, then reparked his car a little farther and continued staring at the flame.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is located in Niland, and I love Niland, the same love every astronaut has experienced for our little blue beach ball on their first spacewalk, a love continually renewed. My Niland II (Ten Checkpoints) speaks to the landscape, histories and communities quite literally by depicting them in video form for hours on end. Despite its simple presentation, My Niland II is the product of a research-based practice, and this will be depicted in the audio content of the piece.

As for the Biennale—this is a site-specific work that only fully resolves in the Greater Niland area (Bombay Beach included), and while exhibition of the piece would be possible at any time in the year, projecting immediately after shooting in an environment designed to promote encounters with challenging works realistically is the optimal context. More people will finally understand that we all really live in our own private Greater Nilands.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I’m done with video conception/development, and have tested multiple rigs on the vehicle. We plan to film on the way to Bombay Beach. I’ll be directing remotely from BB as Lou drives the car from Tucson to Niland. The installation setup is very site-speci

fields.Project Description:
God Is/Sustenance functions as a tandem ritual experience unfolding across Bombay Beach.

Sustenance begins the pilgrimage in the landscape. A series of ritual stations placed throughout the town guide participants into an embodied relationship with the land. Each post centers on a bodily sensation such as listening, breath, grounding, or stillness, offering prompts and sound meditations that slow participants into communion with place.

The stations begin at the entrance of Bombay Beach and extend through key intersections, forming a pathway that leads visitors inward toward the central altar site.

God Is is the culminating encounter. Here, participants find artist Courtney Alexander seated in ceremonial presence, draped in black masquerade (inspired by Central African cosmology and performance) as a reflection of her divinity. Visitors are invited to create and leave offerings, sitting in meditation as they contemplate the sacred mirrored back to them.

The journey moves from land to body, asking participants to recognize divinity in the environment as well as within human form.
fields.Support Needed: We are prepared to adapt the installation to the spatial and environmental needs of the site and are committed to working in alignment with the land and community. Documentation and future iterations of the work will continue to honor Bombay Beach as the originating landscape of God Is / Sustenance.
fields.Abridged Project Text: God Is / Sustenance centers the body and land as sacred sites. A living altar invites offering and reflection on embodied divinity, while immersive stations throughout the landscape guide participants into contemplation, reverence, and renewed relationship with the environment.
fields.Technical Support:
The project requires outdoor installation support across multiple sites within Bombay Beach. Technical needs include solar or generator-based electricity for sound installations, particularly for the audio meditation station.
Weather-resistant speakers, small amplification systems, and projection capability for select nighttime elements may be required.
The central ceremonial site will require structural support for altar construction and seating or ground covering for participants.
Installation assistance for transport, placement, and securing of stations is needed.
All materials will be designed for minimal environmental disruption and safe public interaction.

fields.Audience Engagement:
Audience engagement is participatory, self paced, and experiential. Visitors move through the stations with varying levels of intention. Some may seek out the full experience, while others may encounter a post or altar site by chance.
Whether planned or unexpected, each interaction is designed to return participants to presence with the land. Sound, meditation, and ritual prompts invite moments of listening, stillness, offering, and reflection rather than passive observation.
Some visitors may arrive at God Is first, encountering the embodied altar before moving outward into the landscape. Others may begin within Sustenance and arrive at the central site later.
In either direction, the work asks participants to shift from consumption to reciprocity. The focus is grounding, reverence, and giving back to the environment rather than being entertained by it.
fields.Theme Engagement:
My relationship to The Last Judgement comes from a long standing reflection on Revelation and the idea of divine reckoning. I think less about a God descending to judge good or bad, and more about the lived consequences of how we move through the world.
To me, judgement shows up through what happens when we fall out of right relationship with land, history, and spirit. Bombay Beach already holds the evidence of this rupture through its environmental condition and cultural memory.
Sustenance brings participants back into intentional presence with the land, asking them to confront that relationship directly.
God Is holds the other side of that encounter. Participants sit before an embodied reflection of divinity that feels tangible, mysterious, and unfamiliar, yet deeply ancestral.
Through both works, visitors are asked to reckon with divinity in the environment and in human form, particularly through a body that exists at the intersections of marginalization and challenges dominant images of the sacred.
fields.Risk & Safety:
All stations will be structurally secured and constructed from stable, weather-appropriate materials.
Sound equipment and electrical elements will be safely enclosed and, where possible, solar-powered.
Pathways between stations will remain publicly accessible and unobstructed.
Clear signage will guide participants while minimizing environmental impact.
fields.Location Submittal: Throughout town plus Poetry House
fields.Previous Showing (Grant):
Sustenance is an entirely new project, and a world premiere. God Is/Sustenance will debut in Chicago in 2026 or 2027. We received a 3Arts Advanced Projects matching grant to fundraise $6,000+ towards moving the show to Chicago. We intend to tour God Is/Sustenance as a work that premiered in this iteration at the Biennale. 

God Is will also be re-styled into an immersive performance piece with the film projected during the performance, as well as interactive offering elements, so it will be evolved from the 2024 installation.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We are interested in bringing God Is / Sustenance to the Bombay Beach Biennale because the landscape already reflects many of the concerns at the core of this work.
Our collaboration is rooted in ritual practice, land awareness, and creating spaces where reflection can happen through direct encounter. Bombay Beach holds a visible history of environmental strain alongside creative reimagining, making it a meaningful site to situate this installation.
We are drawn to the town’s threshold quality. It exists between collapse and reinvention, memory and futurity. That tension aligns with how we approach installation as both offering and reckoning.
Presenting the work here feels less like placement and more like continuation of an ongoing dialogue between land, art, and communal presence. We are interested in contributing something grounded, contemplative, and in relationship with the environment rather than imposed onto it.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach holds an environmental history that makes it a meaningful site for this work. The rise and decline of the Salton Sea, alongside the visible shifts in the town’s landscape, reflects long term ecological consequence that remains present in the land today.
The Biennale has cultivated a culture of ingenuity, communal exchange, and creative activation that exists alongside contemporary art practice. There is already a shared understanding that work here can engage land and community beyond commercial frameworks.
What we feel called to bring into this environment is a sacred installation that remains in relationship with the town even outside the Biennale timeframe. A site that visitors and residents alike can return to repeatedly for reflection, offering, and grounding.
The intention is for the work to function as an ongoing point of connection with the land. Not just something encountered once, but something revisited over time.
In this way, the installation operates similarly to monuments or temple sites that people return to for presence, prayer, or contemplation.
It is meant to benefit the community through continuity, offering a space that sustains relationship between people and environment beyond the festival moment.

fields.Artist Name: M. G. Espar with Frenchie, Mike Espar (aka M. G. Espar)
fields.Project Description:
The Silver Tongued Devil: A Speaker-Creature is an interactive sound sculpture constructed out of discarded musical instruments and speakers. (Approximate dimensions: 48” W x 78” H x 58” D.) At first blush, the Creature appears to be the abandoned love child of Dumbo and Johnny 5. But as you approach, it comes alive with sound and light. An ominous tune, both angelic and diabolical, emanates from deep inside its bowels, churning with heavier and heavier distortion, until the music is completely desecrated by noise. Signs beckon you to step up to receive “complimentary compliments!” and “free flattery!” if you dare. Press a button and the Creature speaks a flattering phrase just for you. 

The piece is a resurrection through objects. The primary materials (broken amplifiers, pieces of guitars, semi-truck horns) were received from an anonymous San Diego musician after he passed away last summer. Although his original plan for these items can’t be known, the musical sculpture keeps his spirit alive.
fields.Support Needed: I would like to have the Creature set up and functional for the whole weekend (but would disable the audio overnight). I would like to have an official "debut" of the sculpture at 7:30pm on Friday 3/27, where I would playback an extended piece of music and answer questions about the piece. I am would be happy to change the timing of the "debut" if it conflicts with other events. Lastly, although I would prefer to set it up outside (but near a power source), it could also be effective inside a gallery or trailer. Thanks for reading!
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Silver Tongued Devil: A Speaker-Creature is an interactive sound sculpture constructed out of broken music equipment collected by a recently-deceased San Diego musician. Visit the Creature to hear angels plummeting into the Underworld—and to receive your own flattering fortune!
fields.Technical Support: To power the speakers and lights, the project would need electricity. I envision it being located outdoors near the Zig Zag house, but I am open to other suggestions (indoors or outdoors).
fields.Audience Engagement: People would interact with the Creature by approaching it (triggering music) and then pressing a button to trigger pre-recorded phrases of flattery. Depending on the location of the Creature, the audio could be disabled at certain times of day to avoid conflicting with other events.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Yes. The Creature’s appearance evokes a techno-underworld. Its baseless complements were generated by AI chatbots, showcasing their penchant for supplication and for leading the user down a path of delusion. While “judgement” implies a search for objective truth, the Silver-Tongued Devil exists in a world where facts are malleable and vacuous flattery reigns. 

By incorporating found objects from a deceased musician into the Creature, the sculpture is an act of both resurrection and reincarnation.  

Musically, diabolical distortion swallows up the angelic timbres of choirs, flutes, trumpets, and pipe organ, representing the transformation from saint to sinner. Additionally, the sonic degradation caused by the distortion mirrors the physical weathering of structures in Bombay Beach from extreme sun, heat, and wind.
fields.Risk & Safety: Nope. :-)

fields.Project Description:
there are 7 rooms and 250 artworks over 1500 square feet as of the date of this application, by March 27th it will be 500 pieces of art and probably bigger than it is now. it may expand out on the beach,, but currently it is on 3rd and D streets. it is an art museum, music venue, art gallery, hostel, cafe, and art studio. I want to have an event there that is composed of music and performances. I would love to host a biennale event and other artists. I plan to provide bathrooms and perhaps even showers, and could accommodate guests. if nothing else I would like to have a showing there of my art that it contains but I would love to help the biennale. 
The paintings are of my life for the last 5 years, including Lake Como, Milan, Tuscany, Paris, Vienna, Provence, Normandy, Mexico, and too many places in the United States to list. I particularly love painting musicians, and every painting is done to the rhythm of music.
the buildings glow at night when lit from within, and light shines through them in the day.
fields.Support Needed: I just picked a date. I am flexible completely (eep)
fields.Abridged Project Text: built of art like legos on 4'x6' panels, the installation is composed of 250 paintings digitized onto coreplast. this habitrail of my imagination is a construction of the places people and art i love so much I had to paint a picture of them and build them a cappella.
fields.Technical Support: I would love some help with lighting and power. I have my own, but experts in lights would bring this thing to life. Any construction volunteers would be welcome.
fields.Audience Engagement: I will set up small painting studios where people can paint the musicians or still life vignettes I will set up, I would also be willing to explain what I do in a class setting.
fields.Theme Engagement: my building was quite literally inspired by the Sistine Chapel.

fields.Project Description:
Echoes of a Dying Sea is an instrument disguised as a fish. You walk up, talk into its mouth, and the Salton Sea sends something back. Not symbolism. Your voice goes to an underwater speaker, crosses a stretch of water, hits a hydrophone, and returns as smear, echo, and reverb shaped by salt and distance. 

You shout into a fish and hear what the sea does to you.

The fish is cut from steel plate on a CNC plasma table, welded into a faceted body, then painted, abused, and rusted until it feels like it’s always lived here. Seams are left open so internal light leaks out, a hint that there is wiring and audio gear stuffed in the ribcage.
fields.Support Needed: Main help we want is connecting with others who will be playing music on the beach, and might want to use this reverb.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Echoes of a Dying Sea is a steel fish. Shout into its mouth and your voice travels to an underwater speaker in the Salton Sea, then returns through a hydrophone: transformed by salt and distance into smear, echo, and reverb.
fields.Technical Support: Will need access to the dock to launch a kayak to place the underwater speaker and microphone.  No other support - self-powered.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Two modes:

First, talking to the sea.  Folks wander up because it looks rad, talk to the fish (we have found with the model they talk to the fish's moth?!?), and hear what you sound like modified by the sea. It's delightful and weird. Some folks will also figure out they can hear distant boats or splashes they make themselves.

Second, we can apply the same effect to any instrument.  Think of Echoes as making the Salton Sea into a giant guitar reverb pedal.  I want nothing more than to see someone play a cello at sunset through the sea.
fields.Theme Engagement: Site specific, not theme specific.
fields.Risk & Safety: It's all 12 V, so low risk there. I guess a boat could get tripped up in the wires?
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): None.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We are very excited about making geometric animals, weird interactive sound art, and Bombay Beach. 

Geometric animals: we made our first last year, and it was the most fun we have had making anything, ever.  It was physically and mentally demanding, and the kind of project that made it hard to go to sleep at night because your brain is still buzzing.  We want to make more!

Sound art: we have a history there, from theremin-powered time machines to a previous piece called "Hydrotheremirevebaphone" that was basically everything that made weird sounds. 

Bombay Beach: Our first time out was bringing the Temple of Floating Compression in 2024.  It's still there, and we visit it sometimes, folks send us pictures.  We love the reception we got, and the friends we made.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The Salton Sea is the ideal spot for this - a body of water that often quiet underwater - little boating, few crashing waves. I have a picture of my grandpa water skiing on the lake, which is hard to imagine now. Being in conversation with the sea, literally in conversation where your words are in the water, seems like it is needed.

The Biennale is the ideal event. The project works as a standalone, but needs curious people to walk up, discover it, want to interact.  And more, the musical opprotunities!
fields.Project Stage (Grant): We have a scale model we are still tweaking some - trying to get the feel right.  Started working on the underwater speaker (DIY) and figuring out the signal chain and lighting.  Will start full fabrication soon.  We are doing this for 2026.

fields.Project Name: The Last Catch (what remains as everything drains away)
fields.Project Description: "The Last Catch — What’s left when everything drains away" will be a 5-foot suspended fish skeleton hanging from an oversized hook. The head and tail will be mirror mosaic in a recognizable fancyVANDALS style—glittering, fractured, and reflective. The body will be made by the legend Spenser Little who has agreed to collaborate on this project with me.  He will bend his recognizable metal wire bent-wire words along the fish’s centerline like a skeletal poem. resembling a spine and rib bones. There will be a lighting element as well, I will work with Spencer to decide if the piece itself will light up or iif we want lights to illuminate it. He is a wizard with shadows and light play.  So whatever he has in store will be magic.  We intend for this piece to live in Bombay.
fields.Support Needed: I really feel like this piece could be an epic contribution to the landscape of Bombay.  To allow it to live up to it's potential we'd need some help monetarily.  This is not a project I want to have to skimp on.  I'm hoping to build it to it's greatest potential.
fields.Abridged Project Text: A 5-foot mirror-mosaic fish skeleton hangs from a hook at the Salton Sea. Part relic, part disco ball, part skeletal poem. A collaboration between fancyVANDALS and Spenser Little, it’s a monument to what still glitters when everything else drains away.
fields.Technical Support: We will be bringing everything.  Will need access to electricity and an epic place to hang it.
fields.Audience Engagement: This piece belongs where the people are.  It will glimmer, light up, throw reflections and enrich the environment of chaotic exuberance.  It will be recognizable from afar, but will have surprising details close up.
fields.Theme Engagement: The Last Catch is a skeletal fish suspended at a dying sea—judgement made physical. But it refuses to be grim and sullen. It sparkles, catches light, draws people in. The mirrors create reflection and connection—it's light in the dark. It's about life surrounded by death. It is what Bombay Beach means to me.
fields.Risk & Safety: Depending on placement, we will work accordingly. I'll be taking extra steps and using materials to ensure it will last in the elements
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): This will be a new creation, between myself and Spenser Little.  I don't believe either of us have collaborated with other artists for a project before.  So it's a new concept, a new creation and a new collaboration.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I've been making art at Bombay Beach since before I was an "artist" and before the Biennale was a "thing." It has shaped who I am as an artist. I've brought mirror works to Bombay the last few years, and for this 10-year anniversary, I wanted to create something extraordinary—something that embodies the sea and what Bombay Beach means in connection with a dying ecosystem.

The piece is a 5 foot mirrored fish suspended with a giant hook, will invite viewers from a distance and the steel word spine created by Spenser Little that runs through the body—language literally holding the fish together will invite a closer look at the details. 

To realize this vision, I reached out to Spenser, whose work I deeply admire. We don't really know each other, so I went out on a limb. He accepted the invitation to collaborate, setting the stage for something truly special.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): The image of fish bones is intrinsically connected to the Salton Sea. Though it represents only a fraction of what the sea and Bombay Beach are, a skeletal fish has become an iconic symbol of this place's history. Taking something macabre and making it sparkle and shine—that's what Bombay does. This piece is a physical manifestation of that ethos.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Concept has been dialed. Collaberation with Spenser confirmed.  I will begin my mirror mosaic then move to Spenser's studio in san diego for metal work
