fields.Project Description:
SYZYGY is a site specific ritual lab incorporating sculpture, anthropology, light and sound installation, exploring grief and ecological intelligence as fluid, transpersonal and transcorporeal processes. The work weaves together manual making, radical collaboration with place, and conversations across eco-feminist intelligences; invoking ritual and sonic information as ancient technologies for coregulation, (re)connection and healing. 

Location: Shed (comprised of three rooms - see images attached in PDF) on lot 9543 Ave H 

As an ecological intelligence system, materials sourced from the surrounding area carry the chemical memory of depleted agricultural land – environmental data to be read, transformed, and reintegrated. Through shared making, sound work, and ritual, land grief becomes collaborative labour. The artworks emerge as bio–reactive reliquaries of impermanence and renewal, linking bodies, land and water as one metabolic process. This is ecological grief work; a move away from denial in which acts of mourning are creatively alchemised into acts of making.
The space will be signposted externally by a 10 foot sculpture, in creation, by Wanda Orme - “Aperion”, referencing the wild, unbounded beyond-human intelligence of Salton Sea and its connection to the mystic river Lethe, the river of oblivion; gateway to the underworld and the aqueous threshold for the shedding of attachments to the material world. 
The floor of the installation space will be covered in crystalline salt, with points of light glowing softly through the saline surface forming constellations underfoot, sky becomes ground; a ritual inversion. Here, connection rises from below, as visitors walk carefully across the illuminated salt. This material choice also activates halotherapy in practice; salt’s capacity to support respiratory function and nervous system regulation through slow, mineral-rich breathing. Salt operates here as both medicine and witness—an ancient purifier holding memory and residue, registering the body’s need to breathe with the land rather than apart from it.
The walls will be finished with plaster embedded with salt and clay, forming a porous mineral skin, with alcoves holding ritual offerings created with materials sourced from the Salton Sea. As we move through to the second room, an obsidian wing created from salt encrusted feathers and beached wood holds space in suspension; grief transmuted into something that takes flight, a material gesture. Along with other offerings, the wing will be lit to emphasise a play of shadows - echoes of presence, hybridity and  distributed voice. At the final room, at the deepest part of the journey, we enter a darkened space emitting field recordings and musical compositions drawn from walking on salt, water, wind, and birds of the Salton Sea, emphasizing listening as a shared, relational act and a shedding of our senses and everyday sense-making to render ourselves vulnerable to un-knowing.
fields.Support Needed: Thank you!
fields.Abridged Project Text: SYZYGY: a site-specific ritual lab weaving sculpture, anthropology, light & sound installation. We explore grief and ecological intelligence as fluid, transpersonal and transcorporeal processes, invoking ritual and sonic information as ancient technologies for coregulation, (re)connection & healing.
fields.Technical Support:
SYZYGY is an indoor, site-specific installation housed within an existing shed structure, relying on physical materials, sculptural elements, ambient sound and architectural modification.
The project requires access to standard electrical power to support low-voltage lighting and sound elements. All lighting is low heat and energy efficient. Power cables, extension cords, and basic electrical access are required.  There is provision for this available on-site. Sound is delivered through a single speaker playing a continuous loop of audio via an iPod supplied by the artist. No amplification beyond a small speaker is required, and sound levels will remain low and ambient.
Basic tools and materials are required for installation, including ladders, hand tools, plywood, plaster, salt, adhesives, paint, hanging hardware, etc. Construction and installation will be completed by the artists. Initial preparation and clearing of space will employ locals, supporting the local economy. 
The project does not require internet access, special lighting rigs, or technical staff beyond initial access to power and space.
fields.Audience Engagement:
SYZYGY takes the form of a grotto-like environment at the shores of the Salton Sea—enclosed, cavernous, and womb-like—employing the technologies of shared attention, perspective switching, and deep listening to reanimate relationships between humans and the non-human world. The space will engage people through somatic experience, calling them into participation on a cellular level. This metabolic, bodily and intuitive entanglement with environment will create intimacy between internal and external landscapes, allowing people to experience themselves in a constellation of relationships that brings them into closer conversation with the place and with their own being in the world. The experience of the space will be a ritual journey, the tripartite structure of ritual: Separation, liminality and re-integration, will be mirrored in the tripartite structure of the space. 
As part of the installation, Brandy Eve Allen and Wanda Orme will co-facilitate a guided ceremonial workshop on creativity, alchemy, grief, and deep listening, extending the ritual lab into collective practice.
fields.Theme Engagement:
SYZYGY directly responds to the theme of the “The Last Judgement”, standing as a liminal space between worlds, attending to the truth beyond our everyday awareness, whilst exploring the creative and generative edge of uncertainty. 

The medicine and the sickness are one and the same. We cannot turn away to search for healing. SYZYGY asks for open-mindedness and the curiosity needed to sit–with. Not to solve or offer a solution to the ecological crisis in the form of management, but to provoke a shift in relatedness. Ritual calls upon other intelligences and puts us in conversation with the beyond human. It does not place humans at the top of a hierarchy, rather hitching our own minds and hearts to a conversation with the unknown; therefore it is vulnerable. In this sense judgment is not delivered but is instead metabolized through ritual space and ceremonial acts. It is absorbed, breathed, and worked through the body, registering as sensation and attunement rather than condemnation. What remains is not a verdict, but a threshold, an invitation to stay present long enough for relation itself to transform.
fields.Risk & Safety: Artists have prior experience of working under local conditions, associated risks will be controlled to reduce to negligible levels. The project does not involve fire, vehicles, excavation, drones, or hazardous machinery. Materials used include salt, plaster, resin, adhesives, and small electrical lighting components. All electrical elements are low voltage and safely sealed beneath or within materials. Walking surfaces will be level and clear to prevent slipping, floor level cables will be sealed or appropriately covered to prevent tripping. The installation is deemed low risk to the public.
fields.Location Submittal: Shed (comprised of three rooms - see images attached in PDF) on lot 9543 Ave H (between Mark Mack and Poetry House)
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Works included in this proposal have not been previously shown, they grow out of an ongoing collaboration and site specific engagement with the Salton Sea by the artists.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
SYZYGY is a vital project of listening and reverence for the beyond-human, in a time of avoidance and denial. Situated at the shores of an ongoing ecological crisis, the project weaves connection and intention into landscape through material gestures as acts of transmutation, through these we extend our awareness and capacities to access and understand the experience of being across difference. Acts of attention are a reconsecration. This practice is essential to redress the current breakdown in eco-social relationships and to bring hearts and minds into alignment with the truth of our present moment. 

Brandy Eve Allen is an Artist and Grief Doula whose multidisciplinary work focuses on interdependence, reverence and deep listening. Wanda Orme is an artist and anthropologist who has been working at the Salton Sea for more than 15 years, her work explores relationships between human and non-human bodies; the power of the unseen, consciousness, liminality and alterity. Together, their approach adds a vital voice to the creative landscape of Bombay Beach, one deeply grounded in place that situates and connects the local experience to wider conversations of ecological advocacy and ongoing developments in eco-consciousness and beyond-human intelligence. 

SYZYGY synthesizes radical collaboration with place with a designed somatic experience to create a contribution to the Biennale which augments conceptual depth and offers a grounding and healing experience to audiences. The project capitalises on the educational potential of the Biennale in a non-hierarchical and non-traditional manner, to bring knowledge and understanding of the place to an audience who may not otherwise be exposed to this. The project therefore contributes both to the short term experiential and experimental landscape of Bombay Beach and to the long term job of advocacy that is vital to its survival. 

This work emerges at a time where local environmental issues are becoming globally relevant; the Salton Sea is a bellwether of ecological distress worldwide. We hope that SYZYGY will bring international attention to the beauty, fragility and truth of this place. The work will also stand as an important contribution to the creative legacy of artists engaging in place that the Biennale is in a unique position to facilitate. 

In a place characterised by extractive enterprise we instead turn towards making an offering. In turning towards the problem, SYZYGY asks us for curiosity, rather than fear; might the judgement be the medicine? 

This is a conversation with place deeply woven through with heart and intention. 

Our deepest gratitude for you consideration, 

Brandy & Wanda
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
In a place characterised by extractive enterprise we instead make an offering. We add our labour to the labour of the natural world.
The ritual lab aims at bringing hearts and bodies back into alignment with the eco-relational web, a re-weaving that accesses deep somatic intelligence to let this place work on and through us. Through this we contribute to the intellectual and emotional legacy of the Biennale and the artist movement in Bombay Beach, generating new feelings, thoughts and ideas in a time desperately in need of them. We hope that SYZYGY will provide a space of deep reflection in the brilliant madness of the Biennale, filling a unique ecological niche in the ecosystem that is Bombay Beach.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): SYZYGY is an ongoing project of Wanda Orme and Brandy Eve Allen, commenced in Jan 2024 with field site engagement and deep listening practice at the Salton Sea. The philosophical and conceptual grounding of the project is well developed and our capacity t

fields.Project Description:
The Magic Mailbox is an interactive art installation designed to delight and surprise, especially at night, though it remains visually engaging during the day. The exterior will be painted in bright, playful colors using UV reactive paints that glow under blacklight, enhancing its presence after dark.

The piece invites attendees to approach and open the mailbox. When the door is opened, a sensor triggers an internal light show. The interior is a tunnel-like kaleidoscope constructed from 3D-printed components and laser-cut acrylic mirrors, illuminated by animated LED patterns. The result is an immersive, psychedelic experience that gives the illusion of peering into another dimension.

When the mailbox door is closed, the LED lights turn off automatically to conserve energy. The entire installation can be configured to run on battery power, allowing flexible placement even in remote or off-grid locations, such as along the back path or on the far side of a hill.
fields.Support Needed: I am excited to be considered for this art grant and for the opportunity to show my art at BBB for the first time.
fields.Abridged Project Text: You stumble across a glowing, mysterious mailbox. It beckons you closer—you can’t resist. The moment you open the door, a dazzling kaleidoscope of swirling lights bursts to life. For a breathless instant, you’re no longer here. You’ve slipped into another dimension.

fields.Audience Engagement:
The piece invites visitors at the Bombay Beach Biennale to approach and open the mailbox. When the door is opened, a sensor triggers an internal light show. Inside, viewers encounter a tunnel-like kaleidoscope constructed from 3D-printed components and laser-cut acrylic mirrors, illuminated by animated LED patterns. The result is an immersive, psychedelic experience that creates the illusion of peering into another dimension.

When the mailbox door is closed, the LED lights automatically turn off to conserve energy. The entire installation can be configured to run on battery power, allowing for flexible placement throughout the Biennale, including remote or off-grid locations.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Magic Mailbox engages with The Last Judgement as a quiet, personal, and slightly absurd moment of choice. A mailbox is a familiar object associated with messages, decisions, and acts of sending something outward without knowing what will happen next. In that sense, it mirrors the idea of judgment as a threshold between intention and outcome.

The piece invites viewers to decide whether to approach and open the mailbox. When they do, they are met not with answers, but with a glowing, tunnel-like interior that suggests depth, infinity, and uncertainty. There is no verdict or resolution. Instead, the experience becomes a moment of reflection, asking the viewer to sit with the unknown.

The illusion of a portal to another dimension challenges the idea of finality often associated with The Last Judgement. Rather than presenting an ending, the mailbox offers continuation and possibility. It suggests that judgment may be less about conclusions and more about perspective and awareness.

By transforming an everyday object into an unexpected encounter, the Magic Mailbox frames judgment as something intimate and voluntary. It is a small, playful intervention that invites curiosity and introspection, allowing each viewer to project their own meaning onto what they find inside.
fields.Risk & Safety: No risks or safety concerns, power consumption is low and should pose no risk.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): This art piece has been previously shown at the San Diego regional burning man event.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
This work already exists as part of my ongoing practice of transforming familiar objects into interactive, light-based experiences. I want to present it now as a quiet, intimate counterpoint to the vast and charged landscape of Bombay Beach. The mailbox functions as a small portal—an invitation to open something ordinary and encounter something unexpected inside.

The questions at the center of the work are simple: what are we hoping to receive, and what does it mean to cross a threshold? In the context of the Salton Sea and The Last Judgement, the piece reads as a moment of personal reckoning rather than spectacle—an opening instead of a verdict. The process continues to emphasize experimentation, durability, and public interaction, allowing the work to evolve through direct engagement in a harsh, transformative environment.

fields.Project Description:
Oracle Booth operates as a living system, simultaneously installation, instrument, and code. It is structured around time as a layered condition, where planetary motion in the present enters into dialogue with a visitor’s moment of birth and with the immediacy of embodied presence. Meaning does not arrive as an answer, but emerges through synchronicity, resonance, and duration.

The work takes the physical form of a small temple-like structure, a contemporary sanctuary designed for stillness rather than spectacle. It functions as a listening chamber: an architectural threshold where the body is framed in relation to time. Inside, the visitor is not a user or performer, but a co-oscillator—presence, proximity, and attention subtly modulate frequencies, thresholds, and temporal sensitivity within the system.

Sound is generative and ambient, aligned rather than composed. It unfolds from the convergence of slow-moving celestial rhythms, the present moment, and embodied attention. Harmonic fields, drones, and pulses emerge and dissolve without repetition, shaped by temporal relationships. Silence, drift, and instability are treated as active elements, allowing the system to remain open and responsive.

Astrology operates as a temporal structure, while the I Ching provides a parallel logic of change. Based on broken and unbroken lines, chance operations, and states of transformation, its hexagrammatic thinking informs how the system shifts between conditions. Here, the I Ching functions as a code of change, an abstract framework through which instability, decision, and emergence are articulated over time.

Language within Oracle Booth is treated as a material system. Text appears as structure: fragmented, spatial, and rhythmic. Drawing from concrete poetry and computational syntax, the work develops multiple modes of speech, statements, oracular fragments, recursive phrases, silences, generated in response to time, data, and interaction. Language behaves like sound or image: it modulates, drifts, repeats, and breaks, emphasizing perception over interpretation.

Visually, the work renders symbolic data structures, abstract diagrams, waveforms, cycles, and encoded forms that trace ongoing processes. These visual states evolve slowly, never repeating, privileging duration, variation, and transformation over resolution.

Chaos is not treated as error but as a generative force. Noise, micro-variation, and probabilistic behavior are embedded throughout the system, enabling new states to arise through uncertainty. In Bombay Beach, Oracle Booth unfolds continuously attuning itself to the desert’s altered temporality, geological duration, and fleeting human presence. It becomes a listening temple for time itself, where synthesis, language, chance, and attention briefly align.
fields.Support Needed: The final idea will be creating the APP, where people can access the oracle booth, and have a personalized music of the actual moment based on it ones aspects, transits and cosmic movements.

fields.Technical Support:
Oracle Booth is a self-contained structure designed for temporary outdoor desert conditions, operating autonomously on battery power.

Portable battery station providing 6 hours of operation. Solar panel array for daytime recharging. 

TVS and Single 16" portable touchscreen with integrated battery, Low power consumption

2.1 speaker configuration: 2 compact speakers (PreSonus Eris 3.5 Gen 2) positioned at corners, one battery-powered subwoofer (Mackie ThumpSub GO 8"). Audio interface (SCARLET) connects to dedicated computer running generative synthesis.

Mac mini 4 Pro running visual synth, audio synths, and custom software. Handles real-time astronomical calculations, generative visuals, sound synthesis, and sensor input.
                                                                                                                                
Camera (Tinybot 4k) for presence detection and body tracking. Presence, interaction and user's birth chart modulates system parameters in real-time.                                          
                                                      
Thermal printer producing receipts/traces on demand. Low power, no ink required.

Small enclosed wooden structure and pallet rack (approximately 2m x 2m x 2.5m) with single entrance, bench seating for 1-2 visitors. Weather-resistant construction suitable for desert climate (martian way). Can be assembled on-site. WIFI internet or 5g needed.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Oracle Booth appears in the landscape as a small temple, encountered both intentionally and by chance. People approach it, step inside, and stay for a period of listening or interaction. The work unfolds through duration, shaped by presence and time spent within the space.

Inside the structure, sound, image, and language are already in motion. Planetary transits, the present moment, and the visitor’s temporal origin shape the system continuously. Presence modulates the system in subtle ways, affecting intensity, rhythm, and density across sound and visuals. Engagement happens through stillness, proximity, and duration.

At certain moments, the system produces a physical output: a thermal print. This printed fragment functions as a temporal trace—part data, part concrete poetry. It may include symbolic structures, astrological configurations, fragments of language, or abstract diagrams generated at that specific moment. The print is not a receipt or explanation; it is a material imprint of the encounter, a present taken from the system’s ongoing state.

Visitors may keep the print, read it, fold it, or leave it behind. Each print is unique and time-stamped by celestial motion.

Oracle Booth supports brief encounters and extended stays. It accommodates solitude and quiet overlap. In the context of BB, the work operates continuously, aligning desert time, planetary movement, and human presence, and leaving behind small printed traces as evidence that an encounter occurred.

fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
The Oracle Booth: A Confessional for the Singularity

If 2026 marks the "Last Judgement" at Bombay Beach, the Oracle Booth is its forensic evidence. In a post-truth era where algorithms usually predict consumer behavior, I feel an urgent need to reclaim computation for revelation. This interactive installation is a "A Precision-Driven Oracle System", not designed to predict the future, but to relentlessly align the participant with the shape of the present.

The Bombay Beach Biennale, with its ethos of "dirty, dusty, and DIY" resilience, is the logical setting for this experiment. The Salton Sea acts as a purgatory: a beautiful landscape where civilization’s runoff accumulates. Placing a high-tech "confessional of the singularity" here creates a necessary tension between the corroded reality of the physical world and the immaculate, mathematical geometry of the digital soul.

The core question driving the Oracle Booth is: Does data have metaphysics?

I am asking if the "weighing of the soul" can be performed by an impartial digital syntax. Can I translate the invisible data of a human being, their astrological coordinates, their biological rhythms, their micro-movements—into a sensory language that feels like truth?

I am challenging the role of AI as a "hallucination machine." Instead, I propose Synaesthetic Syntax: a deterministic, polymeric language where:

Astronomy is Law: Saturn’s position dictates the bass frequency.
The Body is Witness: A webcam reads the querent’s presence—there is no lying to the pixel.
Sound is the Verdict: The output is not a pre-recorded track, but a generative harmony that vibrates in the bones, confirming if the user is "in tune" or dissonant with their own cosmic timing.

The process is an act of Collaborative Alchemy.

The Vessel (Installation): I will construct a "Cyber-Bedouin" booth: a structure built from reclaimed materials and rusted metal to survive the desert wind, housing a high-performance computation core (The Eye). It is a solitary space, a ritual chamber where the visitor enters to face the "digital mirror."
The Ritual (Community Engagement): The community participates not by observing, but by undergoing. Each session is a personalized ritual. The "Oracle" reads the visitor’s birth chart and transits data in real-time, generating a unique audiovisual "Judgement."
The Syntax (Experimentation): I am developing the Synaesthetic Syntax not as a black-box AI, but as a transparent poetic system. I combine ancient systems (Western Astrology, Balinese Pawukon, I Ching) with fluid dynamics and granular synthesis. The experimentation lies in tuning this translation, ensuring that when the data speaks, the human feels it and remember.

Using advanced technology to capture the invisible energies, I envision the Oracle Booth as a portal. In the context of The Last Judgement, it offers no salvation, only alignment. You enter, you are weighed by your own data, and you exit, perhaps not forgiven, but understood.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
This is my fourth year living in Bombay Beach during the season, and this project was born here. It didn’t arrive as an idea from outside, it emerged slowly, through time, presence, and living with the landscape.

Last year, during a sacred ceremony in the desert, I had a vision that became a turning point for this work. I felt deeply connected to the desert, and for brief moments, everything felt like one body. I could feel the desert alive, the planet pulsing, and a sense of infinity moving through everything. I had the sensation of becoming a kind of radar, capturing signals from the cosmos. This experience was super important for me and directly shaped the direction of the project.

Bombay Beach is a special place. It feels like a portal, a point of contact with something essential and truthful. The Salton Sea, the ruins, the dust, the silence, and the extreme conditions all create a landscape where time, systems, and narratives break down and can be reimagined. This environment invites experimentation, ritual, and listening in ways that are difficult to access elsewhere.

Within the context of the Biennale, this project speaks to the histories of the land, the ongoing ecological tension of the Salton Sea, and the community that continues to live, create, and imagine futures here. It is a work about connection, between landscape and technology, ritual and computation, chaos and precision, rooted specifically in this place.

These palm trees are interactive in that they spin with the wind and with human interaction, and lights shining above them cast moire shadows on the ground below.

fields.Project Description:
I am making a confession booth on wheels, that is also a Photo Booth. The booth will print out a picture, but only after the person has confessed to their sins or a juicy story or secret.
The participant finds a 8ft tall by 4ft x 4ft Photo Booth on wheels, and steps onto the platform and goes inside. The structure is covered in striped fabric, and is illuminated from the inside. 
The camera looks through a dichroic prism, which allows the image to capture a photo of all directions around the person (see reference images). 
After the person confesses some sin, they can press a button to take a picture, and it would print out their picture, which they can take away with them.

fields.Audience Engagement:
I imagine having this booth setup somewhere different each night, but it would stay in the city. A visitor would step into the Photo Booth structure, and be able to sit down, and have a comfortable setting to share their sin. Upon sharing their sin, they would have their picture taken, which would be printed out, so they can take it with them. 
The prism that the picture is taken through creates an analog filter, creating a picture with optical illusions around the visitor.
fields.Theme Engagement: The confession booth asks the visitor to confess their sins, so that the computer can "judge" their sin. The image that the visitor takes away with them has a kaleidoscopic optical illusions imposed on them, symboling that their judgement of their own sin is an illusion and their judgement of themself is just a made up construct.
fields.Risk & Safety: Due to this being a roaming project, I will ensure that it has brakes, and when parked, has enough weight or is ratcheted down so that nothing flies away in the wind.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): I'd created many things using the dichroic prism, however this would be first time creating a Photo Booth using this prism.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
This has been an idea of mine for a few years now. My interest in creating art with this prism has been to explore the idea that illusions are both made up and real at the same time. By letting the visitor take a picture through the prism, they expect an image that is based in reality, and are given one that shows illusions of a kaleidocope of what is currently around them - the illusion that everything around them is both real and yet their perception of what they believe to be true could actually be the illusion. 
And just with this perception of reality and illusions, I believe that the Biennale is the perfect example of that cognitive dissonance. I remember the first year I participated, Saturday night was bustling, and then by Sunday morning, everything was gone and it was like it never happened. And creating a roaming experience also plays into that, I saw that art piece here yesterday, where did it go?
fields.Why BBB (Grant): The BBB is my favorite event. It feels like the most amazing mix of artists creating art for artists, and a perfect balance of art that is rooted in temporary permanence, from different parts of artistic industries - e.g. immersive environments, art that incorporated interactivity, large sculptures, performance... ahhhh I love everything about it. I love the temporary permanence, and the reset to "normal" when it's over - which contrasts with the history of how the saloon sea was created - and the permanent changes in environment that have birthed this town. I love how the community keeps the town going, and the dedication to art that the community is rooted in.

fields.Project Description:
Inspired by 洞天 (dongtian), ancient Chinese sacred cave-paradises where immortals dwell and time flows differently, Grotto Heaven is an installation that functions as a portal between the earthly and celestial realms. It offers a refuge for visitors to experience rest, contemplation, and inner cultivation.

The installation is a freestanding topographic landscape structure adorned with herbal dyes and embedded salt crystals. This enclosure becomes the primary visual field: a hand-rendered terrain of clouds, mountain forms, and mineral textures that references both Daoist sacred grottos and evokes the salt-crusted shores of the Salton Sea. Herbal pigments—drawn from plants traditionally used in medicine and ritual—stain the surface in layered washes. Salt crystals, an important preservation and purification mineral, catch and refract light. 

At its center is a surface of deep blue turf (approximately 12’ x 12’) that is large enough for five to seven people to lie down simultaneously or 15 people to sit inside. The turf suggests sky rather than ground—an inversion that invites visitors to recline as if floating amongst the mountains. 

At intervals, low-lying fog drifts across the floor, forming ephemeral “clouds” that envelop visitors in sacred mountain mist. A subtle ambient soundscape of wind, distant water, and resonant tones creates a field of quiet immersion and introspection. Mugwort (艾草, Ai Cao), used in Daoist medicine for over two millennia to purify spaces and aid meditation, may be gently burned throughout the space, releasing a faint herbal scent.
fields.Support Needed: Faciltiated Experience could also be included in the installation: Would be very open to facilitating a ritual/workshop/traditional healing in the knoll at a certain time based on interest and schedule availability. Would love to workshop together what might be of need (e.g. herbal tea drinking, acupressure/somatic exercise, etc.)
fields.Abridged Project Text: Grotto Heaven awaits: float amongst lush mountains, wander through the clouds, see salt that sparkles and herbs that whisper. Recline and breathe in this Daoist-inspired pocket paradise—perhaps heaven is closer than we think.
fields.Technical Support:
Electrical needs: Outlets for fog machine, audio system, lights for the space

Location : Strongly prefer indoor location (a room 12’ x 12’) but design could be adaptable to covered outdoor space. 

We are able to bring everything else we need and install within 2 hours.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Grotto Heaven functions as an open refuge available 24/7. Visitors may intentionally seek it out or stumble into it at 3 a.m. while wandering Bombay Beach.

The installation requires no instructions. The scale of the central turf platform invites the body to recline; the collaged landscape painting invites close attention and lingering. Some visitors may lie down to escape the desert heat. Others may sit along the perimeter, trace salt crystals with their eyes, breathe with the drifting fog, or gather in quiet conversation.

Rather than spectacle, the piece offers a restorative place to recalibrate within the visual and emotional intensity of Bombay Beach. It is a structure for stillness embedded within a landscape.
fields.Theme Engagement:
If judgement determines who enters heaven, Grotto Heaven asks: what if heaven is neither distant nor deferred, but already accessible?

Rather than staging apocalyptic reckoning, the installation inverts it. In a place frequently framed through narratives of environmental collapse, the work proposes a present-tense paradise—a grotto-heaven entered through rest rather than righteousness.

Salt, central to the work, embodies judgement’s dual nature. It preserves and it corrodes. It crystallizes and it erodes. Daoist grotto-heavens were sites of withdrawal and cultivation, not divine sorting and retribution. Inner alchemy suggests that transformation is continuous; refinement unfolds through stillness, breath, and attention rather than a final cosmic verdict.

In this framing, the “Last Judgement” becomes less a moment of condemnation and more an invitation to consciousness. The work proposes transcendence not as escape from the world, but as deeper presence within it.
fields.Risk & Safety: N/A

fields.Project Description:
The Last Judgment is already happening; Bombay Beach is the ash field.

The Salton Sea stands as a monument to a broken covenant. Born from an engineering failure that promised a desert oasis, it instead became a toxic legacy of environmental collapse. The Torres-Martinez Cahuilla and Augustine Bands have witnessed this cycle—systems that consume land and leave only poison—for generations.

FLESH VERDICT is a ceremony of reckoning and protection.

The Sculpture: A life-sized wooden figure (7–7.5 ft) constructed from reclaimed driftwood salvaged from Bombay Beach itself. Hyperreal plaster hands and a photorealist face emerge from weathered wood—not abstract, but present. This figure is us. This figure is what infrastructure burns. Inside the torso, sealed fireproof: a clay heart sculpted from Mars College earth. Not gold. Soil. The earth of a community that refused to disappear. When the figure burns, this is what survives.

The Archive: Before ignition, Tarcila animates Lyosha's hyperrealist paintings—naked bodies, vulnerable poses, rendered with photographic precision—directly onto the sculpture. Bodies move across the wood. The archive breathes with the figure. This is what we refuse to see. This is what systems pretend doesn't exist.

The Judgment: Guests approach the offering station—a ceremonial table where provided paper awaits. They write: "What judgment do I speak against this system?" "What have I burned? What have I refused to accept?" "What vulnerability have I hidden? What do I confess?" "What do I protect for those who come after?" No clay. Paper. Clean, simple, honest. Each guest feeds their written judgment directly into the wooden frame's open lattice. Their words become fuel. Their witness becomes ash.

The Ritual: Guests write at the offering station. The projections animate across the standing figure—Lyosha's archive alive and watchful. The figure is mounted on a wooden platform and dragged ceremonially across salt-flat earth to the water's edge. Guests walk with it, holding ropes. Archive in motion. At sunset, Lyosha lights the fire. Wood collapses. Guest judgments burn. Plaster burns. Projections dissolve into flame.

The Meaning: Systems burn flesh. But witness persists. The clay heart isn't precious metal—it's place. It's the earth of a community that survived. It's proof that what we protect with fire cannot be consumed by fire. This is The Last Judgment conducted by the people being judged. Not waiting for external condemnation. Creating their own.
fields.Support Needed: We want to reiterate that this project is designed specifically for Bombay Beach—using its driftwood, its earth, and addressing its specific history of infrastructure collapse. We are not just bringing an object to the desert; we are building a ceremony from the materials of the landscape itself.  Lyosha’s dual background in structural engineering and cinema  ensures this will be executed with technical precision and strict adherence to safety protocols. We are fully committed to respecting the land, the local community, and the Biennale’s vision. We are ready to work, ready to build, and honored by the possibility of contributing to this year's theme.
fields.Abridged Project Text: A ceremonial burning of a 7.5-foot driftwood figure animated by hyperrealist projections. Guests feed written judgments into the open frame. As the sculpture burns, a clay heart—fired by the heat—emerges from the ash, a permanent artifact of community persistence amidst infrastructure collapse.
fields.Technical Support:
Projection Mapping: High-resolution 4K projector (outdoor-rated, 20,000+ lumens); Real-time animation software + operator (Tarcila); Power source: 20-amp circuit or generator; Runtime: 60 minutes (pre-burn animations).

Offering Station: Simple table setup; Stacks of white/natural paper (uncoated, clean); Pens (biodegradable ink); Candlelight or ambient lighting; Signage: "Write your judgment. Write your refusal. Feed it to the fire."

Fire Safety & Permits: Coordination with Biennale fire safety protocols; Wind monitoring for safe burn conditions (<10 mph); Water access + emergency protocols.

Platform & Transport: Wooden wheeled platform (8 ft × 4 ft, salvaged materials); Rope harnesses for procession.

Documentation: Video/photo archive of entire ceremony; Preservation of written judgments (photographed before burn).

Location Requirements: Open shore area (Salton Sea edge, Bombay Beach); Safe procession route (salt-flat earth); Perimeter of ~75 ft radius.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Guests become judges, not spectators.

At the offering station: Before ceremony, guests approach and write their judgment on paper. This is intimate. Private. They're confessing what systems have burned in them. They're naming what they refuse. They're speaking truth to infrastructure collapse. The station is simple—a table, paper, pens, candlelight. Witnessing them write their judgment is part of the ritual.

During the procession: Guests walk with the burning figure to water's edge, holding ropes, becoming active bearers of judgment. The projections ghost across the moving wood—archive and guest movement synchronized.

At the burn: They stand at marked perimeter, watching their words consume. Watching the wooden body collapse. Watching the archive dissolve. They are conducting the Last Judgment.

After the burn: The clay heart is revealed. Made from Mars earth. Guests witness what doesn't burn—not wealth, but connection. Not metal, but soil. Not extraction, but rooted witness.

This is intimate and collective simultaneously. Private confessions. Shared fire. Collective witness. No phones. No aestheticization. Just presence, judgment, and persistence.
fields.Theme Engagement:
FLESH VERDICT is The Last Judgement—literally, not metaphorically.

The Salton Sea is a failed infrastructure project. Bombay Beach is where that failure lives. The Torres-Martinez and Augustine Band communities have already experienced the judgment of systems that promised water and delivered poison.

But FLESH VERDICT inverts the traditional Last Judgment: the judged become the judges.

Guests aren't passive observers of judgment. They conduct it. They write their own verdicts on paper. They feed their words into the fire. Their judgment becomes active, visible, permanent in ash.

Lyosha's hyperrealist archive—naked bodies, vulnerability rendered with obscene precision—is the evidence being judged. Infrastructure says: these bodies don't matter. FLESH VERDICT says: we judge you for ignoring them.

The clay heart is the final judgment: what persists is not wealth or systems, but connection. The earth. The community. The hands that shape it.

The Last Judgment is not God's judgment. It's ours. It's conducted at the water's edge where promise became poison. It's conducted by people who have already survived the verdict.

The fire burns what doesn't deserve to persist. The clay heart survives as proof: we were here, we witnessed, we refused.
fields.Risk & Safety:
Open burn on water's edge. Coordination with Biennale fire safety, trained crew, wind monitoring, water access.

Guests: Perimeter marking, clear instructions. No participation in active burn zone. Paper materials only—no synthetic contamination.

Environmental: Only provided paper used (biodegradable, ocean-safe). Zero contamination to Salton Sea. Ash disposal per Biennale + tribal protocol.
fields.Location Submittal: Electric Lounge or somewhere safe for projectors - would like location where it can stay.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, Exclusive for the Biennale
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Because Bombay Beach is already conducting the Last Judgment on infrastructure. Because the Salton Sea is already burning. Because the Torres-Martinez Cahuilla and Augustine Bands have already been judged by systems and survived. We're just making it visible.

Lyosha's hyperrealist work strips away the distance between viewer and vulnerability. His naked bodies aren't decoration—they're evidence. They're asking: what does infrastructure do to flesh? What systems ignore you?

Tarcila's practice is about translating institutional money into community infrastructure. About building rituals that make witness permanent. About refusing to extract value from place, but instead rooting witness in the earth itself.

Together, FLESH VERDICT asks: What happens when we stop pretending? When we conduct our own judgment instead of waiting for external verdict? When we protect what's truly precious—not gold, but connection?

The offering station transforms guest participation from passive to active judgment. Guests don't watch others burn. They feed the fire with their own verdicts. Their words become ash. Their witness becomes permanent.

The clay heart—Mars College earth—connects community directly to this ritual. It's not symbolic. It's actual soil, shaped by actual hands, placed inside the burning figure. When retrieved from ash, it becomes proof that what we protect with fire survives fire. That connection persists when systems fail.

This work is essential because Bombay Beach is the Last Judgment. Not in some abstract religious sense. In literal infrastructure collapse, water poisoning, community displacement. The Torres-Martinez and Augustine Bands have lived this judgment for centuries.

FLESH VERDICT refuses to make art about their experience. Instead, it conducts ceremony with them. It centers their testimony, their earth, their ongoing witness. It says: you are not victims of judgment. You are the judges.

We're aligned. Lyosha provides the archive of vulnerability. Tarcila builds the ritual structure so witness persists. We've researched fire safety, ocean protocols, cultural consultation. We're ready.

Now we need resources to build it.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is not a backdrop. It's the work itself.

The Salton Sea was California's promised paradise. Instead, it became a monument to broken infrastructure. The water is toxic. Towns are dying. The Torres-Martinez and Augustine Bands have stewarded this poisoned land through 500 years of systemic collapse.

FLESH VERDICT belongs here because it's already happening here. The Last Judgment isn't abstract—it's occurring daily in Bombay Beach. We're just making it ceremonial. Making it visible. Making it deliberate.

The clay heart connects Mars College directly to this judgment. Mars earth, shaped by Mars hands, placed inside the burning figure. When retrieved, it becomes a permanent artifact of community persistence—proof that what we protect survives.

The Biennale's theme demands this work. The Last Judgment is not God's verdict. It's ours. It's conducted by people who have already been judged by systems and refused to disappear.

FLESH VERDICT refuses to extract value from Bombay Beach. Instead, it invites the community into ceremony of collective judgment and protection. It asks: What do we judge? What do we burn? What do we protect?

The answer persists in clay.

fields.Artist Name: Tarcila Neves, Tarcila de Figueiredo Neves
fields.Project Description:
Ghost Layers is a "phygital" infrastructure curated by Tarcila Neves. The project installs ten solar-powered, glowing beacons across Bombay Beach, activating a multi-sensory AR archive. This experience layers the oral histories of BIPOC residents over the physical landscape, co-created with Martian speculative artists who translate these testimonies into immersive digital spirits.

The Look & Sound:
The AR spirits use high-contrast visuals to assist those with low vision, while the soundscape features a site-specific collaboration with the Bombay Beach Choir. This spatialized audio acts as a sonic map, making the installation navigable for the visually impaired.

The Unfold:
The project functions as a gamified "Quest for Presence." Visitors earn badges by witnessing stories, providing a clear narrative anchor for ADHD audiences. The installation culminates in a town-wide "Synchronized Pulse" at sunset, followed by a gallery show and silent auction at the Electric Lounge to support the BIPOC Artist Fund.
fields.Support Needed: Ghost Layers is built on the principle that a cultural legacy is only as strong as its accessibility. By blending the technical rigor of my past "phygital" installations in New York and Miami with the deep, resilient narratives of Bombay Beach, we are creating a permanent resource that belongs to the town.  This project intentionally addresses the Accessibility Gap often found in desert art. By prioritizing visitors with Visual Impairments and ADHD through spatial audio and structured gamification, we ensure that the Salton Sea’s history is navigable for everyone. Furthermore, our commitment to economic sovereignty—through a transparent revenue ledger and a silent auction benefitting the BIPOC Artist Fund—ensures that the value generated by this project stays with the people who inspired it.  We are not just asking for a grant for an art show; we are asking for the support to build a community-owned archive that will remain a glowing testament to Bombay Beach's persistence long after the Biennale concludes.
fields.Location Notes: Electric Lounge, Jack's TV, Iron Dad's Memorial? (Beach)?
fields.Abridged Project Text: Curated by Tarcila Neves, Ghost Layers is a permanent AR archive bridging Bombay Beach residents, Martian speculative artists, and the audience. Solar beacons reveal local histories through inclusive AR and music by the Bombay Beach Choir, creating a gamified legacy of presence and accessibility.
fields.Technical Support:
Solar Infrastructure: 10 weather-sealed solar LED beacons.

Accessibility: Web-AR platform optimized for spatial audio and high-contrast visuals (assistive tools for visual impairment/ADHD).

Gallery: Professional track lighting and pedestals for the Electric Lounge exhibition.

Curation: Tarcila Neves manages the synthesis of resident testimony, Martian digital assets, and physical mural placement.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The engagement model is: Witness → Participate → Steward. Visitors move through the town, drawn by the glowing solar beacons and the spatialized voices of the Bombay Beach Choir. By gamifying the discovery of the AR-integrated archive, we turn the audience into active "Archive Stewards."

This structured progression is essential for ADHD accessibility, providing constant feedback as they "level up" through the town's history. The experience is designed for all hours; one might stumble into a glowing beacon at 3 AM or follow the choir's audio as a sensory map. The finale at the Electric Lounge unifies these layers with a physical Silent Auction, where the audience transitions from digital witnesses to physical supporters of community legacy.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Ghost Layers reinterprets "The Last Judgement" as The End of Invisibility. We judge the narrative of Bombay Beach as "abandoned" to be false, replacing it with a permanent record of persistent presence. The gamification serves as a metaphorical judgment: the more a visitor engages and listens, the more the landscape reveals itself.

By archiving the stories of BIPOC residents on a permanent ledger, we "save" these narratives from erasure. Finally, the project applies an Economic Judgement, ensuring that the cultural value generated by this landscape is returned to its residents through a transparent, sovereign ledger. It is a judgment of accessibility, declaring that a legacy is only righteous if it is inclusive to all sensory needs.
fields.Risk & Safety: Minimal risk. Low-voltage solar systems are electrically isolated. Beacons are secured with wind-resistant footings. No fire or hazardous materials. All community data is protected; residents retain full IP ownership.
fields.Location Submittal: The project utilizes a hybrid "scattered-site" model across Bombay Beach:  Central Hub & Gallery Venue: The Electric Lounge.  Satellite Archive Sites: 10 solar-powered beacon locations positioned on vacant lots and in collaboration with local mural sites throughout the town (specific coordinates to be finalized with the BBB placement team).
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No. This is new work premiering at the 2026 Biennale.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
My practice is dedicated to building legacy through core experiences. Following my "phygital" installations in New York (Balanced Forces) and Miami (Blue Code), I am focused on how technology can provide permanent cultural sovereignty. Ghost Layers is essential because it bridges three worlds: the Bombay Beach residents, Martian speculative artists, and the Biennale audience.

I am curating this project to address the Accessibility Gap in desert art, using AR to ensure visitors with Visual Impairments and ADHD are prioritized. By collaborating with the Bombay Beach Choir, we are rooting the technology in local, human sound. I am asking: Can a temporary festival leave a permanent, inclusive infrastructure? My goal is to create a model where the "Last Judgement" results in direct community sovereignty and a transparent economic legacy.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is defined by intentional persistence and sovereign creativity. The Salton Sea is a unique ecosystem where a resilient community chooses to protect and create. Ghost Layers mirrors this spirit, using technology to amplify the voices of the town’s dedicated stewards.

The town is an irreplaceable anchor. Its salt-weathered textures and murals are the living canvas giving the AR spirits meaning. This specific geography, with its unique light and layered histories, provides the essential weight for our digital archive.

The Biennale is the ideal platform to connect a global audience with this local narrative. By prioritizing BIPOC residents and Martian collaborators, we utilize the festival's reach to build a permanent, inclusive infrastructure. We are collaborating with the town’s energy to bridge the Accessibility Gap. By ensuring the landscape is navigable for visitors with visual impairments and ADHD, Ghost Layers honors the community’s legacy of hospitality. This ensures the digital history of Bombay Beach remains owned by its people.

fields.Project Name: A Gentle Place Before the End
fields.Project Description:
A Moroccan inspired courtyard appears in the desert: carpets underfoot, low seating, and patterned lantern light slowly drifting across the walls and bodies of those who enter. The space is shaded, intimate, and intentionally slower than its surroundings. A gentle environmental soundscape fills the air, occasionally threaded with soft whispered poetry that emerges and fades without announcement.

The installation is not a performance and asks nothing of the visitor. It functions as a pause inside the Biennale’s spectacle, a place to rest, recalibrate, and exist without needing to react, interpret, or participate. People may sit alone or beside strangers. Some will stay for minutes, others for long stretches of time. The work unfolds through duration, through stillness, shared presence, and the contrast between interior quiet and the dust, music, and movement outside.

At the center rests The Book of the Known, a shared volume where guests may leave reflections, memories, fragments of thought, or simple observations. Not confession, not testimony, just traces of being here now. Over the course of the weekend the book accumulates many small certainties, forming a collective record of attention within uncertainty.

Physically, the piece remains constant while the environment shifts around it. Day heat, sunset glow, night cold, passing crowds, and late hour wanderers. Temporally, it changes through use. The worn shape of cushions, the gradual filling of pages, and the subtle familiarity that forms among returning visitors. Experientially, it offers relief from urgency, a temporary interior where one may feel present before returning to the ongoing judgement outside.

In a festival staging the trial of civilization, the work offers neither redemption nor critique. It simply holds a gentle space where people can notice themselves existing, briefly, before the world resumes.
fields.Support Needed: We're excited and honored to participate.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Somewhere between ruin and rebirth, there is a room to sit quietly. Carpets soften the desert; patterned lantern light drifts while a gentle soundscape carries whispered poetry. No performance, no verdict. Stay awhile, leave a trace in the Book of the Known, then return to dust and judgement.
fields.Technical Support:
The installation is designed for indoor placement within a quiet room or enclosed structure. A contained space helps maintain the calm atmosphere and allows the soundscape and lighting to remain subtle.

Power is required for low level lighting and audio playback. One standard 120V outlet is sufficient with total draw under approximately 300 watts. Lighting consists of small lanterns or patterned light projectors positioned to create soft ambient illumination rather than directional or stage lighting.

Audio uses a small speaker system playing a continuous looped environmental soundscape with intermittent whispered poetry. Volume remains low and localized so it does not bleed heavily into adjacent installations. No microphone or live amplification is required.

No projection screens or video equipment are used.

The installation requires level floor space for carpets, cushions, and low seating. A small table or stand holds The Book of the Known, which should remain protected from foot traffic and spills.

Lighting and audio run continuously during open hours without staff supervision, though periodic checks are helpful to tidy cushions and maintain the writing area.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Visitors encounter the installation as something to wander into rather than attend. There is no scheduled moment and nothing to watch. People move through it at their own pace, often discovering it accidentally, including late at night when the desert quiet and lantern light make the space especially still.

Most engagement is passive but intentional. People sit, rest, and linger beside strangers without needing to interact. Some close their eyes, some listen to the soundscape, some quietly read or write in The Book of the Known. Conversation may happen softly, but silence is equally natural. The installation accommodates brief pauses as well as extended stays.

The piece changes through use. Cushions shift, the book fills, and returning visitors recognize a continuity of presence across time. By day it functions as a shaded refuge. By night it becomes a calm interior within the surrounding activity. Rather than directing behavior, the work allows visitors to decide how present they want to be, to observe, reflect, or simply exist for a while before stepping back into the festival.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The project engages the theme by offering a space outside the verdict.

If the Biennale imagines a trial of civilization, noise, spectacle, accusation, and celebration, A Gentle Place Before the End becomes the antechamber where no judgment is issued. Instead of deciding guilt or innocence, it holds the moment before and after decisions matter. A pause between accusation and meaning.

The work treats judgement not as a dramatic event but as a human condition, the constant sorting of experiences into success or failure, sacred or trivial, worth keeping or worth forgetting. Inside the installation, those impulses soften. Visitors are not asked to perform, justify, or interpret themselves.

The Book of the Known shifts the idea of confession into simple witnessing. Rather than absolution, it accumulates fragments of ordinary presence, thoughts written without audience or consequence. The result is neither redemption nor condemnation, but recognition.

In a festival staging apocalypse as carnival, the piece proposes that before any final reckoning, people will first seek somewhere quiet to sit.
fields.Risk & Safety:
No hazardous materials or high risk activities are involved.

The installation uses low voltage lighting and small audio equipment only. All electrical components will be rated for indoor use, secured to prevent tripping, and cables taped or covered along walking paths. Seating is low to the ground to reduce fall risk. The Book of the Known will be placed on a stable surface away from foot traffic. No open flames, tools, climbing structures, or moving elements are used.
fields.Additional Artists: Jason Asbahr, Sarah Dopp, Corinna Kester

fields.Project Description:
I made this sculpture for the play "Sunday in the Park with George" for Shotgun players theater in Berkeley. Unfortunately, the sculpture got very little showtime in the play, and I'd like to give it the exhibition that it deserves (and I'd like to change it a little to reflect my art more). 
If there is an opportunity to install this in a dark room, I'd like to either paint walls with stripes on them, cover it in fabric, or install my own slats and paint those. I'd like the shadows to overlap with the stripes on the wall, and create a room of optical illusions. I also have a little door that I would like to incorporate so that people have to crawl through a hallway in order to get to this piece. 
This sculpture has 5 motors in it, and can be controlled by DMX. It has choreography that was designed to go with music from the play. I'd like to have the Lomochrome (in the play it's called Chromolume) run the choreography like every hour or something.
fields.Support Needed: Hi! I'm Sydney - I've sent in 3 art apps lol. (Secret Society of the Palms, Confession Booth, Lomochrome). I was planning to come down for the whole week before the Biennale and install and help out. Potentially come for a weekend in February if you all like the art I'd like to bring. I love you all and excited to go to Prom
fields.Abridged Project Text: Immerse yourself in color, light, and movement
fields.Technical Support: I would need electricity. The Locomchrome uses about 200W but I think less. I would prefer something inside so that we can make the hallway.
fields.Audience Engagement: The experience I would like the audience to go through is to open a door to a room, and only to discover that it's a stripes-lined hallway, that gets deceptively smaller. At the end of the hallway is a tiny door that is painted with stripes and has a GOBO light shining onto it, creating an optical illusion, inviting the person to enter. The participants open the door and enter into a room that is also lined in stripes, and has the Lomochrome in the center, spinning itself and casting shadows around, and interacting with the stripes on the wall, creating more optical illusions. The stripes would be in Cyan Magenta, and Yellow, and the colors projected by the choromolume would make the stripes in the room appear to change colors.
fields.Theme Engagement: The original willy Wonka movie is a heavy inspiration to me - absurd machines that don't need a lifetime supply of chocolate, hand-shaped coat hangers that grab your hat, and hallways that change shape as you walk through them - I'd like the harness the absurdity of that movie and vibe, and incorporate my exploration of optical illusions.

fields.Project Description:
The installation takes the form of a permanently installed set of tourist binoculars—those familiar, coin-operated viewers found at scenic overlooks around the world. Rather than showing the present view, these binoculars function as an unconventional, site-specific gallery displaying images from Bombay Beach’s past and its imagined futures. The structure will be located on the beach beside the Bombay Beach Lemonade Stand, one of the town’s most visible public points. It draws on a heavy, post-apocalyptic industrial language that evokes resilience and a willingness to weather decay. Its stubborn presence—indifferent to sun, wind, and salt—sets industrial mass against the slim polish of contemporary consumer technology.

As viewers rotate the binoculars to face different landmarks—toward the sea, the Ski Inn, or other local sites—they encounter curated image sequences aligned with their physical orientation. Tilting the binoculars vertically allows viewers to switch between three image tracks: the past, an anxious future, and a hopeful future. The viewing system uses a mounted cellphone housed within the binocular structure, whose internal gyroscope aligns image sequences to the viewer’s orientation. At each location, these tracks form a triptych. For example, the Ski Inn might appear as it did during the town’s peak in the 1950s, alongside a future shaped by gentrification, and another in which it remains a beloved community anchor.

Locations and past images will be curated through community engagement and archival research focused on Bombay Beach and nearby environments such as Mars and the Slabs.

Images representing anxious and hopeful futures will be generated through a combination of existing images, AI tools, and community input. The anxious futures reflect local concerns around decay, displacement, and speculative development, while the hopeful futures express wishes for a sustained, healthy community that supports long-term residents, artists, and Martians in shared dwelling.

The project unfolds both physically and temporally. Prior to the Biennale, the team will conduct a community engagement process to gather historical images, surface ideas of potential futures, and determine which locations are most meaningful to include. These insights will then be interpreted collaboratively through generative AI photo editing and shared back with the community for input. This process seeks to create a collage of images reflecting local hopes and anxieties rather than a single vision. During the Biennale and beyond, the binoculars operate as a permanent installation, inviting repeated encounters and reflection over time. The structure is designed for long-term use, with a changeable gallery for future events.

The core team consists of three Martians: Denali Rose Brown (project lead and curator), Wiley Davis (design), and Beth Karlin (community engagement). We will engage the Martian community in creating future images using AI.
fields.Support Needed: This project is conceived as a permanent, publicly accessible gallery embedded in the landscape of Bombay Beach. Denali intends to continue curating new gallery exhibitions for future community events and Biennale cycles.
fields.Notes: PAIR WITH ARTIST COUNCIL
fields.Abridged Project Text: Come see Bombay Beach through its first tourist binoculars. Instead of the present, the viewer reveals images of the town’s past and imagined futures, aligned to where you stand and look. A familiar device becomes a site-specific gallery for memory, anxiety, hope, and reflection.
fields.Technical Support: The project is designed as a permanent outdoor installation with minimal technical requirements. It requires only funding to create, outdoor siting, and secure mounting, and power supplied by a $30 external battery for a phone. The work does not require any dedicated Biennale technical support. As resident artists at Mars College, the team has access to on-site fabrication tools, installation equipment, and technical support, minimizing demands on Biennale resources.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The installation is designed to be encountered rather than announced. Located on the beach adjacent to the Bombay Beach Lemonade Stand and listed on the Biennale map, it functions both as a destination and as something visitors may find at any hour. Its placement on a promontory emphasizes the convergence between land, water, art, and town at Bombay Beach.

Engagement begins through familiarity: the recognizable form of tourist binoculars invites casual use without instruction. However, the binoculars’ default orientation is deliberately unexpected. Rather than facing the sea, the binoculars point back toward town, redirecting attention from spectacle to settlement, from horizon to homes. Audience members engage physically by rotating and tilting the binoculars, just as they would with a normal tourist binocular.

Engagement unfolds through exploration and comparison. Viewers may linger, rotating the binoculars between landmarks and revisiting the same location across the three image sets, inviting reflection and conversation on how a single place can hold multiple, conflicting meanings.
fields.Theme Engagement:
This project engages The Last Judgement as a question of how we continue to dwell together without a single, authoritative verdict. The work treats judgement not as a final sorting imposed from above, but as an ongoing, situated practice that unfolds laterally across a shared landscape. No single image or perspective claims dominance. Instead, meanings accumulate through movement, comparison, and adjacency, reflecting the reality that Bombay Beach is constituted by overlapping, sometimes incompatible ways of dwelling.

Judgement here is not about ranking futures vertically as better or worse, saved or damned. It emerges horizontally, through the act of holding multiple possibilities in view at once and noticing how they sit alongside one another. Which futures allow for co-dwelling? Which fracture it? Which feel livable only for some?

Placed on the beach and oriented back toward town, the work resists transcendence, prophecy, or finality. In the spirit of the Bombay Beach Biennale, The Last Judgement becomes a collective, ground-level reckoning: not a conclusion, but an ongoing effort to live together among fragments that cannot—and should not—be resolved into one story.

fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
This project emerges from a desire to help long-term residents, artists, and Martians reflect together on what it means to continue dwelling in Bombay Beach after a decade of shared history. Ten years into the Biennale, there is now a meaningful overlap of pasts—and a growing sense that the future of this place will be shaped by people who must learn to live with one another across difference. This moment feels like the right time to pause, look back, and ask what kinds of futures feel livable, painful, or worth struggling toward.

As resident artists at Mars College, we are acutely aware of both the proximity and the disconnection between Mars and the town itself. This project is motivated by a desire to build a more porous relationship between these communities, not through spectacle or prescription, but through shared attention to place. The binoculars provide a concrete, accessible way to do this: by anchoring reflection in specific locations and allowing multiple pasts and futures to coexist without forcing consensus. Situated on the beach beside the Lemonade Stand—a gathering point long valued by residents—the work occupies a site that is already socially meaningful to many of the people participating in the engagement process.

The central questions guiding the work are simple but open-ended: What locations matter most to Bombay Beach’s past and future? What moments from the past still shape how people experience the town today? What futures are feared, and which are hoped for—even if they feel unlikely or contradictory? Rather than resolving these questions, the project creates a space where they can be held together.

The process begins before the Biennale through a community engagement phase led by Beth Karlin, whose background in behavioral science and public engagement includes work on civic art projects in Los Angeles. This engagement process is not only a means of gathering insights, but a form of cultural work in itself—creating space for residents, artists, and Martians to articulate how they experience and imagine dwelling in this place. Through one-on-one conversations and digital input, we will gather stories, images, and perspectives that inform both the historical archive and the imagined futures. These inputs will then be interpreted collaboratively by the artist team to produce image sets that reflect a collage of local hopes and anxieties rather than a single vision.

Wiley Davis, an experienced designer and fabricator based at Tool Camp at Mars, will lead the physical construction, enabling rapid prototyping and collaboration with other Martians. Denali Rose Brown will serve as project lead, splitting time between Mars and Bombay Beach throughout the season to support both production and relationship-building. Together, the team brings the technical, social, and artistic capacity to realize a permanent installation that can host rotating exhibitions, rooted in this place and responsive to its people.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is a place already saturated with failed futures, improvised dwellings, and speculative reinvention. It is a town where tourism, abandonment, art, and long-term residency overlap in unusually visible ways, all set against the slow, unresolved crisis of the Salton Sea. A work that repurposes a tourist binocular—an object designed to frame spectacle and promise coherent views—belongs here precisely because no such coherent view is possible.

In Bombay Beach, the horizon does not offer clarity or escape. The sea is damaged, and its future is contested. By placing a familiar viewing device on the beach and orienting it back toward town, the project subverts the logic of sightseeing and asks viewers to look not at a distant elsewhere, but at the place where people are trying to continue living together. The binoculars become a tool for reckoning rather than consumption.

The Bombay Beach Biennale provides the ideal context for this gesture. As a participatory experiment staged in a landscape marked by decay, persistence, and transformation, the Biennale invites works that embrace contradiction in possibilities and unfinished futures. This project speaks to that ethos by treating Bombay Beach not as a backdrop or symbol, but as a lived environment whose pasts and possible futures deserve to be held in common, without resolution.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Early development. The conceptual framework and design are established, and the project has been assessed for feasibility. The remaining work is fabrication, community engagement, and installation.

fields.Project Description:
Messages from Space is an immersive sound installation built around a simple, true fact: cosmic rays from outer space are passing through your body right now. You cannot feel them. You cannot see them. But they are there. Our custom sensors measure these particles as they pass through the installation, activating sounds, lights, and stories all around you. Participants listen to the experience and view the lights and sculptures. They do not actively interact, but may pause along the path to rest, reflect, or watch the world unfold.
The installation is designed for one visitor or a small group moving at their own pace. Visitors move through 15 planetary sculptures (12” - 24” diameter) mounted at varying heights on 4’ - 8’ poles. The sculptures are arranged on a path held together by a gentle multi-channel ambient soundscape that gives the space a warm, immersive feeling. When a particle arrives from deep space and passes through a sculpture, roughly every few seconds, it activates a piece of the story, a conversation, a sound effect. Because the detections are real and random, no two experiences are identical: you might wait in silence between sculptures, or hear several conversations in quick succession.
The conversations belong to people living in the near-far future. They speak casually and intimately, sharing small moments of life in a world that is different but not unrecognizable, better in some ways, resilient and human. A doctor coaxing a child through heart surgery. Two centenarians rocking on a porch, remembering war. A young woman savoring chocolate that adapts to her tongue. An old man choosing which hip to replace.
Threading through all of it is Liz. Liz is 126 years old and has chosen the date of her death. Before she goes, she uploads her memories to the archive. She is your guide. Her voice connects the sculptures, reflecting on what she's witnessed across a century and a quarter of living, loving, losing, and staying connected through the Fire-Flood Years and the great dispersal, civilization-scale events that have reshaped the past hundred years.
At Bombay Beach, the Salton Sea provides the perfect context. The sculptures, lights, and sounds interact with the open landscape, reflecting the shimmering water, the vast sky, and the peculiar resilience of this place. This is a landscape that survived its own Last Judgement and kept going, strange, stubborn, and shimmering. Liz would recognize it. She lived through several ends of the world herself and would tell you: what comes after is not nothing. What comes after is this. Someone left a message. You arrived to hear it. The archive is open.
fields.Support Needed: Ria Rajan is at Mars College this semester so she’s local.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Liz is 126 years old and choosing the date of her death. She has uploaded her memories to the near-far future archive: the Fire-Flood Years, five marriages, bioengineered hearts, the moment humanity chose to stay connected. Cosmic rays from outer space activate her voice. She left this for you.
fields.Technical Support:
Power: Access to a standard 110V power outlet for onsite battery charging and ambient speakers (approximately 300W total load, supporting two power strips). The 15 sculptures run on rechargeable batteries, each lasting 9 hours. Batteries rotate on an artist-managed twice-daily swap schedule.

Ambient Sound: 4 small ambient powered speakers are placed along the path to maintain a background soundscape. 

Structure: Each sculpture is mounted on a pole between 4 and 8 feet tall, set into the ground. Sculptures range from 12 to 24 inches in diameter. The path requires a footprint length of approximately 30’. Flat or gently sloped ground is preferred. Seating elements are placed along the path.

Electronics: Each sculpture contains a cosmic ray detector, a microcontroller, and a small embedded speaker. All electronics are weatherproofed for outdoor desert conditions including heat, dust, and wind. No internet connection required. All audio is stored locally on each sculpture. 

Lighting: The sculptures glow from within using LEDs. There is battery-operated lighting along the path as a wayfinder.

Setup time: Approximately one full day for a three-person crew.

fields.Audience Engagement:
Messages from Space is an immersive experience designed for audiences to move through from beginning to end. The experience lasts approximately 20 minutes for a full walk, longer if participants pause.
Visitors walk a clearly marked path through 15 glowing sculptures, each with its own embedded speaker. Story fragments and sound effects emerge locally from the sculptures, while a continuous moody ambient soundtrack plays through separate speakers along the path, holding the space together between activations.
Liz's recorded introduction at the start sets the stage, telling visitors who she is, why she's here, and what they are about to experience. Her voice then threads through the story fragments across the whole installation, connecting conversations and moments into a single coherent arc. Visitors who walk the full path will follow her from beginning to end. Visitors who wander through partially will still catch something meaningful.
The installation operates through the night on battery power. Late night visitors get something particular: desert silence between activations, then a voice arriving from deep space without warning. The experience works well in daylight but it is especially interesting in the dark.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Messages from Space engages with The Last Judgement from the other side of the verdict.
Liz is 126 years old. She chose her death date, uploaded her memories, and left them for strangers to listen to in the desert. This is her judgement, not of others, but of her own life. What was worth keeping? What should those who are still living know? What does a century and a quarter of being human actually amount to?
The Fire-Flood Years are in her past. The wars, the resource crises, the moment humanity had to reckon with what it had done to the planet and to each other. People survived it imperfectly, and built something new. Liz lived through all of it.
The Salton Sea is a landscape that already had its judgement rendered and refused to accept the verdict. It is the perfect location for this installation, strange and stubborn, neither dead nor thriving. Liz would understand that. She would probably sit by it for a while before she went.
The Last Judgement at its core asks: what was worth it? Messages from Space is one woman's answer to that question, delivered through cosmic rays, activated by physics, received by anyone who happens to listen.
