fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Sophi Kravitz, Sofy Yuditskaya, and Ria Rajan are co-conspirators with deep roots in Bombay Beach. Sophi brought a film crew to Bombay Beach to shoot a futuristic short (2016), setting off colored smoke above the shore and filming beautiful garbage. Sofy, now based in France, spent years stewarding the Mars College Midterms and Finals structures from 2020 to 2022, acting as a technical advisor for many art installations. Ria was present at the very inception of Mars College and she currently resides there.
Sophi Kravitz’s practice creates electronics-based sculptural installations that translate invisible phenomena into physical experience. Sofy Yuditskaya works with sound, video, interactivity, and salvaged material, exploring techno-occult rituals and technology’s effect on consciousness. Ria Rajan is an intermedia artist whose work focuses on the intimate relationships between people, places, and the technosphere. Together they share a deep fluency in participatory, site-specific, experimental work.
Cosmic rays are invisible, passing through your body right now, unseen. We find this extraordinary and want audiences to feel it. We imagined a character named Liz, 126 years old, choosing the date of her death, uploading her memories so strangers can walk through them a century from now. Her world is different from ours but not unrecognizable. The Fire-Flood Years happened. Humanity reckoned with itself and something survived, imperfect and resilient. What does it mean to still be here in 100 years, still material, still creative, still caring for each other?
We are living through a period of extreme inflection where the dominant narrative is doomsday. Messages from Space pushes back against that. It imagines a future where humanity survived its worst instincts and people live longer and love more freely. We want audiences to walk through that future and feel it as a joyful possibility.
With Ria currently on-site, we hope to involve the local community and residents of Mars College. We will collaboratively integrate local recordings and memories, voices of residents, stories of the Sea, and near-far future messages into the audio landscape as speculative transmissions.
Oliver Tanner brings mechanical expertise to the technical and artistic realization of the work. Bombay Beach is a place that should not exist but does. We find it the perfect place to broadcast a message of survival.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
A poem by Sofy Yuditskaya:
The Salty Sea is full of run off silt sweeping the air and breeze. 
We breathe, we dance, we live, we die with struggle and with ease. 
Her ruins are a monument abandoned tho they seem and yet the glories of this place continue like a dream. 
The secrets left to be sussed out through lucky happenstance.
By wanderers drawn to the salty call by design or by chance.

(Sophi Kravitz) I have been coming to this area since the early 2000s. The Salton Sea stopped me in my tracks the first time I saw it. A body of water that should not exist, in a desert that should not support it, full of dead fish and strange birds and people who chose to stay anyway. I have never stopped thinking about it.

Messages from Space belongs here because Bombay Beach is already living in the future. Not a clean, optimistic, tech-utopia future. The real kind. The kind where something went wrong and people adapted and built a community anyway, on the shore of a dying sea, in the middle of a desert, with an outdoor art museum and a Biennale and a bar. That is exactly the future Liz survived. That is exactly the resilience the work is trying to honor. 
The Salton Sea is also a cosmic ray detector of sorts. It registers everything that has passed through it, every agricultural runoff decision, every water rights battle, every season. The Last Judgement has already happened here. The verdict was complicated. People stayed. That is the whole story.

fields.Project Description:
As you approach the edge of the sea, you can hardly believe your eyes. You wonder if you've drank enough water today as you get closer to what must certainly be a mirage. But upon arrival, it’s clear as day — the truck-sized block of ice is no less real than you are.

While you don’t know where it came from, you do know where it’s going. A blank canvas, an unchiseled block of marble, a receptor of all ideas, hopes, and dreams, is melting into the very sea that itself is melting into air.

Does it represent the shrinking ice caps? Is it a metaphor for the disappearing Salton Sea? Is it a comment on futile efforts to rehabilitate the sea and the planet’s climate at large? Is it a really big block of ice? Yes.

The ACTIVE SEA ENHANCEMENT PROJECT is fully rooted in time and place, ultimately falling victim to both. Its universality in form and material leave interpretation open — is it a call to action or an invitation to watch the slow burn? What is certain in this uncertain world is that if left as is, both the ice and the sea won’t make it to tomorrow.

Under the desert sun myriad rivulets of precious, fresh water slide off the face of the block, trickling into the sea to intermingle with the hyper-saline littoral waters of the Salton. What other beings will be drawn to the ephemeral block, like a reverse salt lick, glistening with the promise of quenching one’s thirst in wind-bitten, sun-baked land? Will they come by night, drawn by an eerie gleam pulsing within the cube, or behold its glory as the sun seeks the horizon, throwing star energy through aqueous translucence in a wet, smoldering blaze? No two moments will be the same.
fields.Support Needed: Is man merely a mistake of God's? Or God merely a mistake of man?
fields.Abridged Project Text:
Cool… so you think you figured out how to solve all of Imperial County’s problems?

HA.

Snowball's chance in hell.
fields.Technical Support: None needed - our team of four will handle transportation and installation of the ice blocks. All lighting elements will be solar powered so no need for a generator.
fields.Audience Engagement: All of the above. Well, hopefully not move through. The ACTIVE SEA ENHANCEMENT PROJECT will be located close to the sea’s edge, so people will be able to walk towards it, touch it, climb on top of it, lick it, etc. By the nature of it being a giant block of ice in the desert, folks will naturally be curious and allowed to find out what it’s all about for themselves. Solar powered lights will shine on and through the sculpture to provide interest in the evening. Due to most people’s inherent understanding of ice and what will happen to it in the desert, attendees to the Biennale will be inclined to return day after day to see just how quickly the ice will melt and how the sculpture will change throughout time.
fields.Theme Engagement: The ACTIVE SEA ENHANCEMENT PROJECT engages with this year’s theme of The Last Judgement like an hourglass sitting on the table of society—a visual manifestation of the little time that remains, slipping and dripping towards the ultimate appraisal of our Earthly actions. Or is it a final act of repentance, hastily returning what’s been taken in the hopes of favorable judgment? One might behold their own reflection gazing back at them in the melting mirrors and cool pools of the cube, prompting ponderous self-appraisal as the final hour nears.
fields.Risk & Safety: This is a remarkably safe project, as the primary and overwhelming material is ice. No fire, ladders, vehicles, boats, drones, excavation, digging, digging out excavators, or excavating out diggers, nor any potentially hazardous materials or actions. We will be using wheelbarrows, wood gurneys, and a small, hand, hydraulic shop crane to stack ice blocks on top of each other which will remain in place through gravity and melt together over time.
fields.Location Submittal: On the shore of the sea (welcome to on the best placement there)
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
As every Biennale comes around, we find ourselves called again to contemplate this unique community, the slow moving tragedy of the Salton Sea, larger climate and societal collapse, and our place and role in the whole production. We want to create this work because we feel it touches on many things we are passionate about and that are pertinent to this current collective moment.

You can’t stand on the shores of the Salton Sea and not think of the larger, systemic and catastrophic changes happening to the planet, climate-related and otherwise. We are unimpressed with what we’ve seen thus far from power-wielding governments, corporations, and an absurd idea that individuals alone can correct this course. We feel that what we’ve seen so far amounts to a drop in the bucket, akin to adding an ice cube to an evaporating sea and patting yourself on the back. We invite viewers to question what solutions to planetary problems look like, in terms of scale and cooperation, and what are the metrics for success? What is enough? Is there an, “enough” today?

The ACTIVE SEA ENHANCEMENT PROJECT encourages viewers to reflect on time and action, again on individual, collective, and planetary scales. When looking at ice in the desert, it’s understood that, without any intervention, it will melt. This large block may last past the Biennale but there is no doubt it will soon cease to exist. By staging the sculpture in the sea, we intend that the parallels (and shared perils) of the ice and the sea are clearly evident. Although on a different time scale, the sea will suffer the same fate as the ice at the hands of the desert climate. You might not be here to witness it either, but without substantial intervention, the sea will eventually cease to exist. What does it mean when you won’t be around for the end result? What does action look like across parties and generations? While the focus is oftentime on the end (the full melting of the ice, the disappearance of the sea), it is the time between now and then when conditions change constantly. How do we navigate constant, novel change?

Finally, we hope to spark joy through simplicity. It is absurd to see a giant block of ice out in the desert. While contemplating heavier questions, we hope the material reality allows for a recognition of the absurdity of life and to find fun in its expression.

Along with the idea of absurdity and shock, we anticipate this to be a relatively closed process. We hope to install the sculpture in the evening so that when most people awake, they find a strange and familiar object on the shores of a strange and familiar landscape.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The ACTIVE SEA ENHANCEMENT PROJECT was conceived and designed with Bombay Beach and the Biennale in mind. The absurdity of the whole endeavor feels right at home on the shores of the Salton Sea, just a bit of a way down from the similarly absurd piece by the State of California called the “Active Vegetation Enhancement Project”—a well-intentioned effort with insufficient funds and follow through, hoping to passively encourage the recolonization of the seashore by native plants. 

After melting, no trash or trace will be left behind and it will be undeniable that the project positively contributed to increasing the volume of the sea and contributing to desalination, perhaps more than any other piece…albeit in an infinitesimal proportion.

fields.Additional Artists: Paul Glavin, Ben Meissner, Graham Sandelski, Cameron Overy

fields.Project Name: A Piece of Hell's Kitchen: The Family Fridge
fields.Project Description: This installation consists of a refrigerator (that no longer operates as intended), and a variety of momentos, souvenirs, and reminders commonly found on a the fridge of a fecund family with lots of love and lots to do. Inside, it will contain beer that’s be re-labled with “Say Yes To Beer, Say No To Fascism” stickers. The beer can be kept cold in a cooler inside the fridge, since the fridge is currently broken, in a yard in Bombay Beach. It can be a permanent, temporary, or seasonal installation. Beer, cooler ice, and various souvenir stickers can be replenished throughout the weekend.

fields.Audience Engagement: This is something that people can either stumble upon or seek out. It's meant to get some laughs--one of its purposes is to provide quick moments of solidarity and informal comedy-based therapy during this incredibly dark time. Additionally, it's trolling those who need much more than trolling, with all that's at stake right now. The installation uses sarcasm as its tool to encourage self-discovery and cult deprogramming for community members who need (but are unlikely) to abandon their harmful alignments.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The installation features a variety of historical figures (political leaders, violent psychopaths, etc) and puts a face to those that cause harm of what the artist perceives as evil. 

According to Google, "The Bible describes the final judgment as a future event when Jesus Christ will judge all humanity, separating the righteous (sheep) from the wicked (goats) based on their faith and deeds, leading to eternal life for believers and eternal punishment for unbelievers, as depicted in Revelation 20 (Great White Throne Judgment) and Matthew 25 (Sheep and Goats). This judgment involves books of deeds and the Book of Life, with outcomes determined by a person's response to God's grace, primarily through accepting Jesus, leading to reward or condemnation". 

Basically, "What Would Jesus Do?!" NOT this fucking shit. That's for sure. That will be on the fridge someplace. Perhaps on a magnet holding up a family photo.
fields.Risk & Safety: No.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No. This is brand new, and was inspired by the acquisition of an amusing Devil mask that prompted a last-minute halloween costume-- Mother of Psychopaths.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): Everyday is an existential crisis for me, and many others. I feel powerless, and I feel not just disappointed, but disdainful, towards those who don’t have compassion towards fellow humans. This includes supporters of the current regime. Basic soft skills like empathy, sympathy, concern, thoughtfulness, sharing, caring, etc…stuff that we’re supposed to learn as toddlers and young children, AND/OR as church members meant to “serve god”… DUDE. It's the basics. However, these are attributes that are evading regime supporters. It’s futile to think I can force someone like this to demonstrate compassion or merely acknowledge sympathy; I know, because I’ve tried, and I’ve cut certain people out of my life due to my failure. It’s their failure though, really; they’re failing to be a positive contributor to society VS being solely transactional and self-interested. This installation is a pivot- instead of trying to ignite the basic emotion of compassion in regime supporters, I’m conjuring up different basic emotions along the lines of shame and humilation. I’m trolling them. It’s a last resort. And the content is amusing for me.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
I’ve been in the Imperial Valley area for about 6 years– half the year here and half the year in NY. I’m working nearby now (in LA, out of financial ruin and desperation…), but Bombay Beach is my home. 

The fridge is here (it was Sonia’s), and impacts of the regime are felt in this region, as are agents of the regime (aka border patrol, homeland security, ICE, etc). There’s also a shockingly disturbing number of regime supporters for which compassion is not enough. They don’t even have compassion for themselves (they’re broke-ass bitches dependent on various tax-funded social programs), for christ’s sake. The pivot to shaming by association is a last resort. But, YOU ARE WHAT YOU SUPPORT. That’s one of the messages I hope to convey. The installation is a mirror– if people can feel good (via giggle inducing-satire) about what they see… great. And if just one person doesn’t like what they see, even better. This is what’s needed to veer us off the doomed path we’re currently on.

fields.Project Description: StarShade is a deployable shade structure about 22' in diameter, and 8' feet tall that literally folds and unfolds, like a pop-up tent. It will unfold in Bombay Beach for the Biennale, and provide temporary shade, solar power, and shelter to all who encounter it.
fields.Support Needed: Full disclosure, I received a $1000 grant from Burners Without Borders earlier this year, to install a variation of this StarShade structure at the Oregon Regional, SOAK, where it will serve as solar education center and charging station, replacing the use of a diesel powered generator. The project has not yet been exhibited anywhere, and I have been spending my time on Mars this year building the new prototype, so that I can go up to Oregon in April and fabricate an entirely new iteration of it to exhibit at SOAK at the end of May. So the structure/concept has already received funding, but it has not been previously exhibited, and receiving funds from the Biennalle will cover the cost of the prototype materials, and allow me to do a full test run and deployment of the structure here in Bombay Beach, so that I can continue to refine and improve the design for SOAK. You can explore the folding and unfolding of the structure, or design your own, here:  https://disruptivelyuseful.github.io/LinkageLab/
fields.Abridged Project Text: StarShade is rapidly-deployable self-replicating open-source solar-powered shade structure that converts sunlight into Love.
fields.Technical Support: Don't need nothin' but sunshine.

fields.Audience Engagement: StarShade provides shade and soaks up solar energy during the day, and re-radiates that energy long after the sun goes down in the form of light, heat, electricity, and music. It is a standalone, self-contained solar energy system and solar education center, that attracts participants with gifts of light and shelter, and teaches them how to build itself, so that it can self-replicate, and spread around the world.
fields.Theme Engagement: I didn't create the project with the theme in mind, but if I had to bullshit an answer on the spot, I would say that I believe that right now is the not the FINAL battle between good and evil, but it is battle between good and evil of our time, and the more people are able to leverage power of the Sun to provide energy, care, enjoyment, and mutual aid to the folks around them, in the face of social, environment, and political disaster, the more we will be able to shine a light in the darkness, and bend the arc of the future towards heaven on Earth.
fields.Risk & Safety: Mostly no, but the structure is made of 2x4 lumber, and contains power electronics, so there is an inherent fire risk, which will be mitigated with well marked fire extinguishers, and proper electronic circuit protection.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, this will be the first showing. It will also be installed at SOAK in Oregon in May, but that will occur after the Biennale.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): Solar power has never been as cheap, good, or disruptively accessible as it is right now. But, there are still significant barriers to entry, and a lack of energy literacy is one of them. I want to create spaces and infrastructures that give people the direct, unmediated experience of the power embodied in sunshine, that attract them with art, light, and music, and then transform their curiosity into knowledge, inspiration, and action. The whole project is designed for replication - it is open-source, and my dream would be to see it self-replicate independently around the world, as each structure and installation provides such obviously tangible benefits, and such clear instructions, that they spread like seeds or spores on the wind, popping up wherever they find fertile ground.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): StarShade is a self-contained "organism". Like an autotrophic algae cell, it can power itself using nothing but sunshine. The more remote, desolate, and off-the-grid the site, the more stark this contrast is. What better environment is there for such an extremophile organism than the shores of the Salton Sea? With some of the best solar energy in North America, Bombay Beach is the perfect the incubator for this seed to grow, adapt, and evolve.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Structural prototype is going up this week, then then the solar panels, lights, and electronics. I would say the current stage of the project is "most of the way through."

fields.Artist Name: Kamau Zuberi Akabueze, Kamau Zuberi Akabueze
fields.Project Description:
SCAR Wash:: a permanent, participatory installation that reimagines the American car wash as a ritual technology for emotional release. Built from the found materials and existing structures of Bombay Beach, it is a full-service detachment station where visitors write down a judgment, a scar, or an internalized verdict they've been carrying — and offer it to the land for dissolution.

We enter a sculptural corridor assembled from shipping containers, industrial pallet racking, and reclaimed materials indigenous to the compound. At the entrance, a timber pergola shelters the Intention Station — white writing panels mounted above a galvanized steel trough filled with Salton Sea salt and sand. The corridor is lined with floor-length muslin fabric strips, hand-stamped with single words: RELEASE. HOLD. FORGIVE. CARRY. LET. 

Vibrational sound is mounted to container walls. At the exit, a Residue Tower :: pallet racking shelves holding glass jars, clay vessels, driftwood bundles, and accumulated remnants of what others have released. The scar as pedestal to dethatched remembrance.

The experience of low-frequency vibrations emanating from the structure itself reflect the historical car wash tunnel. The container walls become resonators — 40Hz, 120Hz, 528Hz — frequencies you feel through your palms when you press them against the steel. Sound design developed in collaboration with Mars College semester mates creates a shifting sonic environment that moves from tension to resolution as you pass through.

The Wind Cycle stage uses leaf blowers to push air through the corridor, moving the fabric strips, lifting paper fragments, pressing against your body. It is the rinse. In the desert heat, it is also relief. But primarily, we feel the lightness of walking without what we've written, and then the specific moment when we place that paper into a clay vessel made from regional Earth, pour in Salton Sea water, and watch the words dissolve. The judgment returns to the land that understands mistakes.

The SCAR Wash operates as a 24-hour accessible installation. During Biennale, it is staffed with volunteers offering tiered experiences — from the Basic Wash (a self-navigated walk-through) to Full Shimmer Service (a guided passage with body work and listening sessions). At night, projection transforms the writing panels into a palimpsest of collected judgments, returned as ghosts for communal witness.

In the off-season, The SCAR Wash stands as Bombay Beach's monument to monumental transition. Visitors walk through at their own pace, observe what has been left behind, and add to the growing archive. The Dry Basin accumulates. The Residue Tower grows. The clay vessels line the shelves, each one holding dissolved words in Salton Sea water, slowly evaporating, leaving only salt and the faintest trace of ink.

The SCAR Wash is a place where we arrive carrying something and leave having given it to the only sea that knows what to do with a beautiful mistake.
fields.Support Needed: I appreciate the opportunity to prepare this proposal. This was not a project that I came into my Mars College experience with an idea for, however, the Thunder Talks on campus were wildly compelling and this concept is a direct manifestation of that vibration which is why my ask is a fully funded project from the review panel. I am appreciative of your sincere review.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Welcome to The SCAR Wash, a full-service detachment from the judgements we carry in the weight of our emotions. Here, you leave the opinions of others to be dissolved into the remnants of a dissolving sea. You may have arrived in a mistaken identity, yet in no way are you a mistake in motion.
fields.Technical Support:
The SCAR Wash is a permanent outdoor installation using materials already of Bombay Beach. Technical needs are straightforward.

Structure: Existing structure :: shipping containers or similar as the frame, pallet racking, timber pergola for the canopy. No earthworks required.

Electricity: Standard 120V power. Peak load approx: 20-30 amps. Battery-powered equipment used for the Wind Cycle.

Sound: Amplifier and 8-10 transducers/speakers mounted onto structural walls as visceral resonators. Collaboration w/Mars College on sound and projection design.

Lighting: Daytime: natural light. Nighttime: low-watt LED filament string lights, ground-level uplights.

Projection (Biennale only): Digital projector (~5000 lumens) for night of writing panels and interior. Bienalle enhancement, not core requirement.

Wind Cycle: 2-3 battery-powered leaf blowers or industrial fans for the air-wash stage. Seasonally valuable for desert heat relief.

Water: Salton Sea water collected in clay vessels made from regional earth. No plumbing, no drainage, no water infrastructure required.

Projected Materials List:

Pallet racking (on-site or sourced locally)
Shipping container(s) or equivalent structure
Muslin/canvas fabric strips
Galvanized steel trough
Clay vessels (locally made)
Writing panels, water-soluble markers
Glass jars for Residue Archive
Found objects, driftwood, salt from shoreline

Build: Community-focused, basic tools, volunteer support. All electrical components weather-protected.

fields.Audience Engagement:
The SCAR Wash is participatory on many levels of engagement. I am proposing the idea as a permanent installation that welcomes Bombay Beach Bienalle as the opening ceremony of this portal to presence.

Yes, people will watch as participants emerge from the SCAR Wash in alchemical release of long-held wounds, and their witness stands as an internal encouragement to do the same. Whether they flow through the physical experience themselves is not as essential as their recognition of the innerversal opportunity to release consciously unconscious weights.

This SCAR Wash is a 24-hour experience that is immersive for those that choose to flow through the sculptural stage for cognitive cleansing or pass by and consider that functional healing is accessible.

During Bienalle, The SCAR Wash will be staffed with volunteers there to support the many layers of service offering - from the Basic Wash (a self-navigated walk through) to Full Shimmer Service (Guided walk through, body work sessions, sessions with SCAR Wash listening technicians).

In the off season, the SCAR Wash will be akin to Bombay Beach's Drive-in. A monument to monumental transition and transmutation where visitors can observe what's been left behind, what has been dissolved into the Sea, what has been cleansed and upcycled into new matter.

The SCAR Wash will be a place for conferences and panels, for dialogue about transition, and acts of releasing tradition, and for artistic exposition.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The scars we carry are often judgments calcified. Someone said "you are X" and we believed them, internalizing external critique as identity. The SCAR Wash confronts the 2026 theme, The Last Judgement, by reframing it not as a final verdict from an external authority, but as a personal act of completion. It is the last time we have to carry a judgment that was never ours to begin with.

Our installation offers a literal and metaphorical process for this personal reckoning. Participants write their judgment - the scar, the sentence - and offer it to the process. They move through a corridor of language, are received by wind and frequency, and witness their written misunderstanding dissolve into Salton Sea water held in a clay vessel made from the land itself. The judgment returns to the earth, absorbed by the beautiful mistake of the Salton Sea.

An experiential transmutation in a functional intention current.

The SCAR Wash proposes that The Last Judgement is not a singular, future event, but a continuous, present-day opportunity. It is a choice to end the trial, to reclaim the energy trapped in the wound, and to declare ones own case against the self closed.
fields.Risk & Safety: The project may involve digging for anchoring purposes only.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
This work is urgent now because our cultural moment is saturated with judgment - social, political, and algorithmic. We carry these verdicts as scars, internalizing external critiques as identity. The SCAR Wash proposes a counter-ritual: a technology for detachment. It asks: What happens when we stop carrying a judgment and instead, offer it to a place that knows how to hold it? Can we build a functional healing apparatus from the literal and metaphorical debris of our landscape?

The process is one of radical resourcefulness - materializing what is already there. My primary collaborator is the site itself. Further collaboration with Mars College for sound design, vessel creation, and car wash aesthetics along with and local Bombay Beach artisans is a key part of the community engagement process. These vessels, made from regional earth, will hold Salton Sea water for the final dissolution of written intentions—the material IS the meaning.

My practice, whether through my work with THE ÅLïEN SCõÖL or my heutagogical frameworks, is centered on building guidance systems for navigating complex internal landscapes. I create tools, frameworks, and experiences that make the invisible patterns of our lives visible and therefore, navigable. 

The SCAR Wash is the physical manifestation of this work. It is a cathedral of found materials, a full-service detachment station built on the shore of an accidental sea, offering a public ritual for private release, timed for the moment of The Last Judgement.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The SCAR Wash uses water from an ecological mistake to encourage the washing away of internalized verdicts in operation at the exact symbolic frequency of the locale. Bombay Beach is a geological beacon for restorative creativity in emotional motion.

Nowhere else could this work exist. Placing a written judgment into a clay vessel, pouring in water from the Salton Sea, and watching the words dissolve is an act of sympathetic magic that only works here. The SCAR Wash is a geological beacon for restorative creativity, a site-specific ritual where the landscape itself is the agent of transmutation.

The SCAR Wash belongs to the Biennale because it is an integrated expression of what this gathering does best: find beauty in the breakdown and build sacred things from the scraps.

fields.Abridged Project Text: While clearing debris at a property on ave A, crews unearthed a putting green once belonging to the old Bombay Beach Country Club from its glory days in the 1960s.A restoration and a re-opening celebration is scheduled to coincide with theopening of Neptune,the latest addition to the BB solar system

fields.Theme Engagement:
*Claude, relate the theme of "the last judgement" with a golf course putting green from 1964.

Edited answer;
 Both the Last Judgment and a putting green are theaters of ultimate consequence compressed into surprisingly small spaces. Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel fresco churns with hundreds of bodies rising and falling across a single wall—heaven above, hell below, with Christ as the central axis. Similarly, a putting green is a tightly bounded stage where the entire fate of a hole is decided across maybe 30 feet of manicured grass. The ball either drops or it doesn't. Salvation or damnation, birdie or bogey.

The green becomes a kind of secular altar—immaculately groomed, set apart from the rough chaos of the fairway (purgatory?). The cup itself: a dark void, 4.25 inches in diameter, into which all striving must ultimately descend. The ball tracking toward the hole has the same inexorable quality as souls being drawn upward or cast down.

Both are final. Once the Last Judgment is rendered, there's no appeal to a higher court. Once the putt lips out on 18, the scorecard is signed in ink. Your reading of the green, like your reading of your life, had to be correct the first time.
There's dark comedy in the parallel: humanity's ultimate spiritual reckoning reduced to whether a small white ball falls into a hole in the ground, judged by men in plaid pants.
fields.Risk & Safety: no hazardous materials
fields.Location Submittal: Neptune, Ave A between 4 and 5.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Yes. This project is on it's 4th itteration. Each year it expands. This year we open a brand new hole and driving range at Neptune.

Frank Sinatra and The Beach Boys played golf here! The putting green is shaped like the Salton Sea! Drive golf balls from the North Side berm driving range! Drive unregistered golf carts in the town of Bombay Beach!

fields.Project Description:
Numen is an ongoing series I have been developing over several years but have never exhibited. It takes its name from the Roman concept of divine presence as something that inhabits — that dwells within a body, a stone, a threshold, a place. Not transcendent. Not elsewhere. Here. The installation proposes that a shipping container on the edge of a dying sea can hold the same charge as a cathedral, that a photograph pinned to a raw wall can function as both document and icon, that a community of sinners and seekers is as worthy a subject for devotion as any saint.

The installation is simple in form: photographs covering the walls of a raw interior space — overlapping, unframed, ranging from palm-sized prints to images nearly six feet tall. No pedestals, no glass, no institutional distance. Just images pinned directly to the surface, dense enough to feel like something between a museum survey and a roadside altar.  The space incorporates draped fabrics as shrouds and partitions, disrupting the space and creating an immersive experience.

The photographs themselves show nude figures painted, wrapped, and draped — bodies transformed into something between flesh and landscape. The figures are both intervention in the landscape as well as being a landscape disrupted and abstracted fabrics and pigments.

For this iteration, the Bombay Beach Binnale community wlll become collaborators: a photoshoot will be planned incorporating willing volunteers, photographed within the same aesthetic, their bodies placed inside the specific fabric of the Salton Sea — crumbling houses, rusted infrastructure, mineral shoreline. The built and the bodily made inseparable.

Against the Biennale’s theme of The Last Judgement, Numen proposes a different kind of reckoning: not divine verdict handed down from above, but sacred presence discovered from below — in bodies, in ruins, in the radical self-expression of a community that has chosen to gather at the edge of catastrophe and make something beautiful — and in doing so, breathe life back into a place the rest of the world wrote off. If this is a judgement, the jury is assembled from the people in the photographs. The evidence is on the walls.

A final shoot at Bombay Beach during the Biennale will produce new images installed in the closing days, so the space grows and is never finished. If the prints fade, blister, or curl in the salt air, that is not failure. It is the work completing itself.
fields.Support Needed: Looking forward to the Biennale!
fields.Abridged Project Text: The NUMEN project situates painted and draped figures within desert and geological landscapes, examining how the sacred is constructed and projected onto flesh and earth. As fabrics, paints, and pigments intervene upon the body, the body itself becomes an intervention within the landscape.
fields.Technical Support:
The installation is intentionally low-tech and adaptable to whatever space is provided.

Photographs will be affixed directly to wall surfaces using magnets in a metal shipping container, or pins, nails, and clips in a built interior. Sheer fabric thresholds and partitions will be hung from ceiling structures — no additional rigging infrastructure required.

If power is available, simple bare-bulb lamps will provide warm ambient light, treated as found objects consistent with the raw aesthetic. Natural or ambient light is sufficient if power is unavailable.

The space should be indoor or covered to protect prints from wind and direct weather. A minimum of 16 linear feet of wall space is needed, though a larger space is strongly preferred — the installation is designed to scale, and more wall surface means a more immersive experience.

Biennale support would be valuable in enlisting community members as cast members for the on-site shoot, which will produce new images installed before the closing weekend. The work involves nudity, which must be clearly disclosed to all prospective participants. Informed consent is essential.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The installation is designed to be moved through slowly. Visitors enter through a threshold marked by sheer draped fabric — the same fabrics that appear in the photographs themselves, creating an immediate continuity between the world of the images and the physical space. The fabric functions as both partition and passage, signaling that something has shifted, that the space operates under different terms.
Inside, the walls are dense with photographs at every scale — small enough to lean in close, large enough to feel enveloped. The overlapping, unframed images reward sustained attention: the longer you stay, the more you see. There is no prescribed path or sequence. Visitors move at their own pace, drawn to what draws them.
The intent is ceremonial. The space is designed to feel like a shrine, not a solemn or forbidding one, but one that invites genuine looking and quiet presence. In a Biennale environment full of noise and spectacle, the installation offers somewhere to slow down and be still.
But it will also work at 3am. The rawness of the space, the density of the images, the fabric moving in the desert air - these elements hold up without explanation or context. Someone wandering in without knowing anything about the work should feel something before they understand anything.
The final images, shot and installed during the Biennale itself, mean that returning visitors will find the space changed, new faces on the walls, the community literally accumulating around them.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Salton Sea is a landscape shaped by human consequence — an engineering accident, fed by agricultural runoff, now slowly receding into salt and toxicity. It is already a kind of judgement, a visible record of what happens when we stop paying attention to what we’ve made.
Numen takes that as its starting point and asks what comes after.
The work places nude figures — painted and draped — inside ordinary landscapes — crumbling buildings, industrial edges, mineral shoreline — and asks viewers to decide what they’re looking at. The images don’t declare themselves spiritual. They simply create enough stillness and strangeness that the viewer brings their own meaning to them. That act of projection — of finding the sacred in the commonplace, of seeing ritual where there is only a body in a ruined building — is precisely what numen describes. Divinity as something we locate, not something handed down.
The concept of numen says that what remains after catastrophe is not absence but presence. The figures in these photographs are people choosing to be seen in a landscape that most would rather look away from, treating that act as devotional.
At the Biennale’s Last Judgement, Numen sides with the living. The ruins are the backdrop. The community is the resurrection.
fields.Risk & Safety:
This project poses no safety risks. There is no fire, pyrotechnics, ladders, vehicles, drones, excavation, or hazardous materials involved. The installation consists of photographs affixed to wall surfaces and sheer fabric hung from ceiling structures. All materials are lightweight and non-toxic. The on-site shoot uses standard camera equipment in accessible outdoor locations.
The installation contains nudity. The work is non-sexual — figures are painted and draped within landscape settings, evoking religious imagery in a fine art context.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, this work has never been exhibited previously.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I am interested in creating this work for the Biennale because Bombay Beach is a landscape of dichotomies—beauty and decay side by side, giving the place a spiritual and emotional resonance that is difficult to locate elsewhere. This tension is precisely what Numen seeks out, and it is why this work needs to be made here. The Salton Sea's ecological fragility creates a backdrop that is not merely visually compelling but conceptually essential—a landscape that already asks the same questions the work asks about the sacred and what remains.
Numen is a photographic series examining the body as a site of spiritual inquiry—situating painted and draped figures within desert and geological landscapes to explore how the sacred is constructed and projected onto flesh and earth, and how viewers impose meaning onto the bodies they encounter.
The Last Judgement resonates with Numen naturally. The work draws from the visual language of religious iconography—bodies adorned, elevated, and scrutinized—but filtered through bacchanalian revelry rather than doctrine. Bombay Beach, with its atmosphere of joyful defiance in the face of collapse, amplifies this perfectly.
The Biennale's community is central to this. I envision the work made collaboratively with artists, performers, and community members—inviting participants to become part of the photographic process itself, whether as models, collaborators in the application of pigments and fabric, or as witnesses whose presence shapes the work.
The installation would present large-format photographic works on site. After the Biennale, the photographs would remain—left to weather and decay in the environment that made them, mirroring the landscape itself.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is a place of stark contrasts—beauty and decay in close proximity. This is not incidental to Numen; it is the work. The Salton Sea's ecological decline mirrors the questions Numen asks about what we consider sacred, what deteriorates, and what meaning we impose on what remains.
The landscape here is specific and unique. The abandoned structures are interventions in the landscape—human presence making a mark on the earth while nature reclaims it alongside. Creation, life, death, and rebirth exist here simultaneously. This dichotomy, of beautiful decay combined with nature, is precisely the environment Numen needs—in the same way that the geological formations of the desert Southwest have shaped the work from the beginning.
The Biennale community matters because Numen asks viewers to construct their own meaning. Making the work alongside other artists, performers, and visitors—and inviting them into the process as collaborators and models—would shape the work in ways that working in isolation cannot.
Leaving the photographs on site after the Biennale to weather and decay feels conceptually right. The work becomes part of the landscape, subject to the same forces acting on everything else here.

fields.Project Description: While It Lasts is a 10 foot steel sculpture installed directly on the shoreline of Bombay Beach. A single vertical form rises from the sand, interrupting the horizontal expanse of the Salton Sea. The work stages a quiet tension between endurance and impermanence. Constructed from untreated steel, the sculpture will continue to weather in the desert environment, registering time as surface change.
fields.Support Needed: Looking forward to participating!
fields.Abridged Project Text:
While It Last 
Everything stands for a while.
fields.Technical Support: None
fields.Audience Engagement: The sculpture’s scale invites an embodied response viewers must tilt their gaze upward, becoming aware of their own physical presence against the structure and the surrounding environment.
fields.Theme Engagement:
While It Lasts engages The Last Judgment through exposure rather than spectacle.

Instead of treating judgment as a singular, apocalyptic event, the sculpture considers it as an ongoing condition. Installed directly on the shoreline the form stands fully exposed to wind, salt, heat, and erosion. In this sense, judgment is enacted by time and environment rather than narrative or symbolism. The work is not waiting to be judged; it is already undergoing it.

The structure introduces a vertical axis within a horizontal landscape marked by abandonment and entropy. Rather than illustrating divine reckoning, the piece holds a posture, upright, provisional, and subject to change. The title reframes final judgment as duration. Everything present the sculpture, the site, the viewer exists temporarily.

Judgment, here, is not thunderous or theatrical. It is steady, indifferent, and already underway.
fields.Risk & Safety: A ladder and potentially some digging. I will have 2 helpers with me for safety.

fields.Project Description: A metal/plastic clothing line as typically used for drying clothes will be installed  and a hanging sculpture of a child's sweater made in paper maché will swing from the clothing wire. A protective layer will be used to protect the sculpture from the elements and to keep the glitter on the piece. The location of the installation will be at the discretion of BBB. It could be fun to install between light poles or behind a structure. It is a whimsical, sweet, but also melancholy piece that people can walk around. It is meant to dazzle and gently move, perhaps difficult to discern from the sky behind it.
fields.Support Needed: I am currently finishing a degree in Ireland, but I live nearby and love the BBB and area. I am in town until March 14 and would need to install before then and leave the piece up indefinitely (I am not back until summer). Thank you (PS I also did 4 big installs at Burning Man 2000-2003 including their "centerpiece" location in 2001 so I have some experience working in similar conditions)
fields.Abridged Project Text: A paper maché child's sweater covered in pale blue glitter like the sky suspended from a clothing line, an homage to the extraordinary in the ordinary.
fields.Technical Support: I need 2 spots to attach eyebolts to, or just to tie off of. The piece is very easy and quick to install. I will need to bring a ladder.
fields.Audience Engagement: People would (hopefully) see something sparkling but be unsure what it is, since it may be the same color as the sky behind it. As they approach, they begin to make out the contours of a child-sized sweater. They can see it gently moving or swaying. This is reminiscent of pre-industrial methods of drying your clothes in the air and should evoke a sense of timeless things and fragility.
fields.Theme Engagement: I'm sure many connections can be made to the theme. I am not quite intending it to be a "judgement" especially not a "final one" though the hanging of the garment could be quite suggestive in the context.
fields.Risk & Safety: No. I think the glitter being sealed is the most important risk factor.

fields.Project Description:
It's a truck camper on the back of a Ford F150 truck with a live edge wood bar on the right side with LED cowboy, cactus, and "Gay Bar" lights in the windows. Plan is to be stationed in the Temple of the Scientific Method lot for the Drag Show and for the Quesadilla Communion, and then mobile as a mobile art car outside Neptune (Kirby's lot for the auction), as well as the beach, SubClub, Bombay Bliss Club Saturday night etc.

The front truck portion will be transformed to be disguised by Rainbow led panels, clouds to disguise the wheels, with a brass bed frame cut in half to represent the "Gates of Heaven" while having a chill space queen bed mattress on the hood as I had in Convivium 2025. The wood stove chimney in the rear has an optional propane fire/flame effect that is operated hands on by me.
fields.Support Needed: If for the map there's a Icon or Logo to indicate that it's present for a particular event, then it might work considering this is mobile. Else, the Temple to the Scientific Method is a decent enough short-hand for a map.
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Tiny Closet On Wheels is a pop-up gay bar art car out of a pop-up truck camper. Cabincore maximilist "man-cave" aesthetics on the inside, and rainbow LEDs on the outside supporting an inclusive vibe and
fields.Technical Support: Just direction. I'm self-sufficient and can also assist as a mobile sound stage since I will have water, propane, power, and PA speakers and mics on board.
fields.Audience Engagement:
I expect to have fun conversations with folks as I offer folks some Franzia, or water from a Coleman water cooler on the bar, and show my camper, assist in chill space, and back of truck. It is "byob" to set expectations low at scale.

At other times, I've set out a propane fire pit and chill space
fields.Theme Engagement:
This "Tiny Closet in the Clouds" with Rainbows and Heaven overtones has been in the works, the Bed Frame heavens gates saw its debut in Saguaro Man, the Arizona Regional Burn in April 2025 and provided a backdrop for the Quesadilla Communion.

This project asks the question "Do gays go to heaven?" Well...they can, if they spend some time in the chill space on the front, (limit to '7 minutes in heaven')

There is a potential level where a Santa hat is donned and judges whether someone is "Naughty" or "Nice"...but I think that'll not make the edit.
fields.Risk & Safety:
It does have a Propane "poofer", a flame effect out of a double walled wood stove chimney on the rear of my truck camper that runs off of 1lb propane bottles. I have fire extinguishers in the back of my camper, fire blanket, and only operate it when I am there, low wind, and stationary.

Driving slowly and generally staying stationary for set-up and display.
fields.Location Submittal: Temple to the Scientific Method

fields.Project Description:
The Bombay Beach Biennale app is a mobile-friendly guide for anyone attending the Biennale on March 20, 2026. It's designed to replace printed programs, scattered text threads, and the question every visitor asks: *"Where do I go? What's happening right now?"*
The app is free, requires no download from an app store, and works in any phone's web browser. Visitors simply open the link and they're in.
The goal is to give attendees a single, beautiful place to find everything they need, so you don't have to answer the same logistical questions a hundred times.
fields.Support Needed: https://bombay-beach-app.vercel.app/map  (the app)
fields.Abridged Project Text: Please download this app at (this address) to see everything going on with the Bienalle and manage your artist presence independently.
fields.Technical Support: I need you to provide data that I will describe in a document, attached, and schedule a meeting to talk about ideas and direction, so what we provide on opening day is actually useful and still pointed in the right direction.

fields.Audience Engagement: People should visit this webpage so they can look at the events as they happen on their phone and receive notifications when events are nearby or upcoming.  Contributors should visit the webpage, sign up using a code we provide, create a profile, and upload all their projects and art exhibits. Then, feel reassured that their project will get more exposure because nobody who's anybody loses their phone.  Everyone should use the photo upload gallery to snap pics of their time at the Biennale, which will be neatly delivered to Dulcinee at the end of the event.

fields.Project Description:
A site-specific sculptural installation and work of ecological advocacy, installed temporarily for Earth Day 2020 at shore of the Salton Sea, now situated permanently in Bombay Beach.

Hope is something that we extend before us, it is a brave anticipation. Balanced between an awareness of the present and an idea of a possible future, it is a kind of double vision. Akasha is gentle yet defiant, reaching and delving. A lure cast to the beyond – into the unfolding depths of earth and sky.

Situated in an area where water once was, its form echoes the flows that have shaped landscapes which to us now appear dry. Akasha calls out to our innate belonging and participation in the processes which animate the world, reawakening our awareness of the continuity between all things — reminding us that it is to the expanse that our souls belong.
fields.Support Needed: please put Akasha on the map, thank you!
fields.Abridged Project Text: Calling to the waters that connect all life, Akasha is a symbol of hope and continuity – things slip through the cracks, life finds a way and through this unusual genesis new ideas are born. Where there is water there is life. At the shore of a dying sea, what can be done?
fields.Technical Support: n/a
fields.Audience Engagement: People will experience the piece through the relative scale of their bodies, approaching it on foot, relating to it as something deeply rooted in this world, and yet not quite of it.
fields.Theme Engagement: n/a
fields.Risk & Safety: n/a
fields.Location Submittal: Outside Poetry House on Avenue H

fields.Project Description: Draw your favorite verses on the Poetry House, the home is meant to be a canvas for the poetry that we carry in our hearts.  First, guests encounter the Hanging Garden of Babble On, a shaded labyrinth of poetry and interwoven flowers. Once inside, there is the lounge area where you can hang with friends, reflect, or read poetry that you find in the library inside. As mystic-in-residence, pharaoh offers tarot readings in the interior of The Poetry House, and reiki in the exterior building.
fields.Support Needed: The Hanging Garden, The Poetry House, and the Reiki / Body Work Center should all be listed separately on the map, if possible. At least the first two <3
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Poetry House is a center for poetry and spiritual restoration, celebrating poetry as a technology that translates emotions into tangible reality. Co-created by Pharaoh and Tao Ruspoli, participants are welcome to write their favorite verses on the walls...
fields.Technical Support: I would like to have the wiring checked out <3. A Queen mattress for Eric.
fields.Audience Engagement: This is something people move through and participate in. They are welcome to leave poems in the Hanging Garden, or on the walls.
fields.Theme Engagement: The entirety of the King James Bible is Shakespearean prose poetry. The Last Judgement requires spiritual alignment, and that is what The Poetry House, through its varied experiences, offers our community.
fields.Risk & Safety: No, just paint but we do that outside.
fields.Location Submittal: The Poetry House 5&H Street (9153 ?)

fields.Project Description:
Aperion is a site specific 10 foot sculpture currently under construction in steel, rope and resin, featuring a night-black dish holding salt water, with wings of glistening charcoal sourced from scorched local wetlands, calling in chimerism, transmutation and alchemy to speak to the beyond-human world. A central dish of salt water takes flight on wings, referencing the Lethe - mystic river of oblivion, gateway to the underworld and aqueous threshold for the shedding of attachments to the material, mortal human world.
Aperion weaves the waters of the Salton Sea into the ecomythology of waters; turning our attention away from what we think we know, calling us into communion with the fertile dark of unknowing, asking what resides there for us, and what we are being asked to let go of?
fields.Support Needed: thank you!
fields.Abridged Project Text: Aperion calls us into communion with the wild, unbounded intelligence of the beyond-human world; featuring a night-black dish holding salt water, with wings of glistening charcoal sourced from scorched local wetlands, drawing upon chimerism, transmutation & alchemy to speak to the beyond-human world
fields.Technical Support: n/a
fields.Audience Engagement: Audiences will encounter the sculpture as they explore Bombay Beach, the piece will be strategically lit using solar power at night for maximum effect.
fields.Theme Engagement: The river Lethe was one of the five mystic rivers of the ancient greek underworld, a threshold transversed by souls on their journey through Hades whose waters induced oblivion in those who drank them.
fields.Risk & Safety: n/a
fields.Location Submittal: 9543 Avenue H

fields.Project Description:
This permanent installation is a commentary on the nature of global consumerism, and how massive cargo ships that ply the seas to deliver goods are eventually decommissioned in third world countries like India and Pakistan, where cheap and expendable human labor are exploited to salvage and recycle much of the industrial materials, leaving behind a skeleton in what can only be described as a ship graveyard. And yet, they bring an eerie sculptural and abstract beauty, resembling massive sculptures. The world’s largest ship breaking yard is in Alang, India, responsible for dismantling a third of the world’s freight and cargo ships. It has 183 ship breaking yards along 9 miles of coast that totals 4.5 million Light Displacement Tonnage (LDT) of capacity.

The yards have generated controversies about numerous environmental, health and safety issues. Between 2009 and 2012, at least 54 workers were said to have died in work-related issues.  

The fact that the very vessels responsible for transporting our disposable goods around the world are in fact disposable themselves is an ironic meta commentary on both consumerism, and its impact on the planet. This of course aligns well with Bombay Beach and the Salton Sea, a body of water that was destroyed by consumerism, in this case from the massive amount of toxic pollutants that leached in from the mono farms in the Imperial Valley. 

Bombay Beach is the perfect, ironic setting for a beautiful sculptural, abstract piece that will resemble a giant ship gradually degrading into the sand. It will be constructed of plywood, lumber and recycled chain to assure the lowest carbon footprint. The bow will contain a DJ booth, and we will light the structure at night. It will be approximately 12' high, 48' long (with gaps), and 16-18' wide.
fields.Support Needed: Honored and excited to be a part of this creative community in this momentous year.
fields.Notes: Tao will talk to Billy Fried & Jorg
fields.Abridged Project Text: A meditation on the nature of mass consumerism and global capitalism, and the devastating impact it has on our built and non-built environments, as well as the exploitation of disposable human capital.
fields.Technical Support: A power source to run the stage and set lighting, as well as the music.

The bow of the boat will contain a DJ platform and booth, and we plan to activate it as a dance space with the ship serving as the backdrop.

fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): Since visiting Bombay Beach, and then attending the Biennale in 2024, we were struck by the creative freedom, the post-apocalyptic setting, and the vibrant community of renegades and anarchists. We were inspired, and vowed to never come back without participating and adding our voice to the circus.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): While so many art and festival gatherings are commercialized and commoditized, we wanted to participate in this grand experiment to create something beautiful and challenging out of the ruins. We are not only artists, but also political activists who want to challenge the status quo. The Biennale and Bombay Beach are so incredibly unique, and a vital experiment in off grid sovereignty and freedom of expression.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): We are fully through the conceptual stage, and have costed and engineered the installation, and are ready to start building

fields.Project Description:
The First Supper positions Eden at the edge of the Salton Sea. Anchored to the desert and constructed to withstand the harsh conditions of Bombay Beach, a lush banquet table rises from the dust. It is lavish, sacred, miraculous, and improbable. An unlikely place for a garden. But a very likely place for magic.

Draped in preserved moss, the table evokes the Garden of Eden; reconstructed in a landscape widely regarded as post-apocalyptic. Here, the end and the beginning are one. The table’s surface is lush, baroque, theatrical: resin grapes, jeweled pomegranates, and golden apples spill across ornate platters. Candlesticks and candelabras retrofitted with solar-powered illumination cast a warm, golden glow after dark. A gilded serpent weaves through the display - suggesting that knowledge, temptation, collapse, and creation are inseparable forces. At the center of the table sits a golden gavel: a symbolic nod to the power the drunken jury holds. 

Along both sides of the table, place settings adorn: each marked with Archetypal titles - The Saint, The Sinner, The Scientist, The Artist, The Philosopher, The Fool, The Tourist, The Local, The Damned, The Divine, The Bewildered, and The Believer. Attached to the place settings are chains, suggesting that the roles we choose to play in the theatre of the divine (and the theatre of the absurd) can act either as bonds, or bondage. 

At the head of the table sits one empty, ornate chair and a placard - anchored in and intentionally immovable. It reads, GOD. 

Next to it is an unbreakable mirror, inviting reflection: Is it us, who judges ourselves? Are we a reflection of the divine? What does it mean to be seen through the eyes of the creator? And, in the last judgement, is there an opportunity for redemption - if we are able to truly see ourselves? Or, are we simply vain beings, casting ourselves in the ultimate role of creator, judge, and jury?

But fear not, The First Supper offers redemption for the pure of heart. Embedded throughout the table are living resurrection plants (Selaginella lepidophylla), desert-adapted botanicals capable of entering dormancy during extreme dryness, and dramatically reanimating when given water. Visitors are invited to participate in this revival. On the table sits a small vessel of water, and a sign reading: THE GARDEN REQUIRES YOUR MERCY. 

The Resurrection plant’s fluctuating states, whether curled and skeletal or open and verdant, will visibly reflect the degree of collective participation. Here, we present an ongoing ritual of intention and rebirth. Do we keep Eden alive? Or do we allow it to decay in the dust? Either way, transformation is enacted at the table.

By day, The First Supper reads as a decadent altar, and by night, as apparition. Powered by the same sun that scorches the desert, the table glows at dusk. Solar-powered elements atop and beneath the table create a warm, sacred radiance - reminding us that even the harshest systems can generate illumination.
fields.Support Needed: In addition to the installation itself, we envision The First Supper as a multi-layered experience that extends beyond observation into participation and delight. If selected, we would like to organize a communal dinner around the table, accompanied by a scavenger hunt that leads participants to the installation, creating a ritualized journey of discovery. At the dinner, guests would share fresh-baked bread, crisp apples, and small culinary surprises, and more -  creating moments of connection and generosity. These gestures mirror the themes of renewal and attention that run through the work, transforming the act of gathering into an intimate, magical performance. Through this layered approach, the installation becomes more than art; it becomes a living, evolving encounter that draws on the history and spirit of Bombay Beach. Participants are invited to step into roles, tend the plants, share stories, and contribute to the table’s ongoing life. Small gestures of care ripple outward, enacting the same renewal and rebirth the installation embodies. The First Supper is both place and event, a banquet for the senses, the spirit, and the imagination. It honors the resilience and creativity of this unique landscape while offering participants the rare opportunity to create, witness, and inhabit Eden… even if only for a few hours. We hope to make this generosity and transformation tangible, leaving an imprint on both the site and those who gather at the table. But we need your help! It is our hope that through the generous offering of the Grant, we will be able to fully bring this to life. Thank you for your time, and for the opportunity.
fields.Abridged Project Text: After the last judgment has fallen, the table remains. A banquet rises from the dust: luminous and alive. Moss drapes its surface. Golden apples glimmer. The table requests your presence and your participation. The ritual of renewal asks: Who judges? Who redeems? And can we recreate Eden, together?
fields.Technical Support: My installation is materially ambitious yet structurally practical, The First Supper is designed to withstand the desert’s conditions, off-grid, without requiring ongoing technical support or electricity. The solar lights are self-contained, there is no reliance on external power, all decorative elements are permanently adhered with industrial adhesive. Though the installation is envisioned as existing outside,  it could also very easily exist within a space indoors.

fields.Audience Engagement:
The First Supper functions as both an installation and an invitation. Visitors may find it, stumbling upon the luminous table in the desert night; or approach it intentionally, as a site of gathering. While the work can be observed, it is truly completed through participation.

Resurrection plants enter dormancy in the desert heat & revive when misted by participants, making care & collective attention part of the artwork itself. The transformation - brittle forms softening into green - places life force power in the hands of the participants. Care itself is the medium. This fluctuating vitality reflects communal engagement throughout BBB: a reminder that attention brings renewal. 

The Archetypal Place Cards create a space for interaction, without forcing performance. Viewers are invited to contemplate what role they play in the theatre of the divine. Some may simply reflect; others may step fully into the archetype.

Intentional lighting calls people through the dark into a place of reverence, revelry & reflection; be it of bacchanal variety or illuminated contemplation. We envision inviting performers, dancers, storytellers & musicians to activate the table, allowing the installation to evolve. In addition, if permitted, we will share edible offerings: fresh baked bread, unexpected delicacies, and of course, crisp, sweet apples - creating a literal banquet of sorts. In this way, The First Supper becomes a living gathering of magic, nourishment, communion, and connection.
fields.Theme Engagement:
In a year devoted to The Last Judgement, The First Supper offers not trial or punishment, but redemption. The end and the beginning are one, and we imagine what comes both before creation, and after the final verdict: not heaven, not hell, but a banquet. Here, it all exists: on one shared table. Everyone is invited.

Aesthetically, the work connects the Renaissance excess & Baroque drama of this year’s theme, and the irreverence central to the BBB. The archetypal place settings remind us that on this great stage, we all have our roles to play. 

The table gestures toward restoration in an era saturated with collapse narratives. Metaphorically, the preserved moss represents an artificial Eden: a paradise, reconstructed. It requires no tending, no vulnerability, no participation. It is beauty without relationship. The living component of the resurrection plants, however, reflect that redemption & renewal are possible, but only through attention and care. When poured into, they expand. When neglected, they curl inward. Much like ourselves. Revival requires presence & participation. We are the judges of what survives.

At its core, the table is a love letter - on a personal level, the work asks: can we be redeemed, reach a state of purity, and begin again? Does it happen with care? Does it happen with judgement? Does it happen without judgement? Does it happen with reflection? With renewal? If Eden was lost, this table asks whether it can be rebuilt, one act of tending at a time.
fields.Risk & Safety: This project does not involve fire, vehicles, ladders, etc, and it poses no known potentially hazardous materials or actions. If positioned outside, we will anchor and rebar the table and ideally allow it to remain as a permanent installation.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): The First Supper is conceived exclusively for the 10-Year Anniversary of the Bombay Beach Biennale. This installation is entirely new, and has never been shown, performed, or installed elsewhere. Designed specifically for this site, it merges immersive installation, participatory ritual, environmental awareness, and art historical reference. The First Supper is uniquely made to engage directly with the BBB 2026 theme of The Last Judgement. This is the first time this will be shown and installed :)
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create this work because, at its essence, it is a Love Letter. This work is for the brokenhearted, the hopeful, the sinners, and the saints. It is a ritual of renewal - enacted by our presence, our participation, and our desire to reclaim what was lost. If the Last Supper marked betrayal, this First Supper imagines repair.

My work combines the mythic and the mystical, the esoteric and the ethereal, and highlights my ongoing desire to bring the metaphysical experience into the material plane. Written in gold, moss, and decadence, the table represents redemption and the opportunity to begin again. In a year devoted to The Last Judgement, this installation asks not who is condemned, but who is willing to tend, to nurture, and to be present. Here, the sacred becomes tangible. Eden is not lost. It waits, luminous and alive, ready to be rebuilt with love.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Installed along the mythic Salton Sea, The First Supper places the symbolism of Eden within a landscape shaped by rise, collapse, and ritualized re-creation. Bombay Beach, once a glamorous desert resort, represents a decayed dream, forgotten and renewed. It stands as both a cautionary tale, and a space of creative rebirth. From the sea rises not ruin, but a community that has transformed abandonment into radical creativity. 

Against this backdrop, my art asks: If we were expelled from Eden, can we build one anyway? Is paradise something we inherit, or something we assemble from what survives? And, if judgement has already come, is paradise something we can build anew?  

The work reflects the paradox of the Salton Sea itself: an oasis, turned ecological warning, turned place of artistic pilgrimage - revived by creatives, thinkers & dreamers. The preserved moss adorning the table evokes an artificial paradise: Eden reconstructed. In contrast, the resurrection plants embody resilience, revived only through nurture and intention. Here, we gather. 

In this iconic town, The First Supper offers a space of redemption and renewal - decadent, sacred & participatory - where rebirth and revelation are enacted through attention and care. It is here, in Bombay Beach, that The First Supper is called into being. On the shores of the Salton Sea, where abandonment sparks imagination, we will transform a small piece of the desert into a living, breathing Eden. All are invited to the table.

fields.Project Description:
Respite Tea House is a living installation of ritual hospitality, a temporary desert chapel disguised as a waiting room.
An immersive theater experience.
White-glove tea service.
Secret ritual ceremonies in Heaven (or Hell’s) waiting room.
Those who know, know
The Bombay Beach Biennale could be described as a little bit of everything, everywhere, all at once. It is chaos, creativity beyond comprehension, curiosity, complexity, and then some.
It is deliriously unique. It is cup-filling. It is a glorious storm.
And yet, after nine years of attending and participating, we’ve found there is one element often missing:
Respite.
One particular evening, we watched a man sing opera in Arabic, threw ass at Café Bosna and Sub Club, sang West Side Story in the street, watched Cirque du Soleil fire-eaters perform on the beach, and carefully dodged trash and glass at The Estates... all in the same night. All while tripping balls.
It was unforgettable.
It was ecstatic.
It was Bombay Beach at its finest.
And still, somewhere in the whirlwind, we longed for a pocket of softness. A place to land. A room where the nervous system could unclench for a moment before diving back in.
Hence: Respite Tea House.
Respite transforms an interior space into a warm, softly luminous haven where guests are received as souls in suspension, but instead of anxiety, they are met with hospitality.
Shoes are removed.
White gloves are worn.
Tea is served with ceremonial precision.
Guests may receive a “Judgment Number” upon entry as a gentle reminder that something is pending, though nothing urgent ever happens.
The room is washed in warm matte white and softened with layered rugs, modular low tea tables, floor cushions, desert-appropriate plants, and subtle reflective surfaces.
Lighting is gentle and golden. Time stretches.
Judgment has been indefinitely postponed due to tea service.
Suspension becomes restorative.
The storm hums outside while inside, there is calm.
Requested location: Formerly known as the “Shaguar Room.”
The space will be transformed through:
Warm matte white treatment on walls and floor
Modular low tea tables 
Layered rugs, floor cushions
Desert plants, restrained decor
Warm lighting
Subtle reflective elements (selective mirror placement)
A small central ritual platform with ceremonial bell
The aesthetic is rugged yet reverent,  a chapel filtered through absurd hospitality. 
The installation is modular, resource-conscious, and designed to thrive in DIY, site-specific conditions.
Respite Tea House is newly conceived specifically for the 2026 Bombay Beach Biennale and has not been previously exhibited or realized in any form.
While the Biennale excels at spectacle, provocation, and ecstatic chaos, Respite offers necessary emotional contrast, a participatory sanctuary centered on restoration, tenderness, and absurd mercy within the storm.
If the Biennale is staging the end of the world, Respite holds the room where everyone catches their breath before the gavel falls.
fields.Support Needed: I really hope we can make something special to give back to this incredible land that has been such a catalyst for growth and exploration in my life! I think this will be super sweet, silly and impactful.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Judgment has been indefinitely postponed due to tea service. Enter a white-gloved waiting room between Heaven and Hell, where bells summon poems, rituals unfold quietly, and sanctuary replaces spectacle. Rest here before the gavel falls. Let it Steep.
fields.Technical Support:
Respite Tea House is designed to function within DIY, site-specific conditions and requires minimal technical support.

Primary needs:
• Access to standard 120V electrical outlets
• Indoor space (requested: Shaguar Room or similar enclosed room)
• Basic ambient lighting control (if available)

Electricity will power:
• Two electric hot water vessels/percolators
• Warm LED lighting fixtures with dimmers
• One small projector and media player for subtle ambient projection
• Small powered speaker for low-volume sound reinforcement (if needed)

Sound requirements are minimal. Performances will be acoustic or lightly amplified. A small self-contained PA system (which we can supply if needed) is sufficient.

Projection will be atmospheric only — soft ambient textures, not large-scale projection mapping. A simple wall surface is sufficient; no screen required.

No stage lighting, heavy audio equipment, or complex tech setup is needed. The installation is modular and self-contained, with setup and strike handled by our team.

We are comfortable adapting to available infrastructure and can operate with minimal production support.
fields.Audience Engagement:
At irregular intervals, one of the white-gloved tea tenders rings a small ceremonial bell.
The room quiets.
Sometimes the bell initiates a brief immersive ritual — some sincere, some gently absurd.
Sometimes it signals a short poem or song.
Sometimes it simply acknowledges the passage of time.
Guests may be quietly invited to “proceed” to a designated ritual corner for intimate tea-based ceremonies lasting 3–5 minutes. These may include:
Shared Steam Ceremony — quiet union
Self-Witness Rite — reflective pause
Administrative Upgrade — cushion reassignment
Clerical Error in Eternity — paperwork anomaly
Mercy Granted — official stamp
Collective Exhale — communal breath
Tea for Tea — voluntary confession
These moments are not announced publicly. They occur when they need to.
Throughout activation hours, short-form poetic and musical and comedic fragments (5–10 minutes) unfold as gentle and entertaining offerings to those waiting, functioning as testimonies, lullabies, or celestial hold music in this bureaucratic limbo.
The room is suspended from fully entering concert mode. It remains a sanctuary. Artistic interventions are reviewed, approved, and delivered with measured excellence. Copy and paste this link to hear some samples of the poetry and music.
https://s.disco.ac/qigyvridxsjn
fields.Theme Engagement:
In 2026’s theme of The Last Judgment, souls are weighed. Verdicts loom. Civilization is placed on trial.
That sounds fucking exhausting.
Respite offers an alternative.
If the Biennale is staging the trial of civilization, we create the waiting room before judgment, reimagined as a sanctuary chapel.
Not Heaven.
Not Hell.
Just the pause before your number is called.
No scales.
No verdicts.
No winged accountants tallying your sins.
Just tea.
Just pause.
Just mercy.
Even the Devil deserves a place to lay his weary head.
And perhaps a moment to steep.
fields.Risk & Safety: Respite Tea House does not involve fire, ladders, vehicles, boats, drones, excavation, or hazardous materials. The installation is fully indoors and low-risk. Tea will be served using standard electric hot water vessels and percolators operated by designated attendants. All electrical equipment will be properly rated and safely distributed via surge-protected power strips. Pathways will remain clear to prevent tripping hazards, and all furnishings will be secured and stable.
fields.Additional Artists: Brooke Burgstahler, Felis La Fleur, Benja, Euphemia
