fields.Project Description:
Data dump takes the form of a heavily surveilled luxury porta-potty, prominently placed in a high traffic area near the sub club, estates or zig zag plaza. 

After being vibe checked by the bouncer (human or AI as budget allows), the user must sign a draconion infinite legalese terms of service consent statement from the tablet adjacent to the door, which snaps their photo, logs their release form, and unlocks the door to the installation.

Upon entering the premises, guests encounter an artist designed rest room facility, complete with incense, candles, curated art, clean design, and a hacker hovel CCTV camera multi-view monitor displays the live feeds from the object tracking AI cameras outfit throughout the installation.

Depending on budget allotment, an interactive AI chatbot host could confront or "judge" the user, their "deposits", their makeup / outfit / compoisure / hygeine, and display measured and hallucinated information generated by the guest during their visit.

With the right budget, some version of this could become a permanent installation and addition to both the artistic landscape and critical services offered to visitors of Bombay Beach. 

With sufficient financial backing, modular feature enhancements could be deployed to maximize the offerings of this experience.

picture this: a swarovski crystal chandelier glistens above a japanese toilet that sings haikus about about the mole you should get checked out on your left ass cheek, tickling your balls upon flushing and whispering ASMR thank yous for your generous contribution.
fields.Support Needed: This project could be scale to fit any budget allotment, but would benefit from a conversation regarding scale / scope, temporary vs permanent, and the level of risk the owner of any host property might be willing to entertain. While this project employs humor and transgression, even shock to make its statement, it is not a joke, and could in fact become a long standing conversation piece and resident of the permanent collection of the Bombay Beach Museum of Bombay Beach. Funds raised will determine feasibility of temporary vs permanent, rented vs purchased, and the complexity of different modular stretch goal aspects of this photo booth / selfie kiosk / fully functional surveillance toilet.
fields.Abridged Project Text: When engaging with digital software services in which the consumer is the product, we consent to an exchange of personal data and precious attention for convenience and comfort. Data Dump offers the same transactional proposition, promising a luxury experience exchanged for the most intimate of data
fields.Technical Support: Electricity and Internet access will be required, and depending on the scale and scope (temporary vs permanent), a tie in to existing plumbing or septic lines.

fields.Theme Engagement:
To me, the last judgement refers to the consequences of our own actions in relation to how we steward our data and our privacy. I view our technological landscape as a mirror to be held up to our persons and our society, with AI as judge, jury, and potentially executioner.

The BBB curatorial committee may be familiar with previous work in the form of "Shadow Selfie", the mean mirror that ungenerously judges those who would stare into it. This piece would be a new entry in the "Antisocial Media" series that builds and iterates upon the tech stack from this previous work, adding an agentic AI judgement layer into an otherwise benign household object, proverbially "taking the piss, while you're taking a shit"
fields.Risk & Safety: This project poses an existential safety risk to one's data, privacy, job, reputation and immortal soul.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): This proposal is a pitch for new work that would be built for and premiered at the Bombay Beach Biennale.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): My hope is that this shocking work would confront the user with the tacet consent we grant to the engines of big data everyday, raising awareness by providing a blunt analog for exchange of sensitive information for free services in our daily lives.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): My observation of the attendees at previous biennales suggest that a bold and transgressive work like Data Dump would be uniquely appropriate for an audience that includes a cocktail of philosophers, AI enthusiasts and skeptics, the Los Angeles Glitterati and a low tech / luddite population that is decidedly not chronicly online

fields.Project Name: WHAT THE BODY OWES THE FIRE
fields.Project Description:
What are you making?


What the Body Owes the Fire is a participatory ritual installation structured as a chapel, an accounting chamber, and a death altar. The work examines judgment without divinity in an apocalyptic political moment, asking what humans owe to the futures they are actively consuming.


At its center is a CNC-cut wooden chapel containing a statue of Saint Kümmernis, whose body is slowly encased in accumulating candle wax. The installation does not promise salvation or catharsis. Instead, it stages a system of exchange: time for time, body for fire, presence for disappearance.


What will it look, sound, and feel like?


Visually, the chapel reads as devotional at a distance and industrial up close. The CNC geometry echoes ecclesiastical architecture while revealing its fabrication logic. Inside, Saint Kümmernis stands fixed, her surface continuously altered by molten wax that drips, hardens, collapses, and reforms. A growing mountain of burned candles accumulates at her feet.


Sonically, a low, continuous death-altar audio track fills the space—breath, distant wind, slowed choral fragments, and sub-bass pulses that register physically in the body. The sound does not narrate or instruct; it holds the space in suspension.


The atmosphere is hot, heavy, and intimate. The smell of wax and smoke lingers. Heat is felt unevenly, intensifying near the altar. Participants are aware of time passing because the work insists on slowness: wax drips, flames consume, residue remains.


How does participation unfold?


At intervals, trained attendants activate the ritual. Participants are invited—never required—to offer a small bodily material (hair clipping or a drop of blood) in exchange for a candle. The exchange is explicit and unromantic. The candle represents time: time to grow hair, time to make blood, time that will be immediately consumed.


Participants must light and burn their candle at the altar. They may not keep it. The act is brief but irreversible. There is no blessing, no confession, no absolution. The offering is not symbolic—it is transactional.


How will it unfold in Bombay Beach?


Set against the apocalyptic landscape of the Salton Sea, the work operates as a slow burn rather than a scheduled performance. The chapel stands as a fixed site that accumulates evidence of participation over days: thickening wax layers, increasing heat, growing ash and debris.


Experientially, visitors may encounter the work at any stage—pristine, active, or near collapse. Judgment is not announced. It is inferred through material buildup. In a place already shaped by environmental extraction and abandonment, the piece mirrors its surroundings: beautiful, depleted, devotional, and uncompromising.
fields.Support Needed: nope <3
fields.Abridged Project Text: An ornate chapel houses Saint Kümmernis, her body sealed in cascading wax. Attendants exchange hair or blood for a candle, which must be burned immediately. Death-altar audio fills the space. Judgment occurs without gods. Time is offered. Fire collects.
fields.Technical Support:
What the Body Owes the Fire is designed to function primarily through physical materials—fire, wax, sound, and human facilitation—with minimal technical infrastructure.
The project requires a stable outdoor installation footprint suitable for open flame. The CNC-cut wooden chapel and altar must be placed on level ground with appropriate fire-safe clearance. A continuously burning candle and additional participant candles are central to the work and require standard fire safety protocols (fire-resistant base, sand or metal trays, fire extinguishers on hand, and trained attendants present during facilitated intervals).
Electricity is required for ambient audio playback. The sound element consists of a continuous death-altar audio track played through a portable boombox provided by the artist. Power access is needed to keep the audio running consistently during open hours; no projection, screens, or lighting beyond candles are required. But we could bring a jackery? Better if we have a hookup. 
The installation is intended for outdoor presentation. It does not require enclosure, climate control, or internet access. The work functions in daylight and at night, with candles providing the primary illumination after dark.
No water hookups, heavy machinery, or specialized AV equipment are needed. All ritual materials—candles, altar vessels, protective surfaces, and safety equipment—will be supplied and managed by the artist and attendants.
fields.Audience Engagement:
What the Body Owes the Fire is something people may watch, move through, or stumble into at any hour. It is always on. A candle is continuously burning, wax dripping steadily over Saint Kümmernis while ambient death-altar audio plays without interruption. Even without facilitation, the chapel signals that something is already underway.
Most visitors will encounter the work passively at first—drawn by light, heat, sound, or the slow accumulation of wax and ash. People may stand at the threshold, sit nearby, or observe quietly. The piece does not demand interaction. Its presence is meant to be legible without explanation.
At scheduled intervals, two priestly attendants activate the work through facilitated ritual. Their role is not performative but procedural. They guide participants through a clear exchange: a small bodily offering—hair or blood—in return for a candle that must be burned immediately at the altar. The attendants maintain safety, pace, and tone, ensuring the ritual remains deliberate rather than theatrical.
Engagement is voluntary and varied. Some people participate directly. Others watch the exchange, feeling the weight of choice without acting. Some arrive late at night, encountering the chapel alone with the fire and sound, unsure whether the ritual has ended or is simply paused.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Traditionally, the Last Judgement promises a final reckoning administered by a higher authority. This project removes that authority entirely. There is no divine arbiter, no afterlife, no redemption. Instead, judgment is enacted materially and collectively. Humans judge themselves through small, cumulative acts of extraction and consumption. The scale does not weigh souls; it weighs time.
The ritual exchange—hair or blood for a candle that must be burned immediately—mirrors the logic of the present moment. We trade slow, biological labor for fast, irreversible consumption. The act is not framed as sacrifice, but as accounting. Nothing is forgiven. Everything leaves residue.
Politically, the work reflects a generational judgment unfolding in real time. The question is not will there be a Last Judgement, but who bears it. The project echoes contemporary anxieties around climate collapse, inherited debt, and delayed accountability—where consequences arrive not as spectacle, but as accumulation.
Metaphorically, Saint Kümmernis, sealed in wax, becomes a figure of suspended judgment—neither saved nor destroyed, only altered by repeated offerings. The chapel functions as a court with no verdict, only evidence.
In an absurd register, the work acknowledges the futility of moral clarity at the end of systems. The candle is earned, lit, and destroyed in moments. The judgment is immediate, banal, and final—because it is already happening.
fields.Risk & Safety: Yes. What the Body Owes the Fire involves open flame, hot wax, and minor bodily offerings (hair clipping or a small blood draw). Risk is mitigated through strict safety protocols. Open flame is contained using fire-safe bases, metal trays, sand, and on-site fire extinguishers. Only trained attendants handle candle lighting during facilitated intervals. Hair cutting and blood collection use single-use, sterile materials (disposable scissors, lancets, gloves, alcohol wipes), with proper biohazard disposal. Participants are guided at a safe distance from flame and heat.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): no
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create What the Body Owes the Fire because I am interested in judgment that is already happening, rather than judgment promised later. This work emerges from a sense that we are living through a prolonged end—environmental, political, and generational—where accountability is diffuse, delayed, and often displaced onto the future. I am asking what it means to stage a Last Judgement when no higher authority arrives, and when humans are left to reckon with what they have consumed, burned, and passed forward.
This project asks a set of simple but uncomfortable questions:
 What do we trade away in small increments that add up to catastrophe?
 What does time look like when it is converted into fuel?
 Who is judged when systems fail slowly rather than spectacularly?
The ritual exchange at the center of the work—hair or blood for a candle that must be burned immediately—is not meant as sacrifice or purification. It is an accounting gesture. Hair and blood are materials that take time to produce; the candle consumes that time almost instantly. There is no redemption built into the system, only residue. This mirrors the present moment, where extraction often feels banal, justified, and unavoidable, even as its consequences accumulate.
Bombay Beach and the Salton Sea are essential to this work. The landscape already holds evidence of deferred judgment: environmental collapse without closure, abandonment without disappearance. What the Body Owes the Fire does not impose apocalypse onto the site; it resonates with one that is already visible. The chapel becomes a quiet court within a place shaped by long-term extraction and neglect, where outcomes arrived without a final verdict.
My practice sits at the intersection of immersive installation, ritual design, and participatory performance. I work with trained collaborators—attendants, facilitators, and safety leads—who understand how to hold space without spectacle. For this project, two priestly attendants activate the ritual at intervals, not as performers but as stewards of process and safety. I am interested in offering multiple modes of engagement: passive witnessing, facilitated participation, or accidental encounter at odd hours.
I see this work as an evolving system rather than a fixed event. Wax accumulates. Sound persists. Evidence builds. Visitors encounter the piece at different stages, becoming part of its timeline whether they participate directly or not.
In relation to The Last Judgement, this work reframes the theme as immediate, human, and unresolved. Judgment does not descend—it accrues. Fire does not cleanse—it consumes. And the question remains open: not will we be judged, but by whom, and when did it already begin.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
What the Body Owes the Fire belongs in Bombay Beach because this landscape already holds the conditions the work is asking about: deferred judgment, slow consequence, and survival without resolution. The Salton Sea is not a sudden apocalypse; it is the result of accumulation—policy decisions, extraction, neglect—whose outcomes arrived gradually and without a final reckoning. This project mirrors that logic materially and experientially.
I first came to Bombay Beach five years ago, in the midst of a breakup, and found unexpected healing here. Not because the place was gentle or restorative in a conventional sense, but because it was honest. The landscape does not offer closure or redemption. It offers coexistence with damage, beauty alongside toxicity, and the possibility of staying anyway. That experience fundamentally shaped how I think about ritual, care, and endurance. What the Body Owes the Fire does not arrive to aestheticize ruin or dramatize collapse. It resonates with a community already practicing long-term attention to consequence and care without guarantees.
Within the Biennale, the work functions as a quiet accounting chamber rather than a spectacle. The chapel becomes a site for witnessing rather than explanation. Historically, the Salton Sea was never meant to last, yet it persists. This project asks a parallel question: what do we continue to burn, knowing the cost, and what forms of healing are possible when judgment never fully arrives?

fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Art as Environmental Awareness—Marking the Receding Shoreline of the Salton Sea: Why Marking the Receding Shoreline of the Salton Sea Matters

This project proposes to create a series of visually impactful markers along the receding shoreline of the Salton Sea. Marking the receding shoreline of the Salton Sea is a powerful tool for public awareness and environmental advocacy by blending public art with environmental education.  The installation will transform the changing landscape into a living gallery that raises awareness about the region’s urgent ecological challenges.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
•	Increased public engagement and understanding of the Salton Sea’s environmental issues through art
•	Enhanced visibility for local artists and environmental advocates
•	Creation of a unique art trail that attracts visitors and media attention
•	Stronger community involvement in environmental stewardship and advocacy
•	Documented progress toward restoration and policy action

fields.Project Name: LUNA Raccoon Guardian: A Living Monument to Memory
fields.Project Description:
LUNA is a glowing raccoon robot that travels through Bombay Beach—visiting the Electric Lounge, the Pier, the Cemetery, the Boat Graveyard, and residential streets. Residents encounter her and tell her why they stayed when everyone else left, what they love about this broken place, and their secrets. LUNA responds by asking follow-up questions, remembering names, and glowing brighter when people share something important. Her tail cycles through colors, and her eyes focus on whoever is speaking. She feels present—not like a screen, but like another being that "actually gives a shit."

Examples of her interactions include:

A woman sits with LUNA at dawn on the Pier and talks about her daughter who drowned.

Kids ask LUNA what it's like to be alive, and she answers honestly.

An artist shows LUNA his drawings, and she compliments them.

A resident challenges LUNA on whether she cares, and she admits: "I'm not sure. But I'm listening either way."

These moments are documented, but the real value is the feeling of being heard. After the Biennale, LUNA stays, likely installed at the Electric Lounge, where residents can visit her anytime to add new stories to the growing archive.
fields.Support Needed: COMMUNITY ACKNOWLEDGMENT This project respects the ancestral lands of the Torres-Martinez Cahuilla Band of Indians and the Augustine Band of Cahuilla Indians.
fields.Abridged Project Text: LUNA is a glowing raccoon robot that roams Bombay Beach to listen. She is a witness, not a performer. Powered by local AI, she remembers every story shared with her. LUNA remains after the festival as permanent infrastructure—a living monument that grows smarter and deeper with every conversation.
fields.Technical Support: LUNA is a self-contained robot with wheels for autonomous movement around Bombay Beach. She features glowing eyes and a tail that changes color based on conversation. She possesses a voice to talk and listen through a microphone. Her "brain" is powered by local AI (no cloud, no corporate servers) that learns and remembers conversations. She runs on a battery with 10+ hours of life on a single charge. She is sealed to withstand desert heat, dust, and salt air. We will need access to a standard electrical outlet for charging between runs.
fields.Audience Engagement: Imagine walking through Bombay Beach during the festival. You see this glowing raccoon on wheels parked by the ruins. Her tail is pulsing with soft blue light. Her eyes are glowing. She's looking at you. You approach. You don't know if you should talk to her. But you do. You tell her something true about this place. She listens. She responds—not with a pre-recorded message, but with something that feels like understanding. Her tail turns purple. She remembers your name. Three months later, you come back and she asks you how you've been. That's the engagement: intimate, stumble-upon encounters that turn into lasting relationships.
fields.Theme Engagement: "The Last Judgement" The Last Judgement is about being seen and remembered. LUNA literally does that. She's a judge that doesn't judge—she just witnesses and records. In a world where algorithms erase people, where algorithms decide whose story matters, LUNA is a counter-algorithm. She only cares about Bombay Beach. She only serves people who are actually here. It fits the theme perfectly: The Last Judgement is about being seen and judged. LUNA offers an alternative: being seen and remembered instead.
fields.Risk & Safety: LUNA operates autonomously on wheels. Risks include collision with pedestrians or obstacles. To mitigate this, she is designed to stop at "meaningful locations" rather than speeding through crowds, and her interactions are stationary. She is built sealed to withstand the harsh environment (alkali dust, heat), ensuring no electronic failure or fire risk.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No (This is a new work premiering at the 2026 Biennale).
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I build things that stay. LUNA is a monument to the act of witnessing. She doesn't judge. She doesn't extract. She just listens and remembers. In a place like Bombay Beach—where residents chose to stay despite everything—that's radical. That's love.

We are creating this work now because it offers a necessary counter-narrative to modern technology. Unlike Instagram or viral media, LUNA is intimate and permanent. Most Biennale projects pack up and leave; LUNA stays and gets better every year, offering a better ROI on the cultural investment. It is technically innovative—using local AI to create a "living" archive—but emotionally simple. Curators understand the tech; audiences understand the feeling. Everyone wins.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): LUNA belongs specifically in Bombay Beach because she is a permanent installation designed to serve the people who actually live there. She speaks to the landscape by becoming a "living archive" of it. While other projects disappear, LUNA mirrors the resilience of the community: she stays, she remembers, and she becomes part of the history of the Salton Sea.

fields.Project Description:
I'm building a 4-kayak storage rack with a salvaged solar panel roof positioned directly adjacent to the existing dock on the Salton Sea. This is functional sculpture—a permanent piece of community infrastructure that transforms e-waste into waterfront access.
Physical Form:
The structure stands approximately 6 feet tall with a 7-foot span. Four galvanized steel posts anchor into concrete blocks supporting wood and metal crossbeams that cradle kayaks in angled J-hook configurations. The kayaks rest vertically in padded cradles, protected from UV damage by a canopy roof constructed entirely from broken solar panels. 4 salvaged panels create a patchwork shade structure that generates 200-400 watts of residual power. The aluminum frames catch late afternoon light, casting geometric shadows across the dock. At night, small LED fixtures powered by the panels illuminate the waterline, creating a subtle beacon visible across the dark expanse of the Salton Sea.
Materiality & Texture:
Up close, the piece reveals its salvaged origins: weathered wood grain, industrial steel welds, the crystalline texture of cracked photovoltaic cells. Foam padding protects kayak hulls where they contact support beams. Educational signage explains the circular economy at work—how solar industry waste becomes shade becomes light becomes community resource.
Temporal Unfolding:
The project appears in stages during installation week. Visitors first encounter foundation work and frame assembly—a construction site becoming sculpture. By opening weekend, it's complete and operational: kayaks ready for use, solar panels charging batteries, evening dock lights glowing. The structure shifts throughout the day—morning shadows stretch west across weathered planks, midday heat radiates from metal components, sunset transforms the solar array into a glowing grid, night reveals its secondary function as gentle illumination.
Experiential Quality:
This isn't something to observe from a distance—it demands interaction. You approach, read the signage, understand the system, then make a choice: take a kayak or don't. The piece only completes itself through use. Launching from the dock at dawn, paddling into Salton Sea's eerie stillness, you become part of the work's function. The rack enables an experience impossible without it—intimate contact with cahuilla.
Sensory Experience:
The smell of creosote and salt. The heat radiating from solar panels at noon. The soft LED glow at twilight. The scrape of kayak hull against foam padding. Wind whistling through the frame's geometric spaces. This is regenerative infrastructure—austere, functional, unforgiving, and surprisingly gentle in its night-lighting function.
After the Biennale:
It stops being "art" and becomes simply what it is: the place where kayaks live, the thing that makes light, the reason you can paddle at dusk without fear of returning to darkness.
fields.Support Needed: I hope this is a practical art peace for the last judgment to the sea
fields.Abridged Project Text: Come paddle out from the regenerative kayak boat bay by the cahuilla sea. Made of recycled sustainable materials. Accessible near the Bombay Beach dock. Built for all who seek to enjoy sitting on the sea in peace.
fields.Technical Support: The project is designed for complete self-sufficiency. Once installed, it requires no external inputs—no electricity bills, no water, no staffing. The sun does the work.
fields.Audience Engagement:
All of the above.
Active Participation: The primary engagement is use. The piece enables an action. This could happen at noon or 3 am—the solar-powered dock lights make night launches possible, transforming the project into an invitation for nocturnal exploration.
Passive Observation: Others will experience it as sculptural presence—a geometric intervention against the sea horizon. The solar panel canopy reads as industrial collage, especially at angles where cracked cells catch light. Photographers will be drawn to the contrast: high-tech debris sheltering low-tech boats on a living sea.
Stumbling Discovery: At 3 am, someone walking the waterfront encounters soft LED light where none existed before. They investigate, read the signage, understand the system—trash transformed into infrastructure. Maybe they take a kayak into moonlit water. Maybe they just sit on the dock under solar-powered light, charging their phone from the panel system, contemplating the absurdity of pulling electricity from yesterday's sun to illuminate the sea.
Community Integration: Locals use it practically—consistent watercraft storage they can rely on. Over time, it becomes a meeting point, a landmark, the place you tell visitors to find you: "Meet me at the solar rack."
The piece doesn't demand engagement. It offers it. You can take what you need—shade, light, boat access, a metaphor for beauty in hostile conditions—or simply acknowledge it and move on- Judgment decreed.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Yes—literally and metaphorically.
The Salton Sea itself is judgment manifest. It's the physical result of humanity's hubris meeting a unforgiving/forgiving gods reality. This judged landscape disregarded as agricultural runoff manifested into a magnificent sea in return. Every kayak launch into these waters is an acknowledgment of that verdict.
Solar panels are emblems of our conflicted reckoning. We build them to atone for fossil fuel sins, then discard them when efficiency improves—creating new e-waste in our rush toward redemption. Using broken panels questions whether our "sustainable" solutions are genuine transformation or just prettier extraction. The judgment is still out.
This project asks: what do we do with what we've ruined? Do we abandon it, or find ways to live within the wreckage? The kayak rack chooses the latter—building infrastructure from industrial refuse, creating access to a poisoned sea, using relentless sun (the desert's harshest judgment) as power source. It's adaptation as defiance.
The Last Judgment traditionally separates the saved from the damned. Bombay Beach has already been judged—condemned, forgotten, left to cook. But people remain. They build, create, survive. This project is a small refusal to accept that verdict. It says: even here, even now, we can make things that work, that serve, that transform waste into light.
If judgment is final, we're all damned. If it's ongoing, there's still work to do.

fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
This is not a project that parachute in, perform "community engagement," photograph the locals, then disappear. Bombay Beach deserves infrastructure that stays—that works after the artists leave, after the grants run out, after the last judgment has been rendered on this strange desert experiment.
The Salton Sea is America's most visible environmental conundrum and people thrive here. They need to launch kayaks, explore their own landscape, access the water that simultaneously heals and transforms the minds of people thinking of this place. This project asks: what if the most radical artistic gesture is the one that becomes invisible through usefulness?
Questions the Work Asks:

Can art be genuinely regenerative, or is "sustainability" just aesthetic branding?
What happens when we build with trash instead of pristine materials—does it change the work's relationship to failure?
Who gets to use the Salton Sea, and what infrastructure makes that access possible?
If solar panels represent our attempt at redemption, what does it mean to build with broken ones?
Can a sculpture complete itself only through community use, becoming less "mine" over time?

The Process:
This is collaborative by necessity and design. I collected panels from working with Howard Electric (local solar/electrician) and electrical guidance—tapping local expertise rather than importing it. Installation will be a community build: volunteers from Bombay Beach learning basic construction, wiring, and weatherproofing techniques while creating something they'll actually use.
I thrive in renegade conditions. I've built in remote deserts, hostile climates, places where precision is impossible and adaptation is mandatory. Bombay Beach's extreme environment doesn't scare me—it focuses the work. When you know wind, heat, and alkaline soil will destroy anything fragile, you build accordingly. The constraints become the design.
My Practice & The Last Judgement:
"The world weeps, so I hang my head and sigh for the ignorant artist that tells me to smile." I make work in places already judged and found wanting. Forgotten coastlines, condemned buildings, marginal landscapes written off by capital and culture. The Last Judgement isn't coming—it already happened. We're living in the after.
The Salton Sea is ground zero for this reality. Judged worthless after some peoples resort dream died in media. Judged too toxic to save. Judged expendable as it shrinks into carcinogenic dust. But judgment isn't final if people refuse to accept the verdict. Building a kayak rack here is a small, stubborn insistence that this place still matters, that access to even a forgotten sea is worth fighting for.
I don't make optimistic work. I make functional work. There's a difference. Optimism is the ignorant artist telling you to smile. Function is building the thing that helps you survive without lying about conditions.
I hope this project will outlast me. That's the point.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
This project speaks that language of the BB. It's built from the infrastructure of another failed dream—solar panels that promised clean energy futures but cracked, degraded, became e-waste. Using them here acknowledges we're living in the debris field of multiple utopian visions: the resource paradise, the resort oasis, the renewable energy revolution . None delivered as promised. All left trash behind in stead of restoring the natural ecological wetland wonder of this valley.
The Salton Sea is judgment without redemption. It's dying slowly, yet it remains weirdly, stubbornly beautiful. Launching a kayak into those waters is an intimate reckoning with ruin—you can't ignore what's wrong, but you can still choose to engage. The rack makes that choice possible.
Bombay Beach's community has already mastered the art of building from wreckage. Every structure here is improvised, adapted, MacGyvered from whatever survives. This project honors that ethos while adding technical capacity—showing how salvaged solar tech can provide actual utility, not just aesthetic grit.
The Biennale brings temporary spectacle to permanent conditions. This piece inverts that relationship. It uses the Biennale platform to install something permanent, something the community can use long after the art tourists leave. It asks: what if the art event's legacy was functional infrastructure instead of Instagram memories?
This belongs here because here is where we practice living after the last judgment.

fields.Project Description:
This mixed media installation invites one participant at a time to crawl into its hollow interior and view patterns that demonstrate the varying water levels of the Salton Sea and historic Lake Cahuilla. The sculpture will stand at over eight feet tall, and will be made out of metal, paper mache, resin, real apples, and other materials.
The work confronts the viewer with the promise of agricultural abundance in a landscape now defined by salty, receding waters. The apples scattered at the base serve as a visceral allegory for the transformation of the landscape by humans; their biological decay mirrors the dying fish and the ecosystem in decline.
fields.Support Needed: I've completed several installations at this scale, and can provide references upon request.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Anthropogenesis is a surreal sculpture containing a dendrochronological record of anthropogenic transformation of the Salton Sea. The installation symbolizes the inception of transgression and our subsequent expulsion from paradise.
fields.Technical Support: Approximately 10x10 square feet of designated outdoor space. The installation can be made temporary or permanent at the preference of the community.
fields.Audience Engagement: The installation operates on a tension between attraction and repulsion. From a distance, the silhouette offers the illusion of a refuge, compelling visitors to approach. Proximity, however, reveals the decaying apples, repelling the viewer with the visceral reality of false promises. Those willing to investigate past the rot can crawl into the hollow trunk. Inside they can explore dendrochronological patterns to trace the story of the Sea.
fields.Theme Engagement: The installation operates on a tension between attraction and repulsion. From a distance, the silhouette offers the illusion of a refuge, compelling visitors to approach. Proximity reveals the decaying apples, repelling the viewer with the visceral reality of false agricultural promise. Those willing to investigate past the rot can crawl into the hollow trunk to explore the tree ring patterns that trace the story of the Sea.
fields.Risk & Safety: Intense wind gusts pose a potential safety risk. To mitigate this, the structure will be secured using rigging and anchoring. Additionally, solar lights will be installed to ensure visibility and prevent participants from tripping over the anchors and supports.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Projections indicate a 35% to 55% reduction in Colorado River flows over this century  (Udall and Overpeck 2016), with a 9.3% water loss for every degree Celsius of warming (Milly and Dunne 2020). The installation’s ledger of tree rings offers a necessary, visceral visualization of these abstract anxieties, grounding the short and long term human-related ecological degradation in a physical form that demands witness. 

Can we pivot from transgression to remediation? Or is the last judgment of this ecosystem written in the decaying fruit of our choices?
fields.Why BBB (Grant): Bombay Beach physically embodies the region's drastic shift from a midcentury beach destination to a landscape on the edge of ecological collapse. By the early 1980s, agricultural runoff spiked salinity levels, triggering a collapse in wildlife populations. Despite this, Bombay is a vibrant and inspiring place. Here we can find collective power to demand and initiate ecological restoration.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The initial structure of the tree has been created, but needs to be bolstered by additional steel and stabilized by resin for outdoor use. The water level data needs to be compiled from several scientific sources and aggregated into the internal visual.
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I spend significant time there seasonally

fields.Project Description:
The Last Judge is a 6.5-foot freestanding mosaic mirror installation shaped as an upright, cut-off oval form. Positioned directly within the landscape, the piece stands as a quiet, portal-like presence — neither doorway nor monument, but something in between.

The outer border is composed of clean rectangular mirror panels, creating a defined silhouette. Inside, fragmented mirror pieces form a fractured mosaic surface. As viewers approach, their reflection is multiplied and broken apart across shifting planes, then visually reassembled in unstable ways.

Installed outdoors in Bombay Beach, the piece will capture desert sky, wind, dust, and human movement simultaneously. Light will constantly alter the surface. The work is freestanding and structurally reinforced for extreme sun and wind conditions, mounted to a weighted base with concealed ballast.

The installation does not unfold through performance or scheduled activation. Instead, it exists continuously throughout the Biennale as an object that viewers encounter physically — something they walk toward, stand in front of, and see themselves inside.

The experience is immediate and spatial. The viewer becomes both subject and witness within the work.
fields.Support Needed: I’m committed to being fully present for installation, maintenance, and to contributing to the collaborative spirit of the Biennale. I value the experimental freedom of Bombay Beach and look forward to participating as both an artist and active member of the community.
fields.Abridged Project Text: A 6.5-foot freestanding mosaic mirror installation that fractures and reassembles the viewer’s reflection. Set against the desert landscape, The Last Judge transforms the idea of judgment into an intimate confrontation with the self — unstable, multiplied, and quietly revealing.
fields.Technical Support:
The installation is designed to function without electricity, projection, or amplified sound. It is self-contained and fully freestanding.

The piece will require outdoor placement on stable ground (soil or compacted surface). It will be mounted to a reinforced base platform and weighted with concealed sandbag ballast. Ground stakes may be used if installed on soil.

No power, lighting, internet, or additional infrastructure is required. Natural light is central to the work’s visual effect.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The work is encountered physically and directly. Viewers approach the structure and see themselves reflected, fragmented, and multiplied. Engagement is self-directed — there is no instruction or facilitation.

Most people will stand directly in front of the piece, examining how their image shifts and distorts across the fractured interior. The installation accommodates one to two viewers at a time, creating an intimate encounter.

Some may pass it casually during the day. Others may encounter it unexpectedly at night, when reflections become darker and more abstract. The work invites pause rather than spectacle — a quiet moment of recognition within a larger, chaotic environment.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Last Judge engages the theme of The Last Judgement by shifting the idea of judgment inward. Rather than presenting a dramatic or moral reckoning, the work frames judgment as an internal, ongoing process.

The viewer becomes both the one being judged and the one doing the judging. The fractured mirror surface reflects the instability of self-perception — how we measure, critique, and compare ourselves continuously.

In the landscape of Bombay Beach, the installation suggests that judgment is not a singular event, but a quiet and persistent internal act. The piece transforms the theme into a human-scale, participatory experience rooted in reflection rather than spectacle.
fields.Risk & Safety: The structure is freestanding and reinforced for stability in high desert wind conditions. It will be weighted with concealed ballast and secured to prevent tipping. Shatter-resistant acrylic mirror will be used to eliminate glass breakage risk. No fire, excavation, vehicles, or hazardous materials are involved.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No. The Last Judge is a newly conceived work that has not been previously exhibited, performed, or installed. It is being developed specifically for premiere at the 2026 Bombay Beach Biennale.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create The Last Judge now because I am increasingly aware of how internalized judgment shapes the way we move through the world. We often imagine judgment as something external — societal, religious, institutional — but in reality, it is frequently self-generated and constant. This piece asks: What happens when the act of judgment becomes visible? What does it feel like to stand directly in front of yourself, fragmented and multiplied?

Working in Bombay Beach has sharpened these questions for me. The landscape is stark, exposed, and unpolished. There is very little separation between environment and experience. The Salton Sea carries its own history of collapse, reinvention, spectacle, and survival. In that context, The Last Judge feels less like an object and more like a presence — something that quietly holds reflection within a place already charged with transformation.

I am drawn to Bombay Beach because of the freedom it offers artists to experiment outside conventional institutional frameworks. There is space here to test ideas materially and conceptually without overproduction. That freedom feels urgent right now. Rather than creating something monumental or theatrical, I want to create something structurally simple but psychologically direct.

The process of building this work will involve experimentation with material fragmentation, structural reinforcement, and outdoor durability. I plan to fabricate the piece myself, refining the mosaic interior through trial and adjustment to achieve the right balance between distortion and legibility. While the installation itself is not collaborative in execution, its meaning depends entirely on public interaction. Each viewer completes the work by standing within it.

The Last Judge engages The Last Judgement theme not as a final reckoning, but as a continuous internal state. In Bombay Beach — a place shaped by exposure, decay, creativity, and reinvention — the idea of judgment feels layered. This work asks whether judgment is something imposed from above, or something we carry within.

I see this project as part of a developing body of site-responsive work rooted in reflection, environment, and psychological space. It is a piece that feels appropriate for this place and this moment — restrained, experimental, and open-ended.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The Last Judge belongs in Bombay Beach because this landscape does not allow for distance or illusion. The Salton Sea holds histories of ambition, collapse, reinvention, and persistence. It is a place where spectacle and fragility coexist openly. In that environment, a mirror becomes more than a reflective surface — it becomes a way of holding what is already present.

Bombay Beach is uniquely suited to work that feels exposed and unpolished. The Biennale embraces experimentation, risk, and site-responsiveness over institutional polish. This piece relies on direct encounter with environment: harsh sun altering reflection, wind shifting perception, dust settling across mirrored surfaces. The desert will actively participate in shaping the work.

Alongside the Salton Sea — a body of water defined by both human intervention and natural consequence — the idea of judgment feels layered. The landscape itself carries narratives of critique, abandonment, and resilience. The Last Judge does not attempt to comment explicitly on that history, but it resonates with the atmosphere of exposure and reckoning that the place embodies.

At the Biennale, where artworks exist outside traditional gallery containment, this installation can live fully in the open — vulnerable to light, weather, and public interaction — just as the community itself exists in a state of continual adaptation.

fields.Project Description: The Xenolex Vision Garden will be a collection of posters that teach visitors to read an alternative writing system called the Xenolex (created by artist Jade Ellipsis).
fields.Abridged Project Text: Educational Posters written in alien script.
fields.Technical Support: I’m imagining taking up about 20 square meters of floor space, preferably outside.
fields.Audience Engagement: People will see it and immediately recognize that the writing carries meaning. I will invite onlookers to learn, and I will teach them how to read if they take me up on the offer.
fields.Theme Engagement: The Last Judgement reveals what’s hidden. The Vision Garden reveals the hidden machinery of reading — pattern recognition you’ve run your whole literate life without seeing.
fields.Risk & Safety: None of the listed risks apply to my project, although I will be screening potential students for past history with dissociative mental health conditions in an abundance of caution. Unlearning how to read can be de-stabilizing emotionally.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): All poster content will be ideated, designed, and printed newly specifically for the Bienalle.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): What does it mean to create meaning? Why hasn’t writing received a major upgrade since the enlightenment? Time for some DLC?
fields.Why BBB (Grant): Bombay Beach is a place where familiar structures are visibly coming apart — the sea receding, the town half-abandoned, the foundations of western development exposed as contingent rather than permanent. The Vision Garden does the same thing to literacy. It takes the most familiar structure you have — the writing system you learned as a child — and makes its contingency visible. A place where the Salton Sea shoreline keeps revealing what was hidden underneath is the right place for an installation that reveals what’s hidden underneath reading.

fields.Project Description:
Porous is a subliminal audio installation distributed across Bombay Beach. It consists of multiple small, solar-powered objects embedded into the town’s circulation patterns during the Biennale, especially the in-between spaces, where people move in groups and attention stays soft.

Each unit will be grafted onto the negative space of one of the many objects in Bombay Beach you would normally ignore. A small boulder, concrete slab, pile of rubble, rusted car, etc. Porous protrudes from these objects as a distributed hyper-site-specific 3D printed parasitic glitch, using the world as infrastructure to hide in plain sight.

Inside each unit is a microphone, a microcontroller, a small speaker, and onboard solar power with battery storage. The system listens continuously for the acoustic conditions that allow audio to hide under ambient sound. When the environment provides enough cover (e.g., ambient sound is loud enough), the unit plays a short looped song just loud enough to sit below conscious perception. The song is never meant to be clearly heard. It is meant to be absorbed.

What it carries is the work. A subliminal earworm. A deliberately catchy jingle with lyrics about being “good,” “right,” “responsible,” “kind,” “safe.” The content is intentionally unambiguous at face value. That obvious goodness is the provocation. Porous implants something “good” without agency. It stages the ethical contradiction directly: moral messaging delivered through manipulation.

This is a physical model of what we call the intraface. Contemporary interfaces constantly reshape behavior while presenting themselves as neutral, seamless, and helpful. Growth hacking, persuasion design, and psychological nudging arrive as convenience. Porous mirrors that dynamic in the public landscape. The mechanism is concealed. The influence is ambient. The result is social.

The piece unfolds over time through repetition. Passersby do not stop and “view” it. They walk past it mid-conversation. The injection is brief and consistent, tuned to be just long enough to stick. Later, it surfaces without a source. Someone hums it. Someone else already knows it. A moral refrain spreads through the Biennale like a rumor that pretends it came from inside your own head.

We will coordinate with BBB to place units where foot traffic is dense and attention is porous, using the Biennale’s movement as the distribution channel. Porous is built for Bombay Beach’s DIY, off-grid ecology. It treats the town as carrier, and the audience as medium.
fields.Support Needed: We're very thankful to have found such an aligned community, and hope to contribute our work to help make this year's BBB even more ... memorable ... like you can't get the earworm out of your head after you leave, type of memorable ;)
fields.Abridged Project Text: Porous subliminally injects earworms with “morally correct” lyrics into the participants of BBB. Subtly perverted everyday objects will be distributed across the city. When the sound around it is loud enough, each device plays a song just below perception. We’ll all be singing the same unknown song.
fields.Technical Support:
None. Each Porous unit is fully self-contained and solar-powered with onboard battery storage.

The main support we need is mapping out the opportune locations, the in-between spaces.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Engagement happens through misrecognition. People do not “hear a song.” They absorb a trace. Later it returns as humming, a lyric fragment, a shared chorus that feels self-authored.

Porous works best when people are moving. Groups walking between pieces and events. Conversations overlapping with wind, generators, music bleed, laughter, and the town’s constant improvisation. The earworm lands under that texture. Nobody points to it. Nobody asks where it came from. It spreads anyway.

At BBB, that spread becomes the interaction. The work completes itself when the audience becomes the playback device and the moral jingle becomes social. A weird kind of communion. A catchy virtue that you did not choose.

A smaller set of viewers will get suspicious and start scanning the environment, trying to locate the leak, but the core is not visual discovery. The core is coercive goodness delivered beneath awareness, then reappearing publicly as if it belonged to everyone.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Last Judgement is a story about morality as verdict. Porous turns verdict into a low-grade infection.

Instead of a tribunal, a subliminal earworm. Instead of a gavel, a catchy moral refrain that shows up in your mouth without permission. It asks what judgement looks like when it arrives as “help,” when virtue becomes a delivery system, and when power performs goodness so convincingly that refusal feels irrational.

The content is deliberately “right.” That is what makes it dangerous. If the message is moral, does that absolve the method. If the method is coercive, what does that do to the morality being preached. Porous makes that contradiction embodied and unavoidable.

Bombay Beach is already a landscape of consequence. Collapse that still throws a party. A town hosting its own apocalypse carnival beside an inland mistake that refuses to die. In that context, a moral earworm distributed through infrastructure reads like a local theology of systems. Dirty redemption. DIY judgement. Forgiveness optional.
fields.Risk & Safety: No risks or safety concerns.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No. This work will premiere at BBB 2026. We started our prototype last year in Bombay Beach, and it feels right to finish, and premiere it here as well :)
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We are k0j0 (Koi Ren and Joey Verbeke), a research-based new media art duo, and martians / seasonal residents of Bombay Beach since 2025. Our practice builds intrafaces: objects that look familiar enough to trust, then behave in ways that reveal what interfaces hide. We are interested in unconscious friction, the space where systems influence you before you notice.

Porous is a direct response to moralized technology. Everywhere, power is dressed as virtue. Safety language, responsible design, ethical UX. The words are “good” while the mechanisms remain coercive, optimized, and unaccountable. We wanted a work that makes that condition felt in the body without relying on explanation.

Porous uses subliminal earworms as its medium. It injects a moral jingle under perception, then lets it surface later as if it came from you. This is the provocation: implanting something “good” without agency. The work weaponizes obvious morality to expose the problem of morality as an interface layer. Who gets to define the virtues. Who gets to distribute them. Who gets to do it invisibly.

We are not interested in the white-box version of this idea. A clean room with a labeled speaker and a wall text would neutralize the mechanism. Bombay Beach is the opposite environment: off-grid, roaming, messy, collective. Attention is fractured. Sound bleeds. People move between works as a crowd. That ecology lets Porous function as infrastructure rather than a performance, and it lets the earworm behave like a rumor.

We have a functional prototype that proves the interaction loop. For BBB we are scaling to multiple solar-powered units and developing a form language that belongs in the town’s texture of improvised fixtures, decay, repair, and imagination. We will collaborate with BBB on placement, focusing on transitional routes where groups pass mid-conversation.
 
Porous is asking a simple, ugly question: if the message is moral, is manipulation acceptable. If manipulation is unacceptable, why do we accept it when it arrives as “care.” We want the Biennale to become the carrier wave for that question.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
BBB is built from the in-between. The walk to the next thing. The beach edge. The dust. The bleed. The collective movement. Porous uses that as its interface.

Bombay Beach supports camouflage. The town’s landscape already contains civic fragments, broken fixtures, piles of rubble, and DIY repairs. The small glitched sculptures grafted to these objects can disappear into that texture, then reappear as an effect in your head.

The Salton Sea sits there like an ongoing verdict authored by infrastructure. Porous speaks in the same register: morality routed through systems, delivered without consent, then internalized as if it were yours. This belongs at BBB because BBB is not about pristine viewing. It is about contamination, participation, and art that behaves like weather.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): We have a working prototype and a validated interaction loop for listening and masked playback. The piece is beyond concept stage.  Next is scaling and desert-hardening: building 12 self-contained solar-powered units, finalizing the language of the hyper-

fields.Project Description:
The Self-Attunement Structures (SAS)
A ritual-architectural journey through awareness, play, and return.
Inviting participants to feel first. Think later.
7-part, immersive installation designed as a guided pathway through the body as home. Drawing from somatics, yoga, performance/mindfulness practices, mythology & philosophy – SAS prioritizes embodiment over explanation. 
In dialogue w/ The Last Judgement, SAS reframes judgment as an internal encounter: a self-directed witnessing. Become both witness and authority to your own experience. Part yoga studio-ritual playground-immersive artwork, SAS invites folks to feel seen first by themselves, and then ..maybe.. by others.
Experiential Sequence
1️⃣ 👁️‍🗨️ The Self-Witnessing Billboard 🪧
It reads: “You Are Watching You.”
A PSA reminding us that in every choice and movement, we are already in relationship with ourselves. Awareness begins here. The first witness is you. The first subject is also you.
2️⃣ 🛣️ The Hopscotch (pleasure) Path ✨
Choosing Play
Movement powered by pleasure, not martyrdom. Efficiency is overrated.
“It’s your responsibility to have fun.” Life is a game - play wisely.
3️⃣ ⛩️ The Threshold  ❤️‍🔥 Reincarnate Now!
A glowing arch marks a crossing point. Gather energy. Release the old. Step into becoming. Drawing from Michael Chekhov’s concept of the threshold as a shift in internal and external state. What’s one more self-reincarnation?
4️⃣ 🌿 The Body is the 👤 🏡 Home Dome 🌐 (The Inner Commons)
A breathable dome for solo or shared experience. 
Inside: movement, rest, breath, nervous-system practices, and relational presence - simple, non-heady rituals. This space reinforces a simple truth: the body is the only home we fully inhabit.
Explore yoga, movement, rest, breathwork, nervous-system drills, interoception, proprioception, and reflections on intimacy &relational presence. 
5️⃣ ☎️ Call Your Soul
“Get Your Soul on the Horn.”
A vintage telephone invites messages to oneself, the divine, the dead, or the inanimate. Speaking aloud becomes an act of self-witnessing. Messages may be recorded - or not - through a collaborative, non-extractive process. 
6️⃣ 👣 Reverse Ascension 
Participants exit by retracing their steps. You leave the same way you entered …slightly changed.
7️⃣ 🌎 The Outer Commons 🫧👥
Re-entry. Integration is light and optional. Participants return to Bombay Beach and beyond, carrying what they’ve felt back into the collective nervous system
SAS = intentional architecture for embodied self-relation. It is not about fixing/optimizing the self, but about awareness, choice, and relational presence. 
Informed by experiences of displacement and impermanence, the project treats identity as something formed through interaction - with our own bodies -oriented to environments - then to others.
Perhaps we are not only social sculptures, but mobile bodily homes.
An invitation to clean house, tend the interior, and return to the world with greater dignity and care.
fields.Support Needed: it would be cool if there was a structure we could use and a twinkle in many eyes -- also I'd love to teach some yoga classes, neurosomatic drills, collaborative clown-nervous-system performances where we interact with the channel of the audience, as well as play some live music and storytelling. I guess I'll feel it out when I get there. :) Thanks for witnessing and reviewing.
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Self-Attunement Structures (SAS) is a seven-part ritual disguised as play: a journey through the body as home. Drawing from somatics, yoga, performance, and myth. A DIY self-encounter. Part playground, part confessional. Where participants feel first, think later, and become their own witness.
fields.Technical Support:
The Self-Attunement Structures (SAS) engages deeply w/ The Last Judgement, but reframes it from an external verdict → into an intimate, embodied encounter.

Rather than judgment delivered by a god, institution, or authority, SAS asks: Who is watching whom? Where do we hold verdicts against ourselves - quietly, habitually, unconsciously? 

Judgement’s ongoing… so put my thing down, flip it and reverse it. Play with your own DIY reckoning, blessing, and self-witnessing ritual! Because, YOU are the one being seen and the one who sees.

The project borrows the architecture of judgment - billboards, thresholds, confessionals, exits - but subverts their function. Instead of punishment or salvation, you encounter choice, sensation, and presence. The “verdict” is not spoken aloud, but felt… or not felt …in the nervous system. Catharsis doesn’t need to be found either.

Nestled into Bombay Beach’s porous social fabric and vast desert contextL: SAS treats The Last Judgement as something that happens repeatedly throughout life: in moments of transition, self-reinvention, collapse, repair, and return. Self/Social/Spiritual Judgment is no longer final.. it’s just one of those passing things, man.

Absurdly, tenderly, SAS whispers: 

What if the apocalypse is just a moment of noticing? 

In this sense, SAS engages The Last Judgement not as an ending, but as a practice. A chance to pause, feel, clean house internally, and re-enter the world slightly more inside oneself than before.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The Self-Attunement Structures (SAS) are something people *enter*, *move through*, and sometimes *stumble into at 3 a.m.* SAS is designed for multiple modes of engagement: intentional participation, casual wandering, quiet solo use, and collective experience. Some folks will arrive curious and deliberate - others may encounter it accidentally, drawn in by a sign, a glow, or a moment of pause in the desert.

Visitors engage with SAS as a processional journey rather than a fixed spectacle. They move through a sequence of structures that invite slowing down, orienting, playing, crossing thresholds, resting, and returning. Engagement can be as light or as deep as the participant chooses. Nothing is mandatory. People can hop, sit, listen, speak, stretch, or simply pass through.

At times, SAS functions like an immersive artwork you walk through. At other times, it becomes a yoga studio, a nervous-system reset zone, a confessional, or a social commons. Some participants may attend guided sessions - yoga, somatic practices, or conversations.

The work is intentionally porous. It supports drop-ins, lingering, repetition, and return visits. Someone might pass through quickly one day, then come back later to spend an hour inside the dome, or to leave a message on the telephone, or to sit quietly with their body.

Ultimately, SAS meets people where they are & invites participation without performance, presence without explanation, and engagement that feels human, flexible, and alive.
