fields.Risk & Safety:
SAS does **not** involve fire, ladders (well, maybe a ladder), vehicles, boats, drones, excavation, or digging - unless building a structure is needed. Potential risks are limited to dust exposure and light physical movement.

Risk mitigation includes: using an existing, stable structure, HEPA air filtration and a HEPA vacuum I already own, dust containment practices, potential PPE (respirator masks, gloves) during setup/cleaning, clear pathways; non-slip ground coverings; and optional, low-impact movement with alternatives always offered.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create The Self-Attunement Structures now, because we’re living in a time marked by burnout, overuse of tech + chasing dopamine loops -leading to increasing levels of dissociation and separation.

By strengthening self-attunement and embodiment at the individual level, SAS supports a healthier human ecosystem overall, where the sum = greater than its parts.

SAS prioritizes accessible, daily embodiment practices to improve quality of life -without asking people to think harder, optimize themselves, or carry more cognitive load.

SAS is built from those practical, survival-tested skills - not theory alone.
My approach comes from my lived necessity. 

After being displaced the LA Eaton wildfire last year & suddenly losing my home/belongings to environmental toxicity + all of my other identity markers (job/business/career, community, relationship, etc.), I’ve moved through dozens + dozens of temporary living situations -while attempting to rebuild safety, identity, and health in real time. Without consistent access to healthcare, I relied on holistic approaches and neurosomatics/nervous-system practices to de-stress, orient, and stay present amid constant shifting.

After all the rando beds I slept in, and water glasses I knocked over in the middle of the night ...I had a wholly original thought:

Wherever I go, there I am. 

The body is the only home we will ever inhabit.

The depths of joy we can reach are directly proportional to the depths we’re willing to connect with ourselves and others. SAS is my attempt to create a playful, low-pressure space where that connection can happen through the body rather than through explanation.

SAS is a non-dogmatic, non-religious installation/studio that invites people to pause, feel, and re-enter the world with more clarity and relational presence. Whether participants have healthy/strained relationships with Self, god, religion, community, spirituality, or nature, SAS offers a space to explore those relationships and to contemplate reverence and devotion in their own way.

Much of philosophy, religion, and contemporary art discourse leans heavily toward conceptual inquiry and examination. I value this deeply, but cognition alone can become dissociating (aka intellectual disassociation), adding insight, but often spinning our wheels on dopamine loops without improving our quality of life day-to-day. 

SAS shifts the inquiry from “What does this mean?” to “What'shappening in my body right now?” 

By inviting slowing down, self-exploration, & can encourage more oxytocin flow (which female nervous systems are motivated by.. as opposed to male nervous systems being fueled more by dopamine).

 Or, you can just go + hang cuz the vibe's cool.

At Bombay Beach, where systems are improvised and collaboration is constant tending to the individual body becomes a way of tending to the collective nervous system. The Self-Attunement Structures support a healthier ecosystem by strengthening embodiment at the personal level, allowing people to return to the world, hopefully, with more clarity and felt sense of safety, belonging and dignity.

And if not.
Maybe try again tomorrow?
I don’t know. I’m not a wizard.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is known for its porous boundaries, experimental culture, and encouragement of questioning hierarchy and social conditioning. While this openness is a strength, it also means people are constantly negotiating where they end and the world begins.

Within the deeply woven, bootstrapped community of Bombay Beach, it feels especially valuable to carve out a space dedicated to tending to the individual body and inner life - whether experienced alone or with others. This supports healthier relationships with ourselves, clearer consent, and more sustainable creative exchange within an intensely relational environment.

Self-Attunement is not a luxury here - it’s a practical skill.

How do we tend to ourselves, and each other, when the world keeps changing? 

What does “home” mean when it can’t be fixed in place?

Permanence is an illusion - SAS is about creating usable presence.

Rather than resisting impermanence, The Self Attunement Structures encourage human practices to continue living inside it. SAS offers a temporary architecture to further learn how to return to oneself… this is one of life’s most valuable lessons to carry with us when the structure disappears.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): This project is in **active development and early execution**.  The conceptual framework, experiential sequence, and core structures of *The Self-Attunement Structures* are fully formed, informed by prior installations, teaching experience, and lived prac
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): "Yes, I live and work in this region"

fields.Project Description:
I propose a walk-through angelic archway. Using a standard garden arch as a structural armature, we will expand its width and volume using concrete and fiberglass, sculpting amorphous, wing-like forms that appear to grow outward and upward. These forms will be encrusted with barnacles collected from the Salton Sea, along with salt and sediment textures.

Barnacles arrived in the Sea when military seaplanes practicing maneuvers inadvertently carried a few stragglers from the ocean. What began as an accidental introduction flourished into billions, permanently reshaping the ecosystem. This descent parallels the fallen angels described in the Book of Enoch—beings who descend and irrevocably alter the earthly order.

Toy planes will be crushed into the top of the archway, referencing the mechanized vectors of this ecological intervention.
fields.Abridged Project Text: An angelic archway rises from the Salton Sea, thickened into wing-like forms and encrusted in barnacles. Walk beneath a gate grown from fallen life, where the accidental descent of sealife mirrors angels’ fall.
fields.Technical Support: We require an outdoor site, preferably along the beach. We were talking with Grant about possibly having this near the canyon as an opening.
fields.Audience Engagement: Visitors will move through the archway, observing its textures and cracks during the day. By night, a soft internal glow will illuminate the inner curve of the arch, creating a quiet moon of light to walk through.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The barnacles covering the arch were not meant to be here. Introduced during wartime aviation practices, they reshaped the Sea’s ecology. Their presence is an unintended consequence of technological and militarized intervention.

In the Book of Enoch, fallen angels descend and alter the world irreversibly. They are bound and held until judgment. The consequences of their descent echo across generations.

By constructing a heavenly gate out these barnacles, the piece suggests that future apocalypse is not sudden change but accumulated, sometimes accidental, action.
fields.Risk & Safety: The archway would be secured with a buried concrete base.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I am a Los Angeles–based sculptor with a background in natural building, art fabrication, and construction.  My practice adopts nature’s own lens—its rhythms, its folding and unfolding—as a means of understanding our lives and responsibilities differently. After attending the biennale for a few years, I wanted to observe and learn from the community and artists. The Last Judgement feels like an appropriate opportunity to contribute. 

At the Salton Sea, barnacles were accidentally introduced by military seaplanes. They proliferated, reshaped the ecosystem, and eventually died off, leaving behind a dense layer of shells and sediment along the shoreline. This proposal highlights this unintended, cumulative consequence of human intervention by making an archway encrusted with these barnacles.

The archway will be built on-site at Bombay Beach, allowing for a collaborative process with interested residents and volunteers. Participants will help collect and prepare barnacles, mix and apply concrete, and adorn the arch. The archway functions as both portal and monument.

Through this work, I ask: How do accidental interventions shape ecosystems and persist as material memory? This project fits within my broader practice of working with site-specific, materially charged interventions that merge ecological history with myth, inviting play and contemplation.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Last time I visited the Salton Sea, my foot got stuck in the mud. I fell on all four and clambered on all this bony surface to save myself. I realized that around me stretched a wide field of barnacles. I was immersed and moved by the material presence of these tiny creatures, which over time created landforms and became infrastructure.

I wondered where they came from. I learned that Balanus amphitrite first appeared in the Salton Sea around 1943–1944, transported from San Diego during Naval exercises. By late 1944, barnacles seemed to outnumber all other forms of life. In their proliferation and die-off, they literally built up the land.

The archway responds to this layered history, a symbolic gravesite for these barnacle “souls” and a meditation on unintended ecological change.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I am in the stage of gathering materials. I have experimented with the metal and concrete and attaching barnacles to it.
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I am visiting from elsewhere

fields.Project Description:
The X-Zoetrope is a 9-foot tall, vertical-axis H-Rotor turbine constructed from industrial-grade PVC and lightweight polycarb. It stands as a skeletal, architectural monument—a "crossroads" (X) of industrial utility and early cinematic wonder. Unlike traditional horizontal windmills, the H-Rotor design allows the installation to function as a giant, wind-powered zoetrope. The vertical slats serve as a visual "shutter," which, when rotated, reveals a persistent animation centered on the "X" theme.

By day, the turbine is a kinetic sculpture reacting to the erratic desert winds. The white PVC armature contrasts sharply with the salt-crusted earth, emitting a low-frequency hum—a mechanical "om"—as wind passes through the hollow tubes. At night, it transforms into a digital-kinetic beacon. A Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W controls high-density WS2812B LED strips, delivering stroboscopic pulses of light. This strobing effect "freezes" the animation frames on the spinning slats, creating the illusion of a floating, 3D holographic "X" dancing within the turbine’s diameter.

The project unfolds in three layers:

Passive Interaction: The turbine spins at the whim of the desert winds, acting as a weather-vane for the local climate.

Active Participation: Guests manually engage with the turbine, physically "cranking" the animation to control the speed and intensity of the visual narrative.

Scheduled Rituals: During specific windows, the installation becomes a centerpiece for curated multimedia performances. Laser projections interact with the spinning slats to project complex interference patterns into the surrounding playa.
fields.Support Needed: Hope to see you in the desert
fields.Abridged Project Text: The X-Zoetrope is a PVC H-Rotor turbine merging kinetic engineering with early animation. Driven by wind or guests, its slats create a shutter effect, while Pi-controlled LEDs provide stroboscopic light. This hybrid transforms the "X" theme into a glowing, desert-activated communal ritual.
fields.Technical Support: The project is largely self-contained. For Tier 1, we provide our own 12V battery systems (manually swapped). Tier 2/3 would benefit from a 110V drop or solar array for high-powered laser and audio integration. We require an outdoor playa location with good wind exposure. To feature the "Hall of Mirrors" collection (Tier 3), we need a secure, dust-protected indoor space or shipping container nearby for delicate works. We provide our own PA system for performance windows but will coordinate with neighbors regarding sound levels.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The X-Zoetrope is designed for 24-hour engagement, transitioning from a wind-driven sculpture by day to an intoxicating optical illusion by night. At 3:00 AM, it serves as a glowing beacon on the playa, drawing in wanderers with its rhythmic pulses of light.

Engagement is primarily tactile and participatory. While many will "watch" the animation from a distance, the core of the experience is "participation as power." We invite guests to step up to the turbine and manually spin the H-Rotor. This physical act puts the viewer in control of the "shutter speed" of the animation; by changing the rotation speed, they change the frame rate of the story being told. It is a communal ritual where the audience becomes the engine. Additionally, the interior space of the turbine—when slowed—invites people to move around it, exploring the relationship between the physical PVC slats and the ghostly Persistence of Vision (POV) effects they produce.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The X-Zoetrope engages with "The Last Judgement" through the lens of ecological accountability and the "Persistence of Vision" as a metaphor for historical witness. In many traditions, the Last Judgement is a moment of total clarity where the "veil is lifted." Our installation mirrors this by using the shutter effect of the turbine to reveal a hidden, glowing animation that is otherwise invisible to the naked eye.

The "X" stands as a mark of the crossroads—the moment of decision. As the turbine spins, driven by the volatile winds of the Salton Sea, it acts as a literal meter of environmental forces. It asks the viewer: Are we merely spinning in circles, or are we powering a vision of the future? The stroboscopic "freezing" of the image represents the attempt to capture a singular moment of truth amidst the chaos of the "end times." It is an absurd, beautiful mechanical prayer at the edge of the world, acknowledging the precariousness of our environment while celebrating the human drive to create light in the darkness.
fields.Risk & Safety: The installation uses a 9ft rotating structure and rebar anchoring. Mitigation: 1. Structural: High-tensile guy wires and 3ft rebar anchors prevent tipping in high winds. 2. Kinetic: The vertical H-Rotor design is safer than horizontal blades; all parts are rounded PVC with no sharp edges. 3. Electrical: Raspberry Pi and battery components are in IP65-rated weatherproof enclosures to protect against dust and moisture. 4. Visibility: The LED system and glow-paint ensure the structure is highly visible at night for pedestrian safety.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Never, this would be a new work premier.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We want to create the X-Zoetrope now because we stand at a precipice—a "Last Judgement" where our relationship with the environment must shift from extraction to collaboration. In a world increasingly mediated by screens, we feel an urgent need to return to the mechanical roots of vision. Why now? because the wind at Bombay Beach is telling a story that we are failing to hear. This work is an inquiry: Can we build machines that don't just consume energy, but visualize the invisible forces that surround us? Can industrial waste (PVC) be transmuted into a spiritual beacon?

Our process is defined by "renegade engineering." The Collective operates as a hive mind, drawing from our diverse backgrounds in the Venice Beach Hall of Mirrors and the San Francisco Video Riot. We will prototype the H-Rotor physics in our Venice studio, but the work is not "finished" until it reaches the Salton Sea. We view the installation as a living laboratory. We envision a process of radical experimentation where the "animation" on the zoetrope slats is iterated nightly based on the day's wind patterns.

Collaboration is central to our methodology. The physical construction is a communal event, and the operation of the piece requires the audience. It is not a static sculpture; it is a dormant instrument that requires human or elemental interaction to sing. By bringing together coders, welders, and visual artists, we are asking if a group of strangers can synchronize their efforts to create a "Persistence of Vision" that outlasts the festival itself. We are not just building a windmill; we are building a communal engine for the imagination.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The X-Zoetrope cannot exist in a white-cube gallery; it would be lifeless. It requires the relentless, specific wind of the Salton Sea to function. It belongs at the Biennale because Bombay Beach itself acts as a kind of "zoetrope"—a place where history spins in a loop of boom, bust, and beautiful decay.

The installation speaks directly to the landscape: the PVC construction mirrors the industrial debris often found on the shoreline, but repurposes it into a generator of light. It speaks to the community's resilience; like the locals who have weathered floods and salinity, our turbine takes the harsh elements and uses them to power its art. The Biennale’s ethos of "decay as a medium" and radical self-expression is the only soil where this project can take root. Nowhere else allows for this specific collision of high-tech coding and raw, elemental survival. The X-Zoetrope is a site-specific dialogue with the horizon line of the Salton Sea, transforming the empty desert night into a canvas for our collective dreams.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Early conception
fields.Additional Artists: Velvate Brale, Snow Leopard, Gold Museum, DJ Cypod, VJ Colours

fields.Project Description:
R1elic is both object and immersive environment: a temporary sanctuary that makes the implicit logic of American zoning visible. We propose to transform the abandoned house at the corner of Avenue F and 4th Street into a monochrome yellow relic. The project will begin with a ceremonial cleansing of the property, inside and out. Our team will carefully clear debris, remove flaking paint, and prepare the structure for its transformation. This act of care is both practical and ritualistic: a preparation of the site for visitation, contemplation, and judgment.

During this process, which will be equal parts labor and liturgy, we will work in the customary color of our informal order, the Church of Now, signaling to passersby that the house is receiving its Last Rites. If neighbors are receptive, the work will unfold to a soundtrack of techno, pop, and house hymnals. 

Once the cleansing is finalized, we will rest and receive the necessary blessing from our High Priestess, Jessica Eriksen, before proceeding to the next sacrament: the repainting. The repainting is a critical step meant both as a visual experience and didactic opportunity. For this particular property, it is essential, according to our customs, that we paint the house yellow to visibly render its R1 single family zoning designation by Imperial County. Yellow is the traditional color used by the American Planning Association to designate low-density, residential use. Painting the home entirely in yellow will provide a new stimulus for the town and interact with the blues, yellows, pinks, and oranges of sunrise and sunset. We imagine the currently blown out windows will be replaced with translucent yellow acrylic, which will fill the interior with golden hues during the day (like a church with stained glass) and emit a warm glow at night (off-grid lighting strategy pending!) 

Inside, we will construct the altar to hold our relic: a small scale model of the house shrouded in translucent yellow fabric. In this sanctuary, we will invite those who curse single family homes and those who worship them. We will invite them to make their prayers for the future society they wish to see. 

Accompanying this piece will be the Imperial County Land Use map of Bombay Beach and text meant to prime meditation on this form of living. This material will be printed on vinyl mesh to withstand the elements and last long term, as the piece is designed to remain post-Biennale. 

In totality, R1elic is meant to be both visually arresting on the outside and serene and contemplative on the inside. The aim is for the piece and its creation process to spark conversations with residents and Biennale attendees. Why yellow? Does so much of the town need to be zoned this way? What else would they like to build here that the county currently doesn’t allow? Or do they wish to protect this architectural and social form?
fields.Support Needed: The project was developed in consultation with Jessica Eriksen and Brady Brim-DeForest. There are several ways to encounter R1elic. It can be read as a nerdy, researched reflection on American zoning and the single-family home, using color and ritual to surface questions about how we organize land and social life. Or it can also be encountered simply as a visual gesture: a glowing yellow house near the Salton Sea that shifts in tone with the desert light and invites curiosity from anyone who passes by. At its core, the project is also genuinely spiritual.
fields.Abridged Project Text: R1elic asks whether the single family home and the associated R1 zoning apparatus that supports it is either 1) an object of sacred reverence, 2) an artifact that can now be appreciated from a distance, or 3) something we need to abolish entirely to achieve urban absolution?
fields.Technical Support:
The piece and project is off-grid and functions without electricity, sound, projection, etc. We do intend to investigate more options for lighting the inside at night, but have not built a concept around this happening. We welcome any input and advice on how to animate or light the project, particularly at night! 

The largest challenge of the piece is the needed on-site time and labor. In a sense, this is a mini-construction project occurring at the scale of an entire house. We will be hauling away trash, scraping off old paint, screwing in new windows, and more. As such, we are planning to have a team of at least three, and have built a grant proposal and budget around not only materials, but the support for the team to be on site 12 hours a day for 3-4 days. Anticipating long days of physical labor, it is also important that we stay near the site and are planning on renting an RV and parking it at the Fountain of Youth RV park near Bombay Beach, allowing for us to keep a steady construction and install pace.
fields.Audience Engagement:
We imagine the vast majority, if not all, of the artwork’s execution to occur in Bombay Beach itself, on display for all to see in the days leading up to the Biennale. Currently, we are planning for a 3 day creation process, beginning two days before the biennale and culminating on the first day of the Biennale. Thus, as the festivities begin, we will be putting the finishing touches on the piece. 

This execution schedule is purposely intended to allow people to watch us paint (read: ritually cleanse) the home and redecorate its interior, and ask us questions while doing so. We also selected, in consultation with the property owners, a home that is on the high visibility corner lot and vacant, thus able to attract and receive visitors at all times of day and night.
fields.Theme Engagement:
R1elic invites visitors to render their own last judgment on the single-family home and the zoning regime that sustains it. If we are separating the wheat from the chafe, in which bucket do we put the vast swaths of bungalows, cottages, McMansions, and cookie cutter homes that dot our landscape? In evaluating the single family home, we imagine that viewers might also bring up their own questions regarding the nuclear family. The clerics (read: artistic team) will do our best to approach these questions socratically. Perhaps we’ll have a ceremony on the last day to declare our judgment on this relic of the 20th century. 

On the physical plane, the vibrant yellow hue of the repainted house can be freely associated with many meanings beyond the R1 zoning color convention. For example, some might call to mind the flag of the Holy See, where yellow represents the gold key of St. Peter and his spiritual authority. Yellow also appears in various Buddhist flags, representing the Middle Way and the Dharma. In Islam, yellow carries a dual symbolism representing aesthetic perfection and the inevitable decay of worldly life, often associated with the story of the cow in Surah Al-Baqarah in the Quran. In short, while all colors are spiritual, yellow perhaps leans a bit institutionally religious (certainly more so than pink or lime green), and as such, from our standpoint, a bit more judgemental.
fields.Risk & Safety: The project does not involve the use of any of the listed items except for vehicles (during construction only) and potentially ladders. We will need to wear hard hats, safety glasses, gloves, and masks during clean-up, and have budgeted accordingly for these supplies. We intend to use the cleaning and painting phase to identify and remove anything that would be potentially harmful to visitors during the Biennale. We will haul away debris in a pick-up rental, and may use ladders to paint and access the roof, but this is optional and we would like to minimize this need.
fields.Location Submittal: 9562 Avenue F, corner of Avenue F and 4th St
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, this will be the first time this work has been shown in any form.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
As an architect, urban designer, and artist whose work examines the cultural and spatial myths embedded in the built environment, I cannot imagine a more fitting context for this project than Bombay Beach and the Biennale’s uniquely experimental landscape.

At its core, my project, R1elic is a meditation on the single-family home and the zoning logic that sustains it, an inquiry into whether this dominant architectural and social form should be preserved, reconsidered, or released. By transforming an abandoned, R1-zoned house into a monochrome yellow relic and sanctuary, the project makes visible an often invisible planning framework that has quietly structured much of American life for nearly a century. Visitors will be invited to encounter the house as both object of reverence and object on trial, and to consider what forms of dwelling and collective life might emerge beyond its boundaries.

My practice frequently explores the intersection of architecture, ritual, and collective imagination. Through One With_Architecture, I work across scales, from bathhouse complexes to communal housing prototypes, always asking how built space shapes our inner worlds and outward relationships. In parallel, my artistic work frequently uses mapping, public installations, and speculative design to invite participation and reflection. R1elic continues this trajectory, but at a larger and more immersive scale: a full architectural transformation unfolding in real time and in public view.

Bombay Beach offers a uniquely resonant setting for this inquiry. It is a place where conventional American spatial logic begins to loosen. Yet even here, zoning maps remain largely unchanged from those that govern suburban America: vast areas reserved for detached single-family homes. This tension between regulatory order and lived improvisation creates fertile ground for reflection. The Biennale’s ethos of radical creativity, accessibility, and participation further amplifies this potential, allowing the work to exist not only as a finished installation but as a process open to conversation, collaboration, and encounter.

Equally important is the temporal dimension of the project. The cleansing and painting of the house will unfold over several days on site, allowing residents and visitors to witness and engage with its transformation. We hope this visible labor of care and alteration will spark dialogue about what it means to maintain, preserve, or transform the built environments we inherit. The completed structure will remain post-Biennale as a lasting artifact and point of reflection within the town.

I am particularly drawn to this year’s theme, The Last Judgement, as an invitation not toward condemnation but toward discernment. What built forms and social arrangements do we carry forward, and which do we absolve ourselves from? How might acts of care, ritual, and imagination help us move toward more collective and liberatory spatial futures?

It would be an honor to realize R1elic within the Bombay Beach Biennale and to contribute to its ongoing exploration of art, place, and possibility.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The modern American system of separating land into exclusive use districts was legitimized by the 1926 Supreme Court decision “Village of Euclid v. Ambler Realty Co.” just three years before Bombay Beach was founded. On the 100 year anniversary of this decision, Bombay Beach remains governed by this same zoning logic, at least officially. The town’s zoning, maintained by Imperial County, can broadly be organized into two large buckets: “Low Density Residential Zone” and  “Open Space Recreation/Preservation.”

R1elic asks whether this inherited structure still serves the town, or whether it is simply the lingering logic of another era. Why, in a place known for artistic freedom and improvisation, do zoning maps still assume detached single-family homes as the default? Why not allow multiple dwellings per lot, collective living arrangements, or entirely new spatial forms to emerge if they have momentum?

Long imagined as both resort and ruin, escape and experiment, Bombay Beach exists at the edge of conventional American development patterns. The Salton Sea itself is a byproduct of infrastructural ambitions and their unintended consequences, a landscape with a mind of its own, shaped by planning decisions yet resistant to control. Within this context, questioning our planning and socio-familial system seems appropriate. R1elic also invites attendees to reflect on what forms of dwelling and community belong and what new futures might be possible along the Salton Sea.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): R1elic is currently about 2/3rds of the way through its planning and development. The core concept, site, and primary gesture are established, and we have secured the enthusiastic support of the property owners. A core team of three, led by Francis, is in

fields.Project Description:
A silent migration appears at the edge of Bombay Beach — a line of extinct bodies moving through a landscape that feels already judged. Life-sized animal forms emerge from wire armatures, their skeletal structures loosely veiled in translucent organza. The fabric’s edges are burned and fractured, as if touched by heat or time, leaving each figure suspended between formation and erasure. They are not restorations of lost species but apparitions: incomplete silhouettes that hover between memory and aftermath.

The procession unfolds directly within the desert, stretching across sand and horizon like a drawing that has stepped out of itself. Under daylight, the figures read as exposed frameworks — fragile lines vulnerable to wind and scale. As evening approaches, a faint ambient glow rises from beneath the ground, revealing a path that seems to pre-exist the animals, as though it has been waiting for their return. Inside each body, synchronized “breathing light” pulses slowly, almost imperceptibly, suggesting a residual biological rhythm rather than movement. The sculptures do not walk; they hold the echo of a march that may have already passed.

Organza responds to desert wind, shifting unpredictably so that the environment becomes the primary animator. At times, the bodies appear solid; at others, they dissolve into air, fading and re-emerging depending on light, distance, and weather.

Temporally, the work exists in cycles of revelation and disappearance. Harsh midday sun exposes skeletal structures; twilight transforms them into translucent forms; night renders them as faint, breathing apparitions hovering just above the sand. Technology remains quiet and embedded, sustaining a fragile atmosphere rather than performing spectacle. Light, vibration, and material vulnerability combine to create a space that feels suspended between extinction and persistence.

Within the mythic and ecological terrain of Bombay Beach, the procession reads like a ritual without witnesses — a slow alignment of bodies moving through a landscape already marked by transformation. There is no arrival point, no clear beginning or end. What remains is a passage: a fragile migration that breathes with the desert, holds the memory of life in its fading light, and dissolves again into the horizon as if it had always belonged there.
fields.Support Needed: 1. The theme of The Last Judgement resonates deeply with us. My practice has long circled the notion of the death drive, tracing post-apocalyptic atmospheres and states of disappearance through works that consider endings not as events, but as ongoing conditions.   2. We have submitted another application but we updated the budget so please look at this one. Thank you!
fields.Abridged Project Text: A silent migration appears beside the Salton Sea: extinct animals breathing dim light beneath torn translucent skins. The desert hums with a distant tremor as a ghostly path unfolds — and those who follow begin to recognize their place within the same procession.
fields.Technical Support: We will need a gas generator to power the LED strips underneath the sculptures (we may be able to source this ourselves). It will be outdoors, so we will likely need lag bolts to secure the structure to the ground. Otherwise, it is an analog project which we set up once and leave alone.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The work is encountered rather than announced. Visitors may first glimpse fragments of translucent bodies emerging from the distance, only gradually recognizing a procession moving across the desert. There is no fixed vantage point; people walk alongside, around, and eventually within the formation, becoming part of its shifting spatial rhythm.

As viewers follow the faint illuminated path and slow breathing light create a bodily awareness rather than a spectacle to watch. The sculptures act as leading figures — not guides toward rescue, but markers of a direction that extends beyond the visible horizon. Engagement unfolds through duration: wandering, pausing, returning at different times of day, or unexpectedly encountering the work at night when the figures feel most ghostlike.

Over time, a quiet realization emerges: the procession does not belong only to the extinct animals. The translucent bodies ahead move like echoes of a future already approaching. Visitors begin as witnesses, but their shadows stretch into the same line, their footsteps aligning with the same slow migration. The boundary between observer and figure dissolves.

The installation proposes extinction not as a single event but as a long, shared passage. The line never ends; it only absorbs new silhouettes until the living realize they have already taken their place among the ghosts.
fields.Theme Engagement:
This project approaches the theme as a slow convergence rather than a singular moment of verdict. The extinct animals appear as a procession that has already crossed the threshold between worlds, moving through the desert as if judgment has already passed and only its echoes remain. Over time, the work suggests that humans will inevitably follow — joining the same migration toward an unseen horizon where distinctions between species begin to dissolve.

The work quietly echoes the myth of Noah’s Ark, imagining not the vessel of salvation but the world that remains after it has gone — a landscape where absence becomes the dominant presence and memory continues to move across the earth. The animals advance not toward rescue but through an after-terrain shaped by time, loss, and persistence.

While the piece does not assign blame directly, it acknowledges that many extinctions carry the trace of human action. The procession becomes both witness and consequence, suggesting that judgement unfolds not as punishment but as duration — a condition shaped by ecological transformation and the limits of survival.

Beyond guilt, the installation proposes an unsettling equality. In the long arc of existence, humans and animals share the same fragile trajectory, becoming silhouettes within a single continuum. The Last Judgement becomes less a final decision than a quiet realization: the line does not ask who is guilty; it simply expands, waiting for every living body to take its place.
fields.Risk & Safety: We may need a ladder to set up some of the smaller parts of the structure, but otherwise the risk is minimal.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No. The project is designed for BBB.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We are two Master of Design (MDes) students from UC Berkeley collaborating across installation art and creative technology. We are drawn to BBB because of its openness to experimental work that exists between myth, landscape, and community — a space where fragility and imagination can coexist. This project emerges from our shared interest in how ecological transformation and technological presence shape contemporary rituals of seeing, remembering, and moving through space.

My artistic practice continues an ongoing exploration of transformation, vulnerability, and what remains of the human when familiar structures begin to loosen. My installations often combine fragile materials, subtle interaction, and atmospheric technology to create environments where bodies appear suspended between presence and disappearance. The procession of extinct animals extends this language into an ecological scale — asking how grief, responsibility, and inevitability might coexist within a single, quiet gesture.

My creative technologist collaborator has been following BBB for several years, and her familiarity with its evolving landscape and ethos has helped shape this proposal. As a creative technologist who has lived in California for most of their life, Elisa is drawn to immersive experiences that challenge ephemerality and the balance between emerging technology and authentic human experience. Their works include a simulated spaceship installation for a weekend-long private art event and a motion-mapped "bioluminescent" wooden artifact that modulated a large-scale projection screen. Together, we approach technology as atmosphere rather than spectacle — using breathing light and responsive systems to create embodied experiences that remain sensitive to wind, light, and duration.

Through this collaboration, we are asking: What does judgment look like when it unfolds slowly rather than all at once? How do we live with the knowledge that human actions have shaped extinction while also recognizing that humans themselves move toward the same ending? Rather than framing judgment as punishment, we approach it as a shared condition — a long migration where distinctions between species begin to dissolve.

The process will involve iterative experimentation with lightweight structures, translucent materials, and embedded electronics carefully designed to respond to desert conditions. The work is conceived as adaptive and responsive, evolving through environmental conditions and the presence of the temporary community that gathers each year.

We envision this project as a fragile procession that feels inseparable from the landscape of Bombay Beach — not an object placed within the desert, but a presence emerging from it. By bringing together material vulnerability, subtle technological systems, and mythic imagery, the installation holds space for uncertainty while acknowledging shared endings across species and time. Ultimately, we hope to create an environment where judgment is not experienced as a singular event, but as a quiet condition already unfolding — a space where viewers can pause, reflect, and recognize themselves within a larger, ongoing passage.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
This work belongs to BBB because the landscape already holds the tension between disappearance and persistence that the procession embodies. Alongside the Salton Sea — a place shaped by ecological transformation, abandonment, and reinvention — the figures feel revealed, as if they have always been waiting within the desert’s horizon. The skeletal structures echo the exposed infrastructures and shifting shoreline, while the translucent organza responds to wind, light, and salt air, allowing the environment itself to complete the work.

BBB's ethos of experimentation, mythmaking, and community-driven creation makes it a rare context where fragility and uncertainty can exist. Rather than presenting a monumental statement, we imagine the installation as something temporary and porous, shaped by the rhythms of the landscape and the people who encounter it. Bombay Beach’s history — as a place of both collapse and imagination — mirrors the project’s interest in transformation as an ongoing condition rather than a final event.

Situated beside the Salton Sea, the procession becomes a quiet reflection on shared endings: ecological, personal, and collective. The desert’s vast openness allows the work to unfold slowly, changing with light, distance, and duration. Within the Biennale, it exists not only as a sculpture but as part of a larger ritual ecology — a gathering of works that reimagine how art can inhabit a place where past futures and speculative worlds coexist.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): We are at the early conception and ideation stage. We are currently testing the material and feasibility of the work.

fields.Project Description:
Chiron Tree is a site-responsive land installation at the Salton Sea that transforms a 12-point sacred geometry pattern into a walkable, functional environment. The work consists of twelve sculptural meditation stations arranged in a diamond-shaped grid, with a central star tetrahedron structure serving as the heart of the installation.

The project uses geometry as a bridge between body, land, and cosmos. Each station represents a different dimension of experience—earth, water, fire, air, memory, navigation, time, stillness, and unity—grounded in physical materials that reflect those qualities. Together, the twelve points form a coherent field that visitors can physically move through.

The Central Star Tetrahedron

At the center stands an 8-foot-tall star tetrahedron (also known as a Merkaba), built from sealed reclaimed wood and reflective metal. This structure functions as:

A gathering platform and performance space

A visual and energetic anchor point

A solar-lit beacon activated at dusk

A wind-activated harmonic instrument

Visitors can step onto the platform to stand at the geometric axis of the entire grid, experiencing the installation from its center.

The Twelve Stations

Surrounding the central structure are twelve sculptural stations, each 4–5 feet tall. Each station includes:

A swiveling 360° seat for directional orientation

A distinct material palette reflecting its elemental quality

A weatherproof plaque describing its theme and symbolic associations

A solar-powered colored light

A wind chime tuned to a corresponding tonal frequency

The materials ground the abstract geometry in tactile, desert-resilient matter—stone, clay, metal, glass, wood, and mineral surfaces. Each station offers a different perspective: inward toward the central beacon, outward toward the desert and sea, or aligned with cardinal and celestial directions.

Spatial Arrangement

The installation follows a vertical diamond configuration:

One point at the base

Three forming a lower triangle

Four at the center tier (with the star tetrahedron among them)

Three forming an upper triangle

One point at the top

This creates a three-dimensional constellation mapped onto the landscape.

Visitor Experience

At dusk, solar lights activate, transforming the grid into a luminous constellation. Wind moves through the chimes, generating subtle harmonics. Visitors can sit, rotate, meditate, perform, or simply observe.

Chiron Tree frames the Salton Sea—often seen as a wounded landscape—not as a symbol of collapse, but as a site of transformation. The installation invites reflection on healing, connection, and expanded awareness, using geometry, material, sound, and light to turn land into living instrument.
fields.Support Needed: Rendered image is very rough draft. and apologies on submitting 39min past 11:59, hope it still counts!
fields.Abridged Project Text: A star tetrahedron beacon and central platform rises as a center point, anchoring a 12-point Grid installation. Each point has symbolic correspondences described on plaque and in construction. Swiveling meditation seats at each point allow visitors to meditate and participate with central beacon.
fields.Technical Support:
Spatial Requirements:

Approximately 60' x 40' footprint following vertical Kathara diamond pattern
Stable desert ground (sand/hard-pack acceptable)
Minimal foot traffic zone but accessible
Visible from distance as landmark

Power & Infrastructure:

No grid electricity (fully solar powered)
No water/plumbing
No amplified sound (acoustic wind chimes only)
No projection equipment

Installation Needs:

Vehicle access for material drop-off (2-3 truck loads)
Assistance identifying suitable location
Possible site prep: leveling if needed
Permission for ground stakes (6-12 inches) or weighted bases

Material Sourcing:

Access to local stone/mineral suppliers for sphere-specific materials (obsidian, carnelian, lapis, quartz, etc.)
Mars College workshop for fabrication
Potential donation/loan of crystals/stones from community members

Maintenance During Biennale:

Daily structural check
Re-tightening hardware
Cleaning solar panels
Adjusting swivel seats
Checking colored light filters

Post-Biennale:

Complete removal and site restoration
Materials recyclable/reusable

What We Don't Need:

Projection mapping
Amplified sound
Lighting beyond integrated solar
Tents/shade structures
Extensive signage

This is intentionally low-tech with high material specificity—each sphere's physical substance matters for energetic transmission.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Visitors encounter the installation as a walkable star map that bridges the earth with the cosmos. The journey unfolds in several ways:

Casual Discovery: A curious passerby follows twelve colored lights arranged in a geometric pattern. They find swivel seats with plaques linking Earth locations, to celestial points. By rotating their seat, they physically shift their perspective between local and cosmic space.

A Systematic Journey: For those seeking a deeper experience, the path serves as a physical "ascension." Visitors move through 12 centers, traveling from the physical, earth-bound levels to higher, crystalline light fields. The walk is designed to feel like a climb through different dimensions of existence.

The Central Platform: During performances, artists occupy a central star-shaped platform while the audience gathers at the 12 surrounding spheres. Because each sphere represents a different "frequency," every viewer experiences the performance from a unique dimensional vantage point.

Nighttime Activation: After dark, the desert transforms into a glowing map. Visitors navigate by color—starting at the red base, climbing through the warm tones of the center, and ascending toward the silver and white "crown" points.

Group Connection: The grid naturally invites collective use. When 12 people occupy the spheres with a 13th person in the center, they recreate a sacred geometric template, turning the installation into a tool for shared meditation and connection.
fields.Theme Engagement:
This reframing shifts the focus from dense cosmology to a more accessible narrative about environmental perception and the power of perspective.
Commonly, a "final judgment" implies a fixed ending or a moral reckoning. Here, however, judgment is reframed as dimensional realignment—a moment to recognize where we actually stand within the spectrum of existence versus where we think we are.
The Salton Sea embodies this shift perfectly. To the casual observer, it appears to be an environmental failure—a "dead sea" defined by a toxic legacy. Yet, through a different lens, this landscape exists as a potential gateway. What looks like collapse at a physical, material level may actually be a necessary reorganization of energy.
In this installation, the asteroid Chiron serves as a guide. Positioned between the structure of Saturn and the chaos of Uranus, Chiron represents the "wounded healer"—the idea that our deepest scars can become our greatest doorways. Much like Chiron, this artwork sits on the thin edge between habitable land and uninhabitable water.
The 12-point grid offers twelve different ways to witness the same landscape:
From a physical level, the Salton Sea is a disaster.
From a cosmic level, the same location may be a necessary before a new form emerges.
The "Last Judgment" then becomes a question: Which frequency are you operating from when you make your assessment? The judgment of the past is complete; the choice of how to perceive the future remains with the visitor.
fields.Risk & Safety:
No fire, deep excavation, drones, vehicles, boats, or hazardous materials.
All structures under 8 feet tall. Star tetrahedron platform has 4-foot standing height with 3-foot access ramp.
Anchoring via:

Weighted bases (sandbags/concrete) for each station
Ground stakes as secondary stabilization
All edges sanded smooth
No exposed wiring
Swivel mechanisms load-tested to 300+ lbs

Structures monitored daily during Biennale. Emergency contact posted. Complete removal post-event.
Natural stones/minerals secured with adhesive and wire to prevent displacement.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): This is entirely new work created specifically for the 2026 Bombay Beach Biennale. The Kathara Grid has been studied and visualized in diagrams, but this represents the first known physical installation translating the 12-point morphogenetic template into sculptural, interactive form. The site-specific relationship between the Salton Sea as wounded landscape and the Kathara teaching of dimensional density transformation makes this work impossible to realize elsewhere.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to build the Chiron Tree at Bombay Beach because I believe this landscape is more than an environmental disaster—it is a "threshold zone" where different layers of reality meet.

In my work at Mars College, I’m interested in treating alternative knowledge systems as functional technologies. For this project, I am using a 12-point geometric grid as a navigation tool to understand a place that neither science nor traditional spirituality can fully explain.

A Choice of Perspective
The Salton Sea is a contradiction: it’s toxic yet biodiverse, a human failure yet an ecological adaptation. My project suggests that how we see this landscape depends entirely on the "frequency" we choose to operate from:

From a purely physical level, the Sea is an ecological disaster.

From a transitional level, it is a zone of incredible adaptation.

From a cosmic level, it is a necessary phase of breakdown before a new form emerges.

I believe all three are true simultaneously. The "judgment" of this place isn't a final sentence; it’s a question of which reality we choose to inhabit.

The Installation as Technology
This isn't just a sculpture; it’s a walkable map. I am working with a team to ensure every detail grounded in physical reality:

Material Frequency: Matter is just solidified energy. To reflect this, I’m using specific materials for each of the 12 spheres—dense carbon and earth aggregates for the lower points, and quartz crystal or clear resins for the higher "crown" points.

The Swivel Seats: I’ve designed the seating to rotate because perspective should never be fixed. To navigate different dimensions of thought, you have to be mobile.

The Central Platform: At the heart of the grid is a star-shaped platform. This is the "crossroads" where the human body aligns with larger cosmic coordinates. Standing there, you are literally at the interface of different scales of existence.

Why Bombay Beach?
I see the desert as an amplifier. I believe certain locations on Earth act as "cue sites"—physical coordinates aligned with a larger universal geometry. By mapping a star-map directly onto the Bombay Beach terrain, I want to show that this "ruined" landscape is actually cosmically positioned for something greater.

The Last Judgment isn't a punishment; it’s a sorting process. We are all finding our true frequency right now. The Salton Sea isn't failing—it’s transmuting. I want to build this to make that process visible, walkable, and real.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
I am building the Chiron Tree at Bombay Beach because this landscape reveals a reality most places hide. In cities, environmental collapse is abstracted and tucked away; here, the receding sea and rising toxicity happen in full view, alongside a thriving, defiant creative community.

I treat the Salton Sea not as a disaster, but as a threshold. Using a 12-point geometric map, I aim to show that what looks like "collapse" on a physical level is actually a "reorganization" on a cosmic one.

Why This Site?
The project is built specifically for this ground. It requires the vast horizon for celestial alignment, the desert wind to carry its tones, and the 33rd parallel—a geographic line shared by other global energy points.

The Experience
The installation translates complex maps into a physical, walkable reality:

12 Spheres: Each is built with materials matching its "frequency," from dense earth minerals at the base to clear quartz at the crown.

Swivel Seats: These allow visitors to physically shift their perspective, moving between local and cosmic views.

The Center: A star-shaped platform where the human body aligns with galactic coordinates.

This work belongs at Bombay Beach because the desert acts as an amplifier. It turns a "ruined" landscape into a functional tool for navigation. I’m not asking for belief; I’m offering a way to stand in the middle of a planetary transition and choose a new perspective.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Late conceptual phase with strong dimensional/geometric framework. Moving into material sourcing and fabrication design.

fields.Project Name: Life in the Balance - the Scales of Justice
fields.Project Description:
Giant Egyptian Anubis scales of Justice
(Large enough to support a human on each end.)

For this Biennale I’d like to interpret the theme ‘The Last Judgement’ by creating a set of giant Scales of Justice based on the Egyptian mythology of Anubis weighing a human heart against a feather to see if they were worthy of entering the Underworld or having their heart devoured instead.

The aim is to make it large and strong enough to support humans. I see it attached to one of the telephone pole on the beach or outside the Subclub.

I feel this biennale needs a call for accountability and justice in a world robbed of it.  It would look striking on the beach, and be interactive, food for thought and right on theme.

We could have an Anubis weighing in opening ceremony and /or involve the Calipatra High School Marching Band again

fields.Project Description:
The Ward’s Echo is a site-specific immersive installation that uses augmented reality to manifest the internal world of Huoshi, a poet and a painter who lives with schizophrenia. She was once confined in a psychiatric hospital, where electroconvulsive therapy was administered in an attempt to correct the hallucinations that had also served as a visual source of inspiration for her art. Yet within her vivid, kaleidoscopic inner landscapes—worlds of infinite possibility—she sought a liberation of the mind from that confinement. As a neuroscientist and artist, I am mapping the "spatial trauma" of clinical confinement onto the entropic beauty of Bombay Beach.

Physically, the project consists of seven "anchor points" placed strategically among the ruins. Experientially, when visitors scan these points with their phones, the desert environment is overlaid not only with the cold, mint-green geometry of the hospital but also with the shifting architecture of Huoshi’s mental world. These hallucinated forms—generated from her memories and emotional experiences within the ward and visually inspired by her wild, mercurial paintings—emerge alongside the clinical structures. Visitors will see "ghost" spaces: grated sliding doors appearing in open lots, rows of peeling chairs in the sand, and the rhythmic ticking of a hospital clock synchronized to the local time, interwoven with fluid, unpredictable spatial distortions drawn from her inner landscapes.

The soundscape features binaural audio: the wind of the Salton Sea layered with Huoshi’s own recorded voice reciting her poetry. Through AI-mediated transformation, her voice is elongated, diffused, and refracted, hovering between intimacy and hallucination. 

As visitors move closer to the water’s edge, the clinical “layers” begin to fracture and dissolve, replaced by abstract visual fields translated from her poetry, with fragments of text drifting intermittently through the air. It becomes a journey from the rigid surveillance of the ward to the boundless expanse of the sea.
fields.Support Needed: Through The Ward’s Echo, I am seeking to transform a clinical history of isolation into a public act of presence. By using solar-powered AR technology, this installation ensures that the 'ghosts' of the psychiatric ward are visible even in the darkest hours of the desert, mirroring the persistent nature of memory itself. This work is a testament to the resilience of the human mind and a tribute to those who, like Huoshi, find their way back to poetry after the silence of the institution.
fields.Abridged Project Text: A spatial AR experience overlaying the architecture of a psychiatric ward onto Bombay Beach. Using the story of a "schizophrenic poet," visitors navigate a landscape where desert ruins meet clinical memories. It is a walk through the boundaries of confinement, perception, and poetic liberation.
fields.Technical Support:
The project requires one primary, weather-resistant marker sign that serves as the main narrative portal and entry point, along with six smaller satellite markers distributed throughout the site. These smaller markers function as subtle anchor points, guiding participants through different spatial layers of the installation.

The project will be hosted via WebAR and does not require a local server. But reliable cellular reception in the placement zones is necessary to ensure smooth loading of digital assets and spatial audio.
fields.Audience Engagement:
People “stumble into” the work while exploring the town. It is a participatory walk that requires movement; the audience must physically navigate between the digital “walls” and furniture of the ward while simultaneously moving through the shifting architectures of Huoshi’s mental world. As clinical structures intersect with hallucinatory forms, visitors are invited to negotiate unstable spatial boundaries, experiencing moments of perceptual ambiguity where inner and outer landscapes blur. The act of walking becomes both a bodily navigation and a cognitive one.

By 3 am, the AR projections take on a more ethereal, disorienting quality against the dark desert. The layered geometry of the hospital and the fluid distortions of her inner world become harder to distinguish, heightening the sense of isolation and the “night-watch” atmosphere of the ward.
fields.Theme Engagement:
For those within a psychiatric system, “The Last Judgement” can take the form of a clinical discharge—the institutional verdict on whether one’s mind is deemed “fit” for society. Diagnosis and confinement operate through systems of evaluation: observation, categorization, and correction. In this context, judgement is medicalized and externally imposed.

This project reinterprets the theme as a tension between institutional authority and personal sovereignty. While the ward renders a person legible through clinical language, Huoshi’s hallucinations and paintings resist such containment. The work asks, who holds the final authority over meaning? Is the “last judgement” the hospital’s decision—or the individual’s reclamation of narrative through art?

Spatially, the installation stages this conflict. The rigid geometry of the ward overlays the desert, only to fracture and dissolve into the fluid architecture of Huoshi’s mental world and the open expanse of the Sea. Judgment shifts from surveillance to self-definition.

Ultimately, the project suggests that the last judgement may not be a verdict imposed from above but an ongoing act of reclaiming one’s own perceptual and creative truth.
fields.Risk & Safety: No hazardous materials. All engagement is digital. I will ensure the marker is placed in a stable, accessible area to avoid any need for excavation or climbing.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No. This is a new project for the 2026 Biennale.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
My practice lives at the intersection of neuroscience and storytelling. While my research at NYU focuses on the mechanics of the mind, my creative work explores the "ghosts" left behind by clinical experiences. After meeting Huoshi in Dali, I realized her story—a poet trapped in a system—could not be contained in a traditional medium.

I want to create this work now because we are living in a global moment of intensified judgement—of bodies, minds, and identities. Questions of “normalcy,” legitimacy, and institutional authority are increasingly visible. Through this project, I ask, who defines mental fitness? What is the boundary between illness and imagination? When does care become control? And who ultimately holds the final authority over one’s narrative?

Bombay Beach feels uniquely aligned with these questions. The community there is radically open, deeply diverse, and unusually tolerant of experimental minds. It is a place where artists, thinkers, wanderers, and outsiders coexist without rigid hierarchy. In such a context, someone like Huoshi would not be reduced to a diagnosis; her inner world could travel outward rather than be confined inward. The installation becomes not only a representation of confinement but also a re-situating of it within a landscape that embraces difference.

The Salton Sea and surrounding desert function as both site and metaphor. The desert’s vastness—its horizon without edges—stands in stark contrast to the geometry of the ward. The sea is unstable, saline, evaporating, yet expansive. It embodies emergence after restriction and openness after surveillance. As the clinical layers fracture and dissolve into this environment, the landscape itself becomes a gesture of release.

This installation is an attempt to bridge the analytic distance of neuroscience with the lived, embodied reality of neurodivergence. It asks whether judgment is an endpoint—or whether it can dissolve into something more expansive.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is a place where time feels suspended, and where landscape itself bears the marks of environmental judgment. The Salton Sea—once imagined as a resort paradise, later declared an ecological failure—carries a history of projection, collapse, and redefinition. In that sense, the site already embodies the theme of “The Last Judgement”: a place assessed, abandoned, and yet persistently reinhabited by artists, wanderers, and alternative communities.

The salt-crusted ruins and peeling facades of Bombay Beach physically echo the interior surfaces of a psychiatric ward—the flaking paint, exposed metal, and institutional geometry. The desert allows for a visceral spatial contrast: the confined, mint-green architecture of the hospital overlaid against the infinite horizon of the Sea. Here, surveillance meets vastness; containment meets expanse.

Equally important is the community itself. Bombay Beach is unusually open, radically diverse, and deeply tolerant of experimental minds. It is a place where unconventional identities are not immediately pathologized. In such an environment, Huoshi’s inner world is not reduced to a diagnosis, but allowed to travel outward into shared space. Presenting this work at the Biennale situates her story within a landscape that already embraces reinvention, making the installation not only site-specific but socially resonant.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Part way through. The digital assets (binaural sound and spatial models) are in production, and the narrative framework is complete.
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I spend significant time there seasonally

fields.Project Description:
Echo Chamber is a space where any sound made by those inside it takes flight in a psychedelic dance of ethereal echoes all around them. 

People are initially drawn in by a highlighted pedestal that seems to have something on display, but exactly what it is on display can't be made out from a distance. By approaching the pedestal to see what it is (a tiny, empty, glass display box), the observer has entered the Echo Chamber and are now standing very near the array of microphones hidden beneath the pedestal's fancy tablecloth. 

Once inside, any sounds the observer makes - a scuff of a foot, a cough, chit chat, etc - will be echoed and amplified and swirl around them via a quadraphonic set of speakers hidden beneath tablecloths on the small tables in each corner of the Echo Chamber.

This will be delightful in a somewhat disorienting way.
fields.Support Needed: There are 2 budgets submitted. One includes an Ecoflow battery that can run the system for an evening. If there is access to AC power, then that reduces the overall budget significantly. I'll be delighted to bring this project to life either way.
fields.Abridged Project Text: We sometimes get lost in our own echos, narrowing our perspective of the wide world around us.
fields.Technical Support: Electricity is necessary for this. As such, I'm submitting 2 budgets - one with an Ecoflow battery that can run the system for a night (I should be able to manage recharges each day), and one budget in case there is access to AC power (in that case we can do without that Ecoflow battery and keep costs down significantly).
fields.Audience Engagement:
This is an area where an observer's sounds get warped into a swirl of echoes all around the observer. 

Discovery is an important part of this work.  They will enter and exit the space, but I expect it will be most powerful when they're drawn in by their own curiosity.
fields.Theme Engagement: We should be aware, when we make judgement, whether we're listening for truths beyond our own, or if we're missing other truths beneath the echos of our own noise.
fields.Risk & Safety: I don't think so.  There are electronics - lights, small speakers, etc. -  but nothing with any unique hazards.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): This is a fresh idea, and I expect it will be one I build upon in the future. The premiere would be this Biennale.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): I attended a wedding at Mars in 2022 and - to my delight - happened upon the Bombay Beach Biennale. I was impressed with the variety and intensity of creativity showcased. This year for the first time, I am a Martian, and I realized this opportunity to participate in such an event is too good to pass up.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
I believe the concept of an Echo Chamber, especially when one ends up in it  inadvertently, is an interesting consideration under the theme of judgement. 

How can we know when our judgement is clouded if we only hear ourselves? How can we remember to listen for other thoughts? other truths? other ideas?
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I've done things adjacent to this (glitch processing live drums and horns for musical performances, for example). Creating swirling echoes in a room is a notch easier. That said, this is a new idea so I haven`t started making it yet. I expect it to be ~2

fields.Project Description:
The Final Weighing is a site-specific installation created from driftwood, fish bones and barnacles, and will be installed on a visible spot on the beach.. Natural shoreline mosaics are preserved in clear resin and mounted into illuminated, cathedral-window–inspired light-boxes.
Three resin panels (approx. 4.5’ x 3.5’) are mounted inside custom welded steel frames fabricated from 1” x 3” rectangular tubing. Each panel is cast directly from selected sections of driftwood and barnacles found in the field, preserving the natural spiral formations alongside embedded debris — driftwood, calcified barnacles, fragments of shoes, plastic. The three arches stand approximately 3 feet apart and are oriented west, toward the sunset. Each structure is stabilized using removable footing sleeves formed within 5-gallon concrete bases, ensuring stability while remaining fully reversible and leaving no permanent site alteration.
In front of the cathedral shaped light-boxes, three rows of reclaimed pew-style benches will be installed. Constructed from salvaged wood materials and
integrated driftwood, the seating places viewers directly in front of the art.
By day, the blackened frames recede while the resin reveals trapped debris in full clarity. At sunset, light passes through the preserved formations. At night, internal illumination activates the panels from within.
The installation suggests a chapel — open to wind, horizon, and exposure.
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Final Weighing preserves a stranded shoreline of driftwood, barnacles, and debris in illuminated resin arches facing the Salton Sea sunset. Installed as a temporary desert chapel, it confronts viewers with what remains after retreat — a reckoning not of souls, but of matter left behind.
fields.Technical Support:
If selected, I would like to discuss placement with Biennale organizers.
While during the Biennale weekend the ideal placement would be on the beach, after the Biennale and for the rest of the year, the installation can be potentially  moved into town, within a safer all-year-round location, agreed with the Biennale organizers.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Visitors approach the installation across open sand and gradually become aware of its spatial pull, giving the illusion of an outdoor chapel and a place to rest, observe the art and meditate.

The work creates duration. Viewers can stay as long as they want. The shifting light changes the perception of the shapes and casted elements.
By revealing different textures and intensities each time, the work rewards repeated visits and invites deeper reflection through prolonged duration rather than a single encounter.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Final Weighing responds to the Biennale’s theme, The Last Judgment, by translating the idea of reckoning from the spiritual to the material.

In Michelangelo’s fresco, bodies rise and fall in suspended motion, caught at the moment of evaluation. The composition is turbulent, circular, and inescapable — a cosmic sorting. In the Salton Sea landscape, a similar sorting has already occurred. Water receded. Matter shifted. What was buoyant, drifted. What was heavy, settled. What could not decompose, calcified. The shoreline became an archive of consequence.

This installation reframes judgment not as divine decree, but as physical process. The sea has already performed the sorting. The desert preserves the result.

By mounting preserved shoreline formations within chapel-like arches, the work invokes the architectural language of judgment while removing any promise of salvation or condemnation. There is no central figure, no scale, no verdict.

Instead, the reckoning unfolds through accumulation and exposure. What remains is visible. What was carried away is absent.
The work suggests that judgment may not arrive as spectacle, but as residue — the quiet evidence left behind after retreat.
fields.Risk & Safety:
There will be some supervised digging, no holes left unsupervised, the footings are removable (concrete into 5 gallon buckets buried into the ground)
There are no risks or safety concerns.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, it will be a brand new art piece
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
The Final Weighing began with time spent walking the exposed shoreline of the Salton Sea. Far from the current waterline, a large field of driftwood appeared arranged in spiral formations. Nearby, hardened barnacle mounds marked a former shoreline, mixed with fragments of shoes and plastic. The landscape felt like evidence of retreat.

The work responds to the Biennale’s theme, The Last Judgment, by translating the idea of judgment into material terms. Instead of souls being weighed, the installation considers how matter is sorted over time. Water recedes. What floats drifts. What sinks settles. What does not decompose remains visible.

The work invites reflection through proximity and duration. It preserves a shoreline formation that continues to disappear, fixing a moment of material reckoning in time.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): The Final Weighing belongs in Bombay Beach because it emerges directly from the Salton Sea’s shoreline. The materials preserved in the resin — driftwood, barnacles, and debris — are not symbolic; they are local and specific. The installation faces the water that shaped them, anchoring the work to place rather than transporting it elsewhere. Bombay Beach and the Biennale have long engaged themes of environmental transformation, impermanence, and reinvention. This piece contributes to that dialogue by fixing a moment of retreat that is actively unfolding. It reflects the landscape’s ongoing change and honors the community’s relationship to resilience and adaptation.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): This project is currently in the development and early production stage. The concept has been defined, site orientation has been determined, and structural design decisions for the steel frames and installation layout have been finalized. A test resin pou
