fields.Artist Name: Kalee Bly Choiniere, Kalee Bly Choiniere
fields.Project Description:
I will be painting at least one large-scale mural before and during the festival, with the intention that it can be experienced both during and long after the 2026 Biennale. My aim in creating public murals is to offer something bright, engaging, and intentionally uplifting for the community and for visitors encountering Bombay Beach, especially those visiting for the first time. 
The mural will be colorful and expressive, drawing from the feeling I experience when I visit Bombay Beach; an atmosphere of surprise, joy, and unexpected connectivity. Using flowing linework and vibrant color, I will create a piece that feels alive and visually stimulating, inviting viewers to pause, look more closely, and spend a moment in reflection. The work will unfold in real time, viewers will be able to see the mural develop over the course of the weekend, watching layers, shapes, and colors gradually come to life. This process is an important part of the experience; painting in public creates opportunities for spontaneous interaction, conversation, and connection. People often stop to ask questions, share stories, or observe, and these exchanges become part of the work’s presence in the space. I hope the mural offers a sense of warmth and openness that resonates with the spirit of Bombay Beach. The finished piece will remain as a lasting visual experience, something that continues to bring energy and a sense of playfulness to the environment, encouraging future viewers to stop, reflect, and connect long after the 2026 Biennale has ended.
fields.Support Needed: Thank you! I love Bombay Beach and I'm really hoping to be part of the Biennale this year :) My friend who has spent the last couple of years living in Bombay Beach has a few leads for me but I am happy to be connected to anyone who might be interested in a mural.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Creating meditative moments of connection to self and others through paint, bright colors, and patterns that feel like a hug for your brain.
fields.Technical Support: Lighting or at least power for lighting would be extremely helpful.
fields.Audience Engagement: During the live painting process, the mural becomes a live, evolving piece. Viewers often stop to observe, ask questions, or share their own thoughts and stories. These spontaneous interactions are an important part of the work, turning the act of painting into a shared experience. Once finished, the mural becomes something people can stumble upon at any time of day or night. In daylight, the colors and linework are intended to be vibrant and energizing; and at night, the piece may feel more contemplative, offering a moment of stillness. I imagine people pausing in front of it, taking photos, sitting nearby, or simply letting their attention rest. The work does not require active participation in a formal sense, but it invites personal interpretation and emotional engagement. My hope is that viewers feel welcome to spend as much or as little time as they like with it, and that the mural becomes a small but meaningful point of connection.
fields.Theme Engagement: My mural engages with The Last Judgement as a personal and collective act of reckoning. I think of the last judgement not as an end, but as a moment of decision, a space in which we choose to swim rather than sink. It represents the final push to give everything we have, to strive for a future greater than what seems available or attainable in the current climate. The work reflects on the responsibility each of us carries as part of a larger community. Judgement, in this sense, is not about punishment but about accountability: asking ourselves what we are contributing, what we are sustaining, and what we are leaving behind. If we cannot always act for ourselves, we can act for something greater; for the well-being of others and for the possibility of a more generous future.
fields.Risk & Safety: Just a ladder - I will make sure the ladder is sturdy.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No! This will be a brand new work.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): I would love to create a piece of work that is timeless and can consistently act as a place of contemplation and engagement for anyone who stumbles upon it. I see this as a time, perhaps more than ever,  a time in need of color, expression, and contemplation; art that doesn't ask anything from us, rather offers us a place of refuge and acts like a deep exhale. I believe this will be a work of art that elicits joy for joy's sake and brings the community of Bombay Beach together through conversation and shared moments of contemplation.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): When I think of Bombay Beach, I am taken back to my origins of 'why' I even make anything or paint anything at all; pure creation for the sake of community and connection. The history of Bombay Beach and the community that has adapted because of its history is a reminder of how creation will always supersede and follow the natural process of destruction. It is a reminder of how often in life we can plan for a certain outcome and either be derailed completely...or adapt. At the end of the day, we have the choice to create something beautiful and new. I would be honored to create a piece of art in space that is resilient and constantly renewing itself.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): In the design phase!

fields.Project Name: Bad Urchin’s Speakeasy Hideout - Geodetic Dome Project
fields.Project Description:
For the 2026 Bombay Beach Biennale, I am building Bad Urchin's Geodesic Hideout—an immersive, interactive climbing structure and gathering space. At its heart stands a 16-foot-tall, 15-foot-diameter geodesic dome. Below, open shade offers respite from the desert sun. Above, a crows nest with hammock-like netting invites visitors to climb, lounge, and look out over the strange beauty of the Salton Sea.

The dome is lit at night with programmable full-spectrum color lighting, entirely solar-powered and self-contained. It will glow like a beacon against the decaying shoreline—a small, warm world of its own making.

Alongside the dome, two distinct vehicles will anchor the space. My 1961 Chevrolet Greenbrier Van, converted into a roving Tiki speakeasy, will serve homemade vegan ice cream and mocktails at sunset. And my ambulance clubhouse—a separate vehicle entirely—will become a live painting canvas throughout the weekend.

Both the dome and the ambulance will be transformed in real time. Throughout the Biennale, anyone is invited to pick up a brush and add their mark. The dome's struts and panels become a collaborative mural, evolving with each passing hour. The ambulance, already a clubhouse, becomes a collective self-portrait of everyone who passed through—layers of color, memory, and mark-making accumulating across the weekend. A nearby 30-foot-diameter star-shaped event pavilion provides additional shade and gathering space for impromptu performances(open to scheduled), conversations, or simply watching the light change.

The installation unfolds across the Biennale weekend in layers. By day, the dome offers shade and a place to rest, climb, or talk story. By night, it glows. At sunset—the golden hour—the speakeasy van opens its window, and the ritual begins: homemade vegan ice cream, a mocktail, a moment of pause as the light shifts over the Salton Sea. This daily gathering becomes its own performance—one where visitors are both audience and cast. Meanwhile, the live painting continues on both dome and ambulance, an open invitation to watch, ask questions, or pick up a brush and contribute.

Physically, the site is designed for flow: dome, ambulance, van, and pavilion form a loose village, each space connected by sightlines and foot traffic. Visitors can wander through, climb up, sit down, paint, rest, or stumble in at 3 a.m. to find the dome still glowing and the netting still waiting.

This is not art behind rope. It is a hideout—a place to be, to meet, to rest, to create together.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Aye! Avast! You have found Bad Urchin’s Geodesic Hideout. Climb up into the crows nest and take a load off, or sit in our shade and talk story. If luck finds you around sunset the roaming speakeasy may show, and if it does you could find yourself with ice cream or mocktail in hand shortly. Aloha!
fields.Technical Support:
To function fully, Bad Urchin's Geodesic Hideout requires the following:

Electrical: None for the dome itself—its lighting is fully solar-powered with integrated battery storage, capable of running nightly through the Biennale without grid connection. The speakeasy van and star pavilion have no electrical requirements.

Sound: No installed sound system. Ambient interaction only.

Projection/Screen: None required.

Location: Outdoor installation only. The dome, van, and pavilion are designed for desert conditions—full sun, wind, and cool nights.

Space needs: A relatively flat area approximately 50 feet in diameter to accommodate the dome (15' diameter), van (parking space), and star pavilion (30' diameter) with comfortable flow between them. Sandy or compacted soil is fine; the dome's footprint distributes weight evenly.

Assembly: The dome can be assembled on-site by one to two people with basic tools; no heavy equipment needed. The van arrives ready to function. The star pavilion is a shade structure requiring simple stake-down or weight anchoring.

Duration: Designed for the full Biennale weekend and, if permitted, could remain as a longer-term installation.

Accessibility: The dome invites climbing but ground-level shade seating ensures participation for all mobility levels. The speakeasy van door is accessible from standing position.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Bad Urchin's Geodesic Hideout is something you stumble into at 3 a.m. —or seek out at sunset with intention. It is a space you move through, climb into, and participate in simply by being there.

By day, people find shade and conversation. They climb the dome's struts to the crows nest, lie back in the netting, and watch the sky. They wander to the star pavilion, sit with friends or strangers, and let the Biennale flow around them.

At sunset, the space transforms. The speakeasy van opens its window, and the ritual begins: homemade vegan ice cream, a mocktail, a moment of pause as the light shifts over the Salton Sea. This daily gathering becomes its own performance—one where visitors are both audience and cast.

Live painting on the van invites direct participation. People can watch, ask questions, or—if the moment calls—add their own mark. The dome itself asks to be climbed, touched, inhabited. There is no wrong way to engage.

At night, the dome glows. It becomes a landmark, a beacon, a place to find your people or lose yourself for a while. Whether at high noon or 3 a.m., the hideout waits.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Bombay Beach Biennale's 2026 theme, The Last Judgement, asks: What comes after the end of the world—repentance, rebirth, or a bacchanal? 

Bad Urchin's Geodesic Hideout answers: all three, served with vegan ice cream.

The Salton Sea is itself a landscape after judgment—a place where ecological collapse meets stubborn beauty, where the dead sea refuses to die. My installation sits within that contradiction. The geodesic dome, a shape born of visionary design, becomes a small ark: a place to gather, to wait, to see what rises.

The crows net offers a vantage point—a seat from which to survey the ruins and imagine what comes next. The speakeasy van, serving small pleasures at sunset, enacts a ritual of communion without doctrine. Ice cream becomes sacrament. Mocktail becomes mercy.

And the live painting? That is resurrection in real time. A surface transformed, layer by layer, by hands that show up and add their mark. The van becomes a collective self-portrait of everyone who passed through.

This is not a final verdict. It is a rehearsal—a carnival of the damned, the divine, and everyone in between, gathered in the shade of a dome that glows because the sun charged it. If this is judgment, let it be sweet
fields.Risk & Safety: This project involves ladders during dome construction and climbing once installed. I will mitigate risk by: building the dome with all joints properly secured; inspecting all climbing points before public access; and posting simple signage indicating the dome is climb-at-your-own-risk. No fire, drones, excavation, vehicles in motion during event hours, or hazardous materials. The speakeasy van serves only non-alcoholic mocktails and vegan ice cream, and will not move during the Biennale.
fields.Location Submittal: Near shoreline and sand box property
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): I would like to build two so I am applying for tier one or two two if possible so double the linked budget.  This is a new project.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create Bad Urchin's Geodesic Hideout because I believe in building spaces where people can simply be together—especially in places the world has written off. Bombay Beach is such a place. The Salton Sea, dying and stubborn, holds a mirror to our collective future: what do we do with landscapes we have broken? My answer, always, has been to build.

I am asking: What does gathering look like after the end of the world? What forms of care survive when systems collapse? And who gets to decide what rises next?

The geodesic dome is my chosen shape because it is both ancient and futuristic—a structure that distributes weight evenly, that stands without central support, that invites climbing and resting in equal measure. It is a small world built on mutual dependence, much like the communities I try to cultivate. The live painting on both dome and ambulance asks another question: What happens when everyone is invited to leave their mark? The surface becomes a record of everyone who passed through—a collective artifact of a weekend, a place, a moment.

The process is as important as the finished piece. I will arrive early to build, and that building will be visible, open, participatory. Anyone who wanders by can watch, ask questions, or pick up a brush. My collaborators are the community itself—Co-Op members who have worked alongside me on installations before, Bombay Beach residents who know this place better than I ever will, and strangers who become friends over ice cream at sunset. The speakeasy van, serving small pleasures, becomes its own kind of collaboration: a ritual of pause and sweetness in a landscape of ruin.

Why now? Because the Bombay Beach Biennale turns ten. Because the Salton Sea's crisis deepens while the world looks away. Because the theme The Last Judgement asks us to reckon with what we have made, and I want to offer not a verdict but a gathering place—a hideout where sinners and saints can sit together in the shade and watch the light change.

My practice has always been about creating containers for connection. From Burning Man installations to nomadic van builds to the daily work of The Aloha Co-Op, I build things that ask to be touched, climbed, inhabited. Bombay Beach is the perfect canvas for this work: a place where abandonment meets imagination, where the line between art and life blurs until it disappears.

Bad Urchin's Geodesic Hideout is not a statement. It is an invitation—to rest, to climb, to paint, to share something sweet, to be human together on the edge of a dying sea. If this is judgment, let it be gentle. If this is the end, let us face it in good company.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bad Urchin's Geodesic Hideout belongs in Bombay Beach because this is a place that refuses to die—and so does my work. The Salton Sea, poisoned and shrinking, is a landscape after judgment. It asks what we owe the places we have broken. My installation answers with a gathering space: a dome to climb, shade to rest in, a van serving ice cream at sunset, surfaces becoming collective art as strangers pick up brushes.

Bombay Beach is not a gallery. It is a town of squatters, dreamers, and stubborn souls who stayed when the water receded. The Biennale amplifies that spirit—no tickets, no commerce, participation required. My project fits because it demands nothing except presence. You can stumble in at 3 a.m. and find the dome glowing. You can sit in the shade and talk story. You can add your mark to the ambulance and leave something behind.

The landscape itself shaped this work. The geodesic dome echoes the iterative, self-supporting structures of desert ecology. The live painting responds to the palimpsest of Bombay Beach—layers of abandonment and reinvention written over one another. And the speakeasy van, serving small sweetness at sunset, honors the ritual of pause that this strange place demands.

If the Biennale asks what comes after the end, my answer is simple: community. Built by hand. Lit by sun. Open to anyone who finds their way here.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The concept is ready to go and should take just a few days to complete on site.

fields.Project Name: We Shall Have the Eyes to See
fields.Project Description:
We Shall Have the Eyes to See is a participatory textile installation unfolding in and around the Poetry House.

The project begins with a public writing session. Participants gather inside the house to listen to the Brazilian song “Juízo Final,” sung by Clara Nunes. The lyrics describe a final judgement in which light returns, evil burns away, and love becomes eternal again. An English translation is shared, and the group is invited to reflect on what “The Last Judgement” means today — personally, politically, spiritually and/ or ecologically.

Participants write short poetic sentences, fragments, or drawings responding to prompts such as: What must end? What deserves to survive? What needs to be seen clearly?

These texts become the foundation of the installation.

Using a custom-trained LoRA (AI image model), I generate painterly color fields and backgrounds that respond to the emotional tone of the writing. The community’s words are central in these compositions. The works are printed on fabric at varying scales: large banner-sized pieces and smaller flag-like textiles.

Some flags will be embroidered by hand, adding a slow, tactile layer to the digitally generated images. The embroidery highlights specific phrases, emphasizing the tension between speed (AI generation) and slowness (hand labor).

The finished flags are installed inside the Poetry House and extend outward, possibly into the beach. Hung from beams, windows, and exterior lines, they move with the wind. By day, sunlight activates the color. By night, interior light illuminates them.

One flag will contain the full English translation of the song’s lyrics, anchoring the installation in its source material.

The work unfolds over time: writing session, image generation, printing, embroidery, installation. The audience witnesses both process and result.

The installation becomes a collective verdict written by the community — a reflection on endings, renewal, and the desire to see more clearly.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Inside the Poetry House, we gather to listen to a song, write and imagine a different Last Judgement. Community-written poetic fragments are transformed into large textile flags - colored, print and hand-embroidered - declaring the possibility of hope and renewal.
fields.Technical Support:
Indoor space: Poetry House interior for writing session and installation.
Outdoor access: ability to hang textile flags from beams, exterior walls, or lines outside the Poetry House and perhaps in other locations around Bombay.
Electricity: minimal, potentially using battery powered lights for illuminating flags at night.
Installation materials: permission to use hooks, lines, or temporary mounting hardware.
Printing: off-site fabrication of textile prints..

fields.Audience Engagement:
This project is participatory at its core.

Audience members are invited to attend the writing session, listen to the song, and contribute their own poetic reflections. They are co-authors of the final installation.

Outside the scheduled session, visitors can move through the textile environment freely. The flags can be read up close or encountered unexpectedly in the beach. At night, the illuminated fabric creates an atmospheric space for quiet reflection.

Some participants may return to see their words transformed into large-scale visual works. Others may encounter the installation at 3 a.m., while walking into the Poetry House.
 
Engagement happens through reading, movement and recognition. The work asks viewers to consider what judgement means when authored collectively rather than imposed from above.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The project approaches “The Last Judgement” as moral rebalancing rather than divine punishment.

In “Juízo Final,” judgement is described as the return of light and the burning of the harmful seed of evil. It is a transformation, not a spectacle. This perspective resonates with contemporary anxieties — ecological collapse, political instability and social fracture.

Rather than presenting a singular vision of judgement, the installation invites collective authorship. The community writes its own reflections on what must end and what should endure. In this way, judgement becomes participatory.

The desert setting intensifies this inquiry. The Salton Sea stands as evidence of environmental consequence. Environmental judgement. Against this backdrop, imagining renewal carries weight.

The work asks: Is judgement destruction or is it clarity? Can a community articulate its own ethical horizon?
fields.Risk & Safety:
No fire, vehicles, excavation, or hazardous materials involved.

Risks include ladder use during installation and fabric hanging outdoors.
fields.Location Submittal: Poetry House and possibly the beach or other spots around town
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): This is new work developed specifically for the 2026 Biennale. While I have previously exhibited textile-based Art installations, this project is a new iteration centered on collective authorship and the 2026 theme.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create this work now because we are living in a time saturated with narratives of collapse. The phrase “Last Judgement” often evokes punishment, apocalypse, or spectacle. I am interested in reframing it as moral clarity - a moment in which harmful patterns burn away and light returns - and hope.

The Salton Sea embodies consequence. It is a landscape shaped by human intervention, excess and abandonment. In this environment, the idea of judgement feels less theological and more material. We are already living with the consequences of past "sins".

As an artist working with AI and textile, I am interested in tension: speed and slowness, digital and hand, collective voice and individual authorship. This project brings those elements together. The writing session centers community testimony. The AI-generated backgrounds provide atmosphere. The embroidery reintroduces touch and time.

Bombay Beach has been an important site in my practice. I have previously installed work at the Poetry House, and I value its history as a site for language, intimacy and experimentation. Returning allows me to deepen that relationship rather than repeat it.

Through this work I am asking: What does judgement look like when written by those who must live with its consequences? Can collective imagination become a form of ethical rehearsal?
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is a place where environmental reality and artistic imagination meet directly. The Salton Sea functions as an unintentional monument to human decision-making. In that context, “The Last Judgement” feels immediate.

The Poetry House provides an intimate site for communal writing, while the surrounding desert expands the installation into a public declaration. The wind, dust, and light of the landscape become collaborators.

This project belongs here because it speaks to consequence and renewal in a place shaped by both. With its strong community, I feel it's the ideal place to bring voices together.

fields.Project Description:
In a world spinning in chaos, division, distraction and unpredictability. Facing down a reality of accelerating ecological collapse...
How do we cope? How do we ground ourselves into the reality of what matters most? How do we take action to do what's required to save ourselves, and save our world?

We come together and realize that we are all inextricably linked, and the only solution is to celebrate life. All of it, in joy. 
Laugh in the face of catastrophe, because we already have the solutions and a way forward.
Dance with the destruction, but take the lead...

An art installation that can be interacted with and used to connect to our network. It's a pay phone booth with an old payphone. The participant answers a call and a video is shown with existential questioning, psychedelic and natural imagery as well as a call to action. It will utilize projection inside, as well as on the exterior of the booth. 
There may be some seating around the outside to allow people to wait, and prime them for the experience, as well as congregate /hang out and have a pop-up DJ, party, and performance.
We would ultimately like to have it be a working phone where people can also call and connect to our collective, collaborate, discuss, and also find support as needed.
fields.Abridged Project Text:
Will you...
Answer the Call?

It is meant to directly engage the viewer one by one, and other people observing as each person enters the phone booth.
The audience can connect directly with Rasza for future participation, ask questions and contribute to the piece.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Yes it will highlight that our ultimate and final judgment is self judgment. We suffer when we live out of alignment with what matters most. Challenge self-limiting beliefs to be set free.

"Your only redemption is to care, and respond, and while you're at it you better party like there's no tomorrow.
In recognition of your life, embedded in this cosmic web of all life...We can love ourselves and each other so hard that we are reborn into a world of a miraculous future. 
Your only death is the limitations you've committed to."

fields.Project Description:
I envision the work unfolding in an intimate, enclosed space — ideally a small room, closet, or other compressed area at the Estates. If that is not possible, I would construct a modest plywood structure on the beach, creating a contained environment that feels confessional, protective, and psychologically charged. The smallness is essential; the viewer should feel as though they are stepping inside a private interior — almost inside a memory, or inside a reckoning.

At the center of the installation is a broken mirror bearing the text: “Mirror, mirror on the wall; you became your mother after all.” The fractured surface reflects the viewer in pieces, implicating them in the narrative of inheritance, resemblance, and inevitability. The mirror becomes both accusation and revelation — a site of judgement.

The work explores the complexity of a mother-daughter relationship at the threshold of loss — dead mother, dying mother, still alive but still haunting. It moves through emotional contradictions: trying not to become her / realizing I am like her. I can’t forgive / I forgive. Fuck her / she did the best she could with what she had. The process of reckoning is nonlinear and unresolved.

As her end approaches, she grows physically weaker, softer, losing her edge. In response, I soften too. There is a role reversal: to some extent she protected me as a child; now I must protect her. The installation holds this shift — anger and tenderness occupying the same space.

Small symbolic objects are placed throughout — fragments of domestic life, items that carry memory. Pages from journals written by a younger version of myself appear alongside texts directly on the walls, layered like internal dialogue. Photographs may surface partially obscured or damaged, echoing the broken mirror.

The piece becomes a space to process inheritance, resentment, protection, and forgiveness — a final reckoning not with heaven or hell, but with lineage. In the end, The Last Judgement is not about punishment; it is about confronting what we carry, what we repeat, and whether we can choose compassion over condemnation.
fields.Abridged Project Text: An intimate installation within a small space, centered on a broken mirror: “Mirror, mirror on the wall; you became your mother after all.” As my mother ages, weakens, and the end draws closer, the work confronts role reversal, resentment, and forgiveness—staging a deeply personal Last Judgement.
fields.Technical Support: I would like a little space at the Estates or at a similar venue.
fields.Audience Engagement: Viewers are invited to touch objects, open small boxes with items, and read pages from journals. They may also write their own reflections directly into a shared journal or onto the walls, becoming part of the evolving dialogue around memory, judgement, forgiveness, and inheritance.

Tension builds as societal collapse feels imminent, but a suspicious calm challenges our assumptions of Western values, and inspire curiosity in imagined alternatives.

fields.Theme Engagement:
This project is newly conceived, and would be presented for the first time at the Bombay Beach Biennale.

First, Precision is a fit for Bombay. The Salton Sea already already feels like a message from the future, as its landscape embodies:
- climate adaptation
- surreal persistence
- beautiful collapse
- strange new beginnings

This project shares that spirit. It is playful, philosophical, unsettling, speculative, and deeply interested in how we adapt when our foundational stories stop working. Bombay Beach is the perfect place to begin telling this story.

Next, Precision is also a fit for the Biennale. This fictive world explores a future that arrives not through catastrophe, but through a value system brought to its natural, absurd end. What comes next will reframe the social contract to account for non-human sources of trust and power, and challenge notions of human purpose, happiness, and flourishing– all while resisting clear moral categories.

Finally, this story also matches the broader social moment. The landscape is bleak, and everyone’s mind is on the imminent future. How bad will it be, and what choices do we have left? Precision explores these questions from a future vantage, offering a constellation of dots to connect backward as we try to make sense of both new tech and the ominously familiar forces we already face daily.
fields.Risk & Safety: Ideas are a most subtle & surreptitious risk to complacency
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Sure haven't!
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): The inspiration for this work just caught me as a sort of confluence of many interests I've held over the years, and so I'm try to feed it as much energy as possible right now. Coincidentally, BBBX is on the horizon and your theme is a perfect match, so this will give me a goal AND offer beachgoers a fun door for intellectual surprises :)

fields.Project Description:
I am making a philosophical computer that allows to produce physical combinations of philosophical concepts. I realized that we had only two alphabets to talk about concepts: either words or numbers. None of this alphabet s are adequate to create new concepts. So I created a new alphabet made entirely of symbols. The goal is to make equations with these symbols in order to calculate visually future philosophical concepts. This symbols are not random, I deduced all of them from the three foundational symbols of our civilization: the totem, the cross and the neo-platonic One. These three symbols are the vertical, the vertical intersecting the horizontal and the circle. For two years I visually deduced 54 Symbols from the first three. I discovered that the major symbols of the West are ductible and transform into each other to create new forms. The philosophical computer is a god machine that allows to calculate new essential concept for the future. The permutation of the sculpture allow to exit philosophical dogmatism and to exit the idea that the future is made of the negation of the past. The history of the West has shown unfortunately many times that an ideology is replaced by an anti-ideology that it self becomes an ideology and often anti-dogmatism becomes dogmatism. To exit this repetition, I would like to introduce through my philosophical computer the idea of permutability of the concept.
I will make a lecture with the sculpture of the philosophical computer in my hand and I will donate the sculpture to the permanent collection of Bombay Beach.
fields.Support Needed: I want to donate the philosophical computer to the permanent collection of Bombay Beach
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Philosophical Computer is a philosophical sculpture entirely permutable that is made of 54 symbols of the great philosophical revolutions of the western world from the totemic God to Post-modernity. The goal is to articulate these symbols together to create new contemporary cultural synthesis.
fields.Technical Support: I do not need any technical support. I just need a scheduled lecture in the program that will have the following title: THE PHILOSOPHICAL COMPUTER OR THE PERMUTABILITY OF THE RELIGIOUS EXPERIENCE BY MARC VINCIGUERRA. I just would love to give this lecture in front of the Portal that I created in 2022.
fields.Audience Engagement: The engagement is through the lecture, I will read my lecture on the philosophical computer and I will carry the object in my hand. I will permute the symbols in front of the audience and explain how they can make their own philosophical equation. To make it theatrical I will give to some people in the audience mini-philosophical computers with an instruction manual that they can keep and they can play with concepts.
fields.Theme Engagement: The relation with the Last Jugement is immense. The philosophical computer shows that you can think beyond absolutism or relativism or worse the absolutism of relativism. In the case of the religious experience the notion of philosophical permutation destroys dogma because a dogma wether it is religious or not is a non- permutable cube that has only one symbol on the six facets of the cubes. So a one dimensional cube is destined to be a judgment machine because there is only One concept which will necessarily enter in a war with other concepts that also claim to be the One. The philosophical computer teaches us how to make concepts from multiplicity and instead of judging multiplicity from one concept. By making conceptual permutations we exit the reification of the cube by making unexpected combinations which allow us for exemple to find religious experiences outside of religion. Both religion and secularization can become dogmas but by permuting the philosophical cube you can find secularism in religion and religion in secularization and many other equations that destroy the idea of jugement. There is no final jugement, there is only a permutable judgment which means that each epoch has its own philosophical equation. Therefore the final jugement is not a reified God that judges man, the final judgment is between man and him-self with the immense responsability to make the right philosophical equation that is not going to turn against him.
fields.Risk & Safety: No risk here just a lecture and the sculpture that I will hold in my hand.
fields.Location Submittal: In front of the Portal.

fields.Project Description: During Mars this year, I will be teaching semiconductor concepts through the sensual medium of baked pie and tarts. It's a play on industrial processes being very precise, logical, rigid, and cold to print billions of nanometer sized transistors on circular wafers to power the AI race in contrast with the softness and sensuality of circular pies and tarts. It's juxtaposing one type of circle to another, and there's a poetic energy to having humans eat transistors that I'm marinating in my head. Every week to other week, I will be hosting a mahjong evening at Mars with an overview of a semiconductor concept taught through and visualized with a pie/tart design. We will then play Taiwanese style mahjong and eat the pie. I will be creating a pie or tart with a specific semiconductor concept visualized starting with basic circuitry concepts such as NOT/OR/AND gates. I will then visualize the latest gate architectures on a pie or tart that make nanometer transistors possible. Some of the designs and recipes are a bit inspired by a book called Pieometry that shows various ways to combine geometry and pie design, but I won't be as focused on math.
fields.Abridged Project Text: As Moore's Law stopped being true in the 2020s and the US is making advanced AI chips again, a semisensualconductor explores a more organic, sensory pie experience of the latest innovations in making atomic sized transistors that power the AI race. What does it mean for a human to eat a transistor?
fields.Technical Support: Electricity, sound, projection/screen for the talk, preferably indoor
fields.Audience Engagement: For the Biennale, I will be giving a talk on how this project progressed at Mars as well as an overview of the details of the TSMC semiconductor efforts in Phoenix as part of my larger film projects. I will show a snippet of a video I filmed of a TSMC engineer as part of my larger film projects. I may also create a pie experience at my Biennale talk to showcase some of the pies made at Mars along with the transistor gate architecture designs on the pie.
fields.Theme Engagement: Moore's Law is the notion that the numbers of transistors that can fit on a microchip doubles every 2 years, and the term was invented by Intel co-founder Gordon Moore in 1965. This Law was valid for many decades until the 2020s when transistors approached lower single digit nanometer size. Transistors aren't traditional on/off switch architectures anymore - at a width of a few atoms, they are operating more as probability fields for where electrons might move. Currently, 2 and 3 nanometer chips are being produced as the most cutting edge chips to power the AI race, and even 1.4 nm is in development in Taiwan by the world's top chip manufacturer, TSMC. But what happens after transistors reach 1 atom's width? What would be past the atom? When we reach the end of the tiniest transistors, we transition from the atomic even more into the quantum field. New materials will have to be integrated, new architectures constantly tested. The end of traditional Moore's Law coinciding with the acceleration of the AI race feels like a reckoning or at least a technological and geopolitical transition point, and pie adds a bit of playful bacchanal energy to throw our hands up in the air at it all.
fields.Risk & Safety: No
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
This work feels like building on the technical and anthropological learnings from being immersed with the recently immigrated industrial Taiwanese community working for the newly built TSMC chip factory in Phoenix, Arizona. I recently gave a thunder talk on this topic at Mars. The US has been looking to bring semiconductor manufacturing back to the US and produce advanced AI chips for geopolitical and supply chain reasons, and so this is a big inflection point for US industry. The US has given TSMC $6.6 billion in grants from the 2022 CHIPS Act and with the total overall investment from TSMC growing from an initial $65 billion to $165 billion. This will result in 6 total factories being built north of Phoenix that will produce advanced AI chips. The first fabrication plant was completed and went into production a year ago in early 2025 to produce 4nm chips. Even though the total production yield for TSMC's new Arizona plants will only reach 1/5 or even less of the capacity of the plants in Taiwan, it signals a more active shift of countries such as the US trying to create more self reliance on a technology that is really the new oil in the age of AI. Countries who control the production of the latest advanced chips control the development of AI progress in many ways. 

This project will be an active engagement with the minds and tastebuds of Mars College as well as those who see the presentation at the Biennale. Another Martian, Carlota, and I are collaborating on some parts of the pie making process, and she is also welding metal shapes to cut designs with as well. Through hosting mahjong game nights and discussing semiconductor architecture through the pie designs, this project will be a highly interactive, playful, and thought provoking experience with the Mars and Biennale communities.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach and Imperial County have been the site for various industrial processes, including lithium extraction and all of its controversies, dynamics, conversations, etc. I have been fascinated by industrial processes in its various forms, and I want to showcase AI race infrastructure specifically to highlight the parts of AI that are very tangible, physical, and anthropological rather than just interfacing with an AI model through a computer. I like playing around with making infrastructure sensuous, more playful, and a bit unexpected. Bombay Beach and Mars are places for introducing the disparate and the unexpected highlighted against the at times harsh environment.  

I am a second year Martian who tends to combine disparate worlds together to create immersive, participatory art experiences. Last year, I collaborated with pseudo and other Martians to make an experimental film gathering Martians for a computational dance in the desert to find the dodecahedron. Since Mars has a heavy AI learning focus and I have been researching the industrial infrastructure side of AI development in Arizona, I wanted to make an experience that showcases the industrial side of AI in a more unexpected physical way that incorporates Martians, and bring the outcomes of that experience to showcase at the Biennale.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): First tests of the concept and developing the designs for semiconductor pie and mahjong evenings. The first will happen in the coming few weeks in February at Mars.

fields.Project Description:
this will be an academic lecture -- part philosophy, part theology, part ethnography of the major camps in AI discourse and how they map onto classical apocalyptic postures: the accelerationists as millennialists racing toward the revelation; the doomers as prophets of wrath; the simulation hypothesis believers who claim that, in a way, the world has already ended; the safety researchers as a priestly class trying to negotiate terms with the coming power. each of these is a distinct theological position.

the talk will also ask what it means to accept technological change while resisting entrenched power structures and pre-determined narratives, and what it means to treat the incomprehensible as a condition that's always been present, not a future to be prophesied.
fields.Abridged Project Text: AI is our era's apocalyptic narrative. This talk traces how the eschatological frame operates as both a belief system and a power structure, concentrating authority in those who claim to understand the coming transformation. how can we engage with the change without submitting to this mythology?

fields.Audience Engagement: i'm envisioning people watching + asking questions. 45 min talk + 15 min Q&A
fields.Theme Engagement: yes! apocalypse narratives
fields.Risk & Safety: only infohazards perhaps
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): no
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
i've spent the past year researching the various quasi-religious perspectives people in the tech world have on AI. what i keep encountering is a discourse that has enormous existential energy but very little originality.

as AI will continues to lead to widespread change, it's important to examine the narratives that might be—intentionally or unintentionally—guiding its development, and keep the door open for imagining better futures.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): the biennale has always been an experiment in how to live and create inside forces you didn't author and can't fully comprehend — ecological collapse, institutional abandonment. The question bombay beach asks — how do you make something that matters when the ground is literally receding beneath you — is the question AI eschatology has no answer for, because it only knows how to think in terms of outcomes: extinction or utopia, doom or salvation. but there has always been a secret third way: you show up, you participate, you make something. that is a posture toward the incomprehensible that the entire AI discourse is missing.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): early conception of this particular framework; have been researching these ideas for years

fields.Project Description:
I have written a book. It's called Empathy Bleed.  

I will display a handbound artist edition (the first copy of the book) on a plinth in the Bombay Beach Museum of Bombay Beach for the duration of the Biennale,  (then for as long as it is welcome to live there), and share excerpts in a live reading in the space upstairs from the museum. 

Book display (downstairs): a hand bound artist edition of the book will be displayed on a plinth beside a reading chair, backdropped by a wash of fabric hanging from the ceiling with the words empathy bleed patched in. Ideally, this will be set against the back wall of the museum. 

Live reading (upstairs): three readers – Janenne Willis, Regina Pharoah Fields and Dulcinee DuGuere – will offer excerpts from the book in a live reading that feels like a hybrid between a philosophy talk and poetry recitation. (Side note: this offering could metaphorically or physically link the Philosophy Conference and the Poetry Salon). Listeners will gather around the table overlooking the Salton Sea, decorated in aesthetical alignment with the book display downstairs (including fabric, water vessels filled with sea water, incense smoke, small pocket size images and words of sea). At the end of the reading the readers will take the book, travel downstairs and offer it in a ceremonial moment to the plinth in the museum.
fields.Support Needed: The date/time is to be determined. As discussed with Dulcinee, I'm open to scheduling the live reading at a time that works best within the Biennale programming, and flows for Regina and Dulcinee, as comrade readers. So far we have slated a daytime moment. Day TBC. I'm also planning to activate my exisiting SEA STATE and UNANSWERABLE QUESTIONS installations with lighting and sound – so please pop them on the map :)
fields.Location (Internal): Bombay Beach Museum of Bombay Beach
fields.Abridged Project Text: Empathy Bleed traces two contrasting bodies of water – California’s receding Salton Sea/ancient Lake Cahuilla and the biodiverse Western Port/Wurnmarrinh in Australia – exploring the charged space in between, empathy and breath. An artist edition of the book, and a live reading with be offered.
fields.Technical Support:
Book display - not essential, ideally I would like to put a lamp beside the reading chair if their is power available

Live reading - possibly no, possibly three microphones and a speaker (I'll be guided by the Biennale team as to whether this is needed in the proposed space?)
fields.Audience Engagement:
Book display: audiences can come and go as they please, anytime of day or night, to sit and read the book in (the relative haven of) the museum

Live reading: audiences are invited to gather for forty minutes to listen to excerpts of the book being read aloud by three readers, upstairs from the muesum (30mins reading, 10mins travelling downstairs to offer the book to the museum)
fields.Theme Engagement:
Yes.

Empathy Bleed is a book rooted in the receding Salton Sea. In the context of The Last Judgement, it is already standing at the tribunal. It treats the Sea not as backdrop, but as witness and evidence – a mirror of our excess, denial, and strange devotion.

By placing the Salton Sea (ancient Lake Cahuilla) in dialogue with the biodiverse Western Port (Wurnmarrinh), Empathy Bleed stages a comparative reckoning: one sea receding under the weight of human intervention, the other still breathing with multiplicity. Between them lies the charged space of othering – an atmospheric corridor of feeling that binds distant ecologies and implicates us all.

The book is both confession and counter-ritual. It asks:
What does sea say in the face of death?
Can empathy function as testimony?
Is breath a form of prayer – or evidence?

Amid the apocalypse, Empathy Bleed offers a quiet but no less baroque gesture: listening. It proposes that judgement is not only spectacle, but intimacy – an inhalation of shared consequence and exhalation of felt grief.
If collapse is choreography, then this work attends to its tempo. It suggests that before resurrection or bacchanal, there is a pause – a long breath out – where we tune in to whether we are spectators, sinners, or kin.
fields.Risk & Safety: No
fields.Location Submittal: Book display: Bombay Beach Museum of Bombay Beach (downstairs). Live reading: Bombay Beach Museum of Bombay Beach (upstairs).

fields.Project Description: Prepared Tape Loops is a live musical performance built from handmade cassette tape loops of varying lengths, played through old Walkmans arranged on a tabletop system. Each loop contains fragments of melody, texture, or rhythm. As multiple Walkmans run simultaneously, the loops drift in and out of sync, creating evolving harmonies and subtle rhythmic shifts over time. The setup is visible and tactile: aging Walkmans, prepared tape loops, and a simple chain of guitar pedals used to shape tone and space. The audience can see the spools turning and hear the gentle warble of magnetic tape in motion. Nothing is hidden; the mechanics of the medium are part of the experience. The sound is layered, melodic, and atmospheric rather than abrasive. Hiss and instability are folded into the music, not treated as noise. In Bombay Beach, the piece can function as a focused set performance. Whether indoors or outdoors, the work invites close listening and quiet attention. The physicality of aging tape technology, repeating and slowly drifting, creates an immersive experience shaped by time, chance, and mechanical fragility.
fields.Support Needed: I’ve visited the Salton Sea several times, attended the Biennale in the past, and have spent time staying in Bombay Beach. I’m excited about the possibility of presenting this evolving work within a community and landscape I already feel connected to.
fields.Notes: email, zig zag & time, we will connect with cody, sound tech. Cody's been connected with the chinese band, group chat
fields.Abridged Project Text: A live musical performance using prepared cassette tape loops of various lengths as source material. Old Walkmans and effect pedal chains build layered, melodic sound in real time. The loops drift in and out of sync as hiss and gentle warble become part of the music.
fields.Technical Support: I require access to standard electricity and a PA system. Otherwise I am fully self-contained and will provide my tabletop setup, including Walkmans, prepared tape loops, mixer, pedals, cabling, and table. I will run sound directly from my mixer to the PA. No projection or special lighting is needed. The setup is low-maintenance and adaptable to a range of spaces.
fields.Audience Engagement: This is a live musical performance that people watch and listen to. The tabletop system of old Walkmans and prepared tape loops remains fully visible, allowing the audience to witness the mechanics of the sound as it unfolds in real time. Engagement comes through listening and observation.
fields.Theme Engagement: While not a literal interpretation, the piece connects to The Last Judgement through the idea of reckoning with the past. Before any final judgement, there is reflection—an internal revisiting of what has been. Cassette tape holds fragments of sound the way memory holds fragments of experience. As the loops repeat and drift, the performance becomes a quiet process of playback and review, where what resurfaces, what shifts, and what fades is shaped by time.
fields.Risk & Safety: No, none of that is needed.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): The cassette tape loop performance is part of my ongoing practice and has been presented in previous iterations. Each performance evolves based on the specific loops, configuration, and environment. The version proposed for BBB would be shaped specifically for the 2026 Biennale, with adjustments to structure and material, and may include newly prepared tape loops as part of its evolution. This particular configuration would premiere at BBB.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I’m drawn to creating this work now because I’ve become increasingly interested in fragility of memory, of physical media, and of the systems we rely on. Cassette tape is obsolete, yet it still holds sound in a tangible way. It stretches, warbles, and degrades over time. That tension between preservation and erosion feels meaningful in this moment. In the context of Bombay Beach and the Salton Sea, erosion carries both environmental and emotional weight. The landscape holds layers of history, ambition, and collapse. Prepared tape loops, repeating and slowly drifting out of sync, echo that persistence and breakdown happening at once. Through this work, I’m asking what remains. What happens when we revisit fragments of the past? What changes through repetition? As the loops phase and shift, they create something larger than their individual parts. I’m interested in how repetition can become immersive and reflective rather than static.
The process is hands-on and experimental. I cut and splice the loops manually, shaping their lengths to influence how they interact. Each performance evolves slightly depending on space, acoustics, and environment.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): I’ve been drawn to the Salton Sea for a while and have already filmed and performed my tape loop setup in the area years ago. There’s something about the stillness and visible wear of the landscape that feels connected to my practice. Cassette tape is fragile and physical. It holds sound, but it also stretches, drifts, and slowly breaks down. The Salton Sea carries a similar sense of time passing and material change. My work doesn’t try to explain the landscape, but it feels at home there. Bombay Beach and the Biennale embrace handmade, process-driven work. A tabletop system of aging Walkmans and looping tape feels aligned with that spirit—simple, visible, and slightly unpredictable. It invites listening and attention, which feels right for this setting.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The concept and mechanics are fully developed, and I’ve already performed earlier iterations of the project. The tabletop system, signal flow, and performance structure are established and functional. For BBB, I would refine the configuration and, time pe
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I am visiting from elsewhere

fields.Project Name: Ashes and Breath: A Rite of Impermanence
fields.Project Description:
This project is a live sonic ritual combining acoustic instruments, voice, synthesizers, electronic systems, bodily movement, and fire within a shared performance environment. The overall musical atmosphere is cinematic and epic in scale, emphasizing extended duration, extreme dynamics, and spatial pressure. The ensemble includes dagu (taiko-style drums), tanbur, Tibetan horn, suona, sheng, pipa, shakuhachi, hurdy-gurdy, layered percussion, a throat singing choir, a female vocalist, a fire dancer, and performers operating electronic synths and gesture-based controller systems.

Gesture control plays a central role in the performance. A range of body-driven control systems—including MIDI gloves, arm-mounted sensors, ring-based controllers, and several original custom-built controllers—are deeply integrated with synthesizers and electronic processing. These systems translate physical movement, direction, and intensity directly into sound, shaping timbre, density, low-frequency structure, spatialization, and electronic effects in real time. Electronic sound is not pre-sequenced or ornamental, but continuously formed through embodied action.

Acoustic instruments and voice are treated as malleable sonic materials. Breath-driven winds, plucked and bowed strings, ritual percussion, and extended vocal techniques are continuously fused, distorted, and reconfigured through synthesizers, electronic effects, and gesture-controlled interaction. Poems and non-religious chants are introduced as sonic elements rather than narrative text, operating through repetition, fragmentation, and resonance.

The performance unfolds in chapters, moving through themes of life, impermanence, suffering, and death, with music and poetry forming the narrative structure rather than linear storytelling. Spatially, the work takes place at ground level, with performers and audience sharing the same physical plane. Black robes reduce visual hierarchy, while fire introduces heat, light, and instability as an active element of the environment.

Sound accumulates over time without release. In the final phase, the performance concludes with a sustained barrage of aggressive drops and sub-bass force, ending not in resolution but in collapse.
fields.Support Needed: Thank you for the opportunity to share this work.  I approach this project with seriousness, adaptability, and respect for the land, the community, and the Biennale’s evolving vision. I am ready to develop it fully in dialogue with the site.
fields.Notes: email, zig zag & time, we will connect with cody, sound tech. Cody's been connected with the chinese band, group chat
fields.Abridged Project Text:
Here, judgement is felt, not delivered.
A sonic ritual using throat singing, ethnic instruments, fire dance, sub-bass and electronic interaction. Rooted in the ancient, oriented toward the future, the performance moves through rupture and collapse, exposing the impermanent nature of mind and matter.
fields.Music Type: • Experimental  • World / Traditional Fusion  • Electronic  • Sound Art  • Performance Ritual
fields.Technical Support:
Audio / Sound
Total microphones required: 6 ultra-narrow directional microphones. Supercardioid or hypercardioid dynamic microphones are strongly preferred.
Microphone Stands
6 Low-profile / short microphone stands 
Input
A minimum of 10 audio inputs is required. The mixing console must provide independent gain, EQ, and dynamics on all inputs.
Monitoring
2 in-ear monitor (IEM) feeds are required (stereo preferred, mono acceptable). Stage monitors / wedges are requested if available.
PA System
A large-scale PA system capable of high SPL with strong, full low-frequency response is required. 
Sound Engineer
A house sound engineer is preferred. If unavailable, performers will bring their own sound engineer.
Power
Multiple power outlets are required on stage .
Lighting / Visuals (Optional)
Stage lighting is preferred but not required. LED screen or projection is welcome but optional.
Advance Communication
If the venue cannot provide the full number of required microphones or other technical equipment, please inform us in advance. We can bring additional equipment or arrange rentals if necessary.
Setup & Soundcheck
Adequate time is required for setup, line check, and soundcheck.
fields.Audience Engagement: At various moments throughout the performance, performers will guide rhythm and collective resonance, inviting audiences to move their bodies and, at certain points, join in humming or non-religious chanting. Participation is always voluntary and without fixed rules or expectations. Audiences may enter, remain, move, withdraw, or return at any time. Interaction emerges through sound, gesture-driven electronic systems, fire, and the shared energy of the space, rather than through instruction or obligation.
fields.Theme Engagement: “The Last Judgement,” in our theme, is a self-initiated reckoning in a world collapsing—no divine court, no savior, just the bewildered deciding meaning amid chaos. This mirrors impermanence: both acknowledge that suffering, death, and change are inescapable. The difference is that impermanence is an ongoing state, while judgement is a symbolic threshold. In our performance, suffering, death, and impermanence are not endpoints but the ongoing process of judgment itself. We endure judgement in change, seek meaning in suffering, and ultimately, we face existence in this impermanent “judgement”—no external redemption, no eternal answers, only the continued feeling, facing, and moving together into uncertain renewal.
fields.Risk & Safety: This project may involve controlled fire performed by experienced fire dancers. All fire activity will follow established safety protocols and be carried out only by designated performers. Fuel will be stored in approved, sealed containers away from the audience. A clearly marked fire perimeter will be established, with fire blankets, extinguishers, and water on site. Performances will not proceed in high wind conditions outdoors. Flammable materials will be cleared, and all flames fully extinguished and checked. If the venue has any safety concerns, the performance can proceed without fire.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant):
This project has not been shown, performed in any previous form. It is a newly conceived work developed specifically for its premiere at the 2026 Bombay Beach Biennale.

The conceptual direction of the piece was shaped in direct response to the Biennale’s 2026 theme, The Last Judgement, which inspired the work’s focus on collective reckoning, collapse, and renewal. While it grows out of my broader artistic practice, the structure, composition, staging, and integrated gesture-control systems are entirely new and have not been publicly presented.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create and premiere this work at the 2026 Bombay Beach Biennale because I was struck by the tone of this year’s theme, The Last Judgement. It does not describe a divine verdict handed down from above. Instead, it suggests a collective reckoning within a world already in collapse—people gathering themselves to decide what remains, what has failed, and what must be faced. There is dust, absurdity, carnival, and pain. For me, “the last judgement” is not an endpoint, but a moment of forced clarity: how do we remain honest in our bodies and perceptions amid chaos, decay, and instability? What are we responsible for? What, if anything, can endure?

This work grows out of my long-standing reflection on impermanence and suffering. Change does not ask permission. Bodies age, relationships fracture, systems erode, and death offers no explanation. In this sense, judgement is rarely a singular moment of decision; it is time exerting pressure—layer by layer, stripping away illusion. It compels us to move beyond binary oppositions (right/wrong, salvation/destruction, beginning/end) and to recognize that endings and beginnings are phases of the same movement. What we call “last” becomes another beginning, and every beginning is already moving toward another ending.

Through music, I seek to practice a decolonial and plural vision. I refuse to treat “ethnic” or “traditional” sounds as decorative samples or exotic signifiers. Instead, I approach diverse sonic traditions and embodied techniques as equal systems of knowledge and contemporary language. By integrating original gesture-based controllers and electronic systems, I aim to propel these traditions forward—into friction with the present—rather than preserving them as artifacts.

The creative process will be experimental and collaborative: developing a chapter-based structure, shaping poetry and non-religious chant materials, constructing gesture-driven sonic architectures, and, where possible, integrating light and fire into the spatial field. Reckoning will not remain metaphorical—it will be felt physically through sound pressure, proximity, and collective presence.

I am not asking how to be saved. I am asking: in a place like Bombay Beach—beautiful and brutal, luminous and toxic—how do we remain capable of honest sensation, shared endurance, and transforming reckoning into a lived act of presence?
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach exists at the edge—geographically, economically, culturally. Long dismissed as failed or peripheral, it has become a site where meaning is rebuilt within collapse. The Salton Sea, born from human miscalculation, reflects a larger truth: what the center calls ruin, the margin learns to inhabit—and reinvent.

This condition resonates with my position. Shaped by Manchu, Han, and Mongolian musical lineages and collaborating with artists from diverse traditions, I stand at the intersection of multiple sonic histories shaped by marginalization. These traditions are often displayed rather than activated, archived rather than evolved—preserved in museums instead of operating as living forces.

My practice resists that logic. By integrating traditional instruments and vocal techniques with original gesture-based controllers and experimental electronic systems, I reposition these sounds as active agents within contemporary structures—operational and future-oriented.

The Last Judgement speaks to reckoning within collapse. In Bombay Beach, judgement is not imposed from above—it is negotiated at the edge. This work extends that negotiation into sound, asking what remains when hierarchies weaken and centralized systems dissolve.

Bombay Beach is not a backdrop but a parallel condition. In shared marginality, collapse does not silence—it reorganizes possibility. When the center declines, the future begins at the margins.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The project is currently in development. The conceptual framework and chapter structure are established, and composition of music and text has begun. One initial rehearsal has been completed to test the integration of acoustic instruments, voice, gesture-

fields.Project Description:
I would like to see this as a performance that dovetails into my own DJ set ideally at the SubClub (Friday night). It could serve as an opener to the Friday night DJing. 

I imagine 16 bacterial samples, with milk jugs containing purple bacteria**, and electrodes poking out the top. Each microbial jar gets mapped to a digital instrument, played through sound software on my laptop.

I would control the audio levels of each one, which would be the only human-driven performance itself. The bacterial choose when to play, based on which sample is more active at a given moment. The performance is non-predictive, and truly improvisational.

There are 3D-printed bases that light up with LEDs when that microbial sample is played, creating a visual link between the bacterial sample and what viewers here.

I could imagine these in the SubClub, on the nooks in the walls and on tables. They could be decentralized for people to look at, as they operate on batteries. There are many ways this could be displayed.

I have a crude version of this project already working! It's not been shown publicly yet, but the wiring and the tech is already done.

I'd be developing the public performance and engagement aspects of this, along with the music development during my time here in February & March.

** Purple bacteria do look purple and they are photosynthesizing, anaerobic bacteria that existed before the great oxygenation event many millions of years ago. They are extremophiles and found in select alkaline environments such as the shores of the Salton Sea.
fields.Notes: email, zig zag & time, we will connect with cody, sound tech. Cody's been connected with the chinese band, group chat
fields.Abridged Project Text:
Last year, I collected microbial samples from the Salton Sea at Bombay Beach. They produce significant electrical signals, like small batteries that vary with sunlight.

This will be a performance where I map these into musical notes with 16 different sample jars and LEDs on each.
fields.Technical Support:
Electrical power and sound system.

If we don't use the SubClub could use my own sound system, which is more than adequate, tops and subwoofer, but that venue feels ideal since it is enclosed and on the beach itself, and can host a large number of people.
fields.Audience Engagement:
I would like this to be a scheduled event. The event itself could be 30 minutes.

If we move this away from the SubClub, it could be a talk + performance. However, the SubClub is a natural spot for congregation, and I've already been in conversation with Tao about doing some DJ/programming for the Biennale on Friday night (to be unfolded still).

We could start the event with a ritual, some sort of fanfare. A talk about the bacteria and how I collected them (15 minutes), and the performance itself would be 15 minutes, so 30 in total.

Maybe I would have people sit down and meditate. There are many ways, which I want to unfold. We could start with silence. Perhaps I will pass out cards or kombucha samples — a sort of communion relating to the theme. This will be developed in the course of finalizing the project.

During the performance, this is a deep listening event for about 15 minutes, which feels long enough to be immersive but short enough not to drag on. I would like to transition from this music to my own DJ set, if the SubClub scheduling works this way.
fields.Theme Engagement:
At Bombay Beach, the apocalypse already happened. The water receded. The fish died. We have toxic air. Beneath the crusty shoreline, inside brine, mud, and biofilm, microbial life thrives.

It is adaptive and ancient, and will outlive humans, perhaps birthing the next post-human, "great" species.

The work asks: What if a final verdict isn’t spoken by God, but by the organisms that inherit the mess? These is, after all, life, that God created, right?

Bacterial Beats suggests we shift judgement away from the divine and toward ecological consequence.

The Bacteria decompose, transform, and metabolize the ruined landscape. Perhaps they are saviors. They break down what human civilization leaves behind, as life begins anew.

Last Judgement is not fire from the sky, from the divine, but how life continues without us, as humans self-extinguish, colonize or evolve.
fields.Risk & Safety: no
fields.Previous Showing (Grant):
It's new work. I did a rough prototype demo in my studio last November for a small group of friends — and with other bacterial samples — as a proof-of-concept.

But that's about it!

The enclosed .wav is what I have produced so far as a short edit, but I want this to be *much* better. It's not in the it's awesome stage of music right now.

Salton Sea bacteria need to debut their performance at Bombay Beach, after all.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create "Bacterial Beats" now because we are living inside a slow apocalypse that no longer feels hypothetical. The Salton Sea is not a metaphor. It is a place where ecological consequence is visible. The fish died. Shorelines are receding. The air smells like decay, Human consequences feel real here.

For years, my practice has focused on building custom sensors that translate biological and environmental signals into sound. I’m interested in revealing hidden processes rather than composing over them. At Bombay Beach, a place already suspended between collapse and reinvention, that gesture feels urgent.

If "The Last Judgement" is a reckoning, I want to shift the voice of judgement away from divine authority or human spectacle and toward the organisms that inherit our debris.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bacterial Beats is site-specific.

The microbial life in the Sea’s water and sediment is not symbolic. It is real, though we cannot see it. Presenting the work elsewhere would turn it into metaphor. At Bombay Beach, it remains literal.

The sound emerges from the same landscape the audience is in. 

My work shares that ethos of the The Biennale, which embraces improbable transformation. Staging art inside collapse, celebration inside residue. It does not attempt to aestheticize disaster.

It listens. That listening feels aligned with the community here: long-term residents, artists, and experimenters who understand that Bombay Beach is an act of improvisation.

There is also history embedded in the shoreline.  failed resorts, agricultural runoff, abandoned infrastructure. Microbes are physical residues of those histories. Translating that metabolic activity into sound allows the Sea’s hidden processes to "speak".
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Finalizing it for an actual performance. Physical part is designed, need new circuits and LEDs. Sound design will be revised. Working on the public presentation.

fields.Project Description:
Every city needs a temple and every culture needs the sacred. 

For this year’s Bombay Beach Biennale, we wish to stage a beautiful, otherworldly installation within an already existing medium sized room or structure at Bombay Beach.This space would host an hour long ritual and performance tied into my musical project, DIA LUNA, featuring myself (Andrea) on vocals/voice looping, and Jonathan on guitar . 

Heathen Temple: Sanctuary would include an altar focal point and be decked out in beautiful reflective items, containing everything necessary to execute three rituals (outlined below) as one seamless performance repeated two consecutive nights of the festival (Saturday and Sunday, ideally). 

Once completed, the rituals would be made into a short video that could potentially remain at Bombay Beach as part of a permanent gallery collection. 

Here is how we imagine the performance unfolding:

RITUAL I: THE CALL (5 min)

Andrea will call in the directions through song  accompanied by guitarist Jonathan and/or a previously recorded original, and bespoke composition.

She will inform the Temple-goers of what will transpire in this hour-long session and will provide materials for those present to participate. 

 RITUAL II: THE CHAMBER OF ANGELS (air) ( 15 min )

Using the tarot card of Judgement as a thematic jumping off point, Jonathan and Andrea, dressed as cranes, ( the Angels of Bombay Beach ) will explore the themes represented in this card in the form of six completely new, two minute long songs composed specifically for the event, using people/places/things from Bombay Beach as inspiration and as a way to discuss the themes within the card itself.

RITUAL III: MY DUAL HEART ( THE ECSTASY AND THE AGONY)  (fire) ( 20 min )

Andrea and Jonathan will perform five DIA LUNA songs and poems relating to the corporeal experiences of love, loss, war, and all the awakenings that come from them. If we choose to believe that God is Love, what better way to explore the divine, than by communing with the earthly agony and ecstasy of human relationships and relationality, both individual and collective? 

RITUAL III:  SHRED YOUR SINS (earth) SING YOUR PRAYERS (water) (10-15 min)

Andrea will cue a pre-recorded musical composition that reflects the conflict associated with personal faults or wrongdoings. 

Those assembled will be asked to write what they wish to release on a piece of paper they will bring to the altar. As they shred their paper, each “sin” will be given an expression through a guitar solo and vocalizations.

Next, Andrea will ask guests to write a prayer or wish and bring them to the altar. Andrea will sing an improvised closing prayer/song to send those wishes out to the world. 

RITUAL IV: ASCENSION (5 min) 

We will close the directions against a fast-paced club song that will end our time together, which will lead into a curated playlist. Those present will be able to stay and enjoy the space together freely afterwards.
fields.Support Needed: We would like to add that if the Biennale is not able to award us the grant to make the space ourselves, we would still like for you to consider hosting our ritual performance in some ready-made place at Bombay Beach. Of course, our preference is to create the whole kit and kaboodle, but we understand there will be many moving parts for you all to consider. Thank you for reviewing our application, and we very much look forward to coming and participating in the Biennale regardless!

Find sanctuary at the HEATHEN TEMPLE with music alchemists DIA LUNA. We welcome your holy wholeness! Shred your sins and sing your prayers!
fields.Technical Support:
For our project, we would require a small or medium indoor space, or an outdoor space that has at least three walls and a ceiling with an open front so people could enter and/or view from the outside. We would require electricity in order to run the musical gear we intend on using. For our technical set up, we intend on bringing two PA speakers, an amp, a mic and various kinds of lights, fan, and smoke machine to give atmosphere to the space. 

We have included a generator in our budget to potentially add further electrical capabilities to our show. Access to a ladder would be helpful as we will need a way to hang fabric and lights from the ceiling and/or walls. Everything else, we intend on sourcing and bringing ourselves.
