fields.Audience Engagement:
Our ritual performance would be primarily spectator driven, with the exception of the third ritual which would work best with audience participation. We are imagining the Temple as a hybrid installation and ritual space that is an aesthetically alluring container for our performances and a spiritual altar for the community to enjoy. 

For the duration of our hour-long performance, we would want an audience committed to being there for the whole piece. However, once the performance is complete, we would like the space to remain active as an installation in the evenings of the festival even when we are not performing. 

For the off times, we imagine The Temple being outfitted with several recorded sound compositions playing on loop, where people could wander in and use the LED candles and shredder to have a private ceremony. At the end of the Biennale, all shredded paper from the ceremony will be donated to a local community garden as compost or to any local artists who work in paper mache or in forms that could benefit from using the paper matter. 

We will also make available pocket sized printed artworks for visitors to take with them. These tiny prints will feature illustrations of abstracted diaphanous angels, created by Dani Gros.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Our project very much engages with the theme of The Last Judgement. I was raised Catholic, steeped in the rituals of mass and the foundational story that Christ was a savior who died for the sins of humanity. However, my own experiences with music and the occult led me to move away from the institutionalized experience of Catholicism into a practice of sacred creativity, embodied ritual, and communion with nature. 

Presently, my personal judgement is that many people need more spaces and practices to assist them in transmuting the raw lead of negative human emotions into alchemical gold. Many also need entry points to validation and connection, and a return to an experience of the sacred that does not regard the flaws of humanity with judgement, scorn, or repression. 

We are considering the Temple as our answer to a way forward, a space that reflects the spiritual connotations of the Last Judgement Theme, but with the things that we find deeply sacred: Creating beauty with our loved ones, having meaningful fun, and crafting a home for big feelings to find integration. Each ritual has the potential to awaken the audience into a refreshed judgement or awareness of themselves.
fields.Risk & Safety: We do not anticipate there will be anything hazardous or dangerous in our space or presentation. We are happy to check in with the space regularly to see that it remains tidy, intact, and hospitable.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, we have not created this installation or performed these rituals in any other space.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
When I first arrived at the Biennale a few years ago, I was struck by the specific ways in which the land itself, along with the blossoming art community present, is a ripe metaphor for larger cultural questions, experimental ways of being, and strategies for survival in a world that is becoming increasingly strange and unmoored. 

We want to create this work for many reasons. Presently, we feel that in our secular culture, music and art are the primary ways in which people have the opportunity to experience the divine in a personal, connective, and non-judgemental way. We believe that part of what ails us culturally is a judgement of humanity so extreme it leads people to repress themselves to the point of snapping into oppressive and otherwise harmful ways of being. We believe that by building The Temple, filled with beauty and light, sensuality, clarity and compassion, embodied art and music, that we will provide the participants a kind of spiritual nourishment that they can carry well beyond the Biennale

Our Temple asks if it’s possible to witness our own messy and complex human experience in tenderness and accountability. Our Temple asks how we can preserve the connection to ourselves, each other, and the magic of the universe through ritual, even as we hurtle towards the future.

Creating this piece together in Bombay Beach would be a natural expansion of our friendship and creative partnerships. The three of us share artistic practices that center rich, cinematic aesthetics, a focus on preserving and honoring the natural world, and a commitment to soulful authenticity and expression. Even our initial introduction to each other was due to these shared values. We met in the same film block at the SLO Film festival, where both of our respective films were featured. Jonathan’s film, Clear, was shot in the mountains of Iceland, as part of his music video series drawing attention to climate change by featuring unique landscapes across the world. Wilderness, the music video Dani and I created and submitted, was shot in the woods of Long Island and took a more introspective approach, using nature-based visuals to underscore the emotional themes of the song: change, dissolution, and rebirth. Both videos featured the land as a character itself, providing wisdom and context for our stories to unfold. Ultimately this kinship led to Jonathan becoming my primary guitar player and musical partner in Los Angeles. 

Building The Temple to house the music and ritual of DIA LUNA would be an extraordinary opportunity for us to collaborate as a collective. We are very eager to put our artistic skills and talents together to craft a magnificent space and experience for those participating in the Biennale to connect to themselves and to the location in a meaningful and beautiful way.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is a place where time bends and varied dualities shine in an incredible microcosm of the world. Recently, I came across Octavia Butler’s idea that “God is Change,” a powerful sentiment which Bombay Beach, a town in constant metamorphosis, perfectly encapsulates. The Heathen Temple would offer a home for people to integrate their own contradictions and evolutions through musical ritual.

In many Indigenous communities, water sources are considered sacred, with worship sites often built nearby, underscoring the intimate relationship between community, land, and Spirit. The Salton Sea offers literal sanctuary to birds in need of habitat and rest. These birds offer a parallel to artists and other marginalized communities nearby, “rare birds” themselves who exist on the fringes of mainstream culture, increasingly threatened by corporate and technological encroachment. Our Temple would build from Indigenous forms of healing and community, leveraging the potent power of art and song to create sacred space to validate the unique nature of all who enter.

The performances themselves are also relevant to Bombay Beach. For example, The Chamber of Angels ritual will incorporate stories and visuals tied to the landscape both in the sound and writing. My Dual Heart on the other hand, will showcase the human condition through my personal lens, sharing my past, present, and future selves, healed and unhealed parts, mirroring the complex duality present at Bombay Beach.

fields.Project Description:
I’m making a live music and clown performance(1-3 sets) built around some new songs and some songs from my just released album *Interruptions*. The music is lo-fi experimental pop. Some songs land gently, others derail on purpose (with one comedy song sprinkled in there called “Hey Todd” - heartfelt song sung from the perspective of a young man’s penis). 

Clown-Wise: Post-fire, a clown in a loud, squeaky hazmat suit is tasked with throwing away belongings labeled “toxic.” The problem is that the objects are innocent and joyful - disco balls, wind chimes, stuffed animals, drums, crystals - which makes the whole thing feel unnecessary, absurd, and emotionally specific. Every movement squeaks. The silent desert gets to watch.

The performance unfolds loosely and durationally. People can drift in and out. There’s no big arc, no climax, no cleanup. When things get too heavy, the piece pauses for grounding gestures - including playing a simple sound recording of a long-lost dog eating cottage cheese.

Logistically, the setup is minimal and self-contained. I’ll be using my own small busking amp and am fully self-reliant for sound and power. The piece is designed to adapt to the conditions of Bombay Beach - wind, dust, distraction, silence - and to meet the environment where it already is.

fields.Theme Engagement: The way our project relates to the 2026 theme of The Last Judgement is rather interpersonal. To be candid, the project of SHRiNKRAY has been hard to maintain these days. With changing lives and evolving passions emphasis has shifted and the future of the project is somewhat up in the air. In applying for this festival, my intention is to give this group another chance to come together and create once more, possibly for the last time ever. Not with the tone of one last chance or of a dying breath but with flavors of acceptance and transformation. I feel this relates to the theme of The Last Judgement as we exist in a world today where everything seems perfectly placed for a fall. Nothing is for certain and we seem to be getting in our own way about it. However maybe this shouldn’t be mourned but rather celebrated.

fields.Project Description:
Dancing in the Dust is a live performance shaped by the idea of Final Judgment, not as a single apocalyptic event, but as a condition we are already living inside. It asks what it means to remain human, creative, and embodied after reckoning has occurred, after ideals have failed, and after consequences are no longer abstract. The title comes from a simple refrain, dancing in the dust on Bombay Beach, which serves as both image and philosophy: movement after collapse, presence without denial.
The performance is driven by music that enters with immediacy and emotional force, captivating the audience and drawing them into a shared interior space. The songs evoke nostalgia and longing, carrying traces of intimacy, belief, and futures once imagined. Beneath that familiarity runs an unease that raises existential questions: What survives scrutiny? What is worth holding onto after everything has been weighed? How do we continue once judgment no longer feels hypothetical?
This emotional framework mirrors the philosophical outlook of Bombay Beach itself, a place that feels like the world after the verdict. Built on promise, undone by consequence, and still inhabited by artists and outsiders who choose to remain. In this context, dancing in the dust is not optimism or escape, but an act of refusal: choosing movement over paralysis, creation over erasure, embodiment over despair.
Visual elements extend this language without resolving it. Abstract, memory-driven imagery, bodies, fire, water, light, appears fragmented and unresolved, like residue or evidence rather than narrative. Choreographed dancers move with restraint and repetition, grounded and physical, embodying endurance rather than spectacle. Their movements suggest bodies under observation—not condemned, but seen—continuing to move after the accounting.
Light shapes the experience through cycles of revelation and concealment, echoing the tension between judgment and mercy, clarity and doubt. A restrained, momentary use of fireworks or pyrotechnics punctuates the performance like a brief flash of reckoning, intense, undeniable, and quickly gone, leaving dust, darkness, and silence behind.
Dancing in the Dust offers no verdict and no resolution. Like Bombay Beach, it exists in the aftermath, where meaning is no longer guaranteed but still pursued. Rebirth here is not salvation or transformation; it is the quiet, imperfect decision to stay, to move, to feel, and to create even when the ground beneath you is already ash.

fields.Theme Engagement:
Yes. Dancing in the Dust engages with The Last Judgement not as a religious event or a final verdict, but as a lived condition. The project is built around the feeling that judgment has already happened—that the consequences of our collective choices are visible, and we are living inside the aftermath.
On a personal level, the work reflects my own reckoning—leaving one life behind, confronting loss, and continuing to create without the expectation of resolution. Politically and culturally, it mirrors places like Bombay Beach that exist after the dream has failed, yet continue to generate art, community, and meaning anyway.

fields.Project Description:
Since childhood, I have been improvising and composing music, all the while finding ways to integrate, 
describe and essentially "score" my life in real time with whatever sounds I had available to me at any given moment. 
After a decade of recording and performing with my heroes, I have shifted focus to creating and performing my own music all over the world.

 Whether in venues, theaters, planetariums or festival stages, my goal when performing is to create an atmosphere, allowing audiences to unlock from their daily routines and transport them outside of themselves to a wider and wilder collective consciousness. With the use of video, light art, costumes and virtual settings, I aim to conjure the spirit of the Sky White Tiger, calling out to the ancient Gods of an Unseen Sky or to the Constellation of Connectivity that drives our consciousness to perform the unimaginable as a united human kind, in pivotal moments when we need this the most. 

If selected for the 2026 BBB 2026 YEAR X
I plan to present an hour long concert featuring original songs, electro/acoustic soundscapes, and layered vocal tapestries, accompanied by movement and whisper wisdom from Sky Psychic Brides.

45-60 minute concert

fields.Project Description:
I'm making an immersive, interactive, sculptural experience. With fire. And music. 

It will look like a steel semicircular form rising from the sand. The core will be truss-like and industrial but it will be covered in steely vine-like tendrils. It invites the participant to step inside, like some sort of alien control booth or viewing platform. This polished steel sculpture will live permanently at Bombay Beach, luring the public into it to consider its purpose and appreciate its strategic framing of the landscape.

Layout wise, the sculptural semicircular element will be 7.5 feet at its widest, 11 feet at its longest, and 5 feet at its tallest. I'll be experimenting with mirror finishes for the steel, otherwise it will be highly polished and clear coated. At night the permanent installation will have an array of solar powered spotlights for dramatic illumination, colored with theater gels and tied down to deeply driven sticks of rebar with anti tampering straps.

Aesthetically, this structure will have as its underpinnings a recognizable but subtle truss assembly holding up a long, thick, undulating top tube that forms the upper edge. This upper edge begins in the ground at the rear of the sculpture, rises and undulates as it moves forward and around the piece. Like a curving handrail, this will be an important visual element. Beneath it, and around the supporting truss structure, will be different diameters of curving pieces wrapping around and through the entirety of the form. The balance of these fulfilling, organic, wild lines and the core structure underneath gives tension and intrigue to the whole piece, especially when it's standing alone.

For the event, I'll be adding electrical wiring, plumbing lines, extra lighting effects, a small separate structure for fuel storage, and a multitude of pyrotechnic devices. The main steel sculpture will be fleshed out and activated.

As the sun nears setting, I'll hook up the fuel lines, test the LPG systems for leaks, and light the pilots. The sculpture will come to life. An array of buttons inside the front of the structure will glow with energy. When you walk inside and dare to press one, a column of fire will erupt from one of the vertical pipes around you and a recognizable musical note will pour forth. The propane that you're allowing to flow through the pipes has been painstakingly tuned to make them resonate and create a one of a kind symphony of fire. 
This is a pipe organ made of steel, brass, fuel, and madness. And you're controlling it.
fields.Support Needed: This is going to be incredible. I can't wait to contribute.
fields.Abridged Project Text:
What is that thing? 
Let's check it out. 
A polished steel form, crawling up from the sands of the beach, a cave for you to enter. 
Within, you see buttons! And pipes and hoses buried inside a writhing snake-like structure. 
What is this thing?
Press button. Fire erupts. Music happens.
:-D
fields.Technical Support:
Oh I need nothing.
This will be 100% me and a handful of friends installing and running the installation. 
The install, operation, and deinstall are totally covered. 
The tools we have in spades. Plus redundancies for critical parts. 
We're good with zero support, but thank you.

A spot to set up with a good view would be cool, though. But that could be almost anywhere.
fields.Audience Engagement:
For audience engagement, the answer is YES to all four of those questions. 

As it's meant to be performed with inside and viewed from the outside, and visible from pretty damn far away, yes it will be something people watch. 

As it's meant to be moved through, the entrance on the back is flanked by the steel sculpture rising from the beach. The glow of the control panel and natural flow of the structure make you want to venture inside. As you do, the form rises around you and envelopes you within it. The movement is effortless and instinctual. Yes, it will be something people move through. 

As it's a sculptural instrument and meant to be played, participation is paramount. The draw of the glowing buttons deep within the structure, the curiosity to touch them, to see what this thing is all about, it's a clear call. Yes, it will be something people participate in. Literally, inside of. 

At 3am, it might be difficult to "stumble" onto this installation. It's more like, what are these flashes of light in the distance? Are those fireballs? Where is that music coming from? All as you approach. All as you're drawn to a: Poof Alert.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Yes. 
When I think of The Last Judgement, I think of your last chance to really gather up all of your skills, energy, focus, and determination, all your friends and family and supporters, all your knowledge and hard-earned wisdom, and put on one last, epic, insanely over the top performance. 
Your magnum opus.
Your masterpiece. 
The culmination of your life's work. 
You dig deep, gather the old crew together for one final adventure, and put on something that you'll present and say: Judge me now. Judge this. I've reached to the skies one final time and this is the height of it. After that, I'm hanging it all up and this work, this all-encompassing accomplishment, is the end all be all.
That's what this theme means to me and this project is the embodiment of it. 
Go for broke one final time.
fields.Risk & Safety:
Yes, my project involves fire.
I've operated pyrotechnic devices professionally, with fire marshall inspection and oversight, for almost ten years now. These are public events where risk mitigation is the absolute most important criteria. I've learned what the marshalls are looking for in terms of safety systems and backup safety systems, and always utilized them accordingly.
Also, the fuel will be disconnected and removed any time we're not actively overseeing the use of fire, and each time it's reconnected we'll be performing a high pressure leak test of every connection in the system.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No it has not. I plan to order the raw steel material in the next week.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create this particular work because I'm chasing a vision. 

I had no explainable reason for thinking that I could make a musical sculptural device in this fashion, in a way that would actually work. None. In fact, I was given information from a few sources that it simply wouldn't work. It wasn't possible. It was a foolish pursuit, and it would end in failure and frustration. 

Well color me foolish, because I started trying anyway.

I began learning about the physics of whistles and horns and any musical device that uses pressurized air to create recognizable notes. I slowly began understanding. Then I would use my air compressor and PVC pipe to make different little devices to see how they actually worked, first hand. Once I was getting reliable frequency stabilization, I'd make duplicates but change their lengths, or outlet mouth shapes, or pressure of inlet air. I kept recording and analyzing and began to understand the math of the changes I was making versus the frequencies produced. 

After reaching that understanding, I consulted with two musician friends about what I was trying to achieve, sent them audio samples, and we started to agree on the notes in the different octaves that I should try to achieve to develop a good overall sound and a usable musical instrument. My formulas worked well, and soon I had a playable instrument running on compressed air. 

Then I introduced fire. I ran those early prototype devices and figured out the correction factor for propane. I built more prototypes to learn about properly feeding them without depleting the accumulation chambers, what size chambers the lower notes demanded, freezing my tanks, etc. 

There's a lot more to that part of the story, but the important idea is that I was creating music. I've been building different prototypes since then, but never found the perfect version to take to the next level and present publicly as a sculptural installation. 

Later I built a very crude, ultra mobile prototype of two rough early notes. A buddy and I mounted these to a tandem bike and took them to Bombay Beach for Christmas, to see if it could possibly survive out there. Well it did survive, and it was awesome.

Now I've iterated that style of musical fire device and found that it's finally a combination that fits my needs. I'm finally ready to move to the next level, build out an installation, and present this work publicly. And what better venue than literally the place where the breakthrough finally happened: Bombay Beach!

Furthermore I struggled for a long time with the sculptural part of this work. I've included some AI generated images that suck but they were part of my process of envisioning the final steel form. Ultimately I'll be building on an aesthetic that I've dabbled with in very small scale work in the past. Stylized burglar bars and heavy duty brackets where the structure is carried in thick steel, but there's a performative and decorative element of winding, organically inspired metal bars weaved throughout the piece. 
On the larger scale of this project I'm convinced it'll look stunningly weird. 

Thank you for reading.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
A huge component of this project is activation and deactivation. Abandonment and possibility. 
What will remain in place in Bombay Beach is a structure that's clearly missing some components but has the bones of something extremely intriguing. This is a mirror to the town itself. This could just stand out there, forever, and slowly decay into nothing but it has some sort of quality that makes you want to engage with it, to breathe life back into its shell, to imagine its history and create for it a new future. That's what I see and what I feel when I'm in Bombay Beach, when I'm near the Salton Sea or out in Slab City. 

This structure will literally be brought in sustenance from the outside world: batteries, propane, lights, love and care and attention. It'll be fed and supercharged back to life to furiously and rampagingly reinhabit our collective and fleeting existence.

After a while of living again, and living hard, it'll return to sleep. It will deactivate, but the lovingly crafted shell will remain as a testament to what it was and what it will be again, with the right inputs of care and attention. 

Just like the entirety of that area, it will scream at those who explore it: POSSIBILITY!! I can be something. Fire up my engines and let me loose because I'm not dead yet! 

That's why the Salton Sea. That's why Bombay Beach. And the Biennale is the perfect opportunity to tell the full story.

fields.Project Description:
The Social Equilibrium Apparatus is a large-scale sculptural installation that adopts the visual language of public infrastructure, civic tools, and institutional design. The work presents itself as a neutral instrument—something engineered for balance, stability, and procedural order—while quietly exposing the unease embedded in systems that claim to optimize society through abstraction, measurement, and control. Its form is deliberately legible, monumental, and functional in appearance, echoing the aesthetics of bureaucratic authority and industrial utility.
The installation functions as a meditation on governance, compliance, and the seductive comfort of “solutions” that promise order at the cost of complexity. It does not propose answers, but stages a space where the viewer must confront the aesthetics of control and the quiet, persistent tension between stability and humanity.

fields.Project Description:
Magic Carpet Ride is a 6-minute eyes-closed journey that makes the invisible visible. There's a layer of reality underneath the visible one: resonance between people. It lives in how we respond to experience — whether we're on the same wavelength when we laugh, when we grieve, when we confront the unfamiliar and strange. Research from Dartmouth's Social Systems Laboratory shows that people with similar neural responses to shared experiences are more likely to connect — that the click we feel with certain strangers isn't random, it's matching frequencies. Normally this reveals itself slowly, over months of friendship. This installation asks: what if we could glimpse it in minutes?
    
Four participants at a time are blindfolded and guided to be seated on a magic carpet — fabric canopy overhead, colored uplights glowing from below, lanterns flickering, incense drifting. They're fitted with consumer EEG headsets that read their brainwaves throughout the journey. A facilitator guides them through a narrated soundscape — a journey with distinct emotional textures. Moments of surrender. Moments of rhythm and pulse. A threshold where something unknown waits ahead. A moment of being truly seen. Throughout, special effects deepen the immersion: shifting scents, gusts of air like a passing storm, tactile surprises that make the imaginal feel real. Their eyes are closed, but their nervous systems are responding — leaning in or holding back, syncing to the rhythm or staying on their own clock, opening toward mystery or contracting away.
    
The EEG captures these responses in real time. Not thoughts, not personality quiz answers — but how their body actually shows up when it's not performing. The data is translated into a "frequency fingerprint": four glyphs printed on paper and sealed with wax. A cosmic verdict on your tribe.
    
The glyphs come with a sparse key, but their real meaning unfolds through comparison and conversation. Find others, show your slip, ask: "Did you get the spiral? What did you feel when it got quiet?" Matching glyphs mean you experienced the ride the same way — your frequencies match. Differences become conversation starters about convergent and divergent inner lives.

At Bombay Beach — where pilgrims arrive from everywhere, knowing no one — this becomes a quest: find the others on your wavelength. And in that recognition, you learn what resonance feels like when it's confirmed. You become a finely tuned tuning fork, calibrated to find your people.
fields.Notes: need a space
fields.Abridged Project Text: A 6-minute blindfolded journey on a magic carpet. EEG captures how your nervous system responds to surrender, rhythm, mystery, being seen — and translates it into four glyphs sealed in wax. Your frequency fingerprint. The quest: find the others on your wavelength.
fields.Technical Support:
Space: Indoor or enclosed space is necessary. We need dimness for the immersive atmosphere and protection from wind and dust for the EEG equipment — the headsets are sensitive and the experience requires stillness. A container, shed, or small building would be ideal. If none is available, we can bring a canvas bell tent (13-16ft diameter) to create the enclosure, but a solid structure is strongly preferred.
    
Power: We need electricity for a laptop and PC server (EEG processing and audio playback), portable speaker, thermal printer, LED uplights, and a small fan. Total draw is approximately 1Kw continuous. We're bringing a 3kW solar-powered generator with battery storage, and may need to supplement with additional solar panels depending on how many hours per day we run.
    
Sound: Self-contained. We're bringing a quality portable speaker for the narrated soundscape. No external sound system needed, though we'll want to be mindful of placement to avoid noise bleed from nearby installations.
    
Lighting: Battery-powered LED uplights and lanterns — we bring these. No overhead lighting needed; dimness is part of the design.
    
EEG Hardware: Four consumer EEG headsets (Muse 2), connected via Bluetooth to a laptop running custom analysis software. Rechargeable via USB.
    
We're largely self-sufficient — the main ask is access to an enclosed space.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Magic Carpet Ride is participatory and intimate — not a spectacle to watch, but a ritual to undergo. Four people at a time, blindfolded, seated together on the carpet, guided through a 6-minute journey. With setup and debrief, expect to give it about 20 minutes of your night. But the engagement doesn't end when the ride does — it ripples outward.
    
Participants leave holding their glyphs: four symbols sealed in wax, their frequency fingerprint. The glyphs come with a sparse key, but their deeper meaning only emerges through comparison. So participants carry them into the Biennale — tucked in pockets, pinned to jackets, pulled out over drinks at the Ski Inn at 1am. "What did you get? Did you get the web? What did you feel when you were being witnessed?"
    
The ride takes six minutes. The quest to find your frequency matches lasts the whole weekend. Strangers approach strangers. Conversations skip small talk entirely and go straight to: "how do you experience the world?"
    
We imagine running sessions throughout the afternoon and into the evening approximately every 20 minutes — perhaps 12 participants per hour at peak times. We will have a facilitator present for each session to guide the journey, manage the EEG headsets, and perform the wax-sealing ritual at the end.
    
The installation itself is contained. The social aftermath is not. That's the design: a private experience that becomes public currency, a shared language for finding the others.
fields.Theme Engagement:
In the traditional Last Judgment, the books are opened and you're read by God. Your deeds weighed, your soul sorted, your fate sealed by an external authority.
    
Magic Carpet Ride opens a different book — the one written in your nervous system and psyche. The EEG reads what's beneath the story you tell about yourself: how you respond to surrender, to rhythm, to mystery, to being truly seen. Not your sins, but your frequencies. Not your deeds, but your resonance.
    
You lie down. You're taken on a journey. You rise holding your verdict — four glyphs sealed in wax. But there's no judge. No winged accountants. The only sorting that matters is the one you do yourself: find the others whose glyphs match yours. Find your tribe.
    
This is judgment without hierarchy. No one tells you if you passed. No one assigns you to heaven or hell. Instead, you're handed a frequency fingerprint and released into the Biennale to discover what it means. The verdict isn't punishment or reward — it's revelation. These are your people. This is how you experience the world.
    
If the Last Judgment asks "what were you worth?" — Magic Carpet Ride asks "who are your kindred spirits?" Not moral accounting, but resonance. Not salvation from above, but connection across. The books are opened, and what's written there is an invitation: go find the others who were written the same way.
    
At the end of the world, maybe that's the only judgment that matters.
fields.Risk & Safety: Minimal hazards. We use burning incense and sage for scent, and a charcoal burner for resin — small, contained flames supervised by the facilitator at all times. The wax seal station uses a glue-gun style melter (no open flame). No ladders, vehicles, drones, excavation, or other hazardous materials.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Magic Carpet Ride is new work, developed specifically for the 2026 Biennale. This will be its premiere.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We're a team of neuroscientists, artists, and experience designers who believe the invisible stuff matters — resonance, attunement, the signals beneath the surface. Melanie Pincus and Noah Barr design immersive art installations and experiences that respond to biosignals. Massimo Bardetto and Rohit Korrapolu are developing neurotech for precision interventions. Together, we're interested in what happens when you make the hidden layers of human experience perceivable.

Magic Carpet Ride emerged from a shared question: what if the resonance we feel with certain people could be made visible? Research shows that people with similar neural responses to shared experiences are more likely to connect — the "click" we feel with some strangers carries real signal. But in modern life, we're not exactly finely tuned instruments for perceiving it. Like anything subtle, it takes practice. What if seeing it helps you learn to feel it? Like tasting wine with a sommelier — once someone names what you're sensing, you can perceive it clearly on your own. We're building a ritual that offers that: a mirror for resonance.

The questions we're asking: Can we glimpse in minutes what normally takes months to discover? Can four glyphs become a doorway to real conversation? When two people match, they discover they met parts of the experience in similar ways — and in that discovery, they feel what resonance actually feels like. From there, might they get curious about expanding their own range — sometimes meeting a moment with poetic sadness, other times with silliness — and in doing so, connecting with a wider range of people?

The timing feels urgent. We're in a loneliness epidemic. Biofeedback offers something rare: information about how your nervous system actually shows up, not the story you tell about yourself.

This project braids together what each of us does best. Noah and Melanie are designing the sensory arc — distinct emotional textures layered with scent shifts, air movement, and tactile surprises, all designed to evoke divergent responses we can measure. Massimo and Rohit are adapting their EEG pipeline and building the glyph generation system. We plan to arrive in Bombay Beach ahead of the culminating weekend to prototype with locals and other artists-in-residence — calibrating the thresholds, learning how to sort neural responses into meaningful patterns, and letting their feedback shape the final experience. After the Biennale, we want to leave something behind: a frequency map of the community — a visual artifact showing patterns of resonance among residents. Who clusters together? Where are the unexpected connections? It becomes a way for Bombay Beach to see itself through a new lens, and a lasting trace of the invisible made visible. We'd also like to offer a workshop on biofeedback and resonance for anyone curious to go deeper.

The Last Judgment asks: what are you, underneath it all? We're asking the same question — but the book we're opening is the nervous system and psyche. You rise from the carpet holding your verdict. The only sorting that matters is the one you do yourself: find the others who were written the same way.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is where strangers become co-conspirators overnight. People arrive from everywhere, knowing no one, looking for something they can't quite name. The Biennale doesn't hand them a program — it hands them a landscape and says: find the others.

Magic Carpet Ride is designed for exactly this. The glyphs become a reason to approach someone you've never met, a way to start a conversation that goes somewhere real. What begins on the carpet continues around fires, over coffee, in the morning light — comparing symbols, trading stories about how the ride felt.

There's something about the Salton Sea that makes this land. A place that was never supposed to exist, that keeps refusing to die, that sits at the edge of collapse and beauty at once. It draws people who want the real thing, not the polished version. The resonance we're trying to make visible isn't polished either. It's underneath, elemental, the unfiltered version of you.

And the community here gets it. This isn't the commercial art world. It's participatory, experimental, a little feral. The question "who are your people?" isn't abstract at Bombay Beach — it's the whole point. We're just offering a new way to answer it.

The Salton Sea is a mirror that reflects what you bring to it. So is Magic Carpet Ride. You lie down, you take the journey, and you rise holding something true about yourself. Then you go find out who else got written the same way.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The concept is fully designed. We've mapped the experience arc and identified the neural dimensions we're measuring. What remains is building out the pieces.          On the tech side, Massimo and Rohit have an existing EEG processing and analysis pipelin
fields.Additional Artists: Melanie Pincus, Noah Barr, Rohit Korrapolu, Massimo Bardetti

fields.Project Description:
Small Mercies is a diptych of two 30 × 30 inch acrylic paintings on canvas, created specifically for the 2026 Bombay Beach Biennale. The panels depict two interdependent realms—Hell and Haven, exterior collapse and interior refuge.

The first painting, Sea Ya in Hell, begins with a lived visit to a beach inside the Sian Ka’an Biosphere Reserve: a place understood to be protected, a paradise, yet discovered mostly-buried under plastic waste, washed-up appliances, and drifting debris. It echoed the Salton Sea’s arc—promise, fallout, and persistence. During that brief encounter, my husband was stung by a Portuguese man o’ war: an enormous, immediate pain that snapped any remaining illusion of safety, or separation, between body and environment.

In the painting, the creature becomes a floating sovereign—hovering above a slow vortex of refuse. Straws and cigarette butts gather into new reefs, fragile architectures where the afterlife adapts. The air shimmers with synthetic haze; food glitters with microplastics; water turns to chemical soup. Everything is familiar, yet uncanny—almost seductive in its sheen.

An afterlife emerges where ecological damage, consumer excess, and bodily vulnerability mingle without hierarchy. Judgment arrives quietly as erosion—entering the body through what we manufacture, consume, and normalize. Harm remains invisible until accumulation becomes unavoidable and we confront the intimacy of our own discards. One small mercy appears as silence itself: an eerie stillness, briefly free of engines.

The second painting, Inner Eden, imagines a psychological and spiritual counter-space formed in response to harm. Its imagery is calm, spacious, and symmetrical, anchored by hybrid chakra flora stacked like a living totem and populated by fungal figures—small mercies given form. Man o’ war tendrils appear here as well, slipping through and keeping the refuge imperfect.

Small beings echo between the two paintings in a yin–yang rhythm. In Hell they surface as stubborn mischief or momentary reprieve, grappling with the sovereign’s tentacles as if to steer what cannot be steered. In Haven they return as reminders—like palm buzzers—small mercies of familiar physicality.

Both paintings are encircled along their canvas sides with painted protective motifs—apotropaic folk remedies and evil-averters drawn from domestic and ancestral traditions. These markings form provisional boundaries. In Sea Ya in Hell, they strain to contain what threatens to spill outward; in Inner Eden, they hold the line against intrusion. The protection remains fragile and human.

Installed together as a paired encounter, one panel lures viewers into a wrecked afterlife, while the other offers an interior refuge that cannot fully close itself off. The work resists resolution: Hell and Haven are not destinations but overlapping conditions. Survival emerges through small mercies and improvised rituals practiced amid ongoing collapse.
fields.Support Needed: Thank you for considering this project. I love how BBB makes room for both reckoning and delight—a feast in the time of plague. I’m committed to finishing Small Mercies at full exhibition quality and installing it cleanly, safely, and on schedule for the 2026 Biennale.
fields.Notes: WE LIKE IT BUT ONLY IF YOU LEAVE IT -- she wants to bring it anyways - does lily want to host?
fields.Abridged Project Text: A two-panel acrylic diptych born from a real encounter at a “protected” beach turned toxic. One panel conjures a wrecked afterlife of debris, beauty, and bodily threat; the other imagines an inner refuge that won’t fully seal. Folk wards ring the edges. Small mercies flicker inside.
fields.Technical Support:
I am proposing the work as a two-panel painted object installation (diptych), wall-hung or displayed on a stable vertical surface. Ideally it will be placed indoors in a gallery/group show setting, or in a fully protected area shielded from wind, dust, moisture, and direct sun.

Required support:
-Two secure hanging points / wall anchors suitable for two 30 × 30 inch stretched canvases 
-A stable wall or vertical mounting surface (no projection or screen needed) 
-Standard ambient or gallery lighting (no special lighting required) 
-Basic installation support for safe leveling, spacing, and alignment of the two panels 

Optional:
-Labels or brief wall text listing the diptych title and panel titles 

No extra electricity, sound, video, or internet is required.
fields.Audience Engagement: Viewers engage the work by moving between panels—reading the diptych as a call-and-response: seduction and warning, wreckage and refuge. From across the room, the contrast lands fast; up close, the paintings reward slow looking as small motifs recur across both realms. Because the protective border continues onto the canvas sides, the piece isn’t fully frontal. Viewers instinctively arc around it to “read” the edges, encountering the paintings as both image and object: a domestic defense that feels intimate, handmade, and slightly futile. The experience shifts with time of day: bright and candy-toxic in daylight, quieter and more devotional at night.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Small Mercies engages The Last Judgement as a lived condition rather than a future event. Judgment in this work arrives through exposure: what we breathe, eat, touch, and normalize. The “hell” panel stages a familiar apocalypse, where consumer debris, ecological consequence, and bodily vulnerability converge into an ordinary afterlife.

The “haven” panel refuses a clean escape. It offers an inner refuge that remains imperfect. The man o’ war crosses into Eden as the one creature that belonged all along—native to the sea before it was remade by us, and cast as evil for its uncanny allure and its sting—keeping the boundary porous and the refuge incomplete.

Nature-derived wards wrap the canvas edges like folk protection—small rituals meant to hold the line when larger systems fail.

The work proposes a Last Judgement without a judge: no verdict from above, only consequence moving through body and landscape. Whether humanity deserves it or not, we sink or swim in the detritus of our own progress.
fields.Risk & Safety: No fire, ladders, vehicles, boats, drones, excavation, digging, or hazardous actions. The work consists of two acrylic paintings on canvas and is safe for public viewing. Standard installation precautions apply: secure mounting to prevent tipping or falling.
fields.Location Submittal: Indoor Biennale gallery/group exhibition venue (assigned by BBB), Bombay Beach, CA 92233
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I was compelled to create Small Mercies because I’m increasingly haunted by how collapse arrives: not only as loud, visible damage but as intimacy—through the air we breathe, the materials we touch, the substances we ingest, and the conveniences we normalize.

The 2026 theme, The Last Judgement, pulled me back to a visceral memory: visiting a beach inside the Sian Ka’an Biosphere Reserve that promised “paradise,” yet, upon approach, revealed itself as a shoreline reassembled by debris-loaded currents—manmade discards forming a new landscape. The shock of that contradiction—punctuated by an injury from a Portuguese man o’ war during the same visit—stayed with me. The Biennale’s theme turned recurring dread into material.

In this diptych I’m asking: What does judgement look like when it arrives as slow accumulation rather than spectacle? What does refuge mean when it cannot fully seal itself off? What small rituals do we invent to stay psychologically intact inside ongoing unraveling?

The two panels—Sea Ya in Hell and Inner Eden—are designed to be read together as a paired encounter. I want the diptych to function as a shared experience: something viewers can move through slowly and feel pulled toward in both directions, recognizing it as both uncanny and familiar. One presents a seductively wrecked afterlife; the other offers an interior counter-space that refuses a clean escape. The edge-work turns the paintings into protective objects, not just images.

My practice is rooted in painting as a way of parsing and testing thought and emotion. I build surfaces that feel simultaneously aqueous and particulate—watery veils, opaque blooms, and pearled shimmer—so figures, garbage, and botanicals drift in a buoyant, sea-lit logic held in deliberate symmetry. I use color, gradations, glow, and storybook motifs as a lure, then destabilize the image through subtle distortions and toxic detail: radiant and uneasy, as if beauty itself has become a contaminant.

Grant support would fund the materials and finishing needed to bring the diptych to full exhibition quality—durable surfaces, refined edge-work, varnish, and professional transport and installation readiness.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Small Mercies belongs in Bombay Beach because the Salton Sea is a living contradiction: radiant surface and accumulated consequence occupying the same frame. It holds histories of promise, extraction, abandonment, and persistence—where beauty and fallout are inseparable, and where the line between “natural” and “manufactured” feels permanently breached.

The Bombay Beach Biennale amplifies that tension by treating judgement as both reckoning and carnival: a feast in the time of plague. In Sea Ya in Hell, debris becomes habitat and architecture—strange scaffolding for survival, pleasure, and denial. Here, that transformation reads not as metaphor but as local texture: a landscape shaped by what drifts in, what gets left behind, and what refuses to disappear. The work’s seduction matters; it mirrors how shimmer can coexist with toxicity, and how catastrophe can become atmosphere—felt and normalized.

At the same time, Inner Eden is not an escape fantasy. It offers a counter-space built from small defenses—apotropaic wards, folk remedies, fungal allies, improvised boundaries—gestures that echo the Biennale’s language of resilience, humor, creativity, and DIY ritual. The diptych asks viewers to notice what repeats, what mutates, and what refuses to stay contained.

In Bombay Beach, The Last Judgement doesn’t read as a distant event. It reads as consequence—already present in the waterline, the air, and the stories the landscape carries.

With doors that open and form a privacy barrier and an inner altar with an engraved mirror of Jesus &/or Mary Mag. looking down on the sinner. A metal tray or mirrored surface will be provided, for those who choose to consume their sins under the vigilance of the Divine. I’m thinking it will be on wheels to move around the town...

fields.Project Name: Liminal Vessel: Omphalos, Marker of a Dead Sea
fields.Project Description:
Liminal Vessel: Omphalos, Marker of a Dead Sea, is a site-responsive sculptural work developed as part of my ongoing Liminal Vessels series. The series references states of transition, thresholds, & in-between places, vessels  historically associated with holding, carrying, and preserving, become spatial instruments: structures that hold reflection, interruption, & memory, particularly within landscapes shaped by still water, erosion, & time. The work is constructed from high-fired stoneware clay, fired to cone 10 to achieve density & durability suitable for outdoor installation. The surface will be finished with a bronze tenmoku glaze, that absorbs & reflects light simultaneously. This dark, bronze surface shifts with changing conditions, echoing the mirage-like qualities of the Salton Sea, where sky and water often collapse into a single visual plane. The sculpture is composed of two vertically aligned ceramic forms that mirror & respond to one another. Each section is pierced with openings that allow light, air, & sightlines to pass through, disrupting the logic of enclosure traditionally associated with vessels. These apertures create internal alignments & reflections, producing visual doubling & illusion. Between the two ceramic sections is a six-inch clear acrylic spacer that functions as a deliberate interruption—a transparent horizon line that separates & connects the forms. Through this band, the surrounding landscape & distant horizon are visually integrated into the structure of the work.
The sculpture will stands approximately 80"-90" inches tall, with each ceramic section measuring roughly 38"h, 10"w, 15"d. A concealed stainless-steel rod runs through the center, providing structural integrity & precise alignment. This internal spine also houses subtle internal illumination that activates the piece at dusk & night. The light is restrained & diffused, revealing interior voids, & emphasizing interior space, reflection, & continuity.
The work will be installed on the firmer shoreline rather than at the water’s edge, ensuring stability within the variable conditions of the Salton Sea. The base system is engineered to sit securely on compacted ground, using a weighted plate and wooden plinth that allow for safe installation & removal without external infrastructure. Fabrication, transport, assembly, & installation will be carried out by myself & my team, making the project self-sufficient, low-maintenance, & logistically streamlined. At the Salton Sea, Liminal Vessel: Omphalos: reads as a reflective marker of what remains between, a monument that connects what is above & below. Its mirrored forms create a mirage-like doubling, that recalls how still water holds and distorts reflection. The work occupies the space of connection, presence and absence, solidity and illusion—becoming a vessel not for water, but for what remains when the water withdraws.
fields.Support Needed: Ill be donating a stone sculpture to the BBBX auction night in the hope to raise funds for other art installations. I love what you are doing and want to be an active part of this creative collective!
fields.Abridged Project Text: At the Salton Sea, Liminal Vessel: Omphalos, Marker of a Dead Sea rises as a witness to a slow collapse. Clay, light and void form a ceremonial object for a post - hope landscape - where judgement lingers, water withdraws, and the sublime persists amid decay.

fields.Audience Engagement: This is designed to be encountered rather than approached with instruction. It offers quiet, a physical threshold where viewers may pause, reflect, or simply wander around its presence. Omphalos does not require participation, although it could be the oracle of the self; it invites contemplation through proximity, light, & scale. During the day, the sculpture stands as a reflective marker on the shoreline. Its dark, surface absorbs heat & light, while the clear acrylic horizon reveals the surrounding landscape through the body of the vessel. Viewers may notice the mirage-like doubling of sky & ground, &the way the pierced openings frame fragments of distance. The experience is slow, meditative, & grounded in stillness, the message comes from within. At dusk & night, the internal illumination activates the sculpture differently. Light emerges  revealing interior voids & shifting the work from object to presence. It becomes a guide or beacon—not directional, but perceptual—encountered unexpectedly by those walking the shore, arriving at night, or wandering through the landscape in moments of solitude. The sculpture functions as a place to consider what is held and what is lost, what remains open, & what appears as illusion. It marks a moment, a pause within a vast environment, offering the audience an experience that changes with time, light, & attention—an invitation to stand at the threshold, however briefly.
fields.Theme Engagement: To some a standing totem may signify a monument, a moment, a beacon, or an oracle. The Omphalos of the Salton Sea. Standing tall with the optical illusion of reflected forms, a single column connects what is above with what is below. The reckoning belongs to the viewer, the artful voyeur claims the judgement within. The relationship between the artwork and the audience is held within the diminishing landscape, the mirage and the horizon.
fields.Risk & Safety:
I would like to drive a few steel pins into the ground to give the work a bit more stability. It will be stable in its structure and assembly as described above.
Establishing its location to maximize the reflection of the horizon and the Salton Sea will require knowledge of the area allowed to install. And best practice advice for staking on the " dry Seabed" avoiding sinking, and disturbing the ground.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): This is a unique piece inspired by the Bombay Beach Biennale X and the Salton Sea. It is a continuation of my exploration of Liminal Vessels: Sculptures from the stillwater Threshold. It will be the first time I've pierce the structure allowing what is held to be more subliminal.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I am proposing Liminal Vessel: Omphalos, the Marker of a Dead Sea as a site-responsive sculptural work for Bombay Beach at a moment when the Salton Sea is both visibly present and irrevocably disappearing. I am drawn to this place now because it exists in a prolonged state of reckoning—neither collapse nor recovery, but something suspended, unresolved, and quietly demanding attention.

My Liminal Vessels series has grown from an ongoing inquiry into thresholds: between water and land, interior and exterior, memory, material and reflection. The word liminal refers to transitional states, while vessel speaks to containment, passage, and care. These works are not functional containers, but spatial markers—forms that hold reflection, interruption, and absence. Omphalos, meaning “navel” or center point, extends this inquiry by positioning the vessel as an axis between what is above and what is below, what is seen and what is believed.

At Bombay Beach, the sculpture stands as a vertical totem that can be read in multiple ways: as a monument, a moment, a beacon, an Axis Mundi or an oracle of the self. Its mirrored ceramic forms create an optical illusion, a mirage-like doubling that collapses sky and ground, reflection and void. A transparent acrylic horizon separates the forms, allowing the actual landscape to pass through the body of the work. The judgement suggested by the Biennale’s theme is not imposed by the object; it belongs to the viewer. Any reckoning occurs internally, shaped by proximity, light, time, and personal interpretation.

The work is constructed from high-fired stoneware and assembled by myself and my team, allowing for a self-sufficient, low-impact installation. While the sculpture does not require direct participation, it invites a form of quiet engagement. Viewers may encounter it deliberately, or unexpectedly—at midday glare or in the darkness of the night, when the interior illumination activates the piece as a subtle guide to an axis. The work offers a place to consider what is held, what is lost, what is connected and what remains unresolved.

My interest in Bombay Beach lies in its layered histories of ambition, abandonment, reinvention, and belief. The Salton Sea is not merely a backdrop but an active collaborator—its horizon, light, and instability shaping how the work is seen and felt. Liminal Vessel: Omphalos is an offering to this landscape: an object held between a diminishing shoreline, a persistent mirage, and the enduring human impulse to seek meaning at the edge of things.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach is not simply a site for installation; it is a place where orientation has collapsed & been rebuilt repeatedly. Along the Salton Sea, histories of industry, recreation, abandonment, & reinvention exist simultaneously. The community lives within a landscape that continually redefines itself—environmentally, culturally, & socially—without clear markers of center or permanence. This condition makes Bombay Beach a uniquely appropriate location for Liminal Vessel: Omphalos.

The concept of omphalos—a center point—& its related idea of the axis mundi traditionally emerge in places where people seek orientation amid uncertainty. These markers do not impose authority; they offer a reference point, a way to locate oneself in relation to land, sky, & consequence. At Bombay Beach, where the shoreline recedes & the horizon remains visually seductive yet ecologically compromised, such a gesture becomes especially resonant.The Salton Sea has functioned historically as a site of projection: economic promise, leisure culture, extraction, & denial. Today, it exists in a state of prolonged aftermath. This work does not attempt to memorialize a singular past or propose a future resolution. Instead, it establishes a quiet axis within the landscape—a vertical marker that reflects the environment rather than commenting upon it. The sculpture’s transparent horizon & mirrored forms allow the sea, sky, & salt flats to pass through it framing the horizon in its ever shifting guise.

fields.Project Description:
Operating Conditions is an in situ sculpture composed of steel armatures and rawhide, positioned directly within the water so the work remains subject to the same forces as its surroundings. The structure rises at two levels, branching in a manner that evokes delta formations and bodily extension. Its vertical emergence reads as a response to pressure, salinity, and accumulation, as though the form has been coaxed upward by the lake’s conditions.

The steel armature establishes a framework of rings, spans, and supports calibrated for tension. Rawhide is pierced and threaded onto this structure so that some sections gather while others are drawn taut. These gestures function as structural decisions. Gathered hide produces folds that hold water, sediment, and rain, creating temporary reservoirs that swell and empty with weather. Stretched hide tightens as it dries and gradually contracts. That contraction pulls against the steel, applying continuous force that can bend or shift the armature over time. Geometry adjusts through use, pressure, and duration.

Because the work is in situ, the rawhide is first soaked in the same water it will inhabit. It absorbs salinity, algae blooms, and suspended toxins. As drying occurs, salt crystals form across the surface and within the fibers, producing mineral structures that thicken through repeated cycles of wetting and desiccation. Residues remain visible, and the hide carries the record of what passes through it. Surface operates as an archive of exposure.

Several lengths of hide trail back into the water, establishing a gradient of material states. Submerged areas stay pliable and swollen while exposed sections stiffen and harden. The transition from saturation to rigidity unfolds slowly and continuously. Each encounter reveals a different moment in this cycle, keeping transformation active.

The sculpture’s placement binds it to the fluctuating waterline. As the sea recedes, more of the steel base appears. The work gradually shifts in relation to the shoreline, serving as a physical marker of recession measured across seasons and years. Change registers spatially, and the sculpture holds a timeline in its own exposure.

Degradation forms part of the work’s life. Sunlight, chemical residues, and abrasion thin the hide and alter its surface. These processes remain visible. The materials register environmental stress in the same way bodies and membranes do under prolonged strain. The sculpture carries these interactions forward, allowing environmental forces to shape its form.

Operating Conditions remains where it is installed. Its development follows the specific chemistry, climate, and pollutants of that site. Structure and skin stay in continual negotiation with the forces acting on them, unfolding as material responses held in place long enough to be witnessed.
fields.Support Needed: Thank you for your consideration of "Operating Conditions." Bombay Beach is an important site where change stays visible and I would like to contribute a piece that listens to those conditions and records them over time. My hope is to add something that feels attentive to the shoreline’s realities and to the community that continues to gather there.
fields.Abridged Project Text: A structure rises from the water in tiers, built from steel armatures pierced with rawhide. The hide is gathered, stretched, and trailing, shifting from pliable to rigid as it dries from the sea, shrinking to tension the frame and mark receding water levels.
fields.Technical Support: This project is self-sufficient.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Since the piece will remain as long as conditions allow, viewers encounter it at the water’s edge during the biennale and in the months and years that follow. Its presence aligns with what already gathers along the shoreline: foamy pollutants, fish skeletons, salt residues, and other visible traces of ecological strain. The sculpture extends these observations into three dimensions, carrying the same materials and pressures into a constructed form that continues to change over time.

Approaching the work involves moving through the actual site conditions that shape it. The shoreline becomes part of the viewing experience, situating the sculpture within the realities of contamination, evaporation, and exposure. The work holds its position as the waterline shifts, offering a fixed point against a moving boundary.

Solar-powered lights provide illumination after sunset, allowing the structure and its surfaces to remain visible at night. The lights trace the steel armature and catch on the salt and textures of the hide, revealing ongoing material changes. Visibility continues beyond daylight hours, supporting sustained encounter and attention. The sculpture stays available to those who return, carrying forward the same environmental forces that formed it.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Operating Conditions enters The Last Judgement through its placement in a landscape where spectacle, damage, and endurance coexist. Installed in situ at the Salton Sea, the sculpture occupies a shoreline where ecological collapse draws artists, pilgrims, tourists, and witnesses. Steel armatures rise from the water while rawhide, soaked in the lake, tightens, crusts with salt, and slowly degrades. The work carries its own gravity within this charged environment.

Confession appears as exposure. The hide holds algae, minerals, toxins, and residues from the water, carrying them into visibility as crystals and stains. Nothing is cleaned or reset. The material keeps a record of contact. In a place where ruins are continually reframed through festivals and images, the sculpture sustains a direct exchange with the site’s chemistry and remains embedded among the ruins.

Forgiveness and reckoning unfold through duration. As the shoreline recedes, the base gradually emerges. The sculpture marks change across seasons through height, tension, and mineral buildup. Each cycle of soaking and drying adds another layer of time.

Rebirth, repentance, and bacchanal circulate in Bombay Beach’s atmosphere. The sculpture engages through presence and transformation. Salt glints in the sun, and solar lights extend its visibility at night. Collapse carries a choreography here, and the sculpture moves within it, holding consequence and beauty in the same body.
fields.Risk & Safety: This project involves humans entering the water and submerging pole foundations for the sculpture. To do this, we will be wearing tyvek suits, fishing waders, rubber gloves, and eye/mouth protection.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): It has not been shown before and is very site specific.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I would like to express my interest in realizing a permanent sculptural installation in the shoreline waters of the Salton Sea. My practice is grounded in materially driven research, with a focus on tension, abrasion, and the ways environments act on bodies over time. The Salton Sea presents a rare site where environmental force is neither backdrop nor theme; it is an active collaborator.

The proposed work is a twelve-foot steel structure anchored directly into the shoreline substrate. Its form draws from arboreal and skeletal geometries—branching, load-bearing, and exposed. The sculpture is engineered for wind and wave resistance through a triangulated anchoring system, while its exposed members are intentionally vulnerable to corrosion and salt accretion. Rust, mineralization, and material fatigue are integral to the work’s evolution.

I approach this piece as a long-duration study in environmental contact. The Salton Sea’s salinity, toxicity, and instability produce a condition where matter records its own limits. Steel in this context does not simply weather; it metabolizes the site. Salt crystals gather along seams, surfaces pit and scale, and structural lines slowly shift in capacity. These processes are allowed to remain visible rather than corrected or sealed away.

My broader body of work examines how force distributes across systems—ecological, social, and material. At the Salton Sea, those systems converge. The shoreline holds histories of extraction, agricultural runoff, climate pressure, and infrastructural neglect. A sculpture placed here enters that field of pressures. It stands, accrues, strains, and eventually yields in parts, while its underlying armature persists.

This project treats permanence as duration rather than fixity. The sculpture remains present across years while undergoing transformation. Change becomes legible through contact with salt, wind, and water. The work asks viewers to consider how endurance operates under continual stress and how vulnerability emerges through exposure.

I am deeply committed to site-responsive practice and to building works that engage environmental realities directly. The Salton Sea offers a context where material, history, and atmosphere meet with unusual intensity. I would value the opportunity to contribute a work that grows from those conditions and remains in dialogue with them over time.

Thank you for your consideration.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach holds a rare density of environmental, social, and material histories in one place. It sits at the edge of a shrinking sea shaped by agricultural runoff, infrastructural decisions, and climate pressure. The landscape carries evidence of accumulation and abandonment at the same time: salt bloom on surfaces, derelict structures, sudden beauty, toxicity, resilience. For a materially driven practice, this is a site where forces remain visible.

My work engages tension, endurance, and the ways materials register stress. Bombay Beach offers conditions where these questions operate in real time. Steel corrodes quickly, organic matter stiffens with salt, wind redistributes surfaces, and the waterline redraws boundaries. The environment participates in the sculpture’s formation, leaving marks that cannot be fully predicted or controlled.

There is also a human dimension. Bombay Beach has become a gathering point for artists, experimenters, and people willing to live alongside precarity. Creative activity here carries a sense of exposure and improvisation. Work placed in this context enters a conversation with a community that understands impermanence firsthand.

I am drawn to Bombay Beach because it concentrates environmental transformation, cultural memory, and material intensity in one shoreline. It is a place where a sculpture can remain in direct contact with the forces it speaks about, and where change stays legible over time.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I am at the stage of prototyping and early build, though given my previous work with steel and rawhide my discovery will be in the reaction the piece has to the environment, not in the fabrication of the piece itself.

fields.Project Description: We’re making a ten-foot Pez dispenser topped with a charactered likeness of Deno, a prolific Pez collector and beloved figure of Bombay Beach. The dispenser might work, the head may lift. Something might dispense. Or not. First you laugh, then you squint, then you realize you’re standing at a memorial of a Legend of Bombay.
fields.Abridged Project Text: A massive pez dispenser Modeled after the late great Deno. A legendary Pez collector and a legendary man. It’s a joyful monument to  sweetness, hobbies, and the small rituals that survive us. Because grief, like candy, is easier to swallow when shared, unwrapped, and a little ridiculous.
fields.Technical Support: The piece lives outdoors on the beach and requires very little to function. No sound, no projection, no grid power. The only electrical element is basic lighting, likely solar, so it can glow quietly at night. Otherwise, it relies on scale, placement, and durability to do the work.
fields.Project Materials: Deno's PEZ dispenser in the desert.png (https://v5.airtableusercontent.com/v3/u/51/51/1774231200000/uYqYTqhbFK9pN9ZfPOVGCg/RO1hLgZNsUN6k2W6DCqQJPNLvjq-DPzMd8LmT3S3Z-CK_HZUPkNZKDZ3deXaRJKaW1t59-fgYcCWRqrQc0mT1mezAudnHyNFlvHNdq50LhyrVJcB3D2r_5Zjt5Wu1X9ranfp3vlvCLa62E5RI7_Eef2p2EgCYict2IbTIllW1G_DeMv3YSJ1lQIMMr99j578/sl7edkSbIlQIeMpfxZXYgPoJRlxv1r6mik-U8m3RUTo)
fields.Audience Engagement:
This is a piece people mostly stumble into on the beach. You spot it, do a double take, and walk closer because a Pez dispenser should not be here. Engagement is informal and curious: people circle it, pose with it, debate whether it works, and maybe try to make it work.
The head lifts. Something might happen. That uncertainty is part of the joke. What begins as novelty  shifts into something warmer once people learn it’s modeled after Deno. The interaction isn’t instructed or performative, it’s social, whimsical, and human.
It’s bright and absurd, a quiet landmark, a toy turned memorial. People don’t just look at it, they encounter it, talk about it, and carry it with them.
fields.Theme Engagement: Not directly. Our project is not a warning or a reckoning, it’s a pause. If it brushes against The Last Judgement at all, it does so sideways and absurdly: a memorial to sweetness, obsession, and small human joys.
fields.Risk & Safety: No fire, vehicles, drones, or hazardous materials will be used beyond standard construction material. All installation will follow basic safety protocols to ensure stability and public safety.
fields.Location Submittal: Tesseract lot. Tao already ok’d
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We want to make this work because Deno should be here and because he isn’t. This piece is a way of keeping him present without pretending he didn’t leave. Deno’s love of Pez wasn’t a hobby; it was a full-fledged performance. He collected them obsessively, joyfully, and with pride.
The idea came the way many ideas do here. In the Bombay Chats, someone casually suggested that someone should make a giant Pez dispenser. We took that as a challenge, a permission slip, and rose to the occasion.
Why now? Because remembrance doesn’t have to be solemn to be sincere. Because not every memorial needs a plaque, a lesson, or a lowered voice. This project asks different questions: What deserves to be oversized? What gets remembered when sincerity is allowed to be a little ridiculous?
The Pez dispenser is an ideal vessel. It’s a toy scaled up to something civic. It looks like it might work. It invites curiosity without instructions. Like Bombay Beach itself, it lives comfortably between earnestness and absurdity.
The process will be hands-on and communal, built with friends, fabricators, and artists who knew Deno and will feel his absence. Collaboration happens the way it usually does here: during the build, through side conversations, shared tools, unsolicited opinions, and the slow accumulation of stories.
Our practice gravitates toward objects that feel almost useful, almost official, and completely out of place. Bombay Beach is where that sensibility feels most at home. The Salton Sea doesn’t need another warning or prophecy. Sometimes it needs a loud, brightly colored reminder that joy, fixation, and affection are worthy of being blown wildly out of proportion.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
This piece belongs in Bombay Beach because this was Deno’s home. Not symbolically, not temporarily, but literally. He lived here, collected here, his community is here. The scale of the work mirrors the scale of his presence in the community: larger than life, kind, and impossible to ignore.
Alongside the Salton Sea, the Pez dispenser feels right. Bombay Beach has always been a place where the personal and the monumental sit side by side, where big ideas are built from small, stubborn obsessions. Deno fit that landscape perfectly.
The Biennale thrives on work that grows out of real lives, real relationships, and real place. This isn’t a sculpture that could be dropped anywhere and still make sense. Removed from Bombay Beach, it becomes a novelty. Here, it becomes a portrait. It holds local memory, local humor, and local grief in equal measure.
The Salton Sea has seen cycles of boom, abandonment, reinvention, and endurance. Deno was part of that continuum, not as a cautionary tale or a myth, but as a neighbor. This piece doesn’t attempt to explain the landscape or redeem it. It simply adds to it: one brightly colored marker of affection, standing where it belongs, because the person it honors belonged here too.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): The project is in the planning stage: fully imagined, carefully scaled, and waiting on funding to become real. The design and logistics are mapped out, materials identified, and the main remaining ingredient is support. With funding in place, the work mov
