fields.Project Description:
Morbid reality check: The world’s tallest ghosts are just simply not very tall. 

HOWEVER: With the addition of but ONE proper TALL ghost, done with no compromise, we could surely transform that short-bus of sad dad slouchy spooks back into a badass unholy-spirited posse of tall banshee-banging FUCK PHANTOMS. 

AND bring the total number of registered town ghosts up to a funnier and luckier number of 33 while we’re at it. 

Which is why I want to just make ONE FINAL, new, properly done, properly TALL Ghost to proudly represent and reaffirm the towns’ claim to being “Home of the WORLD’S TALLEST GHOSTS” for generations to come. 

Yes, that is correct: with the addition of but ONE new spirit, done by the numbers and standing a solid 15+ ft tall, we could conjure some of the dark magic of antediluvian Old Bombay and really get tourists heads to start turning (ideally 360°, Exorcist style).

It would be made in *ostensibly* the same manner as its forefathers… yet without the compromises that resulted when one makes 30 ghosts on a budget of 0. The same compromises that ultimately caused the existing ghosts to succumb to the elements so quickly. (I.e., using a proper mixture of joint compound and latex paint instead of tile grout and whatever paint I could find laying around, and a proper steel frame mounted securely rather than scrap wood in a plastic bucket of concrete, etc. etc..)

And perhaps best of all, to SAVE YOU STRESS: no further space would have to be assigned for its installation, as it could simply take the place of the current “tallest ghost” is located. The former one could easily find a new home in a dumpster perhaps, as it was also amongst my absolute LAZIEST last-minute designs.
fields.Support Needed: I’m broke, so this is only happening if the funding happens.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Bombay Beach is famous for being home to at least 33 of the World’s Tallest Ghosts. Or has it? Claims have been made that our ghosts may be undeserving of the title. We must maintain integrity.
fields.Technical Support: Just materials.
fields.Audience Engagement: They’ll see it as their eyes trail upwards from the awning labeled “WORLDS TALLEST GHOSTS” and then they’ll hopefully chuckle, if not laugh aloud.
fields.Theme Engagement: What says final judgment like a nearly two-story-tall representation of death incarnate?
fields.Risk & Safety: I’ve got experience with making 32 of these damn things. I know what works and what doesn’t. Additionally, I intend to install it much the same way as I installed Wanda’s tentacles — which have survived a goddamn actual HURRICANE. So I know what works and what doesn’t in Bombay.
fields.Location Submittal: Worlds Tallest Ghosts, ZigZag Plaza
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Kinda. Pretty sure I explained that all?
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Consider, please: How the original ghosts were constructed on a practically NEGATIVE budget which I sourced myself (as has been the case for everything I’ve done in this town) and their construction made up exclusively from found local scrap and items shoplifted from Home Depot. To say corners were cut would be ingenuous insofar as the corners never even existed in the first place. As a result, now — a mere 4 years into their afterlife — many of the original 32 have already passed into oblivion, and the most prominent of the collection (located adjacent to the Zig Zag plaza) looking pretty slouchy and tattered… and certainly not TALL

 As one of the last few Americans somehow operating as a lifelong “professional” creative type, unlike the droves of weekend-warrior “artists” who litter the town with their vapid, albeit well-constructed bullshit, I’ve never had the money from a 9-5 to fund any of my endeavors. Thusly, every one of my contributions has been done with a huge asterix attached, in the sense of “if I had the money to do it properly, it would have been THIS much cooler!” The ghosts have taken on a life of their own — I’d really like the chance to justify their existence without that asterix attached.

This is not a memorial to anyone. Nor is it designed to raise awareness for any cause or any number of dead foreign children. Supposedly “the less sense it makes, the more sense it makes”, or so I’ve been told. Let’s see that in practice for once. Or, fuck it, let’s make another Shig memorial instead, right.?
fields.Why BBB (Grant): It’s 32 brothers and sisters all live here, as do I.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Just need money and I could make it happen

fields.Project Description: I am creating a medium-scale flame effect piece. It will be a curl of copper and steel, with flames pulsing from 12 jets of fire. It will be 8 ft tall and 3 ft wide. It will feel warm, look bright and shiny, and sound like clicking and roaring.
fields.Support Needed: I'm looking forward to bringing art to Bombay Beach!

fields.Abridged Project Text: Curl is a new flame effect piece inspired by the math and physics of electromagnetism. Twelve jets of pulsing fire on a beautiful twisting helix of copper and steel.

fields.Audience Engagement: Participants will see it at a distance and be drawn to it. Participants will enjoy the beauty and visual intensity of the flames on the metal. They will gather near to enjoy the warmth of the fire and inspect the intricate technical details.

fields.Risk & Safety:
This is a propane flame effect piece. I am an experienced flame effect artist and have brought pieces to Burning Man, SF Decompression, unScruz, Lawrence Hall of Science in Berkeley, and more, both independently and with the Flaming Lotus Girls.
This piece will comply with best practices and standards for effect construction and site safety, including emergency plans, fire extinguishers, safety shut offs, proper site layout, and conformance to NFPA regulations. fields.Project Description:
"If I were a younger man, I would write a history of human stupidity; and I would climb to the top of Mount McCabe and lie down on my back with my history for a pillow; and I would take from the ground some of the blue-white poison that makes statues of men; and I would make a statue of myself, lying on my back, grinning horribly, and thumbing my nose at You Know Who."
-  Bokonon

How will the world end?

Everyone smells some form of pool-pah and rapture : preppers, billionaires, zoomers, doomers, pagans, evangelists, and Bokononists.  Will we collectively freeze or burn, fire and brimstone style - or will we suffocate in the inescapable trash and pollution enveloping every facet of our culture and lives?

We shall soon gather 63 years after March 18, 1963, the original publication date of Kurt Vonnegut's Cat's Cradle. While fixating on some numerological granfalloon we shall bear witness to our own self-induced suicide. Busy, busy, busy. 

Corporations have stolen the labor, art and ideas of generations past, are stealing our identities, and soon will steal the whole fucking future.

They have amalgamated a stochastic mechanical cancer which makes all other diseases cry. This virus is more reckless than any weapon or chemical so far concocted : armed with seemingly endless capital and oil, they are already wreaking a blinding burning form of havoc.

If you're not yet a Bokononist, the cold reality of *slop-nine* might be a wake up call, a vin-dit. And *Slop-nine* is my saroon. 

This project is both a collective retrospection of how humanity ended up with so much slop and a personal reflection and amalgamation of the slop I've constructed and accumulated in my own life : living beyond the bubbles most people pretend they are trapped in, yet often stuck in my own myopic meanderings.

The nine panels will be assembled from such slop : artworks past, writings, self portraits, notes, and books collected on roadsides. It will be assembled in Reno upon landing on March 13 and installed in Bombay Beach 10 days later.

Slop is always relative to a context : the slop that is being generated today cannot just end up in endless landfills. Slop is the byproduct of an unstable system unable to recycle its waste. Every biological organism or species has coevolved with others such that there is no waste not consumed by some other such organism or species. Humans are the first such specimen to exceed our biological boundaries : and there's no one to clean up after us other than ourselves and those unlucky generations future.

Slop-nine is a showcase that we must not only control the generation of new slop, but utilize the slop of generations past. The fragments of over a decade of artwork reassembled shall complement the many such collages and experiments blooming in the harsh western deserts.
fields.Support Needed: Attached are portfolio samples that could give a sense of the materials being used : and the panels were examples of works I took to Burning Man 2022. For other work see https://wmacmil.github.io/personal%20blog/dusty-libraries/
fields.Abridged Project Text:
Slop-nine has crystalized. A enneaptych (9-ptych) depicting our world's unstable suspension between genesis and extinction, impression and sensation, belief and reality, heaven and hell. 

I offer fragments of a personal journey collaged as an ever-incomplete altarpiece resisting drowning in slop.
fields.Technical Support: Ideally, close access to a generator or power source for night lighting would make things easier. Indoor or outdoor is still very much subject to space availability indoor : it would make assembling the artwork much easier for me. Installation might require access to basic tools that would be ideal to borrow if they are available.

fields.Audience Engagement:
Defying the boundaries of "completed artworks", passers by will be invited to contribute to the artwork itself relative to whatever material I may bring or they may have. The conditions of suchs forms of contribution are currently amorphous. 

The dimensions, display, and directions relative to the viewer are still yet to be decided and subject to change relative to placement. Will it be beside the sea or within a space? I hope to discuss these matters with whomever soon.

In addition, I'd like to give a talk if possible at one of the venues describing slop-nine and the ideas invoked in its creation.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Slop-nine is an interpretation of The Last Judgment. Indeed, Bokononism and ice-nine were Vonnegut's symbolic engagement with the absurdity of religious prophecy post WWII where the potential of nuclear annihilation was a button press away. Armed with more sophisticated yet irrational buttons today, there exist uncountable unstable unfolding catastrophes now in motion over a planet with 10 billion people where billionaires and plastic serve as nihilistic false prophets.

The polyptychal motif underwent an aesthetic transformation in proto-industrial Europe concurrent with  the black death, colonialism, the so-called renaissance and the reformation. Bosch's triptychs such as  "The Garden of Earthly Delights" and "The Last Judgment" exemplify these metamorphoses on top of his own metorphasis. He contrasted morality and spirituality through a perplexingly surrealistic style. Going beyond the doctrine of the holy trinity : Bosch injected the absurdity of our foibles and immanent doom into the mind of the viewer.

The industrial slop of the industrial revolution has devolved into a new form with the advent of a fully corporate-mediated life. Recognizing this, unmitigated and untamed slop is already drowning us. The nine panels will exemplify nine phases of slop. Our current moment : suspended between the potentialities of technology and they're unscrupulous deployments serves as an homage to those works of suspending Christ's judgement between Eden and Hell.
fields.Risk & Safety:
If outside there digging some holes or banging in some rebar to support whatever structure may be necessary.

I don't foresee serious hazards otherwise.

fields.Project Name: Grounded: Loss at the Salton Sea
fields.Project Description:
Concept
A decommissioned boat will be cleanly cut across its width, exposing its interior structure. The two halves will remain aligned but slightly separated to emphasize rupture and absence. The vessel will be partially buried into the dirt of the dried marina floor, appearing as though it ran aground when the water receded.
The boat will look stranded—not staged. Dirt will be packed naturally arc V, the hull, reinforcing the sense that it has settled there over time.

The central message is simple: the boat did not move — the water did.
To further emphasize the scale of loss, a visible marker will indicate the former waterline along
nearby marina posts or structures.

Objectives
• Bring attention to the ongoing shrinkage of the Salton Sea
• Highlight environmental and ecological consequences
• Create a striking, accessible public artwork
• Encourage dialogue about climate impact and wa 
At ter management
fields.Abridged Project Text:
"Grounded: Loss at the Salton Sea" is a site-specific art installation at the Bombay Beach Marina that directly addresses the dramatic water loss and environmental decline of the Salton Sea.
The project will feature a boat cut in half widthwise and partially ~ bedded into the dry marina basin, positionea as if wrecked and permanently stranded. 

The work transforms the abandoned marina into a powerful visual statement about disappearance, environmental fragility, and the consequences of water depletion.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Visitors will be able to walk around the embedded vessel and observe the exposed cross-section.
The contrast between the boat's intended purpose and its current grounded state will create a powerful visual metaphor.
fields.Risk & Safety: No
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Overview
"Grounded: Loss at the Salton Sea" is a site-specific art installation at the Bombay Beach Marina that directly addresses the dramatic water loss and environmental decline of the Salton Sea.
The project will feature a boat cut in half widthwise and partially ~ bedded into the dry marina basin, positionea as if wrecked and permanently stranded. 

The work transforms the abandoned marina into a powerful visual statement about disappearance, environmental fragility, and the consequences of water depletion.

Artistic Intent
The work transforms the marina into a quiet stage of evidence. What remains is not dramatic destruction, but slow withdrawal.
The boat becomes a body without support. A structure designed for movement rendered motionless. A symbol of human expectation grounded by environmental reality.
By cutting the boat open, the installation mirrors the exposed lakebed of the Salton Sea itself-both interiors revealed, both systems vulnerable.
The absence of water becomes a material
presence.
Minimal signage will provide context about historic water levels and environmental change at the Salton Sea.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): "Grounded: Loss at the Salton Sea" uses the image of a severed and embedded boat to make environmental absence visible. Installed at the Bombay Beach Marina, the work turns a site of retreating water into a site of reflection— confronting viewers with w hat remains when a body of water can no longer sustain life or movement.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Conceptual - it will all be built on site in late feb/early march.

fields.Artist Name: Daniel Taylor & Sucre Rosa, DANIEL TAYLOR
fields.Project Description:
"The Toll" is a 4-foot-tall stationary sound sculpture repurposed from an industrial steel propane tank. Suspended from a massive, 8-foot timber gallows frame, the bell serves as a "vessel of breath" for voices silenced by the systems they challenged.

The Look: The exterior is a minimalist, weathered steel cylinder with no external markings, designed to develop a natural patina in the desert air. The interior is a hidden sanctuary of gold and silver vinyl text spiraling into the dome.

The Sound: The bell is struck externally by a heavy, chain-suspended wooden beam (a shumoku). The resulting sound is a deep, a thrum that transitions into a warm, lingering hum.

By reclaiming a piece of industrial scrap—something someone might otherwise see as waste—the project transforms a hollow shell into a loudspeaker for the unshakeable principles of activists like Berta Cáceres and Steve Biko.
fields.Support Needed: Thank you for your consideration
fields.Abridged Project Text: "The Toll" is a sound sculpture made from a recycled industrial propane tank. A heavy piece of industrial waste, transformed into a stationary bell designed to ring out the voices of those who were silenced for their activism.
fields.Technical Support:
The project is largely analog and self-sufficient, requiring an outdoor placement with a stable, level footprint for the 8-foot timber gallows. 

We'll use a solar-charged battery system to power the internal LED lighting that illuminates the text after dark.

We will handle the assembly of the timber frame and the suspension of the 4-foot bell and wooden striker.
fields.Audience Engagement:
This is a participatory ritual rather than a passive object. Participants are encouraged to manually swing the heavy wooden beam to sound the bell, a deliberate act that symbolizes the physical effort required to keep a legacy alive.
After "ringing out" the message, the viewer is invited to look underneath the rim and look up into the dome. Looking inside the literal resonance of the steel, they can read the spiraling poetry of Hannah Senesh and Federico García Lorca in an intimate, meditative space.
fields.Theme Engagement:
This project engages with The Last Judgement through the lens of accountability and the "reckoning" of history. 
"The Toll" suggests that while the state may pass a "judgment" of death upon an activist, the ultimate judgment lies in the resonance of their ideas—which, like a bell, continue to ring long after the "hangman" has finished his work.
fields.Risk & Safety:
The tank has been professionally purged and rendered inert.

The 8-foot timber gallows will be engineered for stability against high desert winds and anchored to prevent tipping.

The external striker is suspended by heavy-duty industrial chains with limited travel to ensure it cannot swing beyond the bell’s impact zone, protecting the participant.

The frame will be clearly marked at night with its own internal glow to prevent accidental collisions.
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): "Yes, I live and work in this region"

fields.Project Description: It's a sculpture made from porcelain tiles and rock. Heavy af. Belongs on the beach.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Broken porcelain pavers form a low field: one side smooth, one side shards, with a few tiles painted as “repairs.” A black dog-shaped stone appears to lift off and leave. The work treats judgement as a sorting system—intact/broken, clean/ruined—and suggests the simplest verdict is to exit the scene.
fields.Technical Support: None
fields.Audience Engagement: It will be discovered on the beach and gazed upon
fields.Theme Engagement:
My installation engages The Last Judgement through broken porcelain pavers arranged as a low horizontal field. One side is smooth and intact; the other is shards, creating a simple “verdict” split: intact/broken, clean/ruined.
A few fragments are painted in bright color, referencing cosmetic repair and the impulse to redecorate damage. A black, dog-like stone appears to lift away from the surface, suggesting rapture as a form of selection—escape, exemption, or release—played with dark humor.
fields.Risk & Safety: Removing sharp edges

fields.Project Description: I’m displaying a series of black and white photos from family and community from the 1940s and 1950s. The photos display life in Coachella Valley from the point of view of my grandmother and great grandmother, who had recently migrated to the Coachella Valley. I am working on a collage aspect of the photos, and a creative way to display the piece that will be weather resistant. I am still working how to display the piece. It may be a small sculpture I create using a cactus skeleton, or if the org has an idea for where this peice is to be displayed? The photos show life and home for my grandmothers family and community, and the Mexican American migrant experience in the Coachella Valley from this era. I’ve recently acquired these photos from my grandmother. My grandmother has dementia and she doesn’t remember who everyone is. Coming to bombay beach, to the Coachella valley which is a home to me, a home my ancestors lived in and chose, I want to honor the photos and showcase them. It is a way for me to embody ancestral knowledge and how this can be passed along.  I want to use these archival family images as a way to untangle and ground personal history and memory. The work is also a way for me to work through themes of displacement, memory, and desert life.
fields.Support Needed: Excited :)
fields.Abridged Project Text: An archival family history and collection of images found in my great grandmothers belongings showcasing life as recent Mexican migrants settled in the Coachella Valley in the 1940's and 50s.
fields.Technical Support: I don't need technical support.
fields.Audience Engagement: I imagine people stumbling into the series. I don’t have lighting or power for nighttime viewing, so I imagine people being able to view the piece during the day. I expect people to run into the series and perhaps ground them into the larger region of the Coachella Valley.
fields.Theme Engagement: I was raised in a catholic family, and my grandma would frequently say to me “we are living in our last days”. (Perhaps catholics were the originators of YOLO come to think of it….) She was always waiting for ‘the last judgement’. My grandma is my muse no matter what, but it feels only appropriate that I honor her with this theme.The woman who is my guardian angel, whose prayers sustain me,  and who ingrained me with catholic guilt. ;)  I am inspired by the altars, the angels, the beautiful virgin marys, the gaudy dramatic catholic art that mexican catholics decorate their homes with, the rich wood sculptures. She will turn 95 one month after BBB, and have been exploring these themes and what comes next. Whatever happens next, I hope she comes back as my abuela in the next life. She is a diehard desert girly, and she has been seasonally displaced due to age, and I want to bring her home and honor her in the desert.
fields.Risk & Safety: This project will not involve anything hazardous.
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I spend significant time there seasonally

fields.Project Description: The Ghost Cadillac captures the essence of this emblematic American car.    I envision installing the piece along the sandy shores of the sultan sea,  or even over the calm reflective surface of the water, so that the piece hovers above its own reflection.  This location will afford the piece plenty of breathing room,  as if this spirit of the Cadillac has landed as an apparition from another realm.
fields.Support Needed: yes,  here are links to two videos open Vimeo that document the piece -  https://vimeo.com/951307223?fl=ip&fe=ec    and. https://vimeo.com/951292332?fl=ip&fe=ec   I uploaded these video above but was not sure that the format transferred correctly.  Thank you for considering this piece,  your event toad the backdrop of the Sultan Sea seem to me the perfect event and venue to showcase the Ghost Cadillac.
fields.Abridged Project Text: Ghost Cadillac is a loving and meticulously rendered full scale sculptural line drawing of a 1972 Cadillac El Dorado.  The 'wire frame' sculpture has no paneling,  so that the effect is like a skeleton leaf,  a transparent and ghostly embodiment of this classic American automobile.
fields.Technical Support: I need very little technical support.  The piece is self contained.  At night the sculpture will be illuminated by el-Wire and this is powered by a series of AA batteries

fields.Audience Engagement: The piece is a sculpture and communicates concept through the visual language of material, line, and form.     Primarily the audience will engage with the piece  through their gaze.     I do plan to invite a musician freind to perform in and near the Cadillac, and so there will be one or two spontaneous musical performances.    The sculpture will be illuminated by el-wire at night, so the piece will continue to engage as an active, ethereal light sculpture throughout the night .
fields.Theme Engagement: 1972, the year this car was first released to the American market,  was a moment of consumer opulence- when comfort and style for the American driver was paramount, and environmental footprint was in large part disregarded.  This El Camino model of Cadillac had the largest engine of any consumer sedan of its class, and boasted a whopping 8 miles per gallon gas efficiency.    Through bringing the ghost of this car back to the present,  I want to reengage the audience with a reflection on our current energy crisis.    After 50 years,  are we still placing opulence and comfort over tender love for the environment?  Are we prioritizing consumer power and coolness over the care for the planet?   If the ship really is sinking,  are we at least making sure the last call to the party is a fun one?   The Ghost Cadillac honors the beauty of human design and asks us-  "At what cost are we making these decadent choices?"   If there was a Dinner Party hosted at the Last Judgement,   the Ghost Cadillac would be how the guests would arrive.
fields.Risk & Safety: no,  the piece is light-weight, with no sharp edges,  is non clime-able and the only lighting system is low voltage

fields.Project Description:
The Carcass monolith will be positioned South of The Only Other Thing is Nothing and East of The Seas May Sink Tomorrow, toward the dock. It will be oriented so as not to block or interrupt the view of other art pieces.

From a distance, the charred obelisk of the Carcass looks like a ruin – a disused staircase growing ever steeper and narrower toward nothing – beside it, a bright red glowing countdown clock. Up close it's a miniature city, occupied by the Karass: ever-diminishing, ever multiplying plaything bodies, collaborating, constructing and competing their way up the edifice toward a waiting rocketship – slender, strange and sexual.

Each new era of toys quadruples in number and quarters in stature as the ledges beneath them shrink in capacity:

- 1 human viewer on a beach – ~68” tall – looks down at a 3.5” step

- 3 woven grass dolls – 17” tall – help each other up a 7” step.  We plan to commission these traditional dolls from Torres‑Martinez Desert Cahuilla craftspeople

- Sixteen rag dolls – 4.25” tall – form a human pyramid up a step 14” high & 6” deep

- 64 lego builders – 1.5” tall – mine blocks to construct a staircase up a step 28” & 3” deep

- 256 toy soldiers & game pawns – 0.5” tall – brawl, orgy and make art, unaware as;

- 1 toy solider & 1 gold game pawn ride a concealed elevator up a 56” step

- 1 priapic rocket ship – 17” tall – waits on top

- 1 woven grass doll – 17” tall – is climbing the rear face of the monolith
A tale of kinship – collective and corrupted – on the crust of an ailing megabeast. 

As to process: Concrete is poured into a 9’-0” tall plywood mold, secured on a concrete base and reinforced with steel. Before the Biennale weekend, the formwork is wrapped in a blue satin shroud by attendants in black jumpsuits.
On Friday night, the same attendants return to burn away all the outer layers with a ceremonial fire, stripping bare the stubborn concrete beneath. Saturday the toys are added and posed in collaboration with passing and invited Bienallians. Then on Sunday night at sunset the attendants return one final time to remove the toys – returning the monolith to its barren state.
fields.Support Needed: The project is fully scoped and ready to build. Grant support will fund primary materials and allow the work to be realized at its intended scale.
fields.Abridged Project Text: A charred concrete monolith rises from Salton’s shore – an exponential staircase piercing the horizon. As each step doubles in height, their populations – toys from the stages of history and childhood – shrink in stature and surge in number.  Above them waits a rocket. A digital clock counts down.
fields.Technical Support: No technical support is required from the Biennale staff.  The sculpture is mostly static, with dolls that are secured and maintained only Saturday / Sunday of the Biennale. Two lightweight elements will be suspended by ropes and manually adjusted throughout the day on Saturday / Sunday. The clock will be battery powered and removed after the Biennale. The lights will be solar powered, and left as a permanent night-light.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The sculpture first moves through larval stages – large wooden mold, satin-wrapped shroud, flaming effigy, scorched carcass, and finally… occupied ruin. A place of play where Biennallians help pose and secure tiny inhabitants in this allegoric high-rise dollhouse.
From afar, the structure seems monolithic and vacant. But on approach, the subtle taper, growing steps and shrinking inhabitants create forced perspective to draw the viewer in – making them the first toy in the tale it tells, or even a god-like child at play with all of them.
Once near enough to see the tiniest residents – a swarm on the narrowest ledge, a single golden figure escaping them by elevator – the viewer might follow its path up the 9ft spire above, and feel the woozy vertigo of this vertical diorama; Human agency made vanishingly small before everything those humans built, which few may stand to survive.

Then every hour, the tale continues: The elevator physically ascends toward the rocket, as a woven grass doll scales the back at the same rate – their ropes wound further up in unison as the blood red countdown clock ticks toward sunset on Sunday evening, inviting the viewer to return for a grand finale.
But instead, when the clock hits 00:00:00, the jumpsuited attendants simply “rapture” all inhabitants away – to be donated to the Bombay Beach Museum of Bombay Beach – leaving the Carcass bare, scorched and desolate – forlorn as the trunkless legs of Ozymandias.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Traditionally, biblical last judgment scenes depict the return of Christ framed by chaos on earth below and peaceful order in heaven above. The Karass Carcass upends this logic by swapping divine topography for civic history. The lower denizens coordinate harmoniously, until the immensity of their self-made terrain drives them into tumult. Instead of a returning saviour, a sole ültramench races a chthonic deity from our collective infant unconscious toward the upper dais.

In form, the Karass Carcass recalls and reframes another final judgment tale; the Tower of Babel – the mythic ancient tower judged for trying to touch the heavens, and toppled into disarray as punishment.

Modern humans pierced the skies with rockets only to find them empty of gods, with no-one left to judge us but ourselves. So instead of Babel’s spiral staircase, the Carcass depicts an exponential one; a built environment compounding over history, until it excretes a rocket pointing to the heavens, awaiting its golden pilot… even as a primordial woven grass titan, equal to the rocket in scale, clambers up.

What will this showdown look like? Does the golden pawn decide who’s worthy of escape? Does the ancestral giant sit in judgment of our history? Does the viewer, as a godlike child at play, judge the actions of their own toys?

This question is left open; answers superposed, players gone before the tale concludes, leaving only the stage on which they played, its shape a reminder of everything it asked.
fields.Risk & Safety:
If permitted, the Friday-night burn will be limited to the sculpture’s disposable exterior formwork, using a minimal amount of kerosene. We will supply all fire-safety equipment, following the same controlled process used for the Sub Club Bench in 2025, which produced a manageable fire. If needed, the local fire truck (Chevy Suburban + water tank) can be engaged.
The sculpture is sited farther from town to reduce risk to inhabited structures. We intend to use PPE and remove all debris (leave no trace).
fields.Location Submittal: Near the shoreline. South of The Only Other Thing is Nothing and East of The Seas May Sink Tomorrow, toward the dock. It will be oriented so as not to block or interrupt the view of other art pieces.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No version of this project has ever been seen or exhibited before.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Karass Carcass is the first outing by Primordial Furniture, a new artist collab making objects for public spaces attempting vivid concrete thought-experiments. Architectural follies making hidden dynamics elegantly literal.
Our shared fascination is the turbulent edge between life and its affordances; infrastructure and whatever it structures, civilization and those it purports to civilize, building codes and the bias they encode – and how all that feels to live inside. A sort of allegorical gonzo dataviz for complex systems – playful, rapidly legible, stubbornly material.

We’re not aiming for art that is Revolutionary, so much as Resolutionary: No scapegoats or saviours, dystopias or utopias, just attempting to reveal the water we’re all swimming in. Perhaps enough to see how the latest revolutionary politic, product, or protocol may instead just be more fuel for the problem it hopes to contain. For us the priority is not commentary, but the clarity of the model.

This first work, then, is a vertical diagram of our modern fixation with scale, as pursued by industrialists, technologists and influencers as a de facto good. For many of them, if it doesn’t scale, it won’t survive.

The Karass Carcass doesn’t argue with that, so much as play it out – sketch-modelling the social physics to consider what scale does to individual agency, trust & coordination. Each step harder to climb, each ledge less liveable – the people tinier, more numerous. To move upward, the figures keep trading something away: intimacy for coordination; coordination for engineering; engineering for pandemonium and bottleneck.

The piece might equally have been called a Model of Moloch. A structure of structures that no-one within fully chose, none steer nor can stop, and yet obliges participation from all. Allen Ginsberg names the feeling in Howl with brutal clarity: 

“Sphinx of cement and aluminum… Moloch!… the incomprehensible prison… whose buildings are judgment… whose mind is pure machinery… whose eyes are a thousand blind windows… whose skyscrapers stand in the long streets… They broke their backs lifting Moloch to Heaven! Lifting the city to Heaven!”
Whilst living, the work is a pubescent seaside Moloch, extruded from the sands of earth and forged in fire. Once dead, it’s a carcass whose shape recalls the tale of those who lived on it, their actions orchestrated by it in ways they often couldn’t see.

The only text that accompanies the piece is a simple plaque on the rear base:

THE KARASS CARCASS
Primordial Furniture, 2026
ka·rass, noun
A network or group of people unwittingly co-ordinated toward some larger outcome.
(Coined by Kurt Vonnegut in the 1963 novel Cat's Cradle, set during a man-made apocalypse.)
car·cass, noun
1. the dead body of an animal.
2. the structural framework of a building, ship, or piece of furniture.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The BBB transcends gallery, institution, even parade route, by making their venue the entire town. Cafes, waterways, residences, strange interstices – all made canvases at the scale of public architecture – and all beside a liquid mirror for environmental consequence. 
The raw concrete of The Carcass inhales the form of Bombay Beach itself, condenses it, and stands it on one end. A charred microcosm of settlement as symbol – a fractal extrusion that self-populates with all of human history as childish devotional; the Karass, its dolly citizens.
The Carcass blurs artwork, landscape and landmark – as atmospherics shift its sometimes stark silhouette, sometimes emerging from or merging with water or sky, its blues blending with backdrop.
The Karass roots deep into locality too, honoring the first humans that occupied this territory. The four traditional woven grass dolls are to be commissioned or made with guidance from local tribe members. 

Then, after the “rapture” on Sunday evening, the figures will be donated to the Bombay Beach Museum of Bombay Beach – refugees from judgement, joining its stubborn community forever.

fields.Project Description:
LooK is a large-scale sculptural installation addressing cyclist safety and shared road awareness.  The work spells the word “LOOK” through industrial steel fabrication, with the letters “L” and “K” flanking a full bicycle whose wheels form the two “O’s.” T he bicycle is painted white, its circular wheels reading visually as eyes alert, open, watching.  The piece measures approximately 8 feet long and 6 feet 6 inches tall, creating a strong roadside-scale presence.
Behind the bicycle stands a wooden telephone pole sculpture.  The bike is physically chained and locked to the pole, referencing the visual language of Ghost Bikes memorial bicycles installed at sites where cyclists have been killed by motorists.  This familiar yet haunting form immediately signals both warning and remembrance.  At the base of the sculpture, a potted flowering plant will sit alongside fabricated metal flowers permanently attached to the structure.  The combination of living and metal blooms transforms the work into a living memorial one that evolves subtly over time through wind, weather, and care.
Visually, the installation is stark and legible from a distance.  The white bicycle contrasts against the desert landscape of the Salton Sea, evoking vulnerability while demanding visibility.  Up close, viewers encounter the material weight of steel, chain, and wood industrial elements that echo road infrastructure itself.
Experientially, the piece invites pause.  It is not interactive in a literal sense, but participatory in awareness. Viewers may approach it as sculpture, memorial, or public safety message.  The word “LOOK” functions as both command and plea a split-second action that can mean the difference between life and death.
In Bombay Beach, the work exists within a landscape shaped by fragility, survival, and exposure.   Installed in open desert light, the sculpture will cast long shadows at sunrise and sunset, reinforcing the idea of presence and absence.  Over the course of the Biennale, the flowers may accumulate offerings, dust, or traces of human interaction, subtly shifting its emotional tone.
As an avid cyclist who has been struck multiple times by vehicles, this work emerges from lived experience.  It aims to spark dialogue around responsibility, visibility, and coexistence reminding us that sharing the road begins with a simple act: to look.
fields.Support Needed: Only the fact that I am Super Excited to be considered for this Awesome opportunity!!!
fields.Abridged Project Text: LooK is a large-scale steel installation addressing cyclist safety and shared road awareness.  A white bicycle forms the double “o,” chained to a telephone pole in the visual language of Ghost Bikes.  Flowers transform it into a living memorial urging drivers and cyclists alike to truly look.
fields.Technical Support:
LooK is a self-sufficient, outdoor sculptural installation designed to withstand the desert environment of Bombay Beach.  The piece does not require grid electricity, sound equipment, projection, or continuous technical maintenance.
A small integrated solar panel will power low-voltage LED lighting used to subtly illuminate the sculpture after sunset.  The lighting will softly highlight the white bicycle (the “O’s”) and portions of the steel letters, allowing the word LOOK to remain visible at night without overwhelming the surrounding environment.  The system will include a rechargeable battery pack housed securely within the structure, enabling automatic dusk-to-dawn operation.
The installation is constructed from welded steel, a reclaimed bicycle, heavy chain, and a reinforced wooden pole element.  All components will be securely anchored for outdoor stability, with consideration given to wind exposure and uneven desert terrain.  The potted flowering plant at the base will require minimal watering during the event; I will monitor and maintain it as needed.
No amplified sound, projection surface, or enclosed space is required.  The piece is intended for outdoor placement and natural interaction with sunlight, wind, and ambient conditions.
Overall, the project is low-impact, energy-efficient, and designed to function independently throughout the duration of the exhibition.
fields.Audience Engagement:
LooK is meant to be encountered whether intentionally, wandered upon at sunset, or stumbled into at 3 a.m. under desert light.  Its scale (8 feet long and 6’6” tall) makes it visible from a distance, while its familiar form a white bicycle locked to a telephone pole draws people closer.  The wheels read as watchful eyes, framed by the letters L and K, spelling L oo K as both command and plea.
The piece is fully approachable.  Viewers are welcome to walk around it, touch it, sit near it, and spend time with it.  The steel, chain, wood, and flowers invite both physical and emotional proximity.  Some may read it immediately as a memorial in the visual language of Ghost Bikes; others may first see it as playful typography before recognizing its weight.
Engagement may be quiet and reflective.  A cyclist might feel seen.  A driver might reconsider a habit.  Someone may leave a flower.  Someone else may simply pause longer than expected.
Because it references lived roadside reality, the work operates between sculpture and social mirror.  It asks viewers to reconsider how they see fellow humans moving through shared space.  The rider’s journey long or short is known only to them.  The piece encourages empathy across that divide.
Ultimately, engagement happens in the moment of recognition: when someone realizes the most important action on the road is also the simplest to look.
fields.Theme Engagement:
My project engages with the 2026 theme, The Last Judgment, through both personal and civic accountability.  While the phrase traditionally evokes a biblical reckoning, my piece reframes judgment as something that happens in a split second on the road.  Every time a motorist and cyclist approach the same stretch of pavement, there is a moment of decision to look or not to look.  That moment can determine life or death.
The installation centers on the word “L oo K,” constructed with the letters L and K framing a bicycle whose wheels form the double “o.”  The bicycle is chained to a wooden telephone pole, echoing the form of Ghost Bikes placed at sites where cyclists have been killed by motorists.  Metal flowers surround it, symbolizing the tokens of love left behind transforming the piece into a living memorial.  It stands as both warning and remembrance.
In the context of the Bombay Beach Biennale a gathering shaped by ecological fragility, survival, and participation the work becomes a call for shared responsibility.  “Share the Road” parallels the Biennale’s ethos of shared space and collective endurance at the edge of environmental collapse.
This piece asks: who is judged, and by whom?  Is it the driver, the system, our infrastructure, or our culture of distraction?  Ultimately, the work suggests that judgment is not a distant, final event it is ongoing.  It lives in our choices, our awareness, and our willingness to see one another.
fields.Risk & Safety:
The sculpture is constructed from steel and wood.  All metal edges will be ground smooth to eliminate sharp corners or protrusions.  The wooden pole element will be thoroughly sanded and sealed to minimize splinters while preserving its natural character.
The piece will be securely anchored using large steel ground stakes and wide base plates to ensure stability in desert wind conditions.  Due to its substantial weight and broad footprint, the risk of tipping is minimal.  No excavation, hazardous materials, electrical hazards, or dangerous actions are involved.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create LooK now because the moment feels urgent.  As both a cyclist and driver, I’ve experienced firsthand how fragile a human body is against steel and speed.  I have been struck multiple times while riding. Each incident came down to a split second failure to look. That moment of inattention becomes a kind of everyday reckoning.
My project engages the 2026 theme, The Last Judgment, by reframing judgment as something immediate rather than biblical or distant.  Judgment happens on the road.  It lives in the instant when a motorist and cyclist approach the same stretch of pavement and a decision is made to see or not to see.  That choice can determine life or death.
The installation spells “L oo K” through industrial steel fabrication.  The letters L and K frame a white bicycle whose wheels form the double “O,” reading as watchful eyes.  The bicycle is chained to a wooden telephone pole, referencing the visual language of Ghost Bikes memorials installed where cyclists have been killed.  Metal and living flowers transform the piece into both warning and remembrance.
At the Bombay Beach Biennale, this work enters a landscape already defined by fragility, survival, and exposure.  The Salton Sea embodies environmental consequence a slow-motion reckoning shaped by human decisions.  In that context, “Share the Road” becomes parallel to shared ecological responsibility.  How do we coexist in limited space? Who bears accountability? What systems fail us?
The process of making will involve steel fabrication, reclaimed materials, and careful construction to withstand desert conditions.  I welcome conversation during installation and throughout the Biennale; the piece is meant to spark dialogue.  Some may approach it as typography, others as memorial, others as social critique.
Ultimately, LooK operates as sculpture and social mirror.  It asks: who is judged, and by whom? Is it the driver, the infrastructure, the culture of distraction, or all of us collectively?
The work suggests that the Last Judgment is not a final event, it is ongoing.  It lives in awareness, in shared space, and in our willingness to see one another.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
LooK belongs in Bombay Beach because it exists at the intersection of fragility, consequence, and shared survival the very conditions that define the Bombay Beach Biennale and the landscape of the Salton Sea.
The Sea itself is the result of human decision-making infrastructure, oversight, neglect a slow-motion reckoning unfolding in real time. My piece reframes The Last Judgment as similarly immediate and human-scaled. On the road, judgment happens in a split second: to look or not to look. That moment carries consequence. In Bombay Beach, surrounded by the visible evidence of environmental accountability, the work becomes part of a larger conversation about shared responsibility.
The white bicycle chained to a telephone pole echoes roadside memorials while standing against an desert backdrop. Its starkness mirrors the vulnerability of both the land and the human body. The materials steel, chain, wood resonate with the region’s history of infrastructure and collapse.
The Biennale’s emphasis on participation over passive viewing aligns with the work’s intent. LooK is not simply observed; it is encountered. It asks viewers to consider how they move through shared space whether on the road, in community, or within a fragile ecosystem.
Bombay Beach is a place where endings and reinvention coexist. In that setting, the sculpture becomes both warning and offering a reminder that accountability is ongoing, and survival depends on whether we are willing to see one another clearly.

fields.Project Description:
The Human Cactus Memorial is a temporary, site-specific sculptural installation created for its debut at the Bombay Beach Biennale.  The work takes the form of an 8' human figure morphed into a saguaro cactus, constructed from interwoven bicycle locks.  The figure stands upright with arms raised toward the sky, holding two fabricated metal planters.
Visually, the sculpture blends human anatomy with the iconic desert silhouette of the saguaro.  From a distance, it reads as a cactus rooted, resilient, solitary.  Up close, the interlocked bicycle chains reveal a layered surface of protection, restraint, and connection.  The material choice reflects my identity as a lifelong cyclist; the locks, typically used to safeguard movement and freedom, are repurposed to build a body shaped by love and memory.
The piece serves as both a personal and communal memorial.  It honors my son, who passed away at four months old due to Trisomy 13.  The raised arms can be read as surrender, prayer, praise, or transcendence a gesture suspended between grief and hope.
Each planter will contain California native plantings: one with succulents and one with grasses or milkweed.  These living elements transform the sculpture from static monument to evolving organism.  Over the course of the Biennale, the plants will respond to light, wind, and care. Growth becomes part of the work’s unfolding.
In the stark desert environment of Bombay Beach, the sculpture will feel both exposed and grounded.  The metal will hold heat from the sun; shadows will stretch dramatically at dawn and dusk.  There is no sound component only wind moving through the structure and surrounding landscape.
Experientially, the work invites quiet reflection.  Viewers may approach it as a memorial, as a hybrid figure of endurance, or as an offering to survival in a place often described as apocalyptic. It is temporary in duration, but rooted in permanence of memory.
Ultimately, the piece transforms personal loss into shared space creating a living form that stands, endures, and grows in the desert.
fields.Support Needed: Just that I am Excited about being able to submit my idea for consideration, and looking forward to looking at all the art work out there this year!!!
fields.Abridged Project Text: The Human Cactus Memorial is an 8' human-scale saguaro formed from interwoven bicycle locks, arms raised in surrender and hope. Created for the Bombay Beach Biennale, it honors my son and embodies endurance in harsh landscapes. Native plantings transform it into a living, growing desert memorial.
fields.Technical Support:
The Human Cactus Memorial is a self-contained outdoor sculpture designed to function independently in the desert environment of Bombay Beach.  The piece does not require grid electricity, sound, projection, or other external infrastructure.
A small, integrated solar panel will power low-voltage LED lighting incorporated subtly into the sculpture.  The LEDs will illuminate the figure at night, highlighting the cactus form and the planters it holds without overwhelming the surrounding environment.  A rechargeable battery within the system will store energy during daylight hours to ensure continuous dusk-to-dawn operation.
The sculpture is constructed from interwoven bicycle locks, steel, and a reinforced wooden structure, all securely anchored with wide base plates and ground stakes to withstand wind and desert conditions.  The living plants in the planters will require minimal watering, which will be managed on-site as needed.
Overall, the installation is low-maintenance, energy-efficient, and designed for outdoor stability, allowing viewers to experience it day or night without additional technical support.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The Human Cactus Memorial is meant to be encountered quietly whether in broad daylight or stumbled upon at 3 a.m. under desert stars.  Viewers are encouraged to approach it, walk around it, and spend time with it.  As a memorial, it invites reflection rather than spectacle.
The figure stands upright with arms raised and open, a gesture that can be read as reaching upward, asking for a hug, or offering one.  Though not religious, the posture suggests transcendence, remembrance, and connection to something larger than ourselves.  The subtle expression and body language create an emotional entry point that is gentle rather than confrontational.
The living plants held in its arms represent care, continuation, and offering.  Visitors may see them as symbols of life persisting in harsh conditions.  The sculpture’s surface of interwoven bicycle locks reveals itself more fully up close, encouraging slower looking and tactile engagement.
Some may experience it as a personal memorial space and pause in silence.  Others may project their own losses onto it. Because it is temporary, its presence feels fleeting like life itself making the encounter more intimate.
Ultimately, engagement happens through recognition.  The work creates space for grief, resilience, and shared humanity in the open desert.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Human Cactus Memorial engages the 2026 theme, The Last Judgment, through ideas of survival, fragility, and what endures when life is tested by forces beyond our control. Traditionally, the Last Judgment suggests a final reckoning. In this work, judgment becomes something quieter and more intimate the confrontation with loss, resilience, and the meaning we construct afterward.
Debuting at the BBB, a site long associated with ecological collapse and reinvention, the sculpture stands as both witness and offering.  The 8' human-scale Saguaro cactus, formed from interwoven bicycle locks, embodies protection, restraint, and connection. Locks typically secure and guard what we value; woven together, they become a body a figure shaped by love and memory.
The work honors my son, who passed away at four months old due to Trisomy 13. The cactus, a desert survivor, symbolizes endurance in harsh conditions. Its raised arms suggest surrender and hope simultaneously a gesture that could be read as prayer, praise, or acceptance in the face of life’s ultimate judgment.
The living planters one with native succulents and one with native grasses or milkweed root the piece in ecological regeneration. Growth becomes an act of resistance. In a landscape often described as “the end of the world,” the sculpture proposes that judgment is not solely about endings, but about what we choose to nurture afterward.
It transforms grief into habitat, memory into growth, and loss into participation.
fields.Risk & Safety: The sculpture is constructed entirely from metal and wood, with all edges smoothed to eliminate sharp surfaces.  It is designed for reflection and contemplation, not physical interaction with hazards.  A large base plate, anchored with steel stakes driven into the ground, ensures stability in wind or uneven terrain.  The stakes are easily removable for installation and deinstallation.  No fire, ladders, vehicles, excavation, or other hazardous actions are required.  The piece is low-risk, durable, and safe for outdoor public engagement.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create The Human Cactus Memorial now because grief does not end it transforms. This work is a continuation of that transformation. It honors my son, who passed away at four months old due to Trisomy 13, and translates private loss into a shared, physical space.
The sculpture takes the form of an eight-foot human-scale saguaro cactus constructed from interwoven bicycle locks. Locks are objects designed for protection symbols of safeguarding what we love. By repurposing them into a human figure, I am redefining protection as something communal rather than individual. The body of the cactus becomes both shield and vulnerability, both endurance and exposure.
The raised arms can be read as surrender, praise, longing, or embrace. In the context of the 2026 theme, The Last Judgment, the gesture suggests a reckoning that is not punitive but human a confrontation with mortality, fragility, and what remains after loss. The cactus, a desert survivor, symbolizes resilience in harsh conditions. It asks: How do we endure? What grows after devastation? What does love look like when it has nowhere physical to land?
Each planter will contain California native species one with succulents and one with grasses or milkweed transforming the sculpture into a living memorial. Growth becomes part of the work. In the stark landscape of the Salton Sea, where ecological imbalance and reinvention coexist, this act of planting carries symbolic weight. The sculpture does not deny harshness; it roots itself within it.
The making process will involve fabrication, welding, and experimentation with interlocking forms to create structural stability and visual rhythm. I welcome dialogue during installation and throughout the Biennale. While deeply personal in origin, the piece is meant to hold space for others’ grief and reflection as well.
This project has not been exhibited before and is conceived specifically for the Bombay Beach Biennale. Bombay Beach is a place where art, survival, and exposure intersect. The desert setting amplifies the cactus form, grounding the memorial within a landscape defined by endurance.
This work deserves funding because it merges material transformation, environmental symbolism, and lived experience into a sculpture that is both intimate and monumental. It is not simply about loss; it is about standing, growing, and reaching upward even in the harshest conditions.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The Human Cactus Memorial belongs in Bombay Beach because it speaks directly to endurance within a fragile landscape. The shoreline of the Salton Sea is marked by environmental consequence, reinvention, and survival against difficult odds. A human-scale cactus rising from that terrain becomes both symbol and witness rooted in harshness, yet reaching upward.
Created specifically for the Bombay Beach Biennale, the sculpture aligns with the Biennale’s history of site-responsive, non-commercial work that embraces vulnerability and transformation. By repurposing bicycle locks objects meant for protection into a woven human form, the piece redefines safety and permanence within a place shaped by instability.
The eight-foot figure, arms raised, humanizes the iconic desert cactus. It grounds personal grief within a collective landscape. In a town often described as apocalyptic or abandoned, the work instead offers presence, resilience, and care.
The inclusion of California native plantings transforms the sculpture into a living memorial that grows within the desert rather than resisting it. This gesture mirrors Bombay Beach’s creative community itself artists cultivating meaning in a place many have written off.
Here, loss and survival coexist visibly. The sculpture does not escape the environment; it stands with it. In Bombay Beach, the work becomes more than memorial it becomes part of the landscape’s ongoing story of endurance and reinvention.

fields.Project Name: THE FOUR HORSES OF THE APOCALYPSE
fields.Project Description:
I’m making functional art, half horsefly trap, half horse legs for the strangest half-horse hybrid of all time.

The top half will be a black-wrapped trough, filled with soapy water, with clear plexi panels above it; these will call in the heat sensing biting female horsefly to them, and when she gets close enough to determine it’s not a horse, she will zoom into the clear plexi and knock herself into the water. There will be an included scoop and receptacle for the dead flies to be taken out.

The bottom half will be sturdy legs keeping the appratus upright, with CNC-cut horsefly legs fastened to the outside of those. As these will be most effective if they can be easily moved or cleaned, the traps will be lightly anchored to cinder blocks to weigh them down.

These art pieces, over time, should help reduce and possibly even work toward eliminating the local horsefly population in the hot months. It may also be that more of these will add to the effectiveness of this aim, so the artist also plans to make the plans available to the town, should anyone want to build additional traps for their property.

The plan is to install at least 1 by the time of the Biennale and the other 3 by the time the artist leaves Bombay Beach in early-to-mid-April. Ideally, most or all of them would be in place by the Biennale! But the true magic of these is in their effectiveness, which means when horesfly season starts to take off in mid-April and later.

There will be a QR code to a document for more information on the project, including instructions on how to build one for themselves.
fields.Support Needed: Photos are all of other designs. Picture a hybrid of those images + CNC cut out horselegs attached to the 2x4 legs. Also included is an image of an email with someone who shared her design online and the effectiveness and longevity of the materials.
fields.Abridged Project Text: I’m going to build 4 horsefly traps (each the size of a small horse), and place them at roughly 4 equidistant points at the outskirts of town (e.g. on property near the corners). These will be functional art pieces, with the top half designed to attract and kill horseflies, and CNC-cut horse legs.
fields.Technical Support: Some help in assembly, including borrowing any associated power tools the artist doesn’t already own will be welcome, although those arrangements have already been made.

fields.Audience Engagement:
Who takes care of the horses of the four horsemen of the Apocalypse?

In Bombay Beach, that would be the locals who live here year-round (or at least through the summer). Their support in maintaining and cleaning the traps will be invaluable. Whether through placing them on private property and entrusted to the residents of that place, or on public property, the artist plans to ask both the Biennale and the town for ongoing funding for supplies, and volunteers to step up to maintain these.

These traps are based on designs found on in deep parts of message boards online; ideally, an artistic tweak on these helps to popularize the base of the design to new audiences in rural communities both locally and around the world to help them fight back against the horsefly in inexpensive and effective ways.

So, visitors to these installations will be encouraged to lend a hand in cleaning them out and sharing photos online or privately to those who they think might be interested in building ther own.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse are harbringers of the Last Judgment aka the apocalypse! 

These art installations are the harbinger of the apocalypse for the horseflies which harass and bite the locals. 

These pests have been judged to be vicious assholes and the penalty is death.
fields.Risk & Safety: Just power tool safety.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to help the town mitigate the horsefly scourge in this project that blurs the line between art and function.

This project will only work through collaboration with year round residents but I hope that it will be of such a big benefit that it will be given gladly.

I also envision this project, particularly if it is successful, will lead to copycat versions in town, throughout Imperial County, and the rest of the world. Currently, the designs are lost in niche forums or blogs; an artistic vision behind them and a media plan will help to seed this design out.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): It's on theme, for the community, and for the Salton Sea to easily take the design and build their own. If hundreds of these are made in the Salton Sea area, it may over several years lead to the deluge of horseflies slowing to a trickle every summer

fields.Project Description:
The sculpture is Queen Martha, Martha is a mermaid, octopus, with a cow skull head with
horns and a moon crown. I would like to build her perched on the rocks with her tail on the sea
side and he body and tentacles rising above the rocks. Beckoning the sea to return to her, the
tentacle coming out of the shore in the distance almost reaching back to her. The piece will be
mixed media with a top layer of Pal Tiya, which I think will be a great material to withstand time
and weather of the harsh conditions of the beach.

fields.Project Description: 1-2 participants will walk into a chapel-like space filled with light, a soundtrack, and an actor/facilitator. Like the god Anubis, they will present the guests with a 3-5 minute participatory scene that includes singing, chanting, and discovery through dialogue. Guests are assigned a cause of death at random and will have an array of artful artifacts at their fingertips when they arrive, each representing a different judgement and path forward out of the room/scenic outcome. Whichever artifact they choose becomes the basis for a themed discussion about how judgement has intersected with their past life, and what judgement they have passed upon themselves. Through creative dialogue and role-play, meaning and interpretation from the symbolism of the artifact will be gleaned and the scene will conclude with a question to each guest - now that judgement on the past has been rendered, can they use their new judgement to bring something new or different into their next life? The judgement is written on a scroll and ceremoniously burned. They leave the space "re-born", letting go of whatever judgements that they may have come in with that are no longer serving them and advancing with a new goal, directive or judgement for their new being. We will allow guests in 1-2 at a time, and run the piece at various times throughout the event.

fields.Abridged Project Text: Welcome, wayward soul! Our condolences on your recent death. Judgement is upon you, as it is for us all. What will you take with you into your next life and what will you leave behind? The Reincarnation Station is interactive theater for 1-2 participants at a time that asks this important question.
fields.Technical Support: We would need power run to a small, indoor space that would lend itself to the scene (something like a chapel, empty room, or art installation with ritual/altar like qualities.) We would like to make use of a fog machine, a few LED lights and speaker (we would provide). We would have an altar table with props adorned on it, a small ash tray for burning incense and small scraps of paper, as well as a fountain pen and ink.
fields.Audience Engagement: This is participatory theater - in can only happen with the audience present. We would like to be placed in a location that is a little off the beaten path so some folks might stumble upon us, or maybe come looking for us on the word of a friend, particularly because our throughput will be on the lower side and unlikely to be able to accommodate large gatherings at one time.
fields.Theme Engagement: This is as literal as it gets at the outset, but we also hope to encourage discussion about what it means to be judged by others, by ourselves, and why judgement exists. We ask questions about how we relate to it in our own lives, both beneficially and toxically. We hope that by placing it in the scenic context of Egyptian and Tibetan mythology, it creates a fun and playful space to engage in personal insight.
fields.Risk & Safety: We would like to have a lighter to light incense and burn small pieces of paper. We would do so in a container that could keep the flame contained and away from flammable objects. We are happy to post outside the space that we are using fog if needed.
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I am visiting from elsewhere

fields.Project Name: Glorious Holy Kings: Queer Heaven in Bombay Beach
fields.Project Description:
Queer Heaven in Bombay Beach honors our existing and robust intergenerational queer community in Bombay Beach, offering them a safe place to be seen and setting the tone for their participation and comfort through the weekend. Through performance, comedy, and audience engagement, we are inviting all participants to be their freest selves at the Biennale.

This is a narrative cabaret as opposed to the true drag variety shows we’ve had in the past. We are budgeting for seven performers, and expect major improvements to our light and sound set up donated by our generous community. 

DJ Mella Tarantella is opening the drag show with a bumping post-sunset set 7:15-8:15, then we'll start the performance. Non-Binary Mother Mary is our host and emcee of this drag variety show inspired by the liberation of Queer Heaven. We have some of our stars from previous years returning for the 10 year anniversary. I can’t promise He Will Rise, but I can promise we will Turn Up. 

We will be hosting it at the Temple to the Scientific Method, creating the gates of queer heaven with white tulle. During the Preacher’s interlude, ushers in crop top white shirts and cutoff ties (think door to door Mormons or JW) will wander the space with collection plates for donations. Audience members will be offered prayer fans so they can testify to the best performances. It’s going to be a blast!
fields.Support Needed: I'm just overjoyed to be able to celebrate queer work in Bombay, and grateful for the Artist Grant program and team! <3
fields.Abridged Project Text: Glorious Holy Kings is a drag show highlighting Kings uniquely designed for Bombay Beach. The Queer community has faced many judgements through the years, and not one of them has stopped us from celebrating our most authentic selves! Kingdom comes in the land of milk, honey, and drag.
fields.Technical Support: We need a projector, two speakers and a subwoofer, a microphone, floor lighting, wash lighting (Cyc lighting) all of which are donated by Isaac of the Gay Bar Art Car and Alex who is doing makeup support for the show.

fields.Audience Engagement: This is a live performance that is mainly watched, with many opportunities for audience engagement. The show opens with a fun back and forth explaining how to interact with the performers in drag culture, and there is lots of call and response throughout.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The life expectancy for Black trans/nonbinary women and femmes is 34 years old. I am 33 this spring. This is my ‘Jesus’ year and statistically, the final year of my life. I am one of thousands if not millions who are living with extremely short life expectancies. We have already been judged. We have been raptured. We have been ravaged. Yet we survive. We thrive. Queerness is a roadmap to survive the end of days.   

Who knows how to be reborn better than a drag queen? Transformation and transmutation is the very business of our work. Drag invites the audience to imagine who they would be if they were without fear, without judgement. In a landscape of work that is likely to include polarities and binaries: saints and sinners, heaven or hell, we aim to transcend that. Our work is to create a Queer Heaven, a place where the injuries of the patriarchy do not exist. To show people what is possible in a brave and free world.
fields.Risk & Safety: It does involve the responsible use of pyro, Isaac has a fire element on the top of his truck. I will have extinguishers on site (budgeted) and talk to a member of the fire team to consult.
fields.Location Submittal: The Temple to the Scientific Method
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): The drag show is new by default in that we never repeat a single number. However, I took this desire to premiere new work quite seriously. I’m revamping the show from a variety show, into a more original narrative cabaret you can only see in Bombay Beach. The girls will be gagged.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Who are you, at the end of days? What do you want to stand for? Will you have been honest about what is in your heart? These are the questions that we want to prompt in our audience, largely using comedy. When we laugh, we are much more open to the ideas of others than if we are lectured to, and that is why the drag show is such a rare opportunity. We get to demonstrate who we are and how our culture frees us, and invites others to do the same.

One of the best things about the Drag Show is that it is a community effort. Dadaonysus is my personal queer elder, and one of the first people to make me feel safe in that regard. When he told me it was his dream to turn the Temple into an LGBTQ center in Bombay Beach, I thought it was a natural progression. This show has helped us build community all over the high and low desert - one of us usually takes a trip to talk to other LGBTQ centers about the show. 

It is important that we center drag kings, who are historically overlooked in the art form. This is great for Bombay in particular because Drag Kings are storytellers. Yes, it is about selling a look, but their performances tend to have more of a narrative element. 

Personally, it means a lot to cultivate a queer space, and to have it be one of the first major performances of the weekend. It is an honor to steward this part of the BB Queer culture.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): Bombay Beach has a historically significant queer community as it has long been adjacent to other queer desert hot spots like palm springs. A good amount of our elders or soon to be elders in Bombay are queer folks, and they always let me know how much the Drag show means to them. We are both creating and honoring queer history in Bombay with this project. We always encourage local folks to participate - we’ve had one local host every year - and have even hosted first time drag queens on our stage. A few different social groups have emerged out of our scene, including a queer mens’ meetup at the Ski!
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I’ve had meetings with every member of the core creative team, and I have verbal confirmation from two hosts. We are in the process of writing the script and developing characters, as well as finalizing our tech needs. Once I know I will have some funding
fields.Additional Artists: Dadaonysus, Isaac Taylor, Alex Pahl Skinner

fields.Project Description:
I live in Washington D.C. and I work in politics. Uh oh - off to a bad start already. When I was younger, I believed that we could change this country for the better - that through hard work, and by convincing enough people of our ideas, we could improve people’s lives. So I set out to get a job traveling the country, working on political campaigns, and instead I got a front row seat to the destruction of our democracy. I open my laptop everyday knowing that nothing I do right now will stop the swift, cruel advance of fascism. And the question becomes: what is left? What do we have when the future we dreamed of is dead, when the present is absurd beyond belief, and when the past is full of ‘if only we had known…’? 

July 2025. My roommate had been out of a job for 6 months (she worked at USAID). It was evening in the swamp - the heat of the day had burned off into something almost comfortable. I’d slept most of the day, mentally incapacitated with a fever. Amidst evening’s respite, I grabbed a jar of olives and went out to eat them on the porch in my sweaty pajamas. With my addled state of mind, and the taste of the olives’ salty brine on my tongue, I thought - ‘ah, what if I was a giant olive’. 

Thus, “Olive” was born. I spent the next 6 months obsessing over how to build a giant olive costume, writing an hour long clown show based around transforming into said giant olive, posting olives on my instagram story, and telling everyone who would listen about this genius idea. All of these things brought me no closer to realizing “Olive”. 

But as famed theater teacher Philipe Gaulier frequently said, “the clown remains optimistic”. So now, I try again. 
For the Biannale, I propose to walk around for a weekend as a giant olive (costume created prior to Biannale), participating fully in the festival. The Olive will be affected by other artists and by the elements - a collaborative project of hope, even as the Olive itself is changed and destroyed by its brutal environment.

Over the course of the weekend, I will write a final performance, to be performed in any location, from 11:00pm - 12:00am, on Sunday the 29th. The performance will be shaped by the Olive's experiences at Bombay Beach, interactions with others, and any profound observations. It will be a work of clown. 

In the Bible, in the midst of the great flood, a dove brings Noah an olive leaf, implying that the waters are beginning to recede. It represents hope, life, and new beginnings after total loss and destruction. Bombay Beach, similarly, strikes me as a place characterized by its state of post-decay. As she walks around Bombay Beach, Olive asks - what does it mean to hope in a place like this? In a country like this? In a world that has become so serious in its absurdity, can something even more absurd lead us to the truth about ourselves and our shared humanity? Or was it all just a fever dream…

fields.Audience Engagement: The project is meant as a conversation between the Olive, and the Bombay Beach Biennale. The bulk of it will be interactions between myself as the giant Olive, and the attendees of the festival. I want the Olive to participate fully in as many varied experiences as possible. The character's personality will be shaped by others. Perhaps even the body, as other artists may contribute to the Olive's design.
fields.Theme Engagement:
What happens after we are judged? After God has said we are good or bad? What if we turn into a giant olive? Perhaps the Olive is both the judger and the judgee. She exists in the limbo between states - between human and object, between life and mere existence. 

In the Bible, in the midst of the great flood, a dove brings Noah an olive leaf, implying that the waters are beginning to recede. It represents hope, life, and new beginnings after total loss and destruction. Bombay Beach, similarly, strikes me as a place characterized by its state of post-decay. As she walks around Bombay Beach, Olive asks - what does it mean to hope in a place like this? In a country like this? In a world that has become so serious in its absurdity, can something even more absurd lead us to the truth about ourselves and our shared humanity? Or was it all just a fever dream…

fields.Project Description:
I’m making a stand-up comedy/tongue-in-cheek collective call to cultdome, custom-made for the BBB. It will be a story about my divorce from a 12-year conservative-Christian marriage and how I found myself at Mars College.

I would like to do two 45-minute to an hour-long shows, where I invite participants to don white robes and flower crowns, and I will ask them – what would it take for you to leave your comfortable life? What if the apocalypse has come in like a lamb? Will we all know, together, when it is time to pick up and go? 

The show will be about pleasure, about the loss of faith (in our structures, our society), and about if instead of our grand search for the I, we should be looking for the We. 

Plus there will be jokes.
fields.Support Needed: Thank you for your consideration!

3/16: confirmed Sunday early evening at the zig zag
