fields.Abridged Project Text: What would it take for you to leave your comfortable life? Join Amy Brown Carver for 45-minutes of stand-up and collective comedy adventure about one woman’s search for freedom, meaning, and the closest flush toilet.
fields.Technical Support: I need a microphone, speaker, a small stage, and some manner of stage lighting.
fields.Audience Engagement:
At the beginning of the show, I’ll lead people into a relatively contained environment (outside or inside). I’ll do some extended crowd work – seating them in a semi-circle so they can see one another – where I pick at the fantasy of running away to the desert, or to the mountains, or to space and leaving all one’s responsibilities behind. When do people go? What makes them stay? 

By the end of the show, I will give each person a white robe and flower crown to put on, welcoming them to their new cult.
fields.Theme Engagement: What comes after the end of the world? Pallet racks, dust storms, and failed polycules… AKA Mars College.
fields.Risk & Safety: No
fields.Location Submittal: Night time - lit with microphone. 50 minutes - Institute back to back with Cameron Play? No Shoes? Saturday night/sunday night - night better
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No. I've worked out bits and pieces of the show in front of audiences before, but this will be the first time I will perform it publicly in its entirety.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create this work because I wonder why so many of us fantasize about leaving our comfortable lives – and why so many of us don’t do it. After divorcing from a twelve-year conservative Christian marriage, leaving a partner and an entire belief system. That rupture sent me searching for meaning, freedom, and pleasure in unfamiliar landscapes. 

This piece asks:
 What would it take for you to leave your life?
 If the apocalypse comes quietly, will we even notice?
 Are we chasing the “I” when we should be building the “We”?

The project is a 45-minute stand-up comedy performance and participatory “call-to-cultdome” experience created specifically for Bombay Beach. I would like to run the show twice over the course of the Biennale in a contained setting, with the audience seated in a semi-circle so they can see one another. I’ll combine structured material with extended crowd work, playfully examining our shared escape fantasies—desert, mountains, Mars. When do we decide to go? What keeps us here?

At the end, each audience member will be offered a white robe and flower crown – which they will be allowed to keep and continue wearing throughout the Biennale – and welcomed into their “new cult.” The gesture is comic, but it points to something real: communities form in moments of instability. So do cults. So does art.

Stand-up comedy is essential to this work because it is accessible. Humor—especially black humor—creates space to talk about faith, pleasure, environmental anxiety, and disillusionment without collapsing into despair. I believe in the politics of joy: laughter as resistance, as collective processing, as survival.

My practice blends memoir, performance, and social experiment. I’m interested in turning audiences into temporary collaborators rather than passive viewers. This piece is deeply site-specific. It belongs in Bombay Beach, beside the Salton Sea, where apocalypse and beauty already share the same horizon.
Ultimately, I want to make something funny, unsettling, and communal—an experience that asks whether freedom means leaving, or whether staying together might be the more radical act.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Mars College -- the “cult” at the center of the piece -- is located one mile outside of Bombay Beach. It is a real, desert-based community experimenting with alternative structures of living, education, and meaning-making. My show plays with the idea of joining a cult, but it does so in direct conversation with an existing community that has intentionally stepped outside conventional life. The proximity matters. The questions are embodied here.

Bombay Beach has long attracted people at personal and cultural crossroads. The fantasy of running away to the desert is part of its mythology. My piece examines that fantasy in real time, asking: What are we escaping? What are we building? What do we do next?
fields.Project Stage (Grant): I have about half of the material written and have workshopped it on stage at the Comedy Fort in Fort Collins, CO during October-December 2025. I still need to write around 20 more minutes and then shape the show into its final arc. For the actual staging
fields.Desert Resident (from Artist Name): I spend significant time there seasonally

fields.Project Description: A venue will be transformed into a cathedral type space, dark with flickering candles, projections will appear during the performance of angels and figures which include works by William Blake, plus lithograph postcards of the early 1900's featuring forlorn women sat at the church organ searching for an answer to prayer. The performance will feature 4-5 angelic tall figures who sing harmonies behind Kitty's main vocal. Kitty will also play organ during the show.
fields.Notes: k 3/14: Lives in her van, Sunday, We'll supply PA & projector
fields.Abridged Project Text:
I sought but to seek it vainly, 
that one lost chord divine; 
which came from the soul of the organ, 
and entered into mine. 
It may be that death's bright angel
Will speak in that chord again,
It may be that only in Heaven
I shall hear that grand Amen.
fields.Technical Support: Electricity, indoor space, organ, PA system with reverb/echo and 5 or 6 microphones, projector and large surface to project onto, preferably a venue with good acoustics.
fields.Audience Engagement: People will sit or stand to watch the performance. Seats will be available before the performance begins.
fields.Theme Engagement: Yes! The Last Chord Divine - the final call of a lost soul's prayer, the seeking of light amidst the darkness of the world. A cry of the soul, a desperate plea for enlightenment and salvation, the search for a higher force to transport our bruised and broken minds and bodies to eternal light.
fields.Risk & Safety: No

fields.Letter of Interest (Grant): Bombay Beach has been an inspiration to my work as an artist and musician for the past 4 years. I first performed for Brenda's 2024 Fashion Show, playing saxophone and singing whilst models walked the catwalk. This will be my own offering, personal to me from a spiritual viewpoint and also significant as I have played organ in church and written songs for Sunday worship at the Seaside Church for the past year. The Last Judgment is the most perfect theme for this offering! It's dramatic, spiritual, and features angelic-like beings and projections from vintage sources. It will be beautiful, and worthy to be part of the Bombay Beach Arts Festival.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): As a voluntary worship leader of the Seaside Church in Bombay Beach, I have been a part of the community for some time, and stayed on throughout the summer of 2025. This is the perfect setting amidst people and a place that I love and hold dearly in my heart.

fields.Project Name: THE VOICE THAT SURVIVED THE SUN
fields.Project Description:
THE VOICE THAT SURVIVED THE SUN is a solo ritual performance by Thee Suburbia, the artistic vessel of Jonathan Clarence Parrish Spence. The work merges live vocals, screamo, spoken text, layered media, a field of flexible acrylic mirrors arranged across the desert floor, a single breakable rubber glass object used in the final act, and a wearable fog vest.

The acrylic mirrors bend and distort reflection. They cannot break and symbolize external judgment that feels rigid, endless, and inescapable. Throughout the early sections of the performance, these mirrors create a fractured landscape that shifts with sunlight and movement.

The piece begins in quiet lament. Suburbia moves through the acrylic mirror field, their image warped and multiplied. Low breath, hums, and scattered textures form an unstable sonic ground. As judgmental voices grow, the fog vest releases thin vapor from the performer’s body, marking the burning. The vapor suggests spiritual overheating and the pressure of being seen through distorted mirrors.

The collapse arrives suddenly. The fog thickens as Suburbia falls to the ground, then disperses, creating the image of a body turning to dust. The soundscape fades into indifference. This is the near erasure, the witch moment where existence feels in jeopardy.

The resurrection emerges in silence. The fog has cleared. Suburbia rises through breath and voice alone. The media shifts from pressure to support, merging with the vocal line as the body reassembles.

In the final threshold, Suburbia lifts what appears to be one of the mirrors. This object is the only piece not made of acrylic. It is rubber glass coated with reflective film so it blends with the field. It represents internalized judgment. Suburbia twists it until it cracks safely. The gesture marks the rejection of the judgment they carried. The broken pieces return to the ground. The performance ends with a quiet exhale and a final vocal offering.
fields.Support Needed: Thank you for considering my proposal. This work is deeply important to me, and I’m committed to creating it with care, clarity, and respect for the site and community. I look forward to the possibility of bringing this ritual to Bombay Beach with full transparency and collaboration.
fields.Abridged Project Text: THE VOICE THAT SURVIVED THE SUN is a solo ritual by Thee Suburbia using voice, screamo, flexible acrylic mirrors, a fog vest, and one breakable rubber glass object. The work moves through burning and collapse into a self driven resurrection and a final ceremonial breaking of internal judgment.
fields.Technical Support:
This performance requires a portable PA or powered speaker capable of projecting in open outdoor space. A battery powered unit is preferred. I will bring audio files on a phone or laptop and need cable or Bluetooth connection. A microphone is helpful but not required. A short sound check is needed to balance voice and playback.

The performance uses flexible acrylic mirrors in multiple sizes for the mirror field. These will be shipped to the festival site or a designated receiving location. I need assistance confirming a safe place to receive and store them. They are lightweight, shatterproof, and safe to handle.

One separate rubber glass object will be shipped for the final break moment. It will be coated with reflective film so it resembles the acrylic mirrors but breaks safely when twisted by hand.

The wearable fog vest will be used briefly during the burning and collapse. It produces cool water based vapor and requires a short outdoor test.

One staff member or volunteer is needed to help guide audience placement and keep viewers outside the mirror field.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The audience observes from outside the acrylic mirror field. The mirrors bend sunlight and reflection, giving each viewer a different perception of the performer. During the burning phase, vapor rises from the performer’s body and interacts with the mirrors, heightening the sense of judgment becoming visible. The collapse and the soft dispersal of vapor create a collective pause.

The audience sees the final object as another mirror until it breaks. This moment is revealed as symbolic rather than literal, as only the rubber glass object is breakable. Engagement centers on witnessing transformation and the shift from external distortion to internal release.
fields.Theme Engagement:
This work treats The Last Judgement as an internal reckoning. The acrylic mirror field represents external judgment, which bends and distorts identity but cannot be shattered by force. The burning phase expresses the weight of being seen through these warped reflections. The collapse into dust represents the threat of erasure through pressure and indifference.

The resurrection rejects outside judgment. Suburbia rebuilds themselves through breath and voice. The final break uses the single rubber glass object, which symbolizes internal judgment. Breaking it safely transforms an oppressive reflection into something powerless. The work reframes judgment as an inward turning point rather than an external verdict.
fields.Risk & Safety:
This performance carries low physical risk. The flexible acrylic mirrors do not shatter and will be placed on flat ground with safe spacing. One staff member or volunteer will keep the audience outside the mirror field.

Only the rubber glass object is breakable. It is designed for safe handling and does not produce sharp edges. The fog vest emits a small amount of cool water based vapor and presents no fire or heat risk. There are no stunts, ladders, vehicles, or elevated platforms. Sound levels will remain safe. All materials will be removed after the performance.

fields.Project Description:
Come to my minivan! 
I will greet you and invite you in... You will enter my little space, sit down on the little couch while being tucked away from the outside world and surrounded by the personal, cozy and playful design of my Mamacita. 
I will be in the driver seat, you will not see me, as the curtains will be shut. You will hear me perform the love poem (spoken word style) that I wrote to Mamacita. You can stay as long as you wish, feeling the space. Maybe have some water or a little snack, read a little, write something in the book... When and if you'll choose to speak to me, I will open the curtains, happily answering any questions you have. Right there, your alone time with Mamacita will cease. I wish and hope you'll get to understand the amount of love, gratitude and appreciation that I have to her- and perhaps experience and feel it as well!
fields.Support Needed: Thank you and best wishes always!

fields.Project Description:
It's a short 12-14 minute play that may have the potential for immersive audience interaction before and/or after. It's the story of a man that's used futuristic ai technology to recreate people in his life that he's valued and lost or always wanted and never had. By the end of the play it's revealed that the obligations of his real life have fallen apart and that he stands on the precipice of his own doom as his made up companions invite him to walk through the portal between life and death. 

It will have a dreamlike atmosphere. It would be great if it could be staged at the opera house and/or as a way to open the AI film festival at the Drive in. Also looking to potentially do a site specific staging at Kirby's Bombay Beach residence called Neptune. I'm also looking to collaborate with a sound designer for the opening and closing of the piece. 

I've cast folks from Mars College and we'll be rehearsing the piece over the next few weeks. Additionally the original playwright will be coming to one of the performances.
fields.Support Needed: **Please be sure to only view/share the script with people on the selection committee. This script is still being developed by the playwright actively. The prospect of bringing urgent soul-provoking theatre to the Biennale is exciting to me. Looking forward to hearing back from y'all!

fields.Abridged Project Text: What would you do if providence found its way into your life after years of struggle. What if the people in your life finally contained traits that matched your ideals. What if one day you discovered that all your dreams were set to come true as you're surrounded by those near and dear to you?

It could also be fun to find an art piece on the beach to stage this near - but I'm not sure what would be the best compliment to it.
fields.Audience Engagement: It's definitely going to be a traditional theatre viewing experience for the main action. Though depending on how many additional actors I can enlist, I may end up involving more immersive elements in the run up to the main action and also potentially after the piece wraps up.
fields.Theme Engagement: It does in fact have connections to the theme. These are the final hours of the main character's life (though this isn't revealed until the end). Elements of his life are being brought to the forefront as he examines how he's shown up across from the people that have meant the most to him. He is judged twice in the play. Once near the beginning and then again at the end. Though there is no escape for him once his fate is sealed.

fields.Project Description:
This is a browser-based project.

Salton Sea Synth is a networked musical instrument built from environmental conditions across Bombay Beach. A distributed set of custom-built sensors by Seamus (BioSymphonic) will measure air quality in the form of airborne particulates (Air Quality Index). These sensors will form a living map of environmental conditions across Bombay Beach.

The installation will center around a visual map showing the locations of the sensors around Bombay Beach. Each sensor will appear as a point on the map that visitors can physically visit and observe. The map will display live data from each location, allowing participants to see environmental conditions as they change in real time.

Each sensor location will contribute differently to the synthesizer. One location might shape pitch or harmony, while another might influence rhythm, density, or timbre (currently in development). Together, the sensors will form a distributed instrument where the geography of Bombay Beach becomes part of the musical structure.

The installation will accessible online through a web-based synthesizer. Visitors will be able to listen to the live environmental soundscape on their phones and explore the same sensor map while moving through Bombay Beach.
fields.Support Needed: No funding needed, or scheduling. I'm working on this! This is in *addition* to my Bacterial Beats proposal, and I will make this happen no matter what, as a Mars Installation (using AQ sensors there), but ones in Bombay Beach would be better for the community and biennale. The AQ sensors will stay in Bombay Beach as an ongoing resource for the community

https://biosymphonic.com/bbaq/


Salton Sea Synth is a browser-based project that visitors encounter through their own movement around Bombay Beach. People may discover the project intentionally or stumble across a sensor location while exploring the town. Each sensor station becomes a small point of discovery where visitors can observe live air-quality data and understand how environmental conditions are being translated into sound.

The project is experienced primarily through phones (or laptops). Visitors will be able to open Salton Sea Synth in a web browser and listen to the live soundscape generated from particulate air-quality data collected at different locations around Bombay Beach. As people move through the town, they can listen to the same environmental signals that are being measured nearby.

QR codes placed at sensor locations and other sites will provide simple access to the project, linking directly to the live online synthesizer and sensor map. This allows visitors to engage with the work at any time of day or night, whether briefly or for extended listening.

The project is designed to be lightweight and accessible, allowing visitors to engage on their own terms while connecting physical locations in Bombay Beach to a shared online sound experience.

This project will "leave a trace" and exist beyond the Biennale.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Rather than depicting a single final moment of judgement, Salton Sea Synth presents a world in which judgement is already unfolding. The changing air becomes a living record of past actions and present conditions, a continuous signal that reflects the accumulated effects of human decisions over time. Airborne particulates drifting across Bombay Beach carry traces of agriculture, industry, drying shoreline, and windblown dust. The synthesizer translates these measurements into sound so that this record can be heard rather than only measured.

Listeners encounter an unseen presence made perceptible through sound. Particulate measurements that normally appear as numbers are transformed into shifting tones that reflect the conditions people are breathing in real time. The project invites a quiet form of listening in which the atmosphere becomes something that can be sensed directly, turning the air itself into a kind of ongoing spiritual presence.
fields.Risk & Safety: no

fields.Project Description: Our bar and performance venue “The Titty Twister” is an interactive theatrical rendition as seen in the movie “From Dusk till Dawn,” and brought to life with our own twists. Once the sun falls over Bombay Beach, the iconic neon sign is lit above fire performers to draw in Biennale’s strange and whimsical inhabitants. The inner décor is traditional gothic mixed with the charm of a western saloon (think batwing swaying shudders, Victorian furniture, candelabras, fine fabrics, swinging chains, dancing cages, and trinkets of old-timey feel). Saturday night will have entertainment running from dusk till dawn (burlesque and freak shows, live music, poetry readings, peculiar talents, pole dancing, performative storytelling, etc) as well as a full-service bar with blood related craft cocktails and wine. Once the final act concludes, DJ’s will spin Romanian trance, 80’s synth, and gothic remixes to keep our guests dancing well into sunrise.
fields.Support Needed: We are truly excited to bring this project to life. Unfortunately, the main thing holding us back at this point is funding. Given the current economic climate, it’s difficult to imagine asking this many professional performers to travel to Bombay without a budget that at least helps cover gas and food.  We are committed to bringing top-notch entertainment to the community, and this grant would play a crucial role in making that possible. Also I wanted to note that I had issues uploading the previous works documents  because the max file size was 20 mb, attached is a google drive link please let me know if you ave any further questions
fields.Notes: k 3/14: confirm if they are coming, didn't receive grant
fields.Abridged Project Text: When The Sun begins to set and the shadows start to rise, the Titty twister comes alive! Step right up and come inside our house of debauchery , where freaks dance the night to the the beat pulsing through our bodies like the blood pouring into our glasses as we slip into the dawn.
fields.Technical Support: Electricity would be tremendously helpful but not a requirement—our crew has generators available. All visual and audio are taken care of in house. Our two biggest concerns are grant money to properly reimburse our professional performers for time and travel, as well as the right sized building/infrastructure to host, decorate, and provide optimal immersion for our guests.
fields.Audience Engagement: All of the above and more! Whether stopping in for a drink, catching a show, or grooving to the music, we consider all our guests to be part of the performance. The play doesn’t pause between acts, nor end when the DJ’s carry the rest of the evening; we seek for our visitors to have forgotten where they came from while becoming entirely immersed and under the spell of where they’ve arrived; to assist in this, our performers, staff, and barkeeps remain entirely devoted to being in character (as seductive vampires) while interacting with each guest through the night. Every one of our actors are unique in their vampiric lineage with performative storytelling, whilst role playing guest interaction (being sure to relay why the Titty Twister was erected in Bombay beach of all places, and how the fate of each weary desert traveler has led them here).
fields.Theme Engagement: The Titty Twister in and of itself, represented a Last Judgement of sorts—turn back and seek redemption from evil, or enter and forsake salvation as a sinner before the eyes of a cruel God. Whether an entirely lost or once prosperous soul, this bar was the very last stop on that long and lonesome highway to hell. A place to fully embrace the hedonistic pleasures of lust and desire without feeling guilt, shame, or remorse—while under the bewitchment of those seven deadly sins. The end times are drawing near; so, what better way is there to go out…than to live the rest of those hellish days partying until the blood runs dry.
fields.Risk & Safety: We are curating an outdoor fire show to reveal the bar’s grand opening Saturday night. We have a conclave of professional fire performers and will be assigning a safety marshal from our team to supervise spotters with proper knowledge in fire safety procedures and danger preventing protocols. This team (equipped with safety blankets and extinguishers) will keep guests at a proper distance until the show has concluded and guests may enter the venue.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No,  as a collective we have put on a number of burlesque shows , and fire performances and interactive stages, but this is our first time coming together to create a completely unique  experience for the people of bombay.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
To be frank, my crew and I are rag-tag hooligans head over heels for debaucherously stylistic performance art.
And like anyone who found deep appreciation for the cult classic, “From Dusk till Dawn,” for a
long time we’ve dreamt of stepping into the Titty Twister’s chaotic and hedonistic bar scene
while providing an experience for patrons of which they’ll never forget (in all the best ways).
This unique opportunity of self-expression which Biennale provides in such a raw, unadulterated
fashion would allow us to fulfill a dream which inspires us—in a setting which inspires us—and
perform for the sake of performance itself. We’ve asked ourselves many questions in the process
leading up to this application, and most of them center around whether we can pull off this kind
of endeavor financially. We have the passion for it, possess the drive, and have worked diligently
to curate the vision—but if we cannot afford to bring it proper life, we do not wish to put forth a
sub-par experience for those we seek to bewilder. Over many years, different events, places, and
interesting canvases, our crew has come together through thick and thin to successfully realize
someone else’s vision and bring joy to those our artistic expressions can touch—but now, with
your help, we seek to shape our own. We adore Bombay Beach’s community and thrive off the
influence of which Biennale has time after time provided us; but now we are willing and able to
be more than spectators—and would love nothing more than to begin a relationship with your
event in a way which inspires the masses in the same ways you’ve inspired us.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
Bombay Beach symbolizes the stark beauty of a desert town on the fringe of civilization and
verge of collapse—yet, is held together solely by an eccentric community of artists with belief in
something more than an ordinary world of mundane experiences. In ways unbeknownst to
common folk, where the Salton Sea sought to deprive, you’ve not only come to thrive, but encapsulate a second coming of the renaissance during a time it’s needed most, and in a place
outright most unsuspecting. From Dusk till Dawn opens with the line, “Somewhere, in themiddle of nowhere…” which just so happens to home one of (if not the) most surreal bar ever seen on screen. The Titty Twister is an ancient Aztec temple masquerading as a contemporaryAmerican watering hole whose neon lights bring life to a place forgotten in time—a spectacle no
different from what Biennale’s unique expression symbolizes for Bombay Beach; a coming-of-age reunion for dreamers, lovers, seekers, creators, misfits, punk rockers, and smooth jazzers
alike. To put simply, there is no better place than your home to erect this installation, and no
better time than now—upon our last judgement.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Bringing our vision and unique spin on the Titty Twister to life is something our crew has been working on for some time now in preparation for the right opportunity to present itself. So, while in the early stages in regard to Biennale, we are much furth

fields.Project Description: Our show has chapters world-wide and can exist within an existing BB environment that consists of a stage area with viewing space for up to 100 guests, and with a intimate breakout space for private poetry readings. We bring lace curtains, lamps, and vintage decor to convey sneaking off for a tryst in one of our poetry whores' private boudoirs. As an immersive theatre experience the most pivotal aspect is the cast of characters that bring the imagined world to life and engage the audience, so structural set up is intentionally minimal.

fields.Audience Engagement: Best experienced as an evening show, the audience enters an immersive concept world of a turn of the century brothel that deals in poetry and cabaret decadence. Aside from enjoying an on-stage show of poetry and burlesque (poetry of the flesh), 3 intermissions allow time to proposition our poets for private 1:1 readings in their boudoirs in exchange for tokens, while a jazz band plays to set the sultry mood.
fields.Theme Engagement: Yes, Sex Work innately draws on archetypal constructs of perversion, desire, and humanity's innate ache for communion. This show empowers sex work as a method of vulnerable connection through both body and mind. Questioning what we have culturally cast out as profane, and what pieces of intimacy we've severed ourselves from.
fields.Risk & Safety: Nope.
fields.Location Submittal: Joe Cole's project space could be one option, but open to recommendation.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): Yes, The Poetry Brothel has 6 US chapters and more worldwide operating their own shows locally 2-4 times per year, plus activations at festivals like New York Poetry Festival and Electric Forest.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Reimagining how intimacy services expose our shared humanity, among hearts, among strangers… is a necessary remembrance in a cultural moment defined by alienation, performance, and transactional connection. Prostitution is said to be the oldest profession, so to prose-titute one’s own eroticism through words confronts societal norms that constrict vulnerably with strangers, and instead, reclaims agency through artistic alchemy.

Within The Last Judgment theme, this work calls for a reckoning of honesty: what we are willing to trade for intimacy, what do we withhold, and what do we secretly long to be witnessed in? What is deemed sacred, and what is cast into the shadows as profane? From a God’s eye view, were they ever truly separate?

Spoken word whispered soul-to-soul in candlelit privacy pierces through the desolation of menial encounter, delving into our shared interior – a meeting ground of our innermost reaches of collective consciousness revealed through the play of emotion, experience, hurt, and ache for union with the mystery that lies beyond and between bodies. Arousal was never limited to form. Alas, we are not alone in feeling.
fields.Why BBB (Grant): BBB’s underground, renegade ethos creates the ideal container for The Poetry Brothel’s inquiry into denied pleasure and unacknowledged longing. As a subversive art form, the work thrives in spaces that welcome risk and challenge moral comfort. So, in a landscape that already dismantles meaning, The Poetry Brothel asks what remains when nothing is promised except raw recognition between strangers.

fields.Project Description:
We propose to transform one of the BB Estates houses into an immersive tea lounge performance.

Last year at BB Convivium, we created a similar experience and would love to bring it back in a new form.

The first room is transformed into an old USSR store with empty shelves — something many Ukrainians remember from childhood. A greeter welcomes guests, offers black tea, water, sugar, and simple conversation. Visitors arrive expecting a cozy tea lounge and sound healing, but instead encounter scarcity and confusion.

After some time, the greeter opens a hidden door.

Behind it is a secret lounge filled with fabric, pillows, rugs, artwork, and warm light. Inside, geishas serve tea, dance, and play. They are slightly silly, a little wild, fully alive. The experience gradually transforms into sound healing. The ending is unexpected: the greeter enters again and tells guests it is time to leave.

The project explores the contrast between scarcity and abundance. Having nothing does not mean your soul is poor. And having material abundance does not guarantee inner fullness. The work invites reflection through humor, surprise, and ritual. 

Talks only a greater, all other actors are speechless there.

This project was created specifically for Bombay Beach and has not been presented anywhere else. Last year, Dmytro and I transformed one of the estate buildings, and it felt like the perfect setting. We would love to use the same building again, if possible.

We can present the performance over two nights, for three hours each evening. Each session lasts 20–30 minutes and accommodates 15–20 guests in the second room. After the scheduled sessions, the lounge can remain open to the public. Last year we served tea late into the night, and guests continued gathering there organically.

We had many happy visitors and would be honored to bring this experience back.

Guests arrive expecting comfort and are first confronted with emptiness. They must sit inside scarcity, confusion, and their own expectations. Then a hidden world opens — abundance, play, ritual, beauty. At the end, they are asked to leave.

The work becomes a quiet judgement of values.
What feels rich?
What feels poor?
What do we think we deserve?
Why are those crazy geishas are Ukrainians? What’s going on?

In the end, nothing material remains. Only the inner state travels with you.

fields.Project Description:
I’m going to make a cross out of two used railroad ties, hopefully sourced locally or within Imperial County. Tao has offered to donate two of his ties from ZigZag, so I just need to assess them.

I will temporarily install it at a TBD location at a rough approximation of the angle it would be if carried on the shoulder of a person. Then, over the course of a few hours, I will paint it using a variety of styles I have developed through my Jupiter Sunsets project, as well as inspired by the Stations of the Cross. And lit up in my trademark UV lights. 

This live show may be done in collaboration with someone playing music; however, it will likely instead just be on a big speaker, using a playlist chosen by the artist as an audio representation of the motifs of the Stations of the Cross. This does not mean religious music or Gregorian chants; rather, it will be like selecting Tupac Shakir’s “Mama” for the Station where Jesus meets his mother.

Railroad ties are treated with creosote, which, beyond the toxicity from touching or being around it, may limit the ability of the paint to adhere to the surface, making the performance art aspect truly ephemeral in nature, as the “finished” piece may have very little paint adhering to it. Doing new things live for the first time is part of my practice, so even I don’t know how the paint will stick to it.

All of the paint that falls onto the drop cloths will be repurposed onto canvases and other materials to make other paintings or art. 

My goal will be to work with a property owner on the beach to install it permanently at a similar angle, so people can position themselves underneath the meeting point. This will involve rebar or other metal reinforcement being installed into poured concrete in the ground and into the wood to make it incredibly sturdy. 

Over time, the wind and environment will naturally wear the wood down, with the paint and wood being baked and changed under the sun.
fields.Support Needed: (1) possible orientation of the cross, touching the ground at 2 points (2) possible orientation of the cross, which would require additional support from the short part of the cross to the ground (3) showing an example of how people can engage with the final, installed version of the piece i.e. bearing their cross (4) an example of me painting something live, in this case an 8x8 mural; I have many different painting styles (5) an example of an object I’ve previously painted (6) an example of me making art in Bombay Beach (Convivium 2025); I painted several murals as part of a larger performance art piece
fields.Abridged Project Text: I’m building a cross out of railroad ties and will do a live art show around it involving paint. Later, I would like to install it somewhere, possibly on the beach. Inspired in part by my Catholic upbringing, the state of the world, and my professional and artistic goals.
fields.Technical Support:
Will need some lighting and electricity to power that and sound, as well as place for this to take place. I’m hoping to work with a lighting designer in town (Robbie) and should be able to handle all electricity needs through him/myself. Likewise, I have a giant speaker that can power the sound.

POP is a likely destination for this to take place; however, there is an option of doing all of this at the final installation point on the beach, so if that place can be located prior to the Biennale, that will save an additional step.

Will also need some people to help move and install this piece as it is quite heavy.

Installing this permanently will need technical advice and guidance on anchoring it safely.

fields.Audience Engagement:
The audience at the live show will be invited to observe, get closer into the splash zone, and even participate by picking out colors or pouring the paint themselves under the artist’s direction. This piece is not intended to be either religious or sacrilegious, but instead to offer a platform for people to engage with the piece as they see fit.

The final installation will offer those who visit it the opportunity to feel the weight of the cross that Jesus himself carried, a way to put themselves in the stylized view of such a person, and to imagine themselves going through a very deep struggle to redeem all of mankind.

There will be subtle encouragement of street artists or other visitors to add their markings to the wood, whether carving or marking or graffiti or prayers written.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Throughout the Catholic season of Lent, observants often attend Stations of the Cross. This devotion commemorates Jesus Christ’s last day on earth from condemnation to burial.

This piece is literally a representation of the last judgement of someone who would be at the center of *the* Last Judgment.

This piece is inspired by the local trains, the interplay of religious themes at work in the town, and how people in this town — whether visitor or long time local — engage with each other.

The artist brings in his Catholic upbringing and his modern spirtuality, as well as his personal and professional aspirations in activism, consulting, organizing, art, and other projects in the notion of “taking up one’s cross” i.e. A CROSS TO BEAR.
fields.Risk & Safety:
The creosote in the railroad ties is toxic; all people involved with handling the wood will need to wear gloves, long sleeves, and possibly other PPE.

To support this heavy piece standing upright, concrete and anchors will be needed.
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
The notions of martyrdom and sacrifice are intrinsically tied to an artist pursuing their craft with a passionate, intense single-mindedness. For many artists, there is a lot of hardship and agony in their journey, and this struggle may be invisible to their friends and family. “Why don’t you get a real job?” when it would be harder indeed not to live a life steeped in creation. A CROSS TO BEAR is a physical representation of this struggle.
In recent years, there has been a co-opting of powerful symbols by far-right extremists, often antithetically so. Among these are patriotic and religious symbols; as Sinclair Lewis said, “"When fascism comes to America it will be wrapped in the flag and carrying a cross." Like the anti-ICE and No Kings protesters carrying American flags, A CROSS TO BEAR refuses to cede this symbol to the far right. And what could be more powerful than making this cross the centerpiece of an explosion of art, creativity, community, and joy, all of which are antithetical to the fascist’s death march into darkness.
This piece is neither intended to be sacrilegious nor religious in nature, although some may claim it is one or the other. Rather, this reimagines the symbol for the modern era, a testament to the sacrifice all of us make in pursuit of our individual godhood.
I want those who engage with the piece after it is installed to consider the weight of the cross compared to the weight of their problems and know that there is more to their journey.
As to the process, I will build the cross with the help of a few collaborators and either ready it to be painted at the POP lot while in search of a permanent way to install it, or install it and paint it at the final resting spot. I have had a fairly lengthy discussion with an artist who has built large-scale art installations using painted railroad ties, so I am aware of the challenges of working with railroad ties and painting them. As all of my art has a live element, and all of my art contains multiple prototypes, I will work through any obstacles or challenges presented live in front of my audience, inviting observers to see a deeper view of an artist deep within the creative process.
I have worked on a number of projects in Bombay Beach over the last 4 years, including live painting under lights, live performance art, building in community, and collaborating with teams to bring projects to life. This will be my first permanently installed piece in town.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The notion of working with railroad ties was first inspired by driving by the piles of old railroad ties alongside the railroad while driving south on the 111 last year. The train is an ever present character in a town full of them, and there are railroad ties used throughout town for various purposes.
Jack’s church on the beach is a beautiful, functional piece of art; the printed out bible quote signs tacked up in a few places make their case in an uncreative, bland, and BORING way. 
This piece is also inspired by the resilience of those in town who carry heavy crosses on their shoulders of poverty, heat, and being a last-mile services community — and those who unnecessarily make themselves out to be martyrs. For both, a real life example that their cross isn’t so heavy.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): Past the conception stage and planning to begin construction before the end of the month.

fields.Project Description:
We are creating a large-scale puppet show, bringing the audience into a surreal and shifting world that never settles on rules of engagement. It will be performed by the same folks who built the puppets: our 6-person puppetry collective. We will also be accompanying ourselves as musicians.

There will be roughly six discrete sections/scenes/sketches in the show, each 5-10 minutes long. While through lines exist in the scenes & transitions, and the intro & outro, each section of the show is mostly independent of the others. The performers in puppets will be mostly silent, soundtracked by the aforementioned musical accompaniment. 

Though we lead the audience along to “dream logic” conclusions, the shows don’t tend to feel “dreamy” – the tangible handmade quality of our puppets and audience interaction keeps the show very much embodied in the physical world. 

Our shows emphasize the clown-y comedy of how ridiculous it is to even attempt ambitious DIY puppetry. Laughs come from the simple communal pleasures of oohing and aahing at extremely silly, cartoon-inspired visual comedy that come off as both high-effort and low-effort. The audience’s pleasures come from the joys of seeing something visually impressive that is also clearly homemade, and they come from seeing something clearly so homemade fall apart in a hilarious way that performers have to roll with in a professional manner. We look for a reaction that is one part “I can’t believe they’re doing this,” two parts “Hey, I could do this too!”, and three parts “But what kind of insane people would spend so much time and effort actually doing this?”
fields.Support Needed: Thank you for considering us!!
fields.Abridged Project Text: Freak Nature’s giant puppet immersive theater have time again narrowly avoided achieving world peace. Enter Out of Body Expo, a world of DIY puppetry and reality-bending experimentation. Buckle up and lock in 4 a live-scored performexperience that lies just beyond what our flesh brains can imagine.
fields.Technical Support:
We will need electricity to power some musical instruments, a mixer, and amps. Shouldn’t need more than a power strip or two, as our setup is not complex. We’re using both mic’d up acoustic instruments and electronic instruments.

Ideally, the show would take place in an outdoor area, with a large barrier/tented area we could utilize as a “backstage” area for puppet storage. A traditional elevated stage would work well, but isn’t required. We can be “site-specific” and utilize quirks of the space, but we’d at least need to perform on somewhat level ground for our own safety. We’d prefer to perform it during the daytime, as we don’t currently have plans for lighting elements needed for a nighttime performance, though we can be flexible. While we could technically stage it on an indoor stage, we plan to have some giant puppets that are 15-20 feet tall and some quite wide, so an indoor space would need to be quite large.
fields.Audience Engagement:
The performance will have an audience, mostly sitting and likely some standing at the edges of the assembled crowd area. The beginning of the show will include a calling of attention to itself, a fanfare or herding of sorts, a dramatic introduction.

While aspects of the performance will include crowd participation and direct interaction with giant puppets, it’s primarily something that will be observed from a static position. However, we always include “4D” elements – an audience member may be eaten by a puppet, they might be danced with, they may be prompted to assist a puppet with a task. 

Because of the structure of the performance being separated into separate scenes that don’t require knowledge of a previous scene, we don’t mind late arrivals and “stumbling in” to the show late in the performance.

We envision it happening in the late afternoon, at sunset, or after sunrise.
fields.Theme Engagement:
Puppetry plays a joke on the idea of any judgment of life – first judgement, Last Judgement, whenever. It’s always been a joke on God / the Gods to claim we can give and take life from clay and fabric. 

A big part of our practice came from interacting with the idea of object oriented ontology, the school of thought that all things – whether physical, incorporeal, or conceptual – can experience reality in some form, on some deep timescale. This led us to puppetry, because a line around what you can call a “puppet” can be loosely drawn around “the minimum amount you can do to give an object the appearance of having life.” If all things really do have some sort of consciousness, and if you as a human can give the appearance of life to anything, wouldn’t heaven and hell also be full of rocks, trees, lice, car engines, electrical impulses, and puppets? The Last Judgment would literally take forever to sort through.

Out of Body Expo in particular is a show that works with the idea of a physical form’s unintended consequences. In our final judgment, the sum of one’s deeds is judged. In Out of Body Expo, the entire show grows from something dead with no affect on how the rest of the show goes, so how could all deeds pre-death be the extent of God’s range of judged time?
fields.Risk & Safety: No!
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): No, it has not been shown before.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
We believe that cheaply made spectacular art heals people. We want people to see our work, and think “I want to make that” -> “I can make that” -> “I will make that” -> “I am making that, and nothing I do is a mistake.” Get there, and the world becomes a better place, world peace achieved. 

Our show asks the question: “Why not do this now? Why not make your ideas reality now?” In anarchist David Graeber’s essay, “On the Phenomenology of Giant Puppets,” he sums up the political power of ephemeral DIY puppets: “Their combination of huge size and lightness…makes them a bridge between words and reality; they represent the ability to start to make ideas real and take on solid form, to make our view of the world into something of equal physical bulk and greater spectacular power even to the engines of state violence that stand against it… for them either to become fully solid, or fully manipulable by a single individual, would contradict the point.” This message about forcing imaginary ideas into reality is always politically relevant, and implicit in all our shows. 

We approach puppetry like improvisers and clowns rather than as technicians. We build the puppets improvisationally, and we improvise parts of our performance based on the reactions audience and puppet. Like junk artists at the Salton Sea, we rip apart old objects and put them back together in surprising new ways, and we rip apart old ideas and put them back together on stage.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
While the surface of our work is comedy, the substance of our shows has consistently addressed the relationship between humans & the natural world, digging into the ways “nature” is filtered and processed by society at large.

It’s in our name, and a huge part of our 2024 eponymous show. That performance was set in a surreal world that never decides if it’s a national park or a theme park, hinting at something broken in our definition of what is “natural.” In creating that show, the Salton Sea was always on our minds. With Bombay Beach’s history as a former wetland, then resort destination, then site of ecological disaster & decline, “Freak Nature” could be understood as some version of the past, present, and future of the Salton Sea, all blended into one semi-natural (post-natural?) space and time. 

This new show, loosely centered around a forgotten machine at a long-gone expo, and the entities that ebb and flow around it, can be understood as a metaphor for Bombay Beach and similar communities. The machine of the old times promises a solution for a dying world, but it never turns on. The things that grow around the dead machine incidentally fulfill the machine’s original promise, through community. We may never reach a point where the Salton Sea is a recovered wetland, but the communities and living history are what continue to bring meaning to the place and fulfill its promise.
fields.Project Stage (Grant): We’ve begun building the new puppets and setpieces for this show, and we have an outline of the arc of the show.  We devise our shows in piecemeal, and different sketches and scenes get reshuffled in and out as we settle on stuff that gels together. Our p
fields.Additional Artists: Deedee Anderson, Bobby Bergin, Aubrielle Hvolboll, Siena Jeakle, Matthew Sater, Mori Wexler

fields.Project Name: Live painting performances with musicians and DJs on stages. Workshops.
fields.Project Description:
This project is a live, collaborative art experience combining on-stage live painting performances with musicians and a series of transformational workshops, unfolding in direct dialogue with the energy, community, and desert environment of BBB in Joshua Tree.

Live Painting Performances (On Stage)

The core of the project takes place on stage with headlining musicians. Each painting begins precisely when the musician begins their set. Sound initiates the process; rhythm, melody, and atmosphere guide the movement of color and form. The painting and the music evolve together in real time and conclude together as a single, shared creation.

This is not decorative or background visual art. It is a co-creative performance—a live dialogue between painter and musician. Every mark responds directly to the music being created in that moment. The audience witnesses the full emergence of the work, from the first gesture to the final symbol, shaped entirely by the live set.

After the stage performance, the painting may be refined on site through subtle details, allowing the work to fully integrate while preserving the integrity of the live moment.

Interactive Paintings with Mirrors

A key element of this project is interactivity through reflection. Each painting incorporates mirror elements embedded within the imagery. After the performance, the completed work is placed offstage and becomes an experiential object rather than a static artwork.

Participants are invited to approach the painting, see themselves reflected within the mythic symbols, and interact with it directly. People naturally take selfies, but more importantly, they see themselves inside the artwork, alongside reflections of others and the surrounding community. The painting becomes a living mirror—revealing connection, presence, and shared identity.

The artwork remains accessible as a space for contemplation, communication, and personal insight. Viewers are invited to sit with the painting, receive messages, and experience guidance through reflection and resonance.

Workshops

Alongside the performances, I offer a series of participatory workshops, exactly as titled:
	•	Art and Meditation
	•	Paint Your Dream
	•	Main Goddesses and Goddess Archetypes in Greek Mythology
	•	Balance Between Masculine and Feminine
	•	Symbols of the World
fields.Support Needed: I have many years of experience collaborating with projection artists, including long-term work with Luma Visuals at Playa Alchemist at Burning Man, as well as other festivals. Through these collaborations, my paintings have been translated into large-scale projections, allowing symbolic, mythological imagery to live beyond the canvas and become immersive, shared environments.  While this project is designed to function simply and independently, I carry a long-term dream of expanding the visual dimension through projection—especially in a place like Bombay Beach, where scale, darkness, and openness invite myth to unfold. My work is deeply rooted in symbols, gods and goddesses, and states of consciousness, and projection allows these archetypal images to surround and speak to the collective in a powerful way.  If opportunities arise for projection collaboration during the Biennale, I would welcome them with excitement and flexibility, adapting to what is possible while honoring the spirit and resources of the event.
fields.Notes: k 3/14: are you still doing this ?
fields.Abridged Project Text: A live painting performance where music, myth, and ancient symbols converge. Gamayuna channels archetypal imagery in real time, transforming sound into vision and the stage into a ritual space. Each work invites the audience into remembrance.
fields.Technical Support:
The project is designed to be technically simple and adaptable. For on-stage live painting performances, focused stage lighting on the canvas and painting area is essential. Lighting should provide consistent, even illumination without strong color distortion, allowing the audience to clearly see the painting process in real time.

Standard electrical access is required to support lighting and any small auxiliary equipment. No heavy power draw is needed.

An optional simultaneous projection of the painting process onto an existing screen or LED wall is welcome, if available. This can be achieved via a live camera feed focused on the canvas, allowing larger audiences to follow the details of the painting as it unfolds. Projection is not mandatory but enhances visibility in larger or nighttime settings.

The project is suitable for outdoor stages and integrates easily into existing musical setups. No indoor space is required. The painting setup has a small footprint and does not interfere with musicians or stage flow.
fields.Audience Engagement:
Audience engagement is fluid and multi-layered. People may intentionally gather for a scheduled on-stage live painting performance or stumble upon it late at night, drawn by music and movement. During performances, the audience witnesses a real-time collaboration between painter and musician, arriving, staying, or moving freely.

After the performance, engagement becomes personal and interactive. The completed painting remains accessible offstage, incorporating mirror elements that invite viewers to see themselves reflected within the imagery.

Workshops offer direct participation, inviting people to create and reflect using symbols and intuition, regardless of prior art experience. Together, the project supports observation, participation, and spontaneous discovery.
fields.Theme Engagement: This project approaches The Last Judgement as an inner moment rather than an external verdict. The live painting performances unfold in real time, without correction or erasure, reflecting the truth of being fully seen in the present moment. Embedded mirror elements invite viewers to encounter themselves inside the artwork, alongside others and the surrounding community. There is no judgement imposed—only reflection. In this way, The Last Judgement becomes an act of recognition, presence, and shared humanity rather than punishment or reward.
fields.Risk & Safety: No
fields.Previous Showing (Grant):
Elements of this project draw from my ongoing practice of live painting performances and collaborative work with musicians and projection artists, presented at various festivals and cultural events internationally. In these settings, I have worked on stage alongside musicians, sometimes incorporating projected visuals or live video to support the painting process.

However, this specific project configuration—combining on-stage co-creation with headlining musicians, interactive mirror-based paintings, and a structured series of workshops designed for BBB—has not been presented before. The work proposed for the 2026 Biennale is a new iteration, developed specifically in response to the context, scale, and community of BBB
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
I want to create this work now because my practice has arrived at a point where live, irreversible presence feels essential. After many years of working with mythology, symbols, and ritual through painting, I am increasingly drawn to moments where creation happens without correction—where sound, image, and time meet and cannot be edited. This is why live painting with musicians, in front of a living audience, feels not only relevant but necessary.

The question at the heart of this project is simple and difficult: what remains when we are fully seen? In the context of The Last Judgement, I am not interested in verdicts imposed from above, but in the quieter, more human moment of recognition—when we face ourselves, our reflections, and our shared responsibility to one another.

Bombay Beach and the Salton Sea feel like the right place for this inquiry. This landscape already carries the weight of judgement: ecological collapse, abandonment, resilience, beauty, and contradiction existing side by side. Nothing here is resolved, yet everything is visible. That honesty deeply resonates with my work. I am drawn to places that do not hide their scars, where transformation is not polished but real.

The process I envision is collaborative and responsive. On stage, I co-create with musicians, beginning and ending each painting in direct dialogue with their live sets. Offstage, the paintings remain accessible, incorporating mirror elements so that viewers see themselves inside the imagery—literally reflected among symbols, myths, and one another. The audience is not outside the work; they complete it.

Workshops extend this engagement further, inviting participants to explore their own symbols, dreams, and inner myths. These are not didactic spaces but open laboratories of intuition, listening, and expression. Community engagement happens organically—through witnessing, reflection, conversation, and return visits to the artwork over time.

Experimentation is central. Each performance is different, shaped by the musician, the moment, the desert air, and the people present. There is risk involved, and that risk is intentional. In a world saturated with repetition and control, I am interested in what emerges when we allow uncertainty.

Ultimately, this project is about shared presence at the edge of judgement—not as punishment or salvation, but as awareness. At BBB, where art and environment already ask difficult questions, I see this work as a living offering: reflective, collaborative, and honest enough to meet the desert where it stands.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
This project belongs in Bombay Beach because it is a place where nothing is hidden. The Salton Sea carries visible histories of ambition, collapse, survival, and refusal to disappear. Beauty and damage coexist openly here, without resolution. That honesty mirrors the core of my practice.

Bombay Beach is not a neutral site—it is an archive. Layers of human intention, environmental consequence, and ongoing presence remain exposed in the landscape. My work engages places where time is felt physically, where history has not been erased or sanitized. The desert here does not offer transcendence; it offers truth.

The Bombay Beach Biennale is unique in that it honors work that responds to place rather than imposing spectacle. It welcomes art that is temporary, imperfect, and in dialogue with community and environment. This aligns deeply with my approach to live painting, which unfolds in real time and cannot be corrected or polished after the fact.

The interactive mirror elements in my paintings resonate powerfully here. Against the vastness of the Salton Sea and the openness of the desert, reflection becomes unavoidable—personal and collective. Viewers see themselves inside the work, alongside others, within a landscape already asking questions about responsibility, visibility, and care.

This project does not attempt to fix or explain Bombay Beach. It listens, reflects, and responds—allowing the place and its people to remain central to the experience.

fields.Project Description:
A shared exhibition between aunt and nephew artists, both with a long personal history with the Imperial Valley and the Salton Sea. 

Jake Wade’s “Derangers” are a series of acrylic on canvas paintings… commentary on how deranged Western history and culture is… the mania, bloodshed, and madness. While simultaneously honoring: this is home. 

Sandi Wheaton’s color digital photographs of the Salton Sea date from 2005-2009, a time when the beaches were still flooded, before the dried lakebed of Bombay Beach became an artist’s canvas.

fields.Project Name: About To Pop Off In Ways You Can’t Even Imagine
fields.Project Description:
In 2025, an ad-hoc group of artists came together at the POP lot (on 5th, stretching from B to C) to do something different. We set out to create a platform of expression for artists of many different stripes to share their art, and to create within an aligned community looking to make Bombay Beach as a whole better through our affiliation with MACEPs, or Mz Aqua’s Community Enrichment Projects, of which POP is one such project.

We are entering Year 2, and we are planning an ambitious program for the Biennale. The lot itself will be transformed by the end of the season as our artists bring in installations and lighting. The large stack of steel trusses will be transformed into the Crane at the other end of town. We will be finishing the floor in the other half of the building, making it a perfect site for talks, classes, workshops, and other sorts of events.

We have been in talks with dozens of artists, dreamers, doers, thinkers, performers, and more to come be part of POP and the Biennale. These will be a mix of locals, Biennale veterans, and aligned artist friends/collaborators who have been intrigued by Bombay Beach for years. 

We are currently confident in being able to house or otherwise find space for anyone coming into town from within our own resources and connections. We are seeking a small amount of funding in order to help facilitate transportation costs for any artists who may need it to make it to Bombay Beach. The vast majority of this budget will be gas money reimbursement, along with a few plane tickets, and some other miscellaneous costs.

This is completely in line with our mission to provide a platform for expression; sometimes, all artists need to shine on stage is someone to build the steps so they can walk onto it.
fields.Support Needed: The Let’s get ready to POP pdf deck is the current (almost finished with the new final version) deck we using to talk to artists about being part of our project.  The rest of these are about the photos attached.   (1) This is an image of POP in action during our Year 1 soft launch party in 2025. (2) This is a view from the outside with the big blue light ground chandelier installation set up, which will make a return this year. (3) This is an exterior shot of the B half of the POP lot (ignore the flooding from the water main that burst on 5th) (4) This is an exterior shot of the C half of the POP lot (ignore the flooding from the water main that burst on 5th)
fields.Abridged Project Text: We’re gearing up for year 2 at the POP lot. We plan to have a variety of art installations, live performances, talks, workshops, etc. We are seeking funding to help facilitate transportation and some small miscellaneous costs.
fields.Technical Support: Ideally, we would be able to coordinate with our back neighbors on some/all of our electrical needs, and making up the rest of the shortfall in solar and generators. Eventually, the entire project will be entirely solar run, but that will be a Year 3 goal.

fields.Audience Engagement:
All of the above, and more. We will have a very diverse range of programming. 

Our most important philosophy is: You are an artist. So, anyone who encounters our space and seeks to engage with it is an artist. Whether that is a new revelation for them or they have been deep within that practice for many years.

POP is a platform for expression, so we envision that, even at 3AM, if someone comes into our space, they are immersed into a creative environment where they can learn from other artists, ad hoc collaborate with them, and, schedule permitting, be facilitated into taking the stage themselves or otherwise sharing their art with others.
fields.Theme Engagement:
The theme of the Biennale is being shared with all of our artists, and we are excited to see how they interpret The Last Judgement. One of the leads for POP, Joe Cole, will be doing his Jupiter Sunsets live art on his A CROSS TO BEAR installation at the POP lot (unless he is able to do it at the TBD final installation site).

Separately, in a manner more closely related to the town, the building on the POP lot used to be a restaurant in the glory days of Bombay Beach. In recent memory, this seemingly abandoned building has been a favorite of the graffiti artists who periodically come through town, representing an earlier iteration of everything this town was. But now, the POP lot will take on a trajectory mirroring what Bombay Beach as a whole has gone through: salvation. Salvation through art — and time and care and commitment and communnal effort.

And what does The Last Judgment not represent but the salvation of all who go through that divine adjudication?
fields.Risk & Safety: There will likely be some digging involved with installing some of the art installations, and the access to the roof is currently limited to those who can clamber up the window box (easy climb). Risk will be mitigated by empowering everyone on site to take safety seriously (safety second?) and that they can be on the look out for any issues. Participants only!
fields.Location Submittal: POP lot (5BC)
fields.Previous Showing (Grant): The first year of this venue project was in 2025, where we had a soft opening that was more of a stress test than anything. We will be making significant upgrades to the property in the next 6 weeks, including our aim to bring 3 full days of programming.
fields.Letter of Interest (Grant):
Like other artists involved with this project, I have made a commitment to MACEPs (Mz Aqua’s Community Enrichment Projects) for the next 5-10 years. The overall mission is to beautify and make functional (or further so) various projects and properties around Bombay Beach, including spaces and art available to the whole town, as well as various functional upgrades. The new Henderson Hall on the corner of B and 2nd is one such project, and we anticipate that this venue will also be separately ready for the community to use by the time of the Biennale.
POP is another one of the flagship venues we are working on. Unlike Henderson Hall, we envision POP continuing to change and evolve over the years, with new installations and artists being part of the presentation. We see POP as a platform for expression, and we are excited to grow the artist community around it this year, drawn from locals, Biennale veterans, and other aligned artists. 
I see the vast potential of continuing to grow a community to create in collaboration with each other. It’s micro version of what takes place in Bombay Beach every winter. It’s a 3 month/years long build week vs the half week build week at Burning Man. 
By bringing in aligned artists and giving them a platform for their expression, whether it is painting a mural or interior decorating or art installations or giving a talk or leading a class or guiding a workshop or playing music or something else, we are enabling those who have something to say or show others. We are giving dreamers tanigiblity. We are introducing highly creative and highly collaborative people to each other, and allowing magic to unfold. With everyone who comes in being invited to learn, grow, participate, collaborate, and be inspired.
And, for me personally, one of my biggest reasons for working on this now is that I am seeking the learning through doing of taking my skills and talents for throwing events, leading large teams, building community, and inspiring people at scale to new heights. I’ve been preparing for decades for these next 6 weeks. This is a giant experiment. Like all of my art forms, POP year 2 is full of prototypes for everything that comes next.
fields.Why BBB (Grant):
The first time I came to Bombay Beach, it was for a long afternoon the week before the 2023 Biennale. If I wasn’t driving toward Phoenix to catch a flight, I would have stayed much longer, although I came back several times that year, including to make some art on my own. I instatly saw the allure and potential of Bombay Beach and the Biennale season to create and collaborate in community, learning from other artists and growing within my practice. Over the last 3 winters, I have spent increasing amounts of time out here and my commitment to the town and this community has grown along with my talents and vision for my art and my creative practice.
MACEPs represent everything that is best about the modern Bombay Beach while authentically staying true to the history of the town. For instance, Henderson Hall is named after Mz Aqua’s dad, and his big truck currently on 1st will eventually be parked outside as a historical monument. All of the MACEPs, including POP are already here in town. Like POP, many of these properties have been unused or in disrepair; we envision bringing beauty, function, art, and community to these properties.
